Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Reduced KL Loss is presented as essential for compact sub-networks, but no ablation compares it to standard full-vocabulary KL distillation on acceptance rate or speedup—its independent contribution is unquantified. - No baseline from non-speculative flash-I/O-optimized inference (e.g., PowerInfer, LLM in a Flash with row-column bundling) is included; it is unclear how much of CATS's wall-clock advantage over vanilla inference is attributable to speculative decoding logic vs. efficient flash scheduling, or whether CATS could be combined with those I/O optimizations. - KV-cache pressure at long sequence lengths is dismissed as non-dominant at small batch sizes, but no experiment tests sequences beyond typical chat lengths; at long contexts the DRAM budget available for L_SV could shrink significantly, and the framework provides no guidance for dynamic L_SV adjustment. - GPT-4o-as-judge quality evaluation on MT-Bench uses a single temperature sweep with no variance estimates—the quality-speed Pareto claim rests on a thin statistical basis. - Adapter training requires the target model and 68,000-sample distillation; practitioners wanting to apply CATS to fine-tuned or private models must repeat this process, a non-trivial prerequisite not analyzed in terms of sample efficiency or sensitivity. Bottom Line Practitioners deploying transformer-based LLMs (7B–13B) on DRAM-constrained edge hardware should read this paper—it is the first speculative decoding method designed around the flash-memory regime and delivers hardware-validated, benchmark-consistent speedups that existing self-speculative methods demonstrably cannot achieve in this setting.
Search Results 2605.11186 PDF
#28 — Highly Innovative (76)

ResiHP: Taming LLM Training Failures with Dynamic Hybrid

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ResiHP, a resilient LLM training system that detects both fail-stop and fail-slow GPU failures and mitigates them via dynamic, cross-dimensional adaptation of tensor, pipeline, and data parallelism. - Why it's important: Hardware failures in large GPU clusters waste enormous resources (Meta reports 178,000 GPU hours lost during OPT-175B training), and existing systems address only individual parallelism dimensions, leaving amplified performance skew uncontained as failures cascade from TP groups through PP bubbles to DP synchronization delays. - How it does it: A workload-aware execution time predictor analytically separates MLP (linear) and self-attention (quadratic) cost components to filter sequence-length-induced iteration-time fluctuations from genuine device degradation, enabling accurate fail-slow detection; a Scheduler then progressively adapts TP group sizes, pipeline layer partitions, and DP workload migration in sequence to contain failure propagation. - The good: On a 256 A100 GPU cluster with mixed fail-stop and fail-slow failures, ResiHP sustains training at failure frequencies (every 30 minutes) that cause all four baselines to abort, achieving 1.48–4.39× throughput improvement over ReCycle. - The missing: The evaluation ceiling is 256 GPUs; the production-scale LLM training runs that motivate the work (e.g., OPT-175B, GPT-4 scale) use 1,000–10,000+ GPUs, and no evidence is provided that the cross-dimensional coordination heuristics remain efficient or correct at that scale. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and ML infrastructure engineers designing resilient large-scale GPU training pipelines should read this; those focused on model architecture or algorithm-level training efficiency can skip it.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (78) is high — addresses a real and expensive production problem with a working 9k-line implementation and demonstrated sustained training under failure regimes that abort all baselines; tempered by no code release and 256-GPU ceiling vs. claimed motivation at 1000s. Novelty (72) — joint cross-dimensional TP/PP/DP adaptation with the analytic MLP+attention decomposition for false-positive suppression is a genuine systems contribution. Rigor (75) — ablations, 95% CIs on aggregate results, convergence validation, but the 25% threshold is un-ablated and strengthened baselines are author-reimplemented. Completeness (75) — SDC scope and framework portability honestly acknowledged. Resilience (72) — survives most scrutiny but the production-scale extrapolation is the obvious attack vector. Non-Obviousness (72) — the workload-aware decomposition that filters sequence-length noise is the genuinely non-obvious bit. Composite: 0.15*72 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*72 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.30*78 = 74.9.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Large-scale LLM training across tens of thousands of GPUs inevitably encounters hardware failures. Under hybrid parallelism (DP + TP + PP), a single degraded device's slowdown does not stay local: one fail-slow GPU in a TP group of 4 delays 3 additional GPUs, which propagates as pipeline bubbles to 12 more across PP stages, and ultimately idles 16 DP-synchronized GPUs — a 25.43× amplification of idle GPU time measured on LLaMA 2-13B with (TP,DP,PP)=(4,2,4). Fail-slow failures are particularly insidious because existing detectors (e.g., Greyhound) use raw iteration time as a proxy for device performance, but real-world datasets with variable sequence lengths (e.g., GitHub) cause inherent iteration-time fluctuations that trigger false positives. Prior systems (ReCycle, Oobleck, Greyhound, Adaptra) optimize individual parallelism dimensions in isolation, creating secondary workload imbalances across the other two dimensions. Key Idea The central insight is that micro-batch execution time under sequence packing decomposes analytically into a linear MLP term and a quadratic self-attention term (T_MB ≈ αN + β∑lᵢ², where lᵢ are per-document lengths), making it possible to predict the expected healthy iteration time via a DAG-based pipeline simulator without an LLM call. Fail-slow anomaly detection operates on the residual after subtracting this prediction, eliminating workload-induced false positives before invoking the expensive validation phase. Once failures are correctly identified, progressive cross-dimensional adaptation (TP first, then PP, then DP) contains propagation at each stage rather than letting it cascade to the next. Methodology ResiHP comprises two decoupled components — Detector and Scheduler — connected via a failure-report channel. Detector (§5): Fail-stop detection uses a hierarchical TCP heartbeat mechanism where node-local monitors aggregate worker liveness signals and a central coordinator tracks node-level status, scaling overhead with node count rather than device count. Fail-slow detection follows Greyhound's Bayesian change-point detection on iteration-time series [1], but inserts a workload-aware filtering step: for each candidate change point, the Detector computes the predicted healthy iteration time via topological traversal of a pipeline DAG G=(V,E) using t_start(v) = max_{u∈pred(v)}(t_start(u) + T_cost(u) + T_edge(u,v)). A change point triggers validation only if the observed time exceeds the prediction by >25%; otherwise it is removed from the series as benign. Model parameters α and β are profiled during an initial warm-up phase. Scheduler (§6) performs three-stage progressive adaptation: (1) TP adaptation: Constructs candidate TP subgroups of power-of-two sizes k ∈ {k_min,...,|G'|}, selects S* = argmax_{k∈K}(k · mean_{i∈S_k} pᵢ) where pᵢ is each device's normalized healthy throughput, greedily prioritizing fast devices while including slow ones when excluded capacity matters. Introduces scatter/gather P2P communication to handle heterogeneous TP degrees across groups — tensors are scattered intra-node over NVLink, then sent once inter-node over InfiniBand. (2) PP adaptation: Repartitions layers across PP stages to balance iteration time, reallocating layers from straggler PP groups to healthy ones. (3) DP adaptation: Formulates makespan minimization (min_X max_{d∈D} T_makespan(d)) subject to execution completeness, dependency preservation, and memory capacity constraints (Eq. 5); solves via a progress-aware online heuristic (Algorithm 1) rather than MIP to meet latency requirements. Critical design decisions: (1) The 25% threshold for triggering fail-slow validation is stated but not ablated — its sensitivity to failure severity and dataset characteristics is unexplored. (2) Power-of-two TP degree constraint is justified by attention-head divisibility and kernel efficiency. (3) Heuristic DP migration solver avoids MIP intractability for online scheduling; the approximation ratio versus optimal is not characterized. Key Results Detection: Micro-batch time predictor achieves 1.19–1.58% MAPE; iteration-time predictor achieves 2.81–5.06% MAPE across Qwen 2.5-7B (8K seq), Qwen 2.5-14B (16K seq), and LLaMA 2-13B (32K seq). False alarms reduced from 3.7–8.7 per job (Greyhound) to 0.0–0.3 per job (ResiHP). Anomaly-detection overhead is 34–49ms per invocation vs. 1.24–1.72s for Greyhound. Fail-slow accuracy >99.0%; fail-stop accuracy 99.6%. Fail-stop handling (Table 6): At 30-minute failure frequency, Oobleck and ReCycle both abort training across all model sizes; ResiHP sustains training at 3.53–5.46 samples/s. At 2h frequency, ResiHP achieves 7.55 samples/s vs. Oobleck 6.04 and ReCycle 4.16 for LLaMA 2-7B. Fail-slow handling (Figure 9): Across weak/medium/severe severities (35%/55%/70% degradation baselines) and six model configurations, ResiHP improves over the degraded baseline by 1.32–3.31×, translating to 1.18–2.30× over Adaptra and 1.22–1.46× over Greyhound. Mixed failures (Figure 10): ResiHP achieves 1.48–4.39× over ReCycle across LLaMA 2 (7B–30B) and Qwen 2.5 (7B–32B); 1.22–4.32× over strengthened ReCycle; 1.04–3.57× over strengthened Oobleck. Large-scale (256 A100 GPUs, LLaMA 2-70B): 1.39× and 1.11× end-to-end throughput over strengthened ReCycle and strengthened Oobleck respectively. Convergence: LLaMA 2-7B trained 2,500 iterations shows identical loss trajectory to fault-free baseline (Figure 12). System overhead: 1.2–1.5% per-iteration time increase; Scheduler planning 1.44s for 32B model; communication reconstruction <2s. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The joint cross-dimensional TP/PP/DP adaptation framework for hybrid-parallel LLM training under coupled fail-stop and fail-slow failures is genuinely new. The workload-aware execution time predictor that analytically decouples sequence-length variability from device degradation is a meaningful technical contribution. The individual algorithmic building blocks — Bayesian change-point detection, layer repartition, makespan-minimizing workload migration — are extensions and combinations of known methods applied to a new and important setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study present: Figure 11 quantifies each Scheduler component's contribution (device exclusion, layer repartition, workload migration) normalized to ReCycle baseline. Multiple independent runs with 95% CI error bars are reported for aggregate results; single-run temporal traces (Figure 14) are reported without confidence intervals, explicitly noted. The 25% fail-slow detection threshold is a critical hyperparameter with no ablation. Failures are deterministically injected (nvidia-smi SM frequency lock for compute slow, side-channel bandwidth contention for network slow) rather than observed from production — the real failure signature distribution may differ. The strengthened baselines (strengthened ReCycle, strengthened Oobleck) are implemented by the ResiHP authors, not the original authors, introducing potential implementation bias. No public code or model weights released. Implementation described as ~9k lines Python within an internal Megatron-LM-like framework. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA A100 hardware; implementation builds on Megatron-LM internals, so porting to other frameworks requires reimplementing framework-specific communication primitives and the primitive dispatcher — the paper acknowledges this boundary explicitly. - Efficient: Detector adds only 1.2–1.5% per-iteration overhead; Scheduler planning costs 1.44s for 32B models; layer transfer during reconfiguration scales with model size but is amortized over training duration. - Effective: Evidence demonstrates sustained training throughput under failure regimes that abort all four baselines, and convergence-equivalent loss trajectories over 2,500 iterations confirm training semantics are preserved. - Scalable: Tested at 256 A100 GPUs maximum; no evidence at 1,000+ GPU scale where production LLM training (the stated motivation) actually runs — scalability is claimed but not demonstrated. Delivers paper form only; no public software artifact. Strengths - The analytical micro-batch time model (linear MLP + quadratic self-attention decomposition) is a principled and practically important contribution that directly solves the false-positive problem in fail-slow detection under variable-length sequence datasets — a real and underappreciated issue in production LLM training. - Progressive cross-dimensional adaptation (TP → PP → DP) is well-motivated by the measured failure amplification hierarchy and represents a genuine advance over prior systems that optimize a single parallelism dimension. - The scatter/gather P2P communication optimization for heterogeneous TP degrees is a non-trivial implementation contribution that enables TP adaptation without multiplicative InfiniBand traffic growth. - The paper validates both throughput and convergence correctness, providing evidence that reconfiguration preserves mathematical training semantics — most resilience systems papers omit this check. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that silent data corruptions (SDCs) are outside scope because they lack liveness or timing signals and require a different detection mechanism. They note that framework-specific communication primitives and primitive dispatch are not portable across training frameworks. They mention that elasticity (automatic parallelism strategy search) is complementary future work. These boundaries are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 256-GPU evaluation ceiling is the most critical gap: the paper's motivating examples (OPT-175B, GPT-4 scale) involve 1,000–16,000 GPUs, and the cross-dimensional coordination complexity, heuristic solver quality, and communication overhead of scatter/gather at larger TP/PP degrees are uncharacterized at that scale. - The 25% anomaly-detection threshold that gates fail-slow validation is never ablated; its sensitivity to mild failures (where the threshold may suppress real detections) or noisy workloads (where it may still generate false alarms) is unknown. - Deterministically injected failures (SM frequency lock, bandwidth contention) are a clean proxy but may not capture the full distribution of production hardware degradation signatures — particularly thermal throttling events and network jitter patterns. - The memory feasibility constraint in DP workload migration is checked per migration, but the paper does not report how often this constraint is binding or quantify the throughput penalty when no feasible migration exists. - The strengthened baselines are author-implemented, not from original implementors, introducing potential implementation quality bias that inflates the relative improvement. - No code release prevents independent reproduction; a 9k-line internal framework cannot be replicated from the paper description alone. Bottom Line A well-executed systems contribution that meaningfully advances resilient LLM training under hybrid parallelism — infrastructure engineers building or operating large GPU training clusters should read it, though the 256-GPU evaluation ceiling and absence of a code release prevent full confidence in the production-scale deployability claims.

#29 — Highly Innovative (76)

Marco-MoE: Open Multilingual Mixture-of-Expert Language Models with Efficient Upcycling

Fan Jiang, Yu Zhao, Chenyang Lyu, Tianqi Shi, Yichao Du, Feihu Jiang, Longyue Wang, Weihua Luo · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Marco-MoE, a family of fully open-source sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models built by fine-grained upcycling of a dense 0.6B-parameter checkpoint, yielding models (8B and 17B total params) that activate only 0.6B and 0.86B parameters per token and outperform comparably-sized dense and sparse competitors across English and 29–64 language multilingual benchmarks. - Why it's important: Expanding multilingual language model coverage within a fixed activated-parameter budget degrades per-language quality (the "curse of multilinguality"), yet dense alternatives that escape this constraint require 5–55× more training compute than this work uses. - How it does it: The FFN weight matrices of a pretrained dense model are sliced column-wise into fine-grained sub-experts, replicated with Drop-Upcycling noise injection, and scaled by N^(1/3) to correct the output-magnitude mismatch between a dense summation and a softmax-gated MoE, followed by a 5.1T-token four-stage multilingual curriculum and cascaded on-policy distillation post-training. - The good: Marco-Mini-Instruct (0.86B activated) achieves an English average of 75.5 and multilingual cultural average of 71.0, beating Qwen3-4B-Instruct (73.3 / 69.1) and Gemma3-12B-Instruct (65.8 / 67.7) while using 5–14× fewer activated parameters, at 1.56×10²³ FLOPs vs Qwen3-4B's 8.64×10²³. - The missing: No inference latency or throughput benchmarks are reported, so the wall-clock efficiency advantage of sparse activation relative to dense baselines of matched quality is not quantified. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on efficient multilingual LLMs or MoE upcycling — the fine-grained upcycling recipe, weight-scaling fix, and expert-activation linguistic analysis are all concretely useful; skip if you need a peer-reviewed result or primarily care about reasoning-heavy tasks.
Why this rank: Systems profile (compact MoE pretraining + recipe). Practical Impact (85) drives the score — fully open weights (29- and 64-language variants), training data mixtures, recipe, and demonstrated quality at 0.86B activated params vs Qwen3-4B at 5–14× the activation budget. Rigor (72) — disciplined 100B-token annealing ablations, per-stage attribution, but no seeds/CIs and the Instruct comparison disables Qwen3 thinking mode. Novelty (65) — fine-grained sub-matrix slicing + N^(1/3) scaling correction is a real technical contribution within a known paradigm. Non-Obviousness (65) for the scaling fix and emergent phylogenetic expert clustering. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) reasonable; missing inference-throughput numbers and learned-routing validation of the uniform-routing assumption. Weighted: 0.15·65 + 0.15·72 + 0.10·65 + 0.15·75 + 0.15·70 + 0.30·85 = 74.9 → 75. Bumped to 76 reflecting open-artifact completeness.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Building small LLMs that cover many languages without quality degradation is hard because a fixed parameter budget forces a trade-off between linguistic breadth and per-language depth. Existing solutions either train at prohibitive scale (Qwen3-4B: 36T tokens, Gemma3-4B: 36T tokens) or use dense architectures that lack the architectural flexibility to assign distinct capacity to different language families. Meanwhile, MoE upcycling (converting a pretrained dense model into a sparse MoE) is computationally attractive but prior work replicated entire FFN blocks, producing homogeneous experts that fail to specialize — a poor fit for the diverse structural demands of multilingual processing. Key Idea The core trick is sub-matrix splitting: instead of copying the full FFN weight matrices to initialize each expert (coarse-grained upcycling), the paper partitions each FFN weight along the intermediate dimension into N thin column slices, each of which becomes a separate fine-grained expert. This produces experts with smaller hidden dimension than the source dense FFN (e.g., 384 or 768 vs. 3072), so more of them can be held per layer (232 or 256 total experts) while keeping only 8 active. Because the MoE output is a softmax-weighted sum (magnitude ≈ 1/N of the dense output), they apply a weight scaling factor of λ = N^(1/3) to all sliced matrices before replication, restoring numerical parity with the dense initialization. Methodology The pipeline has four components. (1) Architecture: standard decoder-only transformer (GQA, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE aligned with Qwen3) with FFN layers replaced by granular MoE layers following DeepSeekMoE. (2) Upcycling: dense Qwen3-0.6B FFN weights are partitioned into N slices → N fine-grained experts; each is replicated M times to reach the target total expert count; Drop-Upcycling randomly zeros indices along the intermediate dimension of each copy and re-initializes those weights from a Gaussian matching the dropped weights' statistics; routers are zero-initialized. Weight scaling λ = N^(1/3) is applied to all projection sub-matrices before replication. (3) Pre-training: 5.1T tokens in four stages using Megatron-LM, Warmup-Stable-Decay schedule, AdamW with load balancing loss (0.01) and router Z-loss (0.001). Stages progressively shift from English-heavy to multilingual-heavy data (62.5% multilingual by Stage 3, 87.5% by Stage 4), with synthetic multilingual QA, STEM, and cultural MCQ data added in later stages; ablations confirm each component's contribution. (4) Post-training: SFT with SLIME framework (1 epoch, ~4000 steps, 64 GPUs), then cascaded on-policy distillation (OPD) — first from Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 (~1900 steps), then from Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Instruct (~1000–1900 steps). OPD trains on student-generated trajectories with dense per-token teacher supervision. Key Results Base models evaluated on 28+ benchmarks via the Light-Eval framework. Marco-Mini-Base (0.86B activated, 17.3B total, 1.56×10²³ FLOPs) achieves English average 63.7 (vs. Qwen3-4B 63.3 at 8.64×10²³ FLOPs), multilingual general 50.9 (vs. Qwen3-4B 48.3), and multilingual cultural 65.0 (vs. Qwen3-4B 65.7 — gap driven by CMMLU 68.0 vs 76.2 and C-Eval 66.0 vs 76.6). Marco-Mini-Global-Base extends to 64 languages (+1.4T tokens) with only 0.1 English degradation (63.6) while improving multilingual advantage to +3.6% over Qwen3-4B. Instruct: Marco-Mini-Instruct scores 75.5 English (Qwen3-4B-Instruct: 73.3), 50.8 multilingual general (first), 71.0 cultural (first). Expert activation correlation analysis (100 documents per language, Pearson correlation of per-layer activation vectors) shows Romance, Slavic, Austronesian, and Indic language families form high-correlation clusters mirroring linguistic phylogenies, while typologically isolated languages (Thai, Arabic, Hebrew) remain low-correlation. Cascaded distillation adds +1.8–2.3 English points and +0.5–1.5 multilingual points across categories. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM / NEW METHOD. The fine-grained sub-matrix splitting upcycling and the N^(1/3) weight-scaling correction are technically novel moves within the known paradigm of MoE upcycling; the multilingual focus with four-stage curriculum and synthetic cultural data generation is an engineering contribution. Applying MoE upcycling to multilingual compact models is presented as a first, and the expert-activation linguistic analysis adds an empirical finding. The OPD cascaded distillation pipeline reuses published components but the full integration is original in scope. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Tables 2–4 isolate the effect of multilingual QA, STEM, and cultural data using 100B-token annealing runs on Qwen3-4B; Figure 5 ablates weight scaling and upcycling vs. from-scratch training over 100B tokens on a 17B-A0.8B model; Table 8 shows per-stage improvements; Table 12 shows cascaded distillation effects. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported — results are single runs on fixed evaluation sets, which is standard for LLM pretraining papers but limits statistical confidence. Code and model weights are publicly released (GitHub: Marco-LLM; HuggingFace: Marco-Nano-Base, Marco-Mini-Base, Marco-Mini-Global-Base, Marco-Nano-Instruct, Marco-Mini-Instruct); training datasets and recipes are also disclosed. This is a preprint and has not undergone peer review. A methodological concern: baseline comparisons for Instruct models compare against Qwen3-4B-Instruct in "non-thinking" mode — the paper notes this explicitly, but Qwen3 models are significantly stronger in thinking mode, which may skew the competitive positioning. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Both models run on commercially available GPUs; the sparse architecture means inference activation cost matches a 0.6B–0.86B dense model, making them suitable for edge and API deployment. - Efficient: Memory footprint is 8B–17B parameters (storage), but the per-token compute cost is equivalent to activating ~0.6B–0.86B parameters; the paper does not report tokens/second or latency vs. baselines, so quantitative throughput comparison is absent. - Effective: Yes — across 28+ benchmarks spanning knowledge, reasoning, translation, and culturally grounded tasks, the models consistently match or exceed baselines with 3–14× more activated parameters; the evidence is strong and multi-dimensional. - Scalable: Demonstrated from 29 to 64 languages with minimal degradation (+1T tokens incremental); no experiments at larger MoE scales (e.g., ≥100B total parameters); the claim that the MoE architecture inherently mitigates the curse of multilinguality beyond 64 languages is asserted but not tested. Trained models are released; no library or inference tool beyond standard HuggingFace is described. Strengths - The N^(1/3) weight-scaling fix is a mathematically well-motivated correction (addressing the dense-sum vs. softmax-weighted-sum mismatch) that demonstrably stabilizes training (Figure 5 shows reduced loss spikes), and will generalize to any fine-grained upcycling scenario. - The empirical expert-activation analysis is a genuine mechanistic finding: language family structure emerges in MoE routing without any explicit linguistic supervision, providing interpretable evidence for why MoE helps multilingual transfer. - Full openness — weights, all training datasets, mixture ratios per stage, and post-training recipes — is rare at this quality level and substantially raises reproducibility compared to contemporaneous releases. - The data ablation methodology (staged 100B-token annealing experiments on Qwen3-4B) is disciplined and produces clean, interpretable attribution of gains to each data component. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) coverage of 29–64 languages is a small fraction of global linguistic diversity compared to 1000-language models; (2) heavy reliance on translation-based and synthetic pipelines for low-resource settings, which may not capture authentic multilingual reasoning; (3) the current framework requires full model retraining to add new languages (not modular/incremental); (4) future work should address more sophisticated routing for extremely long-tail languages. The authors confront these directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The N^(1/3) scaling factor is motivated by assuming uniform routing (p_i ≈ 1/N), but in practice routing is non-uniform; the paper does not verify that this scaling remains optimal or correct under learned non-uniform routing. - Teacher model choice in OPD (Qwen3-30B and Qwen3-Next-80B) is from the same Alibaba model family as the base (Qwen3-0.6B); any inductive biases baked into Qwen3's training distribution will propagate into Marco-MoE-Instruct through distillation. This is not discussed. - No inference latency or throughput numbers are reported, which is a meaningful omission for a paper whose core claim is compute efficiency — a reader cannot verify that the sparse architecture yields actual wall-clock speedup vs. a dense 0.86B model. - The comparison against Qwen3-4B-Instruct uses only non-thinking mode; in thinking mode, Qwen3-4B achieves substantially higher scores on reasoning benchmarks (e.g., MATH, GPQA), making the claimed performance advantage likely narrower than reported for reasoning-centric tasks. - Expert load balance at inference time (expert utilization distribution across layers and languages) is not shown, leaving open whether some experts are underutilized or dead after training. Bottom Line Practitioners building multilingual NLP systems with tight inference-cost constraints will find the Marco-MoE upcycling recipe, the weight-scaling correction, and the released artifacts concretely useful, though the absence of inference latency data and the preprint status merit caution before production deployment.
Recommended 2604.25578 PDF
#30 — Highly Innovative (76)

Analyze Like a Venture Capitalist: Information-Gain and Knowledge Enhanced Graph Reasoning for Startup Success Prediction

faculty.sist.shanghaitech.edu.cn · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MIRAGE-VC, an evidence-grounded LLM reasoning framework that selects compact, task-utility-ranked investment subgraphs from VC networks via information-gain-driven retrieval, augments them with a dual-layer VC knowledge base, and trains a manager model with noise-aware DPO to predict whether early-stage startups will secure Series A funding within 12 months of seed. - Why it's important: VC investment exhibits extreme outcome asymmetry (60% of firms lose money, 10% return 5×+), and prediction under one-sided label noise — observed failures frequently include latent successes with delayed exits — makes standard supervised learning systematically biased against real positives. - How it does it: A lightweight MLP subgraph selector is trained on oracle information-gain labels (cross-entropy reduction scores from a frozen LLaMA-3.1-8B) to greedily expand evidence subgraphs by marginal task utility, then a Qwen3-4B manager is fine-tuned via SFT followed by noise-aware DPO that down-weights gradient updates from observed-negative instances estimated by PU learning to be latent successes. - The good: On PitchBook (9,410 test instances), MIRAGE-VC achieves AP@5 = 35.92% vs. 29.42% for GNN-RAG (strongest baseline), a +22.1% relative gain, confirmed on Crunchbase (AP@5 = 33.54 vs. 28.05), and an anonymization control shows the advantage persists with all entity names replaced, ruling out entity memorization. - The missing: The oracle gain labels are generated by LLaMA-3.1-8B but the deployed manager is Qwen3-4B; there is no ablation confirming that subgraph selector quality transfers across this oracle/manager LLM mismatch, and no precision-recall curves across thresholds are shown to characterize the recall penalty. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on graph-augmented LLM reasoning for off-graph prediction tasks — the IG-based sequential subgraph selection and noise-aware DPO with instance-dependent PU propensity are transferable beyond VC to credit risk and recommendation.
Why this rank: Scored Empirical. Rigor (85) drives the score — anonymization control, ablations on every component, dual-dataset validation, public code, blinded expert eval with LLM-judge calibration. Novelty (78) — IG-driven sequential subgraph expansion and noise-aware DPO with instance-dependent PU propensity are principled and transferable. Non-Obviousness (75) — listwise relative-gain training and STOP action are non-obvious design choices. Completeness (78) honestly flags geographic and temporal-weighting limitations. Resilience (78) anonymization experiment forecloses the obvious memorization objection. Impact (65) — code released, but oracle labeling cost and 4B model dependence limit easy reuse. Composite ≈ 0.20·78 + 0.25·85 + 0.15·75 + 0.15·78 + 0.15·78 + 0.10·65 = 77.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 78 Resilience 78 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Early-stage startup success prediction requires synthesizing heterogeneous relational signals — investor track records, co-investment coalitions, peer-company patterns — embedded in large investment networks. Three concrete gaps motivate this work. First, multi-hop neighborhood expansion produces path explosion (3-hop BFS yields ~780 candidate nodes per target), making indiscriminate context feeding infeasible within LLM context windows. Second, private-market outcome tracking is incomplete: venture exits via quiet acquisitions or delayed IPOs mean observed failures often include latent successes, creating one-sided label noise that standard supervised learning systematically penalizes. Third, local VC datasets lack the domain expertise needed to interpret structural signals — valuation heuristics, staging norms, coalition semantics — that VC analysts apply routinely. GNNs (SHGMNN, GST) capture structure but produce opaque decisions and cannot incorporate external knowledge. RAG approaches retrieve text but lose multi-hop relational topology. LLM-only approaches face a modality mismatch with graph structure and produce hallucinated investment logic without grounding. Key Idea The core insight is to reframe subgraph evidence selection as sequential information-gain maximization: at each expansion step, select the neighbor node whose inclusion in the current evidence set most reduces LLM prediction uncertainty — measured as the cross-entropy drop of a frozen predictor after the subgraph is augmented with that node. This recasts graph retrieval as a learnable, downstream-task-aware selection problem, analogous to attribute selection in decision-tree induction but applied to heterogeneous investment graphs. Training on relative gain rankings (not absolute probabilities) decouples the selector from LLM calibration quality, and a STOP action with fixed gain = 0 lets the selector decide when further expansion is harmful. Methodology MIRAGE-VC operates in three stages. (1) Graph retrieval: A subgraph selector (lightweight MLP over 2304-dim features combining BERT embeddings of baseline vs. candidate verbalized subgraphs) is trained offline on oracle gain labels Δ_vi = CE(y, p_base) − CE(y, p_vi) computed by a frozen LLaMA-3.1-8B over breadth-first expansion trees of depth ≤3. Oracle labeling generates ~16,857 gain labels on 2,000 training companies. The selector minimizes a listwise KL-divergence objective (Eq. 7) over within-group gain rankings. At inference, it greedily expands the evidence subgraph, terminating when all frontier nodes score below STOP. (2) VC knowledge augmentation: A two-layer knowledge base (15 VC theory texts averaging 503 pages, 40 practitioner playbooks averaging 74 pages, both filtered by citation frequency and validated by a VC expert) is indexed via RAPTOR-style coarse-to-fine retrieval; cluster summaries and top-n chunks are appended to the manager prompt. Three auxiliary evidence views — selected subgraph, peer-company analysis (top-k semantic peers), and lead-investor profile (pre-seed biography and deal history) — are constructed per target. (3) Manager training: Qwen3-4B is first SFT'd on preferred reasoning traces from an automatic rubric (format compliance, evidence grounding, internal consistency), then fine-tuned via noise-aware DPO. The DPO objective weights each update by w(x) = max(w_min, 1 − q(x)) where q(x) ≈ Pr(y=1 | x, y_obs=0) is estimated by PU learning with an instance-dependent propensity c(x) calibrated per sector/stage/region bucket via high-confidence OOF anchors. Three critical design choices: (a) relative gain rankings decouple the selector from LLM probability calibration — well-justified; (b) depth-3 BFS cap balances signal quality vs. candidate explosion — empirically motivated; (c) instance-dependent propensity over global SCAR — well-motivated by systematic recording-rate variation across market segments. Key Results On PitchBook (263,729 startups, 1,014,157 individuals; temporally ordered 7:1:2 split; 9,410 test instances): MIRAGE-VC achieves AP@5 = 35.92%, AP@10 = 32.82%, AP@20 = 28.51%, Precision = 24.62%, Recall = 73.30%, F1 = 36.86%, AUC-ROC = 61.59%. Against strongest baseline GNN-RAG (AP@5 = 29.42%, F1 = 34.54%): +22.1% relative AP@5, +5.9% relative F1. On public Crunchbase (196,553 companies; 4,375 test instances; same protocol): AP@5 = 33.54 vs. 28.05, F1 = 33.04 vs. 30.70, AUC-ROC = 59.73 vs. 56.85 for GNN-RAG. Anonymization control: replacing all entity names drops AP@5 by only 1.69 points on PitchBook (35.92→34.23) and 1.40 on Crunchbase; anonymized variant still outperforms GNN-RAG on every metric, confirming structural reasoning drives gains. Zero-shot comparison on anonymized PitchBook: MIRAGE-VC (4B parameters, trained) outperforms Llama 3.3 70B Instruct by +30.0% AP@5 and GPT-5 by +15.1% AP@5. Ablation (Table 3): removing subgraph retrieval costs −6.82% AP@5, replacing IG with random node selection costs −4.32%, removing VC knowledge costs −2.12%; noise-aware DPO gains +1.42% AP@5 over standard DPO. VC expert evaluation (20 held-out companies, blinded pairwise): MIRAGE-VC rated Professional in 100% of cases (vs. 70% base), preferred overall in 85%; LLM judge (GPT-5) scaled to 400 held-out cases confirms 75.6% win rate. Baselines (SHGMNN 2021, GST 2025, BERT Fusion 2025, SSFF 2025, GNN-RAG 2024) are current and include the most recent LLM-based VC predictors. Training runs on 2× RTX 4090 GPUs in ~20 GPU hours for the manager. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. Genuinely novel: (1) information-gain-driven sequential subgraph expansion for off-graph LLM prediction — adapting Quinlan's IG criterion to graph evidence selection with a trained, listwise-supervised selector and a STOP action is a principled and non-trivial contribution; (2) noise-aware DPO with instance-dependent PU unreliability estimation, applying preference optimization under one-sided label noise with per-instance calibrated propensity. The dual-layer VC knowledge base, RAPTOR retrieval, and SFT+DPO recipe are competent applications of existing techniques to a new domain. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover all three major components and both training objectives (Table 3), with five independent runs averaged for all LLM-based metrics. The anonymization control (Tables 4–5) is a rare and methodologically important sanity check that most graph-LLM papers omit. Selector performance is evaluated separately with NDCG@1, StopHit, and NonStopHit metrics (Table 11), confirming the selector learns both ranking and termination. The VC expert evaluation uses blinded pairwise comparison with a pre-specified binary rubric, and LLM judge agreement (87.5% average across criteria) validates the scaling protocol. Code and reasoning results are publicly available at https://github.com/ZhangDataLab/MIRAGE-VC. Two methodological concerns: (a) the oracle gain labels are produced by LLaMA-3.1-8B but the deployed manager is Qwen3-4B — the selector is implicitly tuned to what LLaMA-3.1-8B finds informative, and there is no ablation of oracle/manager LLM alignment; (b) significance tests or confidence intervals are not reported, only means over 5 runs. Oracle labeling required ~30,000 proprietary GPT-3.5 API calls, creating a reproducibility cost barrier despite code availability. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Manager (Qwen3-4B) trains and runs on 2× NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs (24 GB VRAM each), deployable on a single high-end consumer GPU at inference. - Efficient: Training involves ~30,000 GPT-3.5 oracle calls plus ~20 GPU hours for manager fine-tuning; per-query inference cost involves three LLM analysis agents plus retriever plus manager and is higher than GNN baselines, though exact per-query latency is not quantified. - Effective: Consistent improvement on two datasets (one proprietary, one public) and under entity anonymization, plus qualitative expert validation, provide credible multi-source evidence that the method solves the stated problem and not only a proxy metric. - Scalable: Tested at PitchBook scale (263,729 startups, ~1M individuals); greedy subgraph expansion scales linearly in frontier size. The method is not tested at 10×–100× larger scale, and oracle labeling cost grows linearly with training set size — a practical constraint for adaptation to larger graphs. The work delivers a software artifact (code + reasoning trace outputs at GitHub) and a trained pipeline; trained model weights are not explicitly released. Strengths - The IG-based subgraph selector's design choices — training on relative gain rankings rather than absolute probabilities, and the STOP mechanism that learns termination — are principled, not ad hoc, and are directly motivated by failure modes of alternatives (LLM calibration sensitivity, fixed-hop budgets). These details distinguish this from superficially similar graph-RAG approaches. - The anonymization control experiment definitively addresses the entity memorization confound, which is a common and often unacknowledged threat to validity in graph-LLM papers; this is the single strongest piece of methodological rigor in the paper. - The instance-dependent PU propensity model (stratified by sector/stage/region) is a substantive and practical improvement over global SCAR: it captures the systematic variation in exit-recording rates across market segments rather than assuming uniform latent-success probability. - The dual expert evaluation protocol — blinded pairwise expert review on 20 cases calibrated with 87.5% LLM-judge agreement, then scaled to 400 cases — provides interpretability evidence that is proportionate to the paper's claims, not merely anecdotal. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly flag two limitations: (1) geographic coverage — datasets are US/Europe heavy with limited Asian and Latin American representation, and investment patterns, regulatory environments, and entrepreneurial cultures vary substantially across regions; (2) temporal dynamics — the evidence retriever weights all historical investment edges equally regardless of recency, and time-decay weighting is left as future work. Both are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The oracle gain labels are generated by LLaMA-3.1-8B but the manager at inference is Qwen3-4B; there is no ablation of whether the subgraph selector's utility rankings transfer faithfully across this oracle/manager mismatch. If Qwen3-4B finds different evidence informative than LLaMA-3.1-8B, the selector may be misaligned. - The 12-month Series A attainment label conflates business quality with fundraising timing, investor network access, and macro funding conditions (e.g., 2021 bubble vs. 2023 contraction in the dataset span 2005–2023). Sensitivity to the 12-month window choice is not evaluated. - MIRAGE-VC's recall (73.30%) is substantially below GNN-based methods (SHGMNN: 82.37%, GST: 83.54%); the paper justifies this as a desirable precision-recall tradeoff for VC screening but provides no precision-recall curve across thresholds, and does not characterize whether false negatives are systematically biased toward companies with sparse network footprints — a real equity and coverage concern. - The VC expert evaluation covers 20 companies for primary evidence, which is a thin sample given 9,410 test instances; the LLM judge's 75.6% win rate at 400 cases may overstate robustness across diverse company types. - Oracle labeling requires ~30,000 GPT-3.5 API calls, a non-trivial proprietary cost that constrains re-training on new domains despite code release. Bottom Line This is a methodologically careful paper with a principled, transferable contribution — the information-gain subgraph selector and noise-aware DPO formulations are directly applicable to other off-graph prediction domains — and the anonymization control makes its core empirical claims substantially more credible than most graph-LLM work in the space.
Search Results
#31 — Highly Innovative (76)

GAD in the Wild: Benchmarking Graph Anomaly Detection under Realistic Deployment Challenges

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a multi-dimensional benchmark that evaluates nine representative graph anomaly detection (GAD) models under three deployment-realistic challenges—million-scale graphs, extreme anomaly scarcity (down to 0.1%), and missing node attributes—using controlled dataset variants derived from five diverse source graphs including two native industrial-scale datasets totaling over 3.7 million nodes. - Why it's important: Production GAD systems for fraud detection and platform governance routinely face graph scales, anomaly rates, and data quality conditions that standard benchmarks (small graphs, >3% anomaly ratios, complete attributes) never expose, so methods that achieve strong AUC-ROC on legacy datasets routinely fail in deployment. - How it does it: A variable-isolation protocol derives controlled benchmark variants by independently varying graph scale (via GMM attribute synthesis and hierarchical edge construction, validated by KS test), anomaly ratio (three retention strategies: core-cluster, edge-cluster, random), and attribute missingness (10–50%, three imputation methods), keeping all other factors fixed to attribute failures to specific challenges. - The good: At 0.1% anomaly ratio, MLPAE, GCNAE, CoLA, GADNR, DOMINANT, SmoothGNN, and Huge-GAD all achieve zero recall on most datasets, exposing a field-wide failure mode invisible in standard benchmarks. - The missing: The three challenge dimensions are always evaluated in isolation and never combined, leaving the most realistic scenario—a million-node graph with both extreme imbalance and missing attributes—untested. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone designing, selecting, or evaluating GAD methods for real-world deployment; it provides the clearest existing picture of where the field actually stands under production conditions.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (72) — variable-isolation protocol with distribution-preserving (KS-validated) scale expansion is a genuine benchmark design contribution. Rigor (72) — three seeds but no standard deviations reported, which is a meaningful omission at extreme imbalance where stochasticity dominates; NeurIPS submission not yet peer-reviewed. Non-Obviousness (78) — the AHFAN missingness-improves-performance finding and the zero-recall collapse at 0.1% are surprising and well-documented. Completeness (72) — three challenge axes never combined, leaving the most realistic regime untested. Resilience (74) — clear limitations section, though synthetic expansion may understate heterophily-aware methods. Practical Impact (85) — released benchmark with native million-scale industrial datasets directly shapes deployment decisions. Weighted composite: 0.15*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*74 + 0.30*85 = 76.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 72 Resilience 74 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Real-world GAD applications in financial fraud detection, misinformation tracking, and social platform governance operate on graphs with millions of nodes, anomaly prevalence below 1%, and pervasive missing features due to measurement gaps or privacy constraints. Existing benchmarks—dominated by a handful of legacy datasets (Twitter 47k nodes at 6.66% anomaly ratio, PubMed 20k at 3.04%, Credit 30k at 22.12%)—feature small graphs, unrealistically high anomaly ratios, and complete node attributes, none of which match production conditions. This gap means academic rankings of GAD methods have low predictive validity for deployment success, and the field cannot identify which specific challenge (scale, imbalance, or incompleteness) causes any given method to fail. Key Idea: Benchmark realism for GAD must be multi-dimensional and controlled: scale, anomaly ratio, and attribute completeness should each be varied one at a time while holding all others fixed (variable-isolation protocol), so that observed performance differences can be causally attributed to the target challenge rather than confounded. The secondary insight is that reconstructing realistic challenge conditions requires not just larger graphs but careful preservation of degree distributions, attribute distributions, and anomaly-to-normal attribute separation—validated via Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests (p > 0.05 threshold). Methodology: The benchmark is built from five source datasets: Twitter (47,712 nodes, 468,697 edges, 780-dim attributes, 6.66% anomaly), PubMed (19,717 nodes, 44,338 edges, 500-dim, 3.04%), Credit (30,000 nodes, 1,436,858 edges, 23-dim, 22.12%), DGraph-Fin (3,700,550 nodes, 4,300,999 edges, 17-dim, 1.27%), and T-Social (5,781,065 nodes, 73,105,508 edges, 10-dim, 3.01%). Scale expansion generates 500k- and 1M-node variants of the three foundational graphs by fitting Gaussian Mixture Models to original attribute distributions, sampling attributes for new nodes, and constructing edges via a three-tier hierarchical strategy (core/middle/edge layers) that preserves original degree-to-node ratios. Anomaly ratio adjustment reduces ratios to 0.5% and 0.1% via three retention strategies—Core Cluster (retains the densest anomaly cluster), Edge Cluster (retains the most scattered), and Random—with converted nodes receiving neighbor-mean attribute values. Attribute incompleteness injects category-specific masks at 10%–50% and applies mean, median, or neighbor imputation. Nine models are evaluated: MLPAE (attribute-only autoencoder), GCNAE (GCN autoencoder), CoLA (contrastive), GADNR (neighborhood reconstruction), DOMINANT (dual autoencoder), AnomalyDAE (attention-based dual autoencoder), AHFAN (adaptive hypergraph with frequency-domain filtering), SmoothGNN (smoothing-difficulty-based), and Huge-GAD (heterophily-guided). All models use original author hyperparameters (Native Configuration Alignment). Metrics are AUC-ROC, AUC-PR, and Anomaly Recall for detection efficacy; end-to-end training time (seconds) and peak GPU memory (MB) for deployment feasibility. All experiments run on NVIDIA L40-48GB; results averaged over three seeds. Key Results: Scalability: At 500k nodes, 4–5 of 9 models fail with OOM; at 1M nodes, 4–7 of 9 fail; on T-Social (5.8M nodes), only MLPAE runs (all GNN-based models OOM). Memory footprints are severe even at small scale: DOMINANT consumes 44,510 MB on 47k-node Twitter; GADNR uses 18,616 MB on the same graph. Imbalance: At 0.1% anomaly ratio with random retention on Twitter, MLPAE, GCNAE, CoLA, GADNR, DOMINANT, SmoothGNN, and Huge-GAD achieve 0 recall; AHFAN achieves 45.83 recall under random retention but 100 under core and edge cluster retention, revealing sensitivity to anomaly distribution pattern. At 0.5% random ratio on Twitter, AHFAN achieves 99.04 recall while most others remain near 0. Incomplete data: DOMINANT exhibits up to a 20.1-point AUC-ROC swing across imputation strategies on Credit; CoLA varies less than 4.8 points across the same conditions. Counter-intuitively, AHFAN improves from 75.93 AUC-ROC (complete Credit graph) to 99.09 at 50% missing—the authors attribute this to missing attributes reducing attribute-domain distractors and forcing structural signal exploitation. GADNR degrades monotonically under missingness (52.47→46.34 AUC-ROC on Credit with median imputation at 50% missing). Detection performance on expanded graphs is inconsistent: GCNAE drops 5.22 AUC-ROC points on Credit when scaling from 30k to 500k nodes but improves on PubMed expanded graphs, indicating scale sensitivity is dataset-dependent rather than systematic. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is genuinely novel in combining variable-isolation protocol, distribution-preserving scale expansion (KS-validated), and three-axis simultaneous challenge coverage for unsupervised GAD. The finding that seven of nine models collapse to zero recall at realistic anomaly ratios, and that reconstruction-based models are substantially more sensitive to imputation choice than contrastive models, are new empirical findings with direct design implications. The individual components (GMM sampling, KS validation, anomaly retention strategies) are adaptations of known ideas applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for each challenge axis (ratio values: original/0.5%/0.1%; missing rates: 10%/20%/30%/40%/50%; imputation: mean/median/neighbor; retention: core/edge/random). Results are averages of three independent runs with fixed global seed (20). Error bars/standard deviations are not reported in any table—a meaningful omission given the variance in anomaly recall under extreme imbalance. Code and data are released at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Benchmark_GAD-E7A3 (anonymous submission link; not yet permanently archived). Evaluation uses original model hyperparameters without tuning, which is methodologically credible but means some models may be systematically disadvantaged on out-of-distribution graph scales. This is a NeurIPS 2026 submission (as stated in the footer), not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (built benchmark infrastructure and empirical evaluation of deployed models). - Practical: The benchmark runs on a single NVIDIA L40 (48GB), accessible to well-resourced academic labs but not commodity hardware; the 48GB VRAM is above typical workstation configurations. - Efficient: Benchmark construction overhead is modest; the tested models themselves are the bottleneck—DOMINANT requires 44,510 MB and 147 seconds on 47k-node Twitter, making it impractical without high-memory GPUs. - Effective: The benchmark credibly differentiates models across all three challenge axes; the zero-recall result at 0.1% is a concrete, reproducible outcome rather than a soft claim. - Scalable: Two native industrial-scale datasets (3.7M and 5.8M nodes) are included; the synthetic expansion reaches 1M nodes; no model other than MLPAE runs on 5.8M nodes, so the scalability ceiling of current methods is clearly established. The paper does not test any model on distributed infrastructure, leaving open whether hardware rather than algorithmic constraints cause the OOM failures. The paper delivers a software artifact (benchmark code + datasets at the linked repository) in addition to the empirical findings. Strengths: - The variable-isolation protocol is methodologically principled: by varying only one factor at a time across matched graph variants, the benchmark enables causal attribution of performance degradation—a design choice absent from all prior GAD benchmarks. - The zero-recall collapse of seven models at 0.1% anomaly ratio is a strong, specific, and reproducible indictment of the current unsupervised GAD field, not hedged or anecdotal. - The counter-intuitive finding that attribute missingness improves AHFAN's performance (+23 AUC-ROC points on Credit) because it reduces attribute noise and forces structural reasoning is a substantive insight that opens a concrete research direction. - Including two native industrial datasets (DGraph-Fin, T-Social) rather than relying entirely on synthetic expansion insulates the scalability findings from the criticism that synthetic graphs don't reflect real structural properties. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The three challenge dimensions (scale, imbalance, incompleteness) are evaluated in isolation and not jointly—a limitation the authors explicitly state in Section 5, noting that real industrial graphs often simultaneously exhibit all three. The evaluation uses laboratory-grade hardware, so OOM failures might be mitigated with distributed infrastructure; the authors acknowledge this means their scalability findings reflect lab-scale rather than industrial-grade hardware behavior. Only unsupervised GAD is evaluated; supervised and semi-supervised settings are out of scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The scale-expansion procedure generates node attributes via GMM sampling and constructs edges via hierarchical layering—both preserve marginal distributions but cannot preserve higher-order structural properties (e.g., community structure, motif frequencies, heterophily patterns) that many GAD methods specifically exploit. Results on synthetic expanded graphs may therefore understate performance of heterophily-aware methods like Huge-GAD at scale. The model set includes no method specifically designed for extreme class imbalance (e.g., oversampling, cost-sensitive objectives, anomaly-upweighting), leaving open whether zero recall at 0.1% is a fundamental GAD limitation or an artifact of evaluating methods not designed for this regime. Anomaly Recall is reported but AUC-PR receives less analytical emphasis despite being the more informative metric at extreme imbalance (AUC-PR penalizes high false-positive rates that AUC-ROC masks). The paper does not report standard deviation across the three runs, making it impossible to assess whether performance differences between models are statistically significant—critical at extreme anomaly ratios where stochasticity in which anomalies are retained can dominate. The KS-test threshold (p > 0.05) used to validate expanded dataset distributions is necessary but not sufficient: it tests marginal distributions, not joint structure. Bottom Line: A practitioner evaluating or selecting GAD methods gains more actionable information from this benchmark than from any incremental method paper—the concrete evidence that most models OOM at million-node scale and collapse to zero recall below 1% anomaly rate should directly shape both deployment decisions and future research directions.
Search Results 2605.07133 PDF
#32 — Highly Innovative (76)

SymDrift: One-Shot Generative Modeling under Symmetries

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SymDrift, a framework that makes drifting-based one-shot generative models symmetry-aware by constructing the drifting field itself to respect group symmetries, rather than relying on equivariant architectures alone. - Why it's important: Equivariant diffusion and flow-matching models can recover symmetric distributions from non-invariant data but require expensive multi-step inference; drifting models enable single-step generation but, as this paper proves, equivariant generators alone do not recover the symmetrized drifting field, causing training collapse on molecular tasks. - How it does it: SymDrift fixes the mismatch by either (i) finding the optimal group transformation aligning each generated-target pair before computing the drift (explicit alignment), or (ii) computing the drift in a G-invariant embedding space built from type-sorted pairwise distances, which removes symmetry ambiguity by construction. - The good: The invariant-embedding variant achieves AMR-R median of 0.058 Å on GEOM-QM9 conformer generation with a single NFE at 1.4 ms inference time, matching or beating multi-step baselines like ET-Flow (50 NFE, 76.8 ms) and reducing inference cost 40×. - The missing: The practical embedding is not maximally expressive — it discards incidence information and is not injective — and no experiment tests whether this expressivity gap hurts on larger or more structurally complex molecules beyond the small-molecule GEOM-QM9 and RDB7 sets. - Read it or not: Researchers working on fast molecular generation (drug screening, reaction network search) or on the theory of drifting/distribution-matching models should read this; it cleanly identifies and fixes a fundamental symmetry pathology that had not been previously recognized.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (85) drives the score — Theorem 1 cleanly identifies a previously unrecognized failure mode of drifting under symmetry with constructive counterexample and matching empirical collapse to 0% coverage. Novelty (80) — NEW METHOD plus a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING with formal proof. Rigor (70) — theory rigorous and ablation traces each symmetry-handling tier, but no error bars/multi-seed and no released code. Completeness (65) and Resilience (70) pulled down by evaluation restricted to QM9/RDB7 small molecules and unaddressed τ→0 mode-collapse tension. Practical Impact (75) — 40–87× one-shot speedup matches multi-step accuracy, but no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*80+0.25*70+0.15*85+0.15*65+0.15*70+0.10*75 = 73.5.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 65 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Generative models for physical systems such as molecules must learn distributions invariant under global symmetries — rotations and translations in ℝ³ and permutations of identical atoms — formally captured by a symmetry group G acting on configuration space. Training data form a finite empirical distribution that is not G-invariant, so models must recover G-invariance despite this. Equivariant diffusion and flow-matching models achieve this via equivariant architectures, but require iterative multi-step sampling (50–5000 NFE), which is prohibitively expensive for high-throughput applications like virtual drug screening or large-scale reaction network exploration. Drifting models (Deng et al., 2026) offer single-step generation by framing generative modeling as distribution matching via a kernel-based vector field, but no prior work had extended them to handle symmetries. The gap is not merely practical: the paper proves that using an equivariant generator with the unsymmetrized empirical distribution does not recover the drifting field of the symmetrized target distribution — a fundamental mismatch that causes complete training collapse (0% coverage) in experiments. Key Idea: The key insight is that equivariance of the generator is insufficient for symmetry-correct drifting: because the positive drifting field is a kernel-weighted average over target samples and this average does not commute with group averaging (the ratio A/Z is nonlinear in g), the field learned from the raw empirical distribution is biased toward arbitrary representatives of each equivalence class. The fix is to symmetrize the drifting field directly — either by aligning each (generated sample, target sample) pair to its optimal group orbit before computing the drift, or by computing drift in a representation that is G-invariant by construction and thus collapses all orbit members to a single point. Methodology: SymDrift is built on top of the drifting framework (Deng et al., 2026). The drifting loss drives a generator fθ toward a target distribution p via a fixed-point iteration: fθ_{i+1}(ε) ← fθ_i(ε) + V_{p,q_θ}(fθ_i(ε)), where the drifting field V = V⁺ − V⁻ combines an attractive term toward target samples and a repulsive term away from generated samples, both computed as Laplacian-kernel-weighted averages. Mini-batch training samples Nc classes, each with N⁺ positive and N⁻ negative samples. Two SymDrift variants address the symmetry problem: (1) Explicit alignment (Method 1): For each generated sample x and target sample y, find the optimal group element g(x,y) = argmin_{g∈G} ‖x − gy‖ and replace (x, y) with (x, gy) before computing the kernel weights. For rotations, this is the Kabsch algorithm (O(N²)); for permutations, the Hungarian algorithm (O(N³)), making this variant expensive. The paper shows this construction is G-equivariant and approximates the symmetrized drifting field in the low-temperature regime of the Laplacian kernel. (2) Invariant embedding (Method 2): Compute the drift in an embedding space φ(x) that is G-invariant by construction. The embedding collects, for each atom-type pair α, the sorted set of pairwise distances D_α(x) = {‖x_i − x_j‖ : τ(i,j) = α}, and concatenates them across all type pairs. Sorting removes permutation dependence; distance-only representation removes rotation dependence; this makes φ invariant under G = SO(3) × Š_N. The embedding is non-parametric, computed in O(N² log N), and requires no training. The embedding is not injective (discards incidence information), but this is acknowledged as a design tradeoff. Critical design decisions: (a) Two-sided kernel normalization (softmax over both x and y, combined via geometric mean) is preferred over one-sided and improves diversity (coverage); this choice is well-justified by ablation. (b) The embedding variant uses a non-equivariant architecture (DiTMC) without data augmentation — symmetry is entirely handled by the invariant drift, eliminating the need for augmentation that flow-matching methods rely on for non-equivariant parameterizations. (c) The temperature τ → 0 regime motivates hard alignment, but in practice low τ causes mode collapse; this tension is managed but not fully resolved. Key Results: Conformer generation (GEOM-QM9, 133,258 small molecules, δ = 0.5 Å threshold, 1,000-molecule test set): SymDrift_{ET-Flow} achieves Coverage-R mean 95.7%, AMR-R mean 0.118 Å (median 0.059 Å) at 1 NFE / 1.9 ms. SymDrift_{DiTMC} achieves AMR-R mean 0.106 Å (median 0.058 Å) at 1 NFE / 1.4 ms. Both outperform all one-shot baselines (GeoMol: AMR-R mean 0.225, AvgFlow: 0.220, EnFlow-SO3: 0.122) and are competitive with multi-step methods (ET-Flow at 50 NFE: AMR-R mean 0.073 / median 0.047; DiTMC+rPE at 50 NFE: 0.070 / 0.027). Inference speedup over ET-Flow at 50 NFE is ~40×. The ablation (Figure 2) confirms that base drift with equivariant architecture achieves 0% coverage; rotational alignment recovers partial performance; invariant embedding achieves the best result. Transition state generation (RDB7, 11,926 gas-phase reactions): SymDrift_{GoFlow(L)} at 1 NFE / 1.3 ms achieves RMSD mean 0.328 Å (median 0.239 Å), DMAE median 0.092 Å — matching GoFlow (25 NFE, 113 ms) on mean RMSD, improving median RMSD from 0.258 to 0.239 Å, and running 87× faster. Outperforms TsDiff (5000 NFE) on all metrics. Slightly weaker on DMAE mean (0.134 vs. 0.128) vs. GoFlow. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The method contributes two symmetry-aware drifting strategies for one-shot molecular generation. The empirical finding — proven by Theorem 1 and validated empirically — that equivariant generators with drifting models do not recover the symmetrized drifting field is a genuinely new insight not previously stated in the literature. The invariant embedding construction is a new (if simple) representation specifically designed for this objective. The overall approach is a non-trivial extension of drifting to the symmetry-constrained setting, not a straightforward combination of known techniques. Rigor & Reproducibility: The core theoretical claim (Theorem 1) is proved in full in Appendix B.2 with an explicit construction demonstrating the mismatch. Supporting properties (equivariance of alignment-based drift, hard-alignment limit, equivariance of kernel drift under invariant distributions) are separately proved in B.3–B.6. The ablation in Figure 2 directly validates the theoretical prediction (training collapse without symmetry-aware drift). Hyperparameters and training details are reported in Appendix C.4. Error bars / multiple seeds are not reported in the main tables, which is a weakness given that one-shot models can have variance. Code availability is not mentioned in the main paper or appendix. The paper is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Baselines are current (ET-Flow, AvgFlow, EnFlow, GoFlow, DiTMC), with some reproduced by the authors. Conformer recall coverage-R values are reproduced within ~1% of published figures for multi-step methods. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL (molecular generation experiments; systems built and evaluated). - Practical: Both variants run on standard GPU hardware; the DiTMC model has 8.9M parameters and completes inference in 1.4 ms per molecule, deployable on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: Inference is 40–87× cheaper than competitive multi-step baselines; training is more expensive than flow-matching due to hierarchical mini-batch sampling (Nc classes × N⁺ + N⁻ samples) and alignment/embedding computation overhead (O(N² log N) per step for the embedding variant, O(N³) worst-case for alignment). - Effective: On both conformer and TS generation benchmarks, SymDrift matches or exceeds multi-step competitors on accuracy metrics at 1 NFE, demonstrating the contribution solves the stated problem. - Scalable: Tested only on small molecules (GEOM-QM9: up to ~9 heavy atoms in practice; RDB7: up to 7 heavy atoms H/C/N/O). No experiments on larger drug-like molecules, proteins, or larger reaction systems; scalability claims to virtual drug screening remain unvalidated. The O(N² log N) embedding scales polynomially, but the expressivity gap (non-injectivity) may worsen for larger, more complex molecules. No software artifact, trained model weights, or dataset is publicly released (not mentioned in the paper). Strengths: - Theorem 1 and its proof cleanly identify a previously unrecognized fundamental failure mode of drifting models under symmetry, with an explicit constructive counterexample for SO(3) and supporting empirical evidence (complete training collapse at 0% coverage). - The invariant embedding is non-parametric, requires no additional training, has O(N² log N) complexity, and can be replaced with any differentiable invariant representation — the framework is modular with respect to the choice of φ. - Achieves best-in-class one-shot accuracy across all reported metrics on GEOM-QM9 and matches multi-step accuracy on RDB7 at 87× lower inference latency, providing a concrete practical result rather than just a theoretical correction. - The ablation (Figure 2) is unusually clean: it traces the progressive impact of each symmetry-handling strategy (none → rotation alignment → iterative alignment → invariant embedding), directly validating the theoretical framework. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) training time is increased because hierarchical sampling reduces unique classes per batch, slowing dataset traversal; (2) enforcing symmetry adds per-step overhead for alignment or embedding computation; (3) the practical embedding is invariant but not maximally expressive — it retains only type-resolved distance multisets and discards incidence information, so configurations that are structurally distinct but share the same sorted distance multiset collapse to the same representation. They frame the training cost as an acceptable tradeoff given the inference savings, and suggest replacing the embedding with geometric message-passing representations as future work. These limitations are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No evaluation beyond small molecules: GEOM-QM9 targets molecules with few heavy atoms; RDB7 restricts to H/C/N/O with up to 7 heavy atoms. Whether the non-injective embedding causes quality degradation for larger, structurally richer molecules (drug-like, macrocyclic, heteroatom-rich) is entirely untested, making the virtual drug screening claim speculative. - No error bars or multi-seed runs in the main tables. One-shot generative models have stochastic training and the stated improvements over some baselines (e.g., SymDrift vs. EnFlow on AMR-R: 0.118 vs. 0.122) are small enough that significance is unclear. - The low-temperature (τ → 0) regime, which the theory motivates and which is required for sharp distributions, induces mode collapse in the drifting kernel. The paper acknowledges this briefly (citing Sinkhorn-drifting [53]) but provides no solution; the actual τ used is not analyzed in the main paper, leaving the practical operating regime undercharacterized. - The explicit alignment variant (Method 1) is theoretically cleaner but practically limited by O(N³) Hungarian matching; the paper defers a thorough comparison between the two methods to Appendix E without fully characterizing when alignment outperforms the embedding or vice versa. - Code and model weights are not released, limiting reproducibility of the ablations and the invariant embedding construction. Bottom Line: SymDrift makes a concrete theoretical and empirical contribution — proving and fixing a fundamental symmetry failure in drifting models — and a practitioner targeting fast one-shot molecular conformer or transition-state generation will find both the theoretical grounding and the 40–87× inference speedup results directly useful, though the limited scale of evaluation (small molecules only) and absence of released code temper confidence in immediate deployment to larger systems.
Citations 2605.06140 PDF
#33 — Highly Innovative (75)

VDCores: Resource Decoupled Programming and Execution for Asynchronous GPU

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VDCores, a virtual decoupled-core programming and execution framework that exposes asynchronous GPU hardware units (TMA, tensor cores) as independently-schedulable virtual cores executing dependency-connected micro-operations (μops), replacing the monolithic CUDA kernel as the unit of scheduling. - Why it's important: Modern GPU kernels bake memory/compute orchestration into static schedules tuned per workload variant, leaving asynchronous hardware underutilized at kernel boundaries and imposing a growing kernel-specialization tax that scales with every new operator, shape, or architecture change. - How it does it: VDCores virtualizes each GPU SM into a Virtual Memory Core (VMC) and Virtual Compute Cores (VCCs) that execute dependency-driven μop streams; inter-μop dependencies are encoded directly into instruction words at compile time, making runtime dependency tracking a constant-cost per-instruction operation rather than a general graph traversal, keeping overhead below 4%. - The good: Achieves 1.31× geometric-mean speedup (up to 1.68×) over the best per-setting baseline across 48 model/hardware/batch-size combinations while implementing Llama3-1B with 741 GPU lines of code versus 2,065–6,424 for comparable baselines. - The missing: All evaluation is decode-only offline inference on 1.7B–8B models on NVIDIA GPUs; prefill, training, models above 8B, multi-GPU serving, and non-NVIDIA hardware are entirely absent. - Read it or not: Essential for GPU systems researchers and LLM serving infrastructure engineers — the paper offers a credible architectural answer to why monolithic kernels leave asynchronous hardware underutilized and demonstrates the claim with strong ablations.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster across multiple axes. Practical Impact (80) is high — open-source PyTorch-integrated runtime running on commodity NVIDIA H100/GH200/RTX 6000 Pro with 3.1% aggregate overhead, a deliverable that ships rather than promises. Novelty (75) is genuine: instantiating decoupled-core execution on SIMT hardware via compact in-instruction dependency encoding is a real architectural move, not a kernel-fusion variant. Non-Obv (75) is high — the insight that one-to-one store→load chains can be rewritten through shared memory at schedule time discovers fusion opportunities (Embedding+RMS, 28%) that expert-tuned systems miss. Rigor (75) is the cleanest in the cluster: 48 model/GPU/batch conditions, current baselines (vLLM, SGLang, Mirage, ThunderKittens), targeted ablations that isolate auto-overlap from virtual-flow assignment, a step-by-step VMC optimization ablation reaching the 4.2× cumulative figure, and open-sourced code. Completeness (70) and Resilience (70) are held back only by the absence of prefill, continuous batching, multi-GPU, and >8B models, all explicitly acknowledged. Weighted: 0.15*75+0.15*75+0.10*75+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.30*80 ≈ 75.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Modern NVIDIA GPUs expose specialized asynchronous hardware — Tensor Memory Accelerators (TMAs) for async bulk memory movement, tensor cores for matrix computation — that in principle allow memory and compute to overlap continuously. In practice, the CUDA kernel model organizes all of this into a single monolithic execution unit with a fixed start and finish, so idle resources at kernel boundaries cannot be reallocated. Warp-specialization approaches (CUTLASS, ThunderKittens) reduce intra-kernel bubbles, and persistent-kernel pipelines (Mirage) push overlap across tasks, but both preserve the kernel or task as the fundamental scheduling atom. Figure 4 in the paper makes this concrete: even the Mirage Persistent Kernel shows near-zero effective memory bandwidth at task-boundary transitions. Dynamic workloads compound the problem — a kernel tuned for short-context decoding underperforms on long-context or mixed LoRA-rank batches, forcing practitioners to maintain a growing library of specialized kernel variants. The gap is programmability as well as performance: Llama3-1B requires 6,424 LoC and 8 fused tasks in vLLM, versus 741 LoC and zero fused tasks in VDCores. Key Idea The core insight is that asynchronous GPU hardware units should be treated as independent, resource-isolated execution domains whose execution order is determined at runtime by dependency satisfaction, not by static programmer-specified orchestration. Making this tractable on SIMT hardware requires that dependency tracking be cheap: VDCores restricts each memory μop to at most one inter-memory-μop dependency plus an optional compute dependency on the same SM, encodes both relationships as fields in the μop instruction word, and uses compact virtual-flow IDs to capture coarse-grained ordering structure. This eliminates the need for general hardware scoreboards and lets the runtime resolve dependencies with constant per-instruction bookkeeping. Methodology VDCores has three components implemented in roughly 5K lines of C++/CUDA and 4K lines of Python: μop Generator: Takes a PyTorch operator DAG and performs two-tier scheduling. At the global tier, it decomposes each operator into μop streams, assigns μops to VMC/VCC pairs, and performs adaptive tiling — iteratively identifying dominant critical paths in a per-core execution DAG and varying GEMM tile shapes (M, N, or K partitioning) until no dominant path remains, exposing the maximum cross-operator overlap. At the per-core tier, it embeds inter-μop dependency information directly into instruction words (send/recv flags for memory-to-compute transfers, depId fields for memory-to-memory ordering), assigns virtual-flow IDs to encode coarse-grained independence, eliminates redundant barriers for transitively-dependent μops already serialized by in-order flow execution, and performs dynamic data placement: one-to-one global-memory store→load chains between neighboring μops are rewritten to store.local / load.local through shared memory (dynamic fusion), without generating new kernels.
