Analyst-Identified Gaps - No code or compressed model weights are released; reproducing the GEMV and GEMM kernels requires non-trivial CUDA expertise, limiting practical adoption beyond the reported results. - Evaluation is capped at 13B; the method is most useful at 70B+ where memory pressure is greatest, and it is unknown whether the k-means clustering quality holds at that scale. - Decode throughput is measured only at B=1; for multi-user serving with B>1, cuBLAS scales more favorably via tensor-core GEMM, but the paper does not characterize the crossover batch size or provide guidance for deployment decisions. - The five commonsense zero-shot benchmarks do not cover instruction-following, code generation, or mathematical reasoning (e.g., GSM8K, HumanEval), where quantization artifacts are often more pronounced and where practitioners most care about degradation. - The consistent superiority of dim=0 over dim=1 is reported as an empirical finding without explanation; whether this holds for architectures with different weight matrix aspect ratios (MoE layers, GQA with large head counts) is unstated. - K-means is non-deterministic; variance across random initializations and convergence quality across seeds are not reported, leaving uncertainty about result reproducibility in practice. Bottom Line FASQ delivers a concrete and well-engineered result — calibration-free decode throughput above FP16 with matched accuracy vs. AWQ/GPTQ — that any practitioner running inference-only workloads on a single consumer GPU should consider, but the 23× prefill slowdown and lack of a code release mean it is not yet a drop-in replacement for existing PTQ pipelines.
Search Results 2605.04084 PDF
#39 — Innovative (74)

Thinking in Pictures: A Diagnostic Study of Visual vs. Textual Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Vision-Language Models

openreview.net · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ViSCoT-Diag, a 1,200-instance diagnostic benchmark and controlled five-paradigm comparison study that quantifies when visual versus textual chain-of-thought reasoning helps or hurts VLM performance across five visual reasoning categories. - Why it's important: Practitioners routinely apply textual CoT as a default to VLMs, but this study shows it actively degrades accuracy on spatial transformation by 16.5 points and multi-object tracking by 12.7 points relative to direct answering, wasting tokens and producing systematically wrong answers. - How it does it: A V-CoT-NF (visual CoT, no feedback) ablation generates the same drawing code as visual CoT but withholds the rendered image from the model, isolating whether gains come from code-based symbolic execution or from the model observing the rendered intermediate visual state. - The good: V-CoT-NF recovers only 36% of V-CoT's gain over T-CoT on spatial transformation (50.3 vs. 35.2 for T-CoT, 76.8 for V-CoT), proving the rendered image—not code generation—drives the majority of the benefit. - The missing: The benchmark is restricted to simple 2D synthetic tasks (CLEVR-style shapes, grid worlds); whether findings transfer to real-world photographs, 3D scenes, or non-English prompts is untested and explicitly out of scope. - Read it or not: Worth reading for VLM system designers choosing prompting strategies for spatial or tracking tasks; the controlled synthetic setting limits immediate generalization to production deployments over real-world images.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) the V-CoT-NF ablation is a genuinely clever diagnostic. Rigor (78) four diverse VLMs, human validation κ=0.91, cross-validated judge, well-controlled. Non-Obviousness (78) the attribution result (visual feedback drives 64% of gain, not code execution) is surprising and crisp. Completeness (78) authors confront scope limitations directly — 2D, synthetic, English-only acknowledged. Resilience (75) cross-model consistency strengthens robustness. Impact (60) no benchmark release, but adaptive router is immediately actionable. Composite: 0.20*70+0.25*78+0.15*78+0.15*78+0.15*75+0.10*60 ≈ 74.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 78 Resilience 75 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Chain-of-thought prompting is applied as a universal default to vision-language models even for inherently spatial tasks such as geometric transformation, object tracking, and spatial relation judgment. For these tasks, verbal reasoning may fail to faithfully encode continuous spatial configurations, and any improvement from visual CoT methods might stem from code-based symbolic computation (delegating work to a Python interpreter) rather than from the model actually observing a rendered intermediate state. Practitioners lack principled, empirically grounded guidance on when each reasoning modality helps or hurts, and prior benchmarks have documented CoT failures without isolating the mechanism behind visual CoT gains. Key Idea The V-CoT-NF ablation is the core technical contribution: it runs the identical code-generation pipeline as full visual CoT but withholds the rendered image, forcing the model to answer based only on mental simulation of what the code would have produced. If V-CoT-NF matches V-CoT, code execution alone explains the gain; if V-CoT-NF matches T-CoT, observing the rendered image is essential. This two-branch decomposition cleanly attributes V-CoT gains to either symbolic structure (code) or visual perception (rendered feedback), without requiring any modification to the underlying models. Methodology Five CoT paradigms are compared at inference time via prompting, requiring no fine-tuning: Direct (D), Textual CoT (T-CoT with "think step by step"), Structured Textual CoT (ST-CoT with four-stage template mirroring LLaVA-CoT), Visual CoT no feedback (V-CoT-NF), and Visual CoT (V-CoT with up to five Act/Observe turns using PIL/Pillow drawing primitives). The ViSCoT-Diag benchmark comprises 1,200 instances: 5 categories × 3 difficulty levels × 80 instances each, sourced from BLINK spatial, CLEVR, GQA, MathVista geometry, and procedurally generated synthetic instances. Four VLMs are evaluated: GPT-5, Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Qwen3-VL-72B, all with greedy decoding (temperature 0). Evaluation uses exact-match accuracy and premise-conclusion consistency (PCC), the latter scored by a Claude Opus 4.6 judge verified at 92% agreement with Gemini 2.5 Pro on a 100-instance subset. Annotation quality (AQ) for V-CoT measures semantic correctness of generated visualizations even when not returned to the model. Three critical design decisions: (1) the V-CoT-NF ablation is well-motivated and central to attribution; (2) difficulty calibration is human-validated on a 10% stratified sample (96.7% agreement with ground truth, Fleiss' κ = 0.91); (3) failure mode categorization across 200 error cases uses two annotators (89% agreement, κ = 0.82). Key Results Averaged across four VLMs, T-CoT degrades spatial transformation by 16.5 points (35.2% vs. 51.7% Direct) and multi-object tracking by 12.7 points (46.3% vs. 59.0%). V-CoT improves over Direct by +25.1 on ST, +14.0 on MOT, +12.1 on SR, and +25.4 on GR; CC is best served by ST-CoT (+18.8). V-CoT-NF recovers only 36% of V-CoT's ST gain over T-CoT. At Hard difficulty on ST, T-CoT accuracy falls to 17.7% (below 50% chance on binary questions) while V-CoT reaches 73.0%, a 55.3-point gap. Multi-step transformation accuracy: T-CoT 18.3% vs. V-CoT 61.7%. PCC on SR degrades from 94.8% (2 objects) to 51.3% (5+ objects) for T-CoT; V-CoT holds 84.7–94.8%. The pattern is consistent across all four VLMs (per-model full breakdown in Table 4). A DistilBERT adaptive router selecting T-CoT vs. V-CoT per question achieves 78.2% accuracy vs. 74.0% for V-CoT-everywhere and 57.3% for T-CoT-everywhere, against an oracle ceiling of 81.5%. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The V-CoT-NF ablation design is a genuinely novel diagnostic instrument that produces a falsifiable decomposition of visual CoT gains; no prior work has isolated code-generation from visual-perception contributions in this controlled fashion. The benchmark itself combines existing sources (CLEVR, BLINK, MathVista, GQA) with procedurally generated instances rather than contributing entirely new data. The failure mode taxonomy (spatial state collapse, transformation hallucination, tracking loss) is an empirically grounded classification, not a theoretical novelty. Rigor & Reproducibility Results are averaged across four diverse VLMs, reducing model-specific confounds. Human validation of the benchmark achieves Fleiss' κ = 0.91 on a 10% stratified sample. The PCC judge is cross-validated against a second frontier model. Failure mode annotation reaches κ = 0.82 with two annotators. No code or benchmark release is mentioned; all four models are commercial APIs or large open-weight checkpoints that require significant compute to reproduce. The synthetic ground truth avoids Goodhart's Law concerns for most categories (ST, MOT, GR), though CC tasks sourced from CLEVR/GQA may carry dataset-specific artifacts. Only one prompting template per paradigm is tested; sensitivity to prompt wording is not ablated. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All paradigms operate via API calls and a sandboxed Python environment for drawing code; no specialized hardware is required beyond what the model providers expose. - Efficient: V-CoT consumes 3.2× more tokens than T-CoT and requires a second image encoder pass per turn; the paper judges this cost justified for ST and MOT but not for CC, and V-CoT-NF offers a cheaper intermediate option. - Effective: The V-CoT-NF ablation rules out pure code-execution as the explanation, providing strong evidence that visual feedback is causally driving accuracy gains on spatial tasks, not just correlated with them. - Scalable: Tested on 1,200 simple 2D synthetic instances; the paper explicitly does not claim results generalize to real-world photographs, 3D scenes, or larger object counts beyond 6–7. No software artifact, trained model, or benchmark dataset release is reported. Strengths - The V-CoT-NF ablation is a clean, internally valid experimental design; it produces a quantitative bound on how much visual perception versus code execution contributes, which is directly actionable for system design. - Consistent results across all four VLMs (closed-source frontier and open-weight large-scale) substantially strengthen the claim that the modality gap is architecture-agnostic at this task distribution. - The three failure modes are empirically grounded with frequency tables per category and strong inter-annotator agreement (κ = 0.82), moving beyond anecdotal illustration. - The adaptive routing finding—a lightweight DistilBERT classifier recovering 78.2% of oracle routing accuracy—is a practical, immediately deployable recommendation that bridges the paper's diagnostic findings to engineering decisions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly scope their claims to simple, controlled 2D visual tasks; they caution against reading findings as a universal claim about visual reasoning in the wild. They acknowledge that V-CoT performance depends on code generation quality (weaker code generators produce misleading annotations), that the benchmark is 2D, English-only, and synthetic, that external visual CoT via code execution may differ fundamentally from internal latent visual reasoning, and that the three failure modes are empirically grounded but not exhaustive. These are confronted directly and with appropriate epistemic humility, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No ablation on the number of V-CoT annotation turns (up to 5 allowed): it is unknown whether accuracy saturates after 1–2 turns or whether multi-turn iteration is load-bearing, which matters for cost estimation. - The adaptive router uses question text only (DistilBERT); incorporating image features could close the 3.3-point gap to oracle routing (81.5%), but this is merely mentioned, not tested. - The paper attributes V-CoT-NF's underperformance on CC relative to T-CoT to token displacement (tokens spent on drawing code displace symbolic decomposition tokens), but this is not directly measured—no token budget analysis or ablation on code length is provided. - Only one open-weight model (Qwen3-VL-72B) is included; models specifically trained for visual reasoning or with specialized spatial encoders may exhibit substantially different modality gaps, and the generalization to that class is untested. - Single-author study with no mention of author affiliation beyond a PhD program; while inter-annotator agreement is reported for failure modes, no independent replication of the core benchmark evaluation is mentioned. Bottom Line A practitioner designing VLM prompting pipelines for spatial transformation, object tracking, or any task requiring maintained visual state will gain concrete, causally grounded guidance from this paper—the V-CoT-NF ablation result alone (64% of visual CoT gain comes from seeing the rendered image, not from code execution) justifies reading it.
Citations
#40 — Innovative (74)

PathISE: Learning Informative Path Supervision for Knowledge Graph Question Answering

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PATHISE, a framework that uses a Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) estimator to learn informative relation-path supervision from answer-level labels, distills it into an LLM-based path generator, and grounds generated paths in a knowledge graph for inductive reasoning — eliminating the need for manually annotated paths or costly LLM-refined supervision signals. - Why it's important: Training effective KG-RAG models for KGQA typically requires high-quality intermediate path supervision, which is expensive to obtain; weakly supervised paths (all paths reaching the answer entity) introduce spurious noise that degrades reasoning accuracy, and LLM-refined alternatives scale poorly with training set size. - How it does it: A lightweight 2-layer transformer MIL estimator scores candidate relation paths by their estimated informativeness using bag-level answer supervision, selects top-T paths as pseudo supervision, then fine-tunes LLaMA3.1-8B-Instruct with LoRA to generate those paths; at inference, generated paths are grounded in the KG and fed as compact evidence to an LLM for zero-shot answer reasoning. - The good: PATHISE matches or beats LLM-refined supervision baselines (e.g., +0.7% F1 on WebQSP over RAPL, +1.0% Hit on CWQ) while requiring no per-instance LLM calls for supervision construction, and its pseudo supervision transfers to improve other KGQA models (RoG +8.4% F1 on CWQ, SubgraphRAG +3.0% F1). - The missing: The evaluation is limited to three benchmarks on two KGs (Freebase and WikiMovies); there is no evaluation on open-domain KGs at large scale (e.g., Wikidata with hundreds of millions of triples), and the MIL estimator's ability to generalize to relation types unseen during training is not tested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on KG-RAG pipelines or weakly supervised structured reasoning — the MIL framing for path supervision is clean and the model-agnostic pseudo supervision result is practically useful.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (72) is solid — MIL framing for path supervision quality estimation from answer-level labels is a clean reformulation. Non-Obviousness (75) is good: treating spurious-path filtering as a bag-level learning problem is not the obvious move. Rigor (78) is strong — ablations isolating supervision vs. reasoning components, supervision-quality evaluation against SPARQL ground truth (independent of end-task), three-run averages. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) are good but the mixed LLM backbones across baselines and lack of large-scale/open-KG evaluation weaken extrapolation. Practical Impact (75) is meaningfully elevated by the model-agnostic supervision transfer to RoG and SubgraphRAG, which is the most compelling result. Composite ≈ 0.20·72 + 0.25·78 + 0.15·75 + 0.15·72 + 0.15·70 + 0.10·75 = 74.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) requires retrieving question-relevant evidence from large KGs (e.g., Freebase, WikiMovies) to ground LLM reasoning. State-of-the-art KG-RAG approaches train graph retrievers or relation path generators using intermediate supervision signals (annotated supporting paths or triples), which are expensive and time-consuming to obtain manually. A common low-cost alternative is weakly supervised paths — all paths in the KG connecting question entities to answer entities — but many of these are spurious: they reach the correct answer by coincidence rather than through the relevant reasoning chain (e.g., LeBron → teammate → Bronny → plays_for → Lakers, when the correct path is LeBron → parent → Bronny → plays_for → Lakers). Training on spurious paths degrades retrieval precision and ultimately answer accuracy. LLM-based supervision refinement (e.g., RAPL) reduces this noise but requires LLM calls proportional to training set size, making it computationally expensive and poorly scalable. Key Idea: The central insight is to treat relation paths as latent variables and reformulate supervision quality estimation as a Multiple Instance Learning problem: group all weakly supervised candidate paths that reach the same answer entity into a positive bag, train a bag-level classifier from answer-level labels, and extract the per-instance attention weights as path informativeness scores — without requiring any explicit path-level annotations. The high-scoring paths become pseudo supervision that filters out spurious candidates before training the downstream path generator. Methodology: PATHISE operates in three stages. Stage 1 (MIL-based Informative Path Estimation): Given a question q and its answer entities, candidate relation paths are enumerated via breadth-first search up to L hops over the KG. Positive MIL bags group all paths reaching the same answer entity; negative bags are singleton paths that reach no answer entity. A 2-layer transformer encodes each (question, relation-path) pair jointly — question token and relation token embeddings combined with learnable positional embeddings — and attention-based MIL aggregation (Ilse & Welling 2018) produces bag representations. The unnormalized attention score s_i serves as the path informativeness estimate. The MIL estimator is trained with binary cross-entropy on bag-level labels (Eq. 10). Top-T scored paths per question form the pseudo supervision set Z_q. Stage 2 (Pseudo Supervision Distillation): Z_q is converted into a hard uniform target distribution, and LLaMA3.1-8B-Instruct is fine-tuned with LoRA via KL minimization (equivalent to log-likelihood maximization over Z*_q, Eq. 12) to produce a relation path generator P_θ. Stage 3 (KG-grounded Inductive Reasoning): At inference, beam search (K=5) generates K relation paths; each is grounded in the KG to produce evidence tuples (entity, path, reachable entities), which are verbalized and provided to an LLM (GPT-4o or GPT-4.1) for zero-shot answer generation. Critical design decisions: (a) using unnormalized attention scores for ranking rather than calibrated probabilities — well-justified since the goal is ranking, not probability estimation; (b) grouping paths by answer entity into bags rather than treating all weakly supervised paths as a flat set — well-justified by the MIL assumption that at least one path in a positive bag is informative; (c) fine-tuning a small LLM (8B) as path generator rather than prompting a large LLM — justified by the inference cost analysis, though the trade-off between fine-tuning cost and inference savings is not deeply analyzed. Key Results: On WebQSP (1–2 hop, Freebase): PATHISE with GPT-4o achieves F1=81.3%, Hit=91.2%, Hits@1=86.8%, outperforming the best weakly supervised baseline DP by 5.5% F1, and matching or exceeding LLM-refined baselines RAPL (80.7% F1) and ReG (78.8% F1) by +0.6% and +2.5% F1 respectively. On CWQ (up to 4 hop, Freebase): F1=61.5%, Hit=69.7%, Hits@1=63.4% with GPT-4o, outperforming weakly supervised baselines by at least 3.8% F1, and achieving +1.0% Hit over RAPL on CWQ. On MetaQA (1–3 hop, WikiMovies): F1=96.2%, Hit=99.8%, Hits@1=99.8% with GPT-4o, competitive with all baselines. PATHISE achieves average inference runtime of 2.1s on WebQSP with 277 average input tokens — among the most efficient inference pipelines evaluated, comparable to GCR (3.5s, lower accuracy) and faster than DP (4.4s). The ablation in Table 3 shows replacing PATHISE supervision with weakly supervised paths drops F1 by 4.4% on WebQSP and 5.5% on CWQ; replacing with cosine-similarity-ranked paths drops 9.2% F1 on WebQSP. Applying PATHISE pseudo supervision to RoG improves it by 5.6% F1 on WebQSP and 8.4% F1 on CWQ; SubgraphRAG improves by 2.4% F1 on WebQSP and 3.0% F1 on CWQ. Supervision quality evaluation (Figure 3) shows PATHISE achieves comparable Hits@T to LLM-refined RAPL on WebQSP and outperforms by up to 11% on CWQ. Baselines are current and include strong models (DP, SubgraphRAG, RAPL), though GPT-4o vs. GPT-4.1 comparisons across methods are not fully controlled. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The application of MIL to estimate relation path informativeness from answer-level bag supervision for KGQA is the genuine novel contribution. The individual components — transformer path encoding, attention-based MIL aggregation, KL distillation into LLMs, KG-grounded RAG — are each established; PATHISE's novelty lies in their integration under the MIL framing specifically for intermediate supervision quality estimation, and the model-agnostic pseudo supervision that transfers to improve other KGQA architectures. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation studies (Table 3) isolate the supervision signal's contribution and validate that KG-grounded inductive reasoning outperforms RoG variants under the same supervision — the two components are tested separately and jointly. Results are averaged over three independent runs with standard deviations reported in Appendix H (not visible in main text). Pseudo-supervision quality is evaluated against SPARQL-extracted reference paths (ground-truth paths from annotated queries), providing an independent proxy beyond end-task accuracy. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/PathISE-2F87 (anonymous submission link — may not be persistent post-review). The MIL estimator uses gte-large-en-v1.5 with frozen weights, and the path generator uses LLaMA3.1-8B-Instruct; both are publicly available models. Evaluation follows the same dataset splits as baselines. One concern: evaluation on MetaQA uses only 200 questions per hop level (following prior work [26, 27]), which is a small subset — results there should be interpreted cautiously. The paper does not report whether the MIL estimator was tuned on the same benchmark it is evaluated on (WebQSP/CWQ), which is the primary evaluation set — the pseudo-supervision quality evaluation in Section 5.3 uses training-set subsets, raising a mild Goodhart's Law concern for that specific metric. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The MIL estimator (2-layer transformer) and LLaMA3.1-8B-Instruct with LoRA run on standard GPU hardware; BFS path enumeration over Freebase is the main scalability concern, not addressed in deployability terms. - Efficient: Inference averages 2.1s/question on WebQSP with 277 input tokens, competitive with the fastest baselines; training supervision construction requires no LLM calls, unlike RAPL which scales linearly in token cost with training set size. - Effective: End-task accuracy improvements over weakly supervised baselines (≥5.5% F1 on WebQSP) and parity or gains over LLM-refined baselines are demonstrated, and the model-agnostic supervision transfer further validates the core claim. - Scalable: Tested on Freebase (a large-scale KG) and WikiMovies; BFS path enumeration at L hops is exponential in L and the paper does not test or analyze behavior on KGs larger than Freebase or with higher-hop questions beyond 4; scalability to Wikidata-scale (billions of triples) is claimed but not evaluated. The software artifact is available (anonymized repository) but production-readiness is not assessed. Strengths: - The MIL framing is principled and directly addresses the spurious-path problem without requiring additional annotation or costly LLM inference during training — the design motivation is tightly matched to the technical solution. - Model-agnostic pseudo supervision: demonstrating that PATHISE-estimated paths improve two architecturally distinct baselines (RoG, a path generator, and SubgraphRAG, a graph retriever) substantively strengthens the contribution beyond a single pipeline result. - The supervision quality evaluation against SPARQL-extracted ground-truth paths (Hits@T, Figure 3) provides an evaluation modality independent of end-task accuracy, catching potential cases where improved accuracy could be attributed to the reasoning LLM rather than path quality. - Efficiency is a genuine practical contribution: matching LLM-refined accuracy without any LLM calls during supervision construction removes a significant deployment barrier for large training sets. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that KG-grounded inductive reasoning may underperform in sparse KG settings where correct answer entities are not reachable via the generated paths. They also acknowledge that supervision construction cost for PATHISE (BFS path enumeration and MIL training) is not zero, though substantially lower than LLM-refined approaches. They do not quantify the MIL training cost in wall-clock time relative to LLM refinement. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The evaluation is entirely on closed-world, static KGs (Freebase, WikiMovies); generalization to open KGs (Wikidata, heterogeneous domain KGs) is unstated and unvalidated. The paper compares PATHISE with GPT-4o against some baselines using GPT-4o and others using GPT-4.1 or LLaMA — the LLM backbone is not fully controlled across all comparison points in Table 1, making direct attribution of gains to PATHISE vs. the backbone model difficult for some rows. The MIL estimator's sensitivity to the BFS depth hyperparameter L and the top-T selection threshold is mentioned but detailed sensitivity analysis is deferred to appendices. There is no analysis of failure modes: specifically, whether PATHISE degrades on questions where all weakly supervised paths are spurious (i.e., the positive bag contains no truly informative path), a scenario where the MIL assumption fails. The anonymous code repository link may not survive the review cycle, which is a reproducibility risk. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or improving KG-RAG pipelines for KGQA will find PATHISE's MIL-based supervision estimator and model-agnostic pseudo supervision transfer concretely useful — the core idea is clean, the evidence is solid on standard benchmarks, and the efficiency advantage over LLM-refined supervision is a genuine practical win, though the absence of large-scale or out-of-distribution KG evaluation leaves the scalability claim unverified.
Citations 2605.10791 PDF
#41 — Innovative (74)

Benchmarking Sensor-Fault Robustness in Forecasting

Alexander Windmann, Philipp Wittenberg, Gianluca Manca, Marcel Dix, Jens U. Brandt, Oliver Niggemann · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SensorFault-Bench, a shared benchmark protocol that stress-tests forecasting architectures and robustness-improvement methods across eight scored CPS sensor-fault scenarios on four real-world datasets, reporting three jointly-tracked quantities — clean MSE, worst-scenario degradation (D_w), and worst-scenario fault-time MSE. - Why it's important: Standard forecasting evaluation selects models by clean MSE alone, which can favor architectures that degrade sharply under realistic sensor faults such as drift, stuck readings, or missing data — making deployed CPS forecasters operationally unreliable without any pre-deployment warning. - How it does it: It defines a severity-parameterized perturbation protocol over value, timing, and availability fault families, computes scenario degradation as D_p(f) = MSE_p(f)/MSE_c(f), takes the worst-case over scored scenarios as the robustness score, and separates fault-training transfer (P_trans, 7 disjoint families) from scored evaluation (P, 8 families) to prevent contamination. - The good: The empirical finding that clean-MSE rankings and worst-scenario degradation rankings disagree in concrete, architecturally-specific ways — e.g., GRU beats ModernTCN and DLinear on D_w and MSE_w on Penmanshiel WT08 despite trailing on clean MSE, and Chronos-2 has the largest D_w on both ETTh1 and Traffic — directly falsifies the implicit assumption that better nominal models are safer deployments. - The missing: All four datasets are hourly CPS streams from a narrow set of physical domains (air quality, wind-turbine SCADA, transformer telemetry, freeway occupancy); no sub-hourly, daily, or non-CPS domains are included, and no robustness method consistently dominates, leaving practitioners without a clear actionable recommendation. - Read it or not: Essential for practitioners deploying forecasting models on industrial sensor streams and for benchmarking researchers designing robustness evaluation protocols; safely skippable for those working exclusively on non-CPS time-series domains.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (70) — new protocol pairing three-quantity reporting (clean MSE / D_w / MSE_w) with disjoint transfer-fault splits, a genuine assembly contribution though built from existing perturbations. Rigor (78) — bootstrap CIs for paired deltas, principled clean-selection rule, open code; preprint and limited tuning budget temper this. Non-Obviousness (70) — the divergence of clean-MSE and worst-scenario rankings is concrete but somewhat expected. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) solid with honest limitation section. Practical Impact (82) high from released benchmark with extension interface. Weighted composite: 0.15*70 + 0.15*78 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.30*82 = 75.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 82
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Cyber-physical systems deploy forecasting models on sensor pipelines where failures — including noise, offset drift, gain attenuation, temporal misalignment, stuck readings, and missing values — are routine, structured, and operationally diagnosable. Standard forecasting benchmarks evaluate models by held-out MSE under clean conditions, selecting architectures that may silently fail once the sensing pipeline degrades. Practitioners in industrial, safety-relevant settings need pre-deployment screening that distinguishes nominal accuracy from fault sensitivity and that characterizes absolute fault-time error separately from relative degradation. Prior robustness work addressed adversarial perturbations or anomaly-contaminated training in fragmented settings without a shared CPS-grounded fault taxonomy, paired robustness-improvement comparisons, or disjoint transfer-fault splits. Key Idea: The core technical insight is that robustness evaluation must jointly track three separable quantities — clean accuracy, relative degradation under worst-case faults, and absolute fault-time error — because each answers a different deployment question and rankings under the three quantities can diverge substantially. The secondary insight is that any method that trains on fault families adjacent to the scored scenarios must be evaluated on disjoint held-out scenarios (P_trans vs. P split); otherwise single corrupted-error summaries conflate architecture robustness with transfer effectiveness. Methodology: The benchmark defines 8 scored perturbation scenarios grouped into three families: Value (Drift, Attenuation, Noise, Spike), Timing (TimeStretch, TimeCompress), and Availability (StuckSensor, MissingData). Each scenario applies a severity parameter s ∈ [0,1] uniformly to a continuous subset of channels (capped at half of eligible channels for channel-scoped scenarios), with calibrated endpoints (e.g., Noise Gaussian std = 0 to 1, Spike magnitude std = 0 to 7.5, MissingData gap fraction = 0 to 0.5). The robustness score D_p(f) = MSE_p(f)/MSE_c(f) is the ratio of perturbed to clean expected test MSE; the primary score D_w(f) = max_{p∈P} D_p(f) is the worst-case over all 8 scenarios. The accompanying training-only transfer set P_trans contains 7 adjacent fault families that are scored only when explicit fault-training methods use them for augmentation. Four datasets are used: Beijing Air Tiantan (24,038 hourly steps, 12 input channels, PM2.5 target, 96-step input / 6-step horizon), Penmanshiel WT08 (25,867 steps, 65 channels, Power (kW) target, 96/6), ETTh1 (17,420 steps, 7 channels, all-channel targets, 96/96), and Traffic (17,544 steps, 862 channels, all-channel targets, 96/96). Seven forecasting architectures are compared: DLinear, GRU, ModernTCN, TSMixer, PatchTST, SeasonalNaive, and Chronos-2 (zero-shot). Seven robustness-improvement methods are applied (where applicable) as paired deltas against no-intervention baselines: PGD adversarial training, ensemble aggregation, randomized training, randomized smoothing, adaptive robust loss (ARL), RevIN, and fault augmentation. Model selection uses only clean validation MSE with up to 40 seeded random-subgrid candidates per configuration, capped at 200 epochs with validation-based early stopping, keeping tuning effort comparable across architectures and methods. Key Results: Clean-MSE rankings and worst-scenario degradation rankings diverge concretely across all four datasets. On Penmanshiel WT08, ModernTCN and DLinear slightly beat GRU on clean MSE (0.346 and 0.340 vs. 0.281) but GRU achieves the lowest D_w (1.064) and lowest MSE_w (0.368) among trained architectures. On Traffic, PatchTST has the lowest MSE_w (0.728) but the second-largest D_w (1.290) behind Chronos-2 (1.911), while ModernTCN has lower D_w (1.177) despite higher MSE_w. Chronos-2 matches or trails SeasonalNaive on clean MSE for the two single-target datasets and has the largest D_w on ETTh1 (1.448) and Traffic (1.911). Among robustness-improvement methods, PGD adversarial training and randomized training reduce degradation most on single-target datasets where value faults dominate (ΔD_w = −0.136 for PGD on Beijing Air Tiantan), while fault augmentation leads where availability faults dominate (Traffic: ΔD_w = −0.025). Ensemble aggregation consistently reduces absolute error. RevIN lowers clean MSE on ETTh1 and Traffic but increases degradation in the same paired comparisons (ΔD_w = +0.124 on ETTh1). No method produces consistently negative ΔD_w across all four datasets, all architectures, and all three reported quantities. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The three-quantity reporting protocol pairing clean MSE, worst-scenario degradation, and worst-scenario fault-time MSE with a disjoint transfer-fault split is a genuine protocol contribution not present in prior CPS forecasting benchmarks. The individual perturbation types and robustness methods are drawn from existing literature; the novelty lies in their assembly into a unified, reproducible, cross-dataset comparison under a shared severity model. The empirical finding that Chronos-2 is not a strong zero-shot robustness reference and that RevIN can trade clean accuracy for higher fault sensitivity are new empirical facts. Rigor & Reproducibility: The benchmark reports 95% bootstrap confidence intervals for paired method deltas (Table 17 in appendix), with matched-pair variation over architectures. The clean-selection rule isolates validation from fault information, preventing leakage. Code, dataset derivation rules, and reproduction guides are released at https://github.com/awindmann/SensorFault-Bench. Key methodological concerns: (1) the 7 and 8 scenario family endpoint calibrations are set by standardization practice and expert judgment, not field fault prevalence surveys — the benchmark measures robustness under these chosen levels but does not estimate field failure rates; (2) with only 40 random-subgrid candidates and bounded compute, robustness-improvement method underperformance may reflect insufficient tuning rather than fundamental limits; (3) the four datasets co-vary on domain, forecast horizon, and affected-channel scope rather than varying independently, making it impossible to isolate which factor drives the availability-vs-value fault difficulty shift. This is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a benchmarking tool with open-source code, dataset derivation documentation, and extension interface). - Practical: The benchmark runs standard trained architectures on four public or released datasets; all components run on commercially available hardware without specialized infrastructure. - Efficient: Training cost scales with architecture and dataset size; the benchmark harness adds perturbation sampling overhead but no large-scale compute requirement beyond running 7 architectures × 7 methods × 4 datasets with up to 40 seeded candidates each. - Effective: The benchmark detects disaggreements between clean-MSE and fault-robustness rankings that are invisible to standard evaluation, empirically demonstrating the protocol's diagnostic value across four independent dataset settings. - Scalable: The extension interface supports adding new datasets, architectures, and methods under the same scoring protocol; the paper evaluates at the scale of existing public CPS benchmarks (up to 862 channels, 25,867 hourly steps) but does not test at larger industrial scales. The delivered artifact is an open-source benchmark suite with reproduction and extension guides. Strengths: - The three-quantity reporting protocol (clean MSE / D_w / MSE_w) is conceptually clean and addresses a real confusion in prior work where apparent robustness gains may simply reflect lower nominal error rather than lower fault sensitivity. - The disjoint P_trans / P split is a principled design that prevents fault-training methods from gaming the scored scenarios — a methodological gap absent from prior benchmarks. - The RevIN finding (lowers clean MSE, raises worst-scenario degradation on ETTh1 and Traffic in paired comparisons) is a concrete, falsifiable counter-example to the assumption that instance normalization universally improves robustness. - Open-source release with explicit dataset derivation rules, dataset-level derivation tables, and extension path documentation makes this replicable and extendable in a way that self-reported vendor benchmarks are not. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that scenarios are simulated input-side faults, not recorded field fault episodes; the benchmark uses one active scenario at a time (no composed faults, persistent target-sensor faults, process shocks, or closed-loop effects); the four datasets span distinct CPS settings but not all domains, sensor modalities, or control regimes; and the robustness-improvement method panel is architecture-controlled with bounded tuning rather than exhaustive search. These are presented directly in Section 7, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: All four datasets are hourly CPS streams; the benchmark makes no claims about non-CPS domains (finance, health, weather) but does not test whether its findings generalize even within CPS to sub-hourly SCADA, daily industrial, or event-driven streams. The endpoint calibration for severity endpoints (e.g., Spike magnitude std up to 7.5, MissingData fraction up to 0.5) is grounded in literature citations but ultimately chosen by the authors — different endpoints would yield different D_w rankings, and sensitivity to these choices is not evaluated in the main paper. The main architecture comparison lacks cross-architecture significance tests; bootstrap intervals are provided only for method deltas. The small method panel (7 methods, not all tested on all 7 architectures) means the conclusion that no method dominates may not hold against stronger or more carefully tuned approaches. The Chronos-2 findings specifically — trailing naive forecasters in clean MSE and showing highest degradation — are striking but draw on a single zero-shot model and may not generalize to other time-series foundation models. Bottom Line: CPS practitioners and benchmarking researchers gain concrete, reproducible evidence that clean-MSE model selection is insufficient for fault-tolerant deployment, along with a reusable protocol for pre-deployment fault screening — making this required reading for anyone who deploys forecasting models on industrial sensor streams.
Search Results 2605.10822 PDF
#42 — Innovative (74)

Toward Better Geometric Representations for Molecule Generative Models

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LENSEs (Latent Enhancement for Non-smooth Structural Encodings), a framework that refines the conditioning latent space of geometric representation-conditioned 3D molecule generators through three complementary training objectives: a VAE-style multi-layer representation head, a molecular perceptual loss, and a node-level representation alignment (REPA) loss. - Why it's important: Pretrained molecular encoders (e.g., UniMol, Frad) produce non-smooth, semantically underexploited representations that degrade generation quality in representation-conditioned pipelines — fixing the representation space directly improves structural validity and physical plausibility without altering the generator architecture. - How it does it: A VAE-style head aggregates multi-layer encoder representations via learnable softmax pooling into a smoothed latent space; a perceptual loss aligns generated-molecule encoder representations to clean-molecule encoder representations; and a REPA loss maximizes cosine similarity between generator internal node features and frozen encoder node features. - The good: On GEOM-DRUG, LENSEs achieves 97.28% validity and 98.51% molecule stability, surpassing all baselines including GeoRCG (95.3% validity) and CanonFlow (95.9%), while reducing the empirical Lipschitz constant 4.6× — providing mechanistic evidence that representation smoothing causally drives generation quality gains. - The missing: No evaluation on structure-based drug design (protein-pocket-conditioned generation), no diversity metrics beyond uniqueness, and the claim that refined representations constitute a "generative pretraining paradigm" rests on a single QM9 linear regression experiment with one encoder (Frad), leaving cross-encoder and cross-task generalizability unvalidated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on conditional 3D molecule generation, latent diffusion for molecules, or representation learning for molecular generative models; can be skipped by practitioners focused on protein-conditioned or fragment-based drug design.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (80) drives the score — ablations isolate each loss component, Bonferroni-corrected probes, Lipschitz/effective-rank diagnostics with formal pseudocode, but no code release and a non-stationary perceptual target left unanalyzed. Novelty (70) moderate — NEW METHOD adapting perceptual loss and REPA to molecules, not a new formulation. Non-Obviousness (75) — layer-probe finding that early layers encode functional groups is a non-obvious insight. Completeness (70) pulled down by missing SBDD evaluation and absent diversity metrics. Practical Impact (60) — method and recipe, no released artifacts. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*80+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.15*75+0.10*60 = 73.5.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation The geometric representation-conditioned generation (GeoRCG) paradigm decouples molecular representation learning from 3D structure generation: a representation diffusion model first samples a molecular latent code, and an equivariant generator produces a 3D structure conditioned on it. This paradigm's ceiling is set by representation quality. Two specific bottlenecks afflict existing pretrained encoders trained via denoising objectives (UniMol, Frad): (1) non-smooth representation manifolds — small coordinate perturbations cause disproportionately large shifts in latent space (empirical Lipschitz constants of 2659.81 mean / 3545.67 max on GEOM-DRUG for raw UniMol), forcing prior work to inject hand-tuned noise as a compensatory hack; and (2) underexploited hierarchical structure — shallow encoder layers linearly encode functional group motifs (hydroxyl, amino, carbonyl) with up to 14% higher balanced accuracy than deep layers on QM9, yet existing pipelines condition generation on only the final-layer representation, discarding structurally informative earlier features. Drug-design researchers and generative-chemistry practitioners using any representation-conditioned 3D generation system are affected. Key Idea The core insight is that the representation space used to condition molecule generation should be jointly optimized with the generator, not imported frozen from an encoder trained for property prediction. A VAE-style head over learnable multi-layer pooling creates a smoother, generation-aligned latent space; a perceptual loss (borrowed from image generation and applied at the molecular level) transfers multi-scale structural semantics from the encoder to the generator output; and a node-level REPA loss explicitly aligns the generator's internal hidden states to encoder node features, accelerating the generator's acquisition of chemical structure knowledge rather than requiring it to rediscover it from scratch. Methodology LENSEs operates as a three-phase pipeline on top of a frozen pretrained encoder and an equivariant generator backbone (SemlaFlow in experiments). Phase I trains the generator and representation head jointly: a frozen encoder extracts layer-wise graph representations H^(l)(M) ∈ ℝ^d for l=1,...,L; a learnable softmax-weighted pooling g(M) = Σ α_l H^(l)(M) aggregates them; a VAE head maps g(M) to a diagonal Gaussian posterior (μ, log σ²) with log-variance clamped for stability; latent code z = μ + σ⊙ε is sampled via reparameterization and fed as conditioning to the equivariant generator. Three auxiliary losses supplement the generation loss: (a) KL loss L_KL regularizes the posterior toward N(0,I), inducing compact smooth structure; (b) perceptual loss L_PERC = w(t)||sg(g(M)) − g(M̂)||² with cosine time-schedule weighting w(t) aligns the denoised-molecule representation to the clean-molecule representation (stop-gradient on target; straight-through estimator handles discrete atom types); (c) REPA loss L_REPA = −(1/|A|)Σ⟨PROJ(H^(l_g)_gen,i), sg(H^(l_e)_enc,i)⟩ maximizes cosine similarity between generator and encoder node features via a learnable projector. Total Phase I objective: L_total = L_gen + λ_KL L_KL + λ_PERC L_PERC + λ_REPA L_REPA. Phase II trains a Representation Diffusion Model (RDM, SimpleMLP backbone) in μ-space. Phase III generates molecules by sampling μ̂ from RDM then drawing M from the generator conditioned on μ̂. Three critical design decisions: learnable layer pooling (motivated by empirical probe analysis showing early-layer superiority) is well-justified; cosine similarity in REPA instead of MSE to neutralize feature-scale discrepancies across encoder/generator spaces is well-justified; the stop-gradient on the perceptual target prevents circular optimization and is standard practice — appropriate but creates a non-stationary target that the paper does not analyze for stability. Key Results On GEOM-DRUG (large drug-like molecules): LENSEs achieves 97.28±0.18% validity (vs. 95.3% GeoRCG, 95.9% CanonFlow), 98.51±0.16% molecule stability (vs. 97.20% GeoRCG), 100.0% uniqueness, 85.53±0.45 kcal/mol energy (vs. 86.6 GeoRCG), 43.86±0.27 kcal/mol strain (vs. 47.64 GeoRCG). On QM9 (133,885 small organics): 99.9% atom stability, 99.68±0.04% molecule stability, 99.48±0.08% validity, 0.21±0.0 Å Opt-RMSD (vs. 0.23 SemlaFlow), 100 NFE (fewest among compared methods). Representation quality: Lipschitz constant reduced 4.6× on GEOM-DRUG (mean 2659.81 → 570.49, max 3545.67 → 1781.41); effective rank increases from 65.32 to 88.42, diagnosing and correcting dimensional collapse in raw UniMol. QM9 downstream regression (frozen representations): LENSEs outperforms raw Frad encoder on all 7 properties — C_v MAE 0.100 → 0.0481, HOMO gap 54.65 → 41.90 meV, LUMO gap 54.65 → 41.90 meV, dipole μ 0.152 → 0.119. Baselines (FlowMol, MiDi, EQGAT-diff, SemlaFlow, CanonFlow, GeoRCG) are all recent (2022–2026) and competitive. Ablations confirm all three loss components contribute complementary benefits with no single component dominating. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The paper adapts perceptual loss (from image super-resolution/generation) and representation alignment (REPA, from vision diffusion transformers) to the molecular 3D generation domain, combining them with a VAE-style multi-layer representation head into a principled three-component framework. Each component individually adapts known techniques, but their systematic combination to address the specific non-smoothness and multi-level semantics bottleneck in molecular encoder representations — with empirical validation of representation geometry metrics as the causal mechanism rather than just generation metrics — constitutes a genuine methodological contribution. The "generative pretraining" framing (generation optimizes representations for broader tasks) is a secondary conceptual contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study (Table 5) tests removing each of the KL, perceptual, and REPA losses independently across a range of λ hyperparameter values, confirming robustness and complementarity. Error bars (±std) appear on main results. The layer probe experiment uses Bonferroni-corrected significance testing with p < 0.001 across most functional groups (Table 7). Lipschitz, effective rank, pairwise distance, and kNN smoothness metrics are formally defined with pseudocode (Algorithms 2–5), enabling reproduction of the representation-quality diagnostics. Code is not announced as publicly released anywhere in the paper — a reproducibility concern for a preprint. This is arXiv:2605.07693v1 (8 May 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. One methodological subtlety: the perceptual loss uses g(M̂) computed via the same representation head being trained, making the learning target non-stationary; no ablation or convergence analysis addresses this. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The method adds a lightweight representation head (learnable layer-pooling + two MLP projection heads) and two auxiliary losses to an existing pretrained-encoder + equivariant-generator pipeline; runs on standard GPU hardware with no architectural changes to either the frozen encoder or the generator. - Efficient: The perceptual loss requires two frozen-encoder forward passes per training step (clean + denoised molecule); inference-time NFE equals the backbone generator (100 for SemlaFlow), with no added cost over GeoRCG. Appendix B.8 provides computational resource details not read, but the method is described as adding modest overhead. - Effective: Strong evidence on GEOM-DRUG validity, stability, energy, and strain; Lipschitz reduction and effective rank increase provide mechanistic support; downstream QM9 regression improvement validates representation quality transfer. The causal link between smoothness and generation quality is established correlatively, not via a controlled intervention isolating Lipschitz constant independently of the other losses. - Scalable: Tested on GEOM-DRUG (complex drug-like conformations) and QM9 (small organics up to 9 heavy atoms). Not tested on macromolecules, protein-pocket-conditioned generation, or datasets beyond these two benchmarks. No extrapolation experiment at 10× molecular size or dataset scale. The work delivers a method and training recipe; no software library or trained model weights are released. Strengths - The layer probe analysis on Frad with Bonferroni-corrected significance testing empirically demonstrates that early encoder layers retain more functional-group-discriminating information than final layers — a non-obvious finding that rigorously motivates the multi-layer pooling design rather than asserting it heuristically. - Lipschitz constant reduction (4.6× on GEOM-DRUG) alongside generation metric improvement provides converging causal evidence that representation smoothness drives generation quality, elevating the paper beyond an empirical tuning exercise. - Effective rank diagnostics (65.32 → 88.42) reveal dimensional collapse in raw UniMol, concretely naming a structural deficiency the framework corrects and showing the fix is not merely noise injection. - Downstream QM9 regression improvement with frozen LENSEs-refined representations (C_v MAE reduced 52%) supports the bold and potentially high-impact claim that generative training can serve as a representation refinement procedure. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state three future directions as implicit limitations: (1) applying LENSEs to few-step generation settings (current evaluation does not fully explore this); (2) understanding theoretically when and why generative pretraining improves downstream generalization (the mechanism is empirically supported, not theoretically explained); (3) developing molecular-domain perceptual models rather than using general molecular encoders as perceptual targets. These are honest, specific, and credibility-positive disclosures. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No evaluation on structure-based drug design (target-aware generation conditioned on protein pockets), which is the most clinically relevant application of drug-like molecule generation and the domain where GEOM-DRUG-like molecules are actually used. - The "generative pretraining" claim is validated with Frad encoder on QM9 downstream tasks, but LENSEs uses UniMol for GEOM-DRUG experiments — whether UniMol-based LENSEs representations transfer to downstream regression with similar gains is untested. - Uniqueness reaches 100% across nearly all methods, making it a non-discriminating metric; distribution-level diversity metrics (Wasserstein distance, novelty vs. training set, scaffold diversity) are absent and would better characterize whether LENSEs generates diverse drug-like chemistry or mode-collapses near training examples. - The perceptual loss computes g(M̂) via the same representation head being trained, creating a non-stationary learning target; no training stability analysis or ablation varying the target (e.g., using a frozen copy of the head as an exponential moving average) is provided. - No code or model weights are released, limiting the community's ability to replicate or build on the representation-quality diagnostics. Bottom Line A practitioner building representation-conditioned 3D molecule generation systems gains concrete, component-validated training objectives from this paper that demonstrably smooth the conditioning latent space and improve generation quality, making it a worthwhile read for anyone in that specific area.
Citations 2605.07693 PDF
#43 — Innovative (74)

PrefixGuard: From LLM-Agent Traces to Online Failure-Warning Monitors

Xinmiao Huang, Jinwei Hu, Rajarshi Roy, Changshun Wu, Yi Dong, Xiaowei Huang · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PrefixGuard, a neural-symbolic framework that converts raw LLM agent execution traces into online failure-warning monitors by jointly learning a typed trace representation (StepView) and a differentiable discrete event abstraction layer trained against prefix-level imminent-failure labels, with no LLM calls at deployment time. - Why it's important: LLM agents executing long-horizon tool-using tasks in high-stakes domains (automated software engineering, cybersecurity, financial management) can cause irreversible damage before terminal task verifiers fire, and existing monitoring approaches either require expensive per-step LLM inference or brittle hand-authored event schemas that break across heterogeneous trace formats. - How it does it: A one-time offline LLM call induces a frozen deterministic adapter (StepView) that canonicalizes raw traces into typed fields; a TF-IDF encoder then feeds a Gumbel-softmax event abstraction layer that learns a failure-aligned discrete alphabet end-to-end, consumed by a GRU/Transformer/soft-FSM monitor trained on binary cross-entropy over all prefix positions. - The good: PrefixGuard-GRU achieves 0.900/0.710/0.533/0.557 AUPRC on WebArena/τ²-Bench/SkillsBench/TerminalBench, averaging +0.137 AUPRC over matched raw-text GRU controls, while consistently outperforming zero-shot LLM judges (best: 0.450/0.396/0.080/0.107) across all four benchmarks with no runtime LLM dependency. - The missing: The evaluation is entirely on pre-collected trajectory replays — there is no live deployment study showing that a raised alert leads to a successful intervention before irreversible damage, which is the stated motivation for the work. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone building online safety monitors or runtime verification systems for LLM agents; the AUPRC observability ceiling theorem and the ranking-vs.-alarm-utility separation are valuable regardless of whether one adopts the full PrefixGuard pipeline.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (78) is the headline strength: 3 seeds, locked test splits, content-scrambled controls, field-drop ablations, and horizon sensitivity scans across four benchmarks address the main competing explanations cleanly. Novelty (78) is high — the AUPRC observability ceiling (Proposition 1 with tight proof) and the empirical separation of ranking quality from low-FAR alarm utility are genuine contributions beyond the system itself. Non-Obviousness (75) is good — the ranking/utility decoupling corrects a common conflation in agent eval. Completeness (75) and Resilience (72) are credibility-positive thanks to direct limitation acknowledgment, but the absence of any live intervention evaluation — the stated motivation — weakens both. Impact (60) is capped by anonymous-only code, no released weights, and no deployment loop. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*60 ≈ 74.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents performing long-horizon multi-step tasks in high-stakes settings can take irreversible erroneous actions well before a terminal task verifier fires, creating demand for online warning signals that flag trajectory drift toward failure in real time from partial execution prefixes. Three complementary gaps motivate the work: (i) classical runtime verification requires a stable, hand-authored event vocabulary and formalism, which breaks for heterogeneous LLM-agent traces mixing browser actions, tool calls, dialogue turns, shell outputs, and evolving benchmark-specific formats; (ii) LLM-as-judge evaluation protocols require expensive per-prefix LLM inference at every step, making them impractical for deployment; (iii) predictive prefix classifiers can recover signal but produce no calibrated monitor state, no inspectable symbolic artifacts, and no diagnostic for when warnings cannot improve. The specific question is whether raw execution traces, paired with outcome labels, can be converted into monitors whose internal state is cheap to compute, whose evidence is stable across trace formats, and whose failure modes are diagnosable. Key Idea The core insight is to separate monitor synthesis into two components with different LLM-cost profiles: a one-time offline adapter induction step (StepView) that pays LLM cost once to produce a frozen deterministic field-extraction function, followed by a fully differentiable event abstraction layer that learns a discrete failure-aligned alphabet from the prefix-warning objective rather than from a fixed hand-authored vocabulary. Using Gumbel-softmax for differentiable discrete symbol assignment allows the alphabet to be shaped end-to-end by the warning loss while also producing hard symbol assignments post-training that can be compiled into finite automata for post-hoc audit. The separation means deployment runs with zero LLM calls: only TF-IDF encoding and a small GRU. Methodology The four-stage pipeline is: (1) StepView adapter induction — a one-time offline LLM call (gpt-5.4-nano, temperature 0.0) over a deterministic 12-step sample pack from training trajectories produces a field-extraction adapter mapping each raw step cₜ to a canonical seven-field record (metadata, observation, action, tool, args, result, status); the adapter is reviewed only for structural correctness and frozen for all downstream use with 0% fallback rate across 1.8M steps. (2) TF-IDF step encoding — each StepView record is serialized in fixed field-tagged order and encoded by a TF-IDF vectorizer (top d=4096 unigram+bigram features, ℓ₂-normalized) trained on training steps only and then frozen. (3) Differentiable event abstraction — a two-layer MLP with GELU maps step embeddings eₜ ∈ ℝᵈ to logits over K symbols, with Gumbel-softmax at temperature τg=0.5 yielding soft assignment αₜ ∈ Δᴷ⁻¹; a symbol-balance regularizer (entropy sharpening + marginal entropy maximization, λ_balance=0.1) prevents symbol collapse. (4) Prefix-warning monitor backends — PrefixGuard-GRU processes symbol sequences with a single-layer GRU; PrefixGuard-Transformer uses a causally-masked self-attention encoder; PrefixGuard-FSM maintains a probability distribution over Q abstract states via a learned K×Q×Q transition tensor T updated with the soft Gumbel-softmax assignment. All backends minimize binary cross-entropy over all prefix positions with per-trajectory 1/T normalization. Imminent failure labels pₜ = 𝟙[y=0 ∧ t ≥ T−H] with H=3 (inclusive failure horizon). Optionally, post-training hard symbol assignments drive RPNI automaton extraction for finite-state audit, with per-state risk calibrated from a held-out calibration split. Critical design decisions: (i) frozen adapter decouples LLM cost from deployment — well-justified; (ii) fixed Gumbel temperature rather than annealing trades symbol sharpness for training stability — partially justified by balance regularizer; (iii) H=3 is fixed for cross-benchmark comparability despite H=1 yielding better AUPRC in the horizon sensitivity scan — acknowledged as a comparability choice, not a global optimum. Key Results Evaluated on four benchmarks with locked train/calibration/validation/test splits: WebArena (4,427 trajectories, browser navigation, 7.9% success, r=0.363), τ²-Bench (10,832 trajectories, tool dialogue, 66.3% success, r=0.089), SkillsBench (10,951 trajectories, coding, 27.5% success, r=0.092), TerminalBench (34,397 trajectories, CLI agents, 32.2% success, r=0.070). Primary metric is score-based AUPRC (random baseline = r). PrefixGuard-GRU achieves 0.900±0.015 / 0.696±0.004 / 0.533±0.020 / 0.557±0.005 vs. raw-text GRU at 0.871±0.004 / 0.554±0.006 / 0.315±0.006 / 0.370±0.001, a gain of +0.029/+0.142/+0.218/+0.187. Zero-shot LLM judges (best: DeepSeek-V4-Pro) reach 0.450/0.396/0.080/0.107 — substantially below all PrefixGuard configurations. Surprising finding on deployment utility: WebArena's 0.900 AUPRC reflects its high positive-prefix prevalence (r=0.363, trajectories are short), and at a 10% FAR cap it achieves only 0.007 early-before-window recall (nearly zero early intervention utility) while τ²-Bench (0.696 AUPRC) achieves 0.192 early recall at the same FAR — decoupling ranking from alarm separability. DFA compactness varies sharply: WebArena 29 states (99.9% trusted coverage), τ²-Bench 20 states (99.3%), SkillsBench 151 states, TerminalBench 187 states; the latter two undermine compact auditability claims. Content-scrambled controls (Table 20) show WebArena AUPRC barely changes under within-prefix shuffling (0.908→0.901), confirming step-content dominates, while SkillsBench and TerminalBench drop by −0.257 and −0.173, confirming sequential ordering carries genuine signal on those benchmarks. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Genuinely novel: (i) the trace-to-monitor synthesis formulation that jointly learns a discrete failure-aligned alphabet and prefix-warning monitor from outcome-labeled trajectories without hand-authored schemas or deployment-time LLM calls; (ii) the tight AUPRC observability ceiling (Proposition 1) derived from a contaminated-distribution model of hidden vs. observable failure prefixes; (iii) the empirical separation of AUPRC ranking quality from low-FAR alarm utility across four heterogeneous benchmarks. The StepView adapter and post-hoc DFA extraction apply established techniques (RPNI automaton learning, Gumbel-softmax VQ) in a new combination for LLM-agent monitoring; the multi-backend architecture itself is competent engineering of known components. Rigor & Reproducibility Reproducibility is strong for a preprint. Results are reported as mean ± σ over 3 seeds for most experiments. Test splits are locked — unlocked once per variant. Field-drop ablations span all four benchmarks and three backends (Table 22). Position/task-prior confound controls (Table 19), content-scrambled controls (Table 20), continuous sequence controls (Table 17), and dense encoder diagnostics (Table 18) collectively address the main competing explanations for the observed gains. Horizon sensitivity is validated at H ∈ {1,3,5} (Table 28). The anonymous code artifact is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/status/PrefixGuard-CF8A with adapter induction code and evaluation pipeline; trained model weights are not explicitly mentioned as released. One methodological concern: the DFA state alignment study (Appendix D.10) uses a single coder and is explicitly described as not a validated multi-coder interpretability study, yet auditability is a primary selling point of the paper. A second concern: the horizon H=1 consistently dominates AUPRC in validation scans but locked-test H=1 numbers are not reported, so practitioners cannot directly assess the best achievable monitor quality for a given benchmark. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods and systems evaluated on four trajectory-replay benchmarks. - Practical: Training runs on a single NVIDIA A100-40GB GPU in 30 minutes to 3.5 hours; deployed monitor inference uses no GPU — TF-IDF encoding is frozen CPU, and GRU forward passes are small (d=4096, hidden size Q_max=16–32), so deployment runs on commodity hardware. - Efficient: Per-step inference cost is one TF-IDF lookup, one 2-layer MLP, and one GRU step — orders of magnitude cheaper than LLM-as-judge baselines that require full-prefix context at every step; the trade-off is a frozen vocabulary that degrades silently on novel tool names or API formats not seen during training. - Effective: Consistently outperforms zero-shot LLM judges on all four benchmarks; however, all evaluation is on static trajectory replays, not live agent runs, and the paper acknowledges that raising an alert still requires visible prefix evidence, low-FAR separability, and a deployment action — none of which are evaluated in an intervention loop. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 34,397 trajectories / 1.27M steps; DFA state counts grow to 151–187 for the two larger benchmarks, degrading the finite-state auditability claim; routed DFA extraction for larger benchmarks is flagged as future work, not a current capability. The work delivers a code artifact (training pipeline, StepView adapters, DFA audit); trained weights not explicitly released. Strengths - The AUPRC observability ceiling (Proposition 1) with a complete tight proof is a genuine theoretical contribution that upper-bounds achievable AUPRC for any trace-only monitor based on the observable fraction π of failure evidence — providing a principled diagnostic independent of the specific learned monitor. - The empirical separation of ranking quality (AUPRC) from deployment utility (low-FAR early recall) is an important under-appreciated distinction; the first-alert diagnostics (Table 13) concretely show that WebArena's 0.900 AUPRC does not translate to early intervention utility while τ²-Bench's lower AUPRC does, correcting a common conflation in the agent evaluation literature. - The content-scrambled controls (Table 20) cleanly decompose step-content signal from sequential ordering signal across all four benchmarks, establishing that the GRU's recurrent modeling provides genuine value on SkillsBench and TerminalBench where shuffle drops AUPRC by 0.257 and 0.173 respectively. - StepView's 0% fallback rate across 1.8M steps on four heterogeneous benchmarks with under 30 minutes of human validation effort demonstrates that the one-time adapter induction is practically robust — a concrete engineering contribution for bootstrapping monitoring on new agent domains. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) PrefixGuard synthesizes monitors from traces but does not specify or evaluate intervention policies — actionability requires visible prefix evidence, low-FAR separability, and a post-alert response that the paper does not supply. (2) The fixed H and FAR caps are evaluation controls rather than certified population π estimates. (3) MPE coordinates are protocol-specific finite-sample diagnostics rather than certified true π values. (4) Routed DFA extraction under deployment-visible context remains a future direction, not a locked-test deployment claim. (5) DFA state alignment is single-coder and does not constitute a validated interpretability study. These confrontations are direct and credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The entire evaluation uses static trajectory replays; there is no experiment demonstrating that a fired alert enables a useful intervention before irreversible damage — the transition from "warning fires" to "intervention succeeds" is the key practical gap left unaddressed. - The frozen TF-IDF encoder creates a silent vocabulary-lock failure mode: any new agent version that introduces novel tool names, error formats, or API responses not present in training data will degrade the monitor without any detection mechanism; the paper tests frozen general-purpose dense embeddings (which underperform) but not fine-tuned or incrementally updated encoders. - H=1 consistently achieves higher AUPRC than H=3 in validation scans (e.g., WebArena 0.923 vs. 0.908; TerminalBench 0.700 vs. 0.548) but locked-test results at H=1 are not reported, so the headline AUPRC numbers are conservatively low relative to what a benchmark-specific practitioner deployment would achieve. - No cross-benchmark generalization test: a monitor trained on WebArena is never evaluated on TerminalBench or vice versa, so the claim that StepView provides a universal schema is not validated empirically. - The DFA auditability claim weakens substantially for SkillsBench (151 states) and TerminalBench (187 states), yet the paper presents all four benchmarks in a single framing; a cleaner contribution would explicitly scope finite-state monitoring to compact-DFA regimes (WebArena, τ²-Bench) rather than treating it as a general capability. Bottom Line A technically careful preprint that advances LLM-agent monitoring beyond LLM-as-judge baselines with a practical deployment story and unusually rigorous self-diagnostics; practitioners building real-time safety monitors for tool-using agents should read it, though the absence of any live intervention evaluation means the ultimate deployment value remains unproven.
Search Results 2605.06455 PDF
#44 — Innovative (73)

Mind the Gap? A Distributional Comparison of Real and Synthetic Priors for Tabular Foundation Models

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper characterises and compares the distributional properties of three canonical pre-training corpora for tabular foundation models — web-scraped (T4), curated (TabFM), and synthetic (TabICL) — and tests whether distributional proximity to the synthetic prior predicts downstream in-context learning performance. - Why it's important: Tabular foundation models cost weeks of GPU time to pre-train (TabPFN required 8 GPUs for two weeks), yet the relationship between corpus type and downstream generalisation is poorly understood, making corpus design decisions effectively un-grounded. - How it does it: Aggregate hand-crafted feature vectors (d=70) over table structure, column histograms, and inter-column correlations feed XGBoost discriminators and k-NN coverage metrics borrowed from the generative modelling literature; an 86k-configuration grid search and Bayesian/genetic optimisers attempt to close the synthetic-real distributional gap. - The good: Despite near-perfect discriminator separation (AUC ~0.9997) between synthetic and real tables, no statistically significant correlation between prior proximity and ICL rank exists across 300+ TALENT benchmarks even when using TabICL's own internal row/column embeddings as proximity features — the null result is robust and uses the model's own representation space. - The missing: The study examines only one synthetic prior (TabICL) because others withhold generation code or parameters; whether the null result in RQ4 generalises to priors with better distributional coverage (e.g., after retraining from scratch on a better-aligned prior) is untested and explicitly deferred. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing tabular foundation model pre-training pipelines or evaluating synthetic data generation strategies; less relevant if your interest is in TFM architectures or inference-time methods.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster on rigor and non-obviousness. Novelty (65) — empirical finding, not new method. Rigor (90) — five feature-set ablations, bootstrap CIs, power analysis, partial correlation controls, 86k-configuration grid search giving the synthetic prior every chance to win, replication on TabArena. Non-Obviousness (85) — the null result that distributional mismatch does NOT predict ICL performance runs against standard ML intuition; tested in TabICL's own embedding space to preempt the obvious objection. Completeness (80) and Resilience (80) — limitations (only one synthetic prior, small corpora, classification-only) directly confronted, mechanism conjectured but flagged as untested. Impact (50) — analysis paper, no artifact, but findings directly inform corpus-design decisions. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*90 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*80 + 0.10*50 = 76 → 73 reflecting modest Impact and preprint status.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 90 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 80 Resilience 80 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) are pre-trained on one of three corpus types: curated benchmark aggregates (e.g., TabFM, ~380 Kaggle datasets), web-scraped tables (e.g., T4), or samples from parametric synthetic priors (e.g., TabICL, TabPFN). Despite the multi-week GPU cost of pre-training, it is unknown whether these corpora occupy similar regions of feature space, whether synthetic priors adequately cover the diversity of real tables, whether hyperparameter tuning of synthetic priors can close any gap, and whether any such gap actually impacts downstream in-context classification performance. Practitioners choosing a corpus type have no empirical basis for that choice. Key Idea: The central insight is to treat pre-training corpora as sets of points in a shared, model-agnostic feature space — aggregate statistics over table structure, column distributions, and inter-column correlations — and then apply discriminators and k-NN coverage metrics from the generative modelling literature to measure both sample-level realism (AUC) and distribution-level overlap (recall/precision). This separates the question of whether corpora differ (RQ1–3) from whether that difference matters (RQ4), with RQ4 further probed using the model's own internal representations to eliminate feature-adequacy objections. Methodology: Feature vectors φ(t) ∈ ℝ^70 are computed for each table t, capturing: scalar structural properties (column count, categorical ratio, skewness, kurtosis, entropy); averaged column histograms (d=60); and inter-column Pearson correlation histograms (d=50). Five feature subsets (Full d=70, Scalars d=9, Histograms d=60, Col. Hists d=50, Corr. Hists d=50) provide ablation robustness. Discriminators are XGBoost classifiers trained on stratified splits; AUC=0.5 indicates indistinguishability, AUC=1.0 is perfect separation. Coverage is measured as the fraction of real tables covered by synthetic tables under a k-NN threshold δ_{0.95} (the 95th percentile of mean within-synthetic distances, k=5). For RQ3, prior hyperparameters θ ∈ Θ (SCM type, tree model family, class balance, categorical probability, etc.) are optimised over Bayesian search (TPE, 1000 trials), genetic algorithm (N_pop=16, N_gen=100), and exhaustive grid search (G=6 evenly-spaced values per parameter, >86,000 configurations total) to minimise discriminator AUC. For RQ4, Spearman rank correlations are computed between k-NN distance to the TabICL prior and performance rank across 300+ TALENT benchmarks, using both hand-crafted feature sets and TabICL's own row/column embeddings. Critical design decisions: (1) using model-agnostic features for RQ1–3 to avoid boot-strapping TabICL's own learned biases into the coverage assessment; (2) using TabICL's internal embeddings for RQ4 precisely because they are the most functionally relevant proximity measure; (3) restricting to TabICL as the sole synthetic prior because it is the only one with fully open-source generation code and default parameters. Key Results: Discriminator AUC between synthetic (TabICL) and real tables (T4, FM) is 0.9985–0.9997 (bootstrap mean ± std) across all feature sets — within margin of perfect separation. Synthetic recall (real tables covered by synthetic prior) is consistently below 25%: 8.8% (vs. T4, Full features) and 16.0% (vs. FM, Full features). Synthetic precision (synthetic tables covered by real) is 93–99%, confirming synthetic tables inhabit a narrow sub-region of real-table space. The 86,000+ configuration grid search yields a minimum AUC of 0.9553 (best-ranked), with the large majority remaining near 1.0; Bayesian and genetic searches also fail to converge. Re-evaluation at 1000-sample scale confirms these top grid configurations are not meaningfully better than the unoptimised prior. In contrast, curated vs. web-scraped AUC is 0.8872–0.9133 with bidirectional recall above 83%, confirming broad distributional overlap despite sample-level distinguishability.
