For RQ4, across six feature representations and three performance metrics on 300+ TALENT benchmarks, no correlation reaches statistical significance at p≤0.05 (maximum |r|=0.145 for rank correlation, p=0.172); this holds in TabICL's own embedding space (|r|<0.14) and survives partial correlation controlling for dataset size, dimensionality, and class balance. Results replicated on the TabArena benchmark. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is not a new method but a systematic empirical characterisation of a gap that the TFM community has assumed but not measured. The finding that distributional mismatch does not predict downstream performance is genuinely non-obvious — it runs counter to the standard ML intuition that training distribution coverage determines generalisation. The methodology (applying generative-model evaluation metrics to corpus comparison) is a straightforward transfer of existing tools to a new domain. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation over five feature sets confirms findings are not specific to one representation. Bootstrap confidence intervals (200 re-samples) are reported for all AUC values. Coverage ablation over k and threshold δ is reported in Appendix B.2. Statistical significance testing (p-values) is applied to RQ4 correlation results. Power analysis is stated: at power ≥0.8 for p≤0.05, correlations of |r|>0.2 would be detectable. Partial correlation analysis controls for three dataset-level covariates. Code is stated as available on GitHub (URL withheld for blind review, not yet public at submission). The analysis is restricted to TabICL due to closed-source parameters of TabPFN, Mitra, and others — this is acknowledged as a limitation. The grid search required 6 nodes × 2 NVIDIA Grace CPU Superchips × ~23 hours; the compute cost is reported. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.06343v1, submitted May 2026); results are not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (measurement study, no novel trained system). - Practical: Experiments run on CPU hardware (Grace CPU Superchips); discriminator and coverage analyses require no GPUs and are reproducible on commodity hardware. - Efficient: The 86k-configuration grid search required ~23 node-hours on 6 high-memory CPU nodes; the primary analysis on 200–1000 table samples per corpus is inexpensive once features are extracted. - Effective: The discriminator and coverage metrics reliably detect the known synthetic/real gap and validate self-consistency (same-corpus AUC ≈ 0.5), supporting their validity as measurement tools. - Scalable: Corpus sizes are limited (200–5000 tables per comparison at evaluation scale; FM has 384 tables), which is a constraint noted by the authors; findings at larger corpus scales are not tested. The paper delivers no software artifact beyond analysis code; findings are intended to inform corpus design decisions in future TFM training runs. Strengths: - The null result in RQ4 is credible precisely because it is tested in TabICL's own internal representation space — the single strongest possible counter-argument to "your features are inadequate" is pre-empted by design. - The 86k-configuration grid search is an unusually thorough optimisation effort that gives the synthetic prior a genuine opportunity to improve before the null is accepted. - Characterising all three corpus types simultaneously rather than pairwise exposes the asymmetry (synthetic ⊂ real, curated ≈ web-scraped at distribution level) which provides a mechanistic explanation for the empirical observation that few curated datasets can match web-scale performance. - The explicit taxonomy of prior openness (Table 3) reveals a systemic problem in the TFM field — most priors are effectively proprietary IP — and is a useful reference independent of the paper's main findings. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: (1) only TabICL is studied as the synthetic prior because others withhold generation code or parameters, and findings may not transfer; (2) small corpus sizes (e.g., FM=384 tables) limit statistical power; (3) RQ4's null result cannot rule out that a prior with better coverage (e.g., after retraining from scratch) would show a performance effect; (4) the mechanism underlying the null result is conjectured (ICL's inference-time conditioning compensates for out-of-distribution pre-training; SCM inductive biases act as regularisation) but not directly tested. The authors confront these limitations directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The feature space is intentionally simple (aggregate statistics, no learned representations), which is adequate for RQ1–3 but means inter-table structural patterns (e.g., bimodal column distributions, complex inter-column interactions) are compressed into scalar summaries. The downstream performance correlation (RQ4) uses only classification benchmarks (TALENT/TabArena); regression and imputation tasks, which TabICL also targets, are not evaluated. The study evaluates the unoptimised TabICL prior against T4 and FM, but TabICL's actual pre-training used a specific θ that is not necessarily the default; it is unclear whether the training configuration used for the published TabICL model is in fact the unoptimised default used here. The partial correlation analysis controls for table size, dimensionality, and class balance, but not for domain (medical vs. financial vs. other), which may independently drive both prior distance and performance. Bottom Line: A practitioner designing a tabular foundation model pre-training corpus gains a concrete, well-evidenced finding: synthetic SCM-based priors are structurally mismatched to real tables and cannot be fixed by hyperparameter tuning, yet this mismatch does not measurably hurt downstream ICL performance — suggesting the compute cost of synthetic prior optimisation is not justified by the available evidence, and curated datasets are a viable substitute for web-scraped ones at much smaller scale.
Search Results 2605.06343 PDF
#45 — Innovative (73)

Inference-Time Refinement Closes the Synthetic-Real Gap in Tabular Diffusion

Eugenio Lomurno, Filippo Balzarini, Francesco Benelle, Francesca Pia Panaccione, Matteo Matteucci · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TARDIS, a three-stage inference-time refinement framework that operates on a frozen pre-trained tabular diffusion backbone to close the downstream utility gap between synthetic and real tabular data. - Why it's important: Downstream ML models trained on synthetic tabular data consistently underperform those trained on real data, limiting deployment in privacy-constrained settings (healthcare, finance, etc.), and all prior attempts to fix this gap require retraining the generator. - How it does it: TARDIS applies Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR) — steering reverse diffusion via the gradient of a symmetric Chamfer functional between candidates and a real reference batch (continuous), then subsampling the overproduced candidate pool by minimizing the same functional post-hoc (discrete), unifying both levels under a single objective. - The good: Across 15 heterogeneous tabular benchmarks, TARDIS achieves a median +8.6% relative improvement over real-data utility (95% CI [+3.3, +16.4], Wilcoxon p = 0.016, 11/15 strict wins) and improves over TabDiff on all 15 datasets (mean +12.9%, p < 10⁻⁴), running in 0.9–79.4 minutes on a single 2017 consumer GPU. - The missing: TARDIS is validated on a single backbone (TabDiff); transfer to TabSyn (latent-space diffusion) and TabDDPM (multinomial diffusion) is claimed but not empirically tested, and the oversampling factor M = 50 is held fixed without characterizing how results change as M is swept. - Read it or not: Practitioners deploying synthetic tabular data in privacy-restricted domains and researchers studying inference-time compute scaling for generative models should read this; those focused on generator architecture improvements can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (78) are the pulling dimensions — reframing the synthetic-real gap as an inference-time rather than training-time problem is a sharp conceptual move, and the BCR unification of continuous guidance and discrete subsampling under a single Chamfer functional is principled. Rigor (75) is strong: TARDIS×1 ablation cleanly isolates that discrete selection (not guidance) drives the gap-closing, plus 15 datasets, 5 seeds, Wilcoxon tests, and consumer-GPU reproducibility. Completeness (72) and Resilience (68) are limited by single-backbone (TabDiff only) and single-downstream-learner (XGBoost only) evaluation, plus M=50 held fixed. Practical Impact (68) is reduced by no released code/artifact. Weighted ≈ 73.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: When direct access to real records is restricted — common in healthcare, finance, and social science — synthetic tabular data enables downstream ML training. Diffusion-based generators currently set the state of the art, but downstream learners trained on their outputs approach but rarely match real-data utility, creating a persistent synthetic–real gap. Prior work narrows this gap exclusively at training time by designing better generator architectures (TabDDPM, STaSy, CoDi, TabDiff), each adding complexity while yielding marginal headline-metric gains. The implicit assumption is that the gap is a generator-quality problem. This paper challenges that assumption, asking whether a frozen pre-trained backbone can be refined at inference time to close or reverse the gap without any retraining or architectural changes. Key Idea: The symmetric Chamfer functional — which measures bidirectional nearest-neighbor distances between two point sets in a learned representation space — provides a single objective that simultaneously enforces fidelity (synthetic samples close to real) and coverage (real samples close to synthetic), and this same objective can be minimized at two complementary operational levels: continuously, via score perturbation during reverse diffusion, and discretely, via post-hoc batch ranking over an overproduced candidate pool. Grounding both stages in the same functional (BCR) ensures they reinforce rather than conflict, and the representation space φ (either z-score-normalized features or a per-dataset β-VAE encoder) makes the Chamfer distance differentiable over mixed-type tabular inputs without requiring a canonical metric. Methodology: TARDIS operates as a three-stage pipeline on top of a frozen backbone G_θ : Z → X × Y. Stage I (Oversampling): Draws M · N_r samples (M = 50 fixed) from the latent prior, creating a noise pool D_noise. This candidate redundancy is the raw material for Stage III selection. Stage II (Bidirectional Chamfer Guidance): Denoises D_noise via reverse diffusion, optionally perturbing the predicted score at each step t_g with the gradient Γ(t) · ∇_{x_t} C(x_t, x_r), where x_r is a real reference batch and C is the bidirectional Chamfer functional (Equation 3). The guidance scaling family Γ(t) ∈ {constant, linear, cosine, sine} is a hyperparameter. The representation map φ is either the identity on z-score-normalized features or the encoder of a per-dataset β-VAE (latent dim d ∈ {4, 8, 16, 32}, KL annealing schedule from five families), trained on D_real. This addresses the absence of a canonical differentiable metric for mixed-type tabular data. Stage III (Refinement Selection): Selects K · N_r samples from the M · N_r candidate pool D_cand by minimizing the same Chamfer functional via batch ranking (Chamfer Sampler), optionally preceded by Generative Knowledge Distillation (GKD) that relabels candidates using soft targets from an XGBoost teacher trained on D_real. Four alternative samplers (Stratified, IBOSS, HDBSCAN, MDSampler) are included in the inner grid search. All Stage II hyperparameters and the GKD toggle are tuned per dataset by Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) search over the validation utility. Critical design decisions: (1) Fixing M = 50 rather than sweeping it — motivated by GPU throughput but leaves the saturation curve undercharacterized. (2) Per-dataset TPE search — well-justified empirically (recovers the BCR-aligned configuration on 9/15 datasets) but makes deployment dependent on real validation data. (3) BCR unifying continuous and discrete stages — the key structural claim, supported by the ablation showing Stage II alone (TARDIS×1) achieves only mean +1.4% over TabDiff, while the full pipeline achieves +12.9%. Key Results: Across 15 UCI/Kaggle benchmarks (binary classification ×5, multiclass ×4, regression ×6; instances 666–53,940; features 8–46), evaluated under the TSTR protocol with XGBoost as the downstream learner: - TARDIS achieves median Δ% = +8.6% over real-data utility (95% CI [+3.3, +16.4]), Wilcoxon p = 0.016, 11/15 strict wins, parity on Diamonds. - Against TabDiff backbone: mean +12.9%, p < 10⁻⁴, 15/15 wins. - Stage II guidance alone (TARDIS×1, K = 1): mean +1.4% over TabDiff, not significant over real (p > 0.05) — the discrete selection stage carries the dominant gap-closing effect. - Largest gains on low-utility or imbalanced datasets: Diabetic Retinopathy (+39.5% vs. real, 1,151 instances), Student Performance (+20.9%, 666 instances), News (+16.7%). - Negative result: Beijing regression (−12.1% vs. real), where XGBoost on real already exploits near-linear structure leaving little headroom. - Fidelity (Precision 0.949) and diversity (Recall 0.995) preserved at TabDiff level; privacy (DCR-1 share 0.499±0.013, NNDR 0.824±0.107) also preserved. - Runtime: 0.9 minutes (Solar Flare) to 79.4 minutes (Bank, ~45,000 instances) on Nvidia GTX 1080 Ti (12 GB, 2017 consumer GPU). - Baselines are the three strongest current tabular diffusion models (TabDiff, TabSyn, TabularARGN) selected via a controlled comparison on 10 shared benchmarks. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is (1) framing the synthetic–real tabular gap as an inference-time rather than training-time problem, (2) adapting Chamfer Guidance (previously image-domain, continuous only) to mixed-type tabular data via per-dataset representation maps and extending it to a bidirectional continuous-plus-discrete framework under a single functional, and (3) the BCR unification principle. The individual components (Chamfer distance, score guidance, soft-label distillation, TPE search, post-hoc subsampling) are individually known; the combination and its application to tabular synthesis is new. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: the TARDIS×1 condition (K = 1, Stage II only) isolates the continuous guidance contribution; the cardinality sweep K ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 10, 20} empirically validates the predicted monotone-then-plateau BCR saturation argument. Five random seeds per configuration with bootstrap 95% CIs and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests provide appropriate statistical coverage. Per-dataset configurations are fully reported (Appendix F, Tables 4–6). The evaluation uses a 90/5/5 train/validation/test split seeded identically across models, preventing leakage. The downstream learner is XGBoost exclusively; transfer to other learners is not tested. All experiments run on a single Nvidia GTX 1080 Ti (12 GB, 2017 GPU), making results reproducible on consumer hardware. Privacy is assessed via DCR-1 share and NNDR proxies without formal differential-privacy guarantees or membership-inference attacks, which the authors explicitly acknowledge. No code repository URL is mentioned in the paper (preprint); reproducibility depends on the appendix detail level, which is thorough. This is an arXiv preprint (v1, 7 May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods evaluated through experiments on 15 benchmarks). - Practical: Runs fully on a single Nvidia GTX 1080 Ti (12 GB, 2017 consumer GPU) with no requirement beyond the pre-trained backbone, making it deployable without specialized infrastructure. - Efficient: TARDIS adds 0.9–79.4 minutes of wall-clock overhead over the backbone inference time (median 16.1 minutes), dominated by M = 50 oversampling, which is GPU-throughput-bound and shrinks on newer hardware; Stage III selection adds 5–38% of total time depending on dataset. - Effective: The evidence shows it improves downstream TSTR utility on 11/15 datasets vs. real data and 15/15 vs. TabDiff, with a clear failure mode (high-utility datasets near metric ceiling) characterized empirically. - Scalable: The largest tested dataset has ~54,000 instances (Bank, 45,211; Diamonds, 53,940); behavior at 10×–100× this scale is not tested, and the M-sweep is unexplored, leaving the cost-utility tradeoff at large N_r uncharacterized. The paper stops at paper form; no code, trained models, or software artifact is released (not stated as available). Strengths: - The reframing of the synthetic–real gap as an inference-time problem is conceptually sharp and empirically well-supported: the TARDIS×1 ablation cleanly demonstrates that Stage II guidance alone is insufficient, establishing that the discrete selection stage (not just guidance) is the dominant mechanism — a counterintuitive finding relative to the image-domain precedent. - BCR's unification of continuous guidance and discrete subsampling under a single Chamfer functional is a principled design choice with formal properties (non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry) that justify using it as a shared optimization target across both levels. - The TPE search's empirical recovery of BCR-aligned configurations on 9/15 datasets without explicit constraint — confirmed by the sampler-guidance coupling analysis (Table 6) — provides structural validation of the BCR claim beyond aggregate metrics. - Experimental scope (15 datasets, 3 task types, controlled backbone selection, per-dataset configuration reporting, privacy and diversity metrics alongside utility) is substantially more rigorous than typical tabular synthesis papers that evaluate on 3–5 datasets with a single task type. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state: (1) TARDIS is validated on a single backbone (TabDiff); transfer to TabSyn and TabDDPM is not empirically tested, and Stage II score perturbation does not apply to autoregressive (TabularARGN) or variational (TVAE) decoders. (2) M = 50 is fixed, leaving the M-dependence of the saturation knee uncharacterized. (3) Per-seed variance reflects pool generation only, since headline configurations use deterministic samplers; downstream-learner variance beyond XGBoost is not tested. (4) Privacy is assessed via DCR-1 and NNDR proxies without formal differential-privacy claims or membership-inference evaluation. The authors confront these directly and propose three concrete future directions, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Single downstream learner (XGBoost): all TSTR results use XGBoost for both the downstream learner A and the GKD teacher f_GKD. Whether BCR-refined synthetic data benefits neural or linear learners differently is untested; since the GKD teacher architecture matches A, this creates a potential circularity for the GKD component specifically. - No wall-clock comparison to training a new generator: the paper argues inference-time refinement is preferable to retraining, but provides no runtime comparison to fine-tuning or retraining a smaller/faster generator on D_real, which would directly test the claimed efficiency advantage. - M = 50 without sweep: the saturation argument in Appendix A is a principled informal argument, not a theorem; the empirical validation fixes M = 50 and sweeps only K, leaving the (M, K) joint landscape unexplored and the cost-benefit tradeoff at different M uncharacterized. - The TPE search requires a real validation split, which is the same data used to score utility — a mild Goodhart concern for the per-dataset configuration selection, though the test split is held out identically. - Regression performance is weaker than classification (GKD disabled by construction, MDSampler preferred on regression datasets), but the mechanism explaining why BCR's coverage term is less effective for regression targets is not analyzed. Bottom Line: Practitioners deploying tabular diffusion in privacy-constrained settings gain a directly actionable, backbone-agnostic refinement procedure that demonstrably closes the synthetic–real gap at inference time on consumer hardware, with the caveat that cross-backbone empirical validation and a released codebase are still needed before treating this as production-ready.
Search Results 2605.06261 PDF
#46 — Innovative (73)

SLayerGen: a Crystal Generative Model for all Space and Layer Groups

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SLayerGen, the first crystal generative model that enforces invariance to any of the 80 layer groups (governing 2D diperiodic materials such as monolayers and bilayers) while retaining the ability to model all 230 space groups for bulk crystals. - Why it's important: Diperiodic materials — thin films, monolayers, bilayers — exhibit exotic physics (superconductivity, valleytronics, nonlinear optics) that depends directly on layer group symmetry, yet every prior generative model either ignores layer groups entirely or spuriously treats these structures as triperiodic, producing symmetry-incorrect outputs. - How it does it: SLayerGen factorizes generation into four sequential stages — categorical group sampling, coarse-to-fine autoregressive lattice discretization, transformer-based Wyckoff/element autoregression, and group-equivariant coordinate diffusion using a group-wrapped normal with anisotropic noise covariance to handle the aperiodic dimension correctly. - The good: SLayerGen achieves the best Jensen-Shannon divergence over layer group distributions on three of four diperiodic benchmarks and the highest or second-highest Stable Unique Novel (S.U.N.) template rate on every dataset, demonstrating genuine generalization beyond training Wyckoff templates. - The missing: No code or model weights are released, evaluation relies on Orb-v3 MLIP as a proxy for DFT (introducing approximation errors), and no ablation isolates the contribution of the anisotropic noise covariance fix versus the layer group embeddings versus the corrected ASU definitions. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or benchmarking generative models for 2D materials; the layer group embedding construction, ASU enumeration methodology, and the hexagonal coordinate bug fix are directly reusable even outside the SLayerGen architecture.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (80) is the high point — first generative model unifying all 80 layer groups + 230 space groups, with standalone contributions (ITC error corrections for layer groups 7 and 46, anisotropic GWN). Rigor (70) acceptable but no component-level ablation isolates the anisotropic noise vs. ASU corrections vs. embeddings; no error bars or seeds. Non-Obviousness (75) — the equivariance failure for hexagonal groups and the aperiodic-noise mismatch are subtle insights. Completeness (70), Resilience (65) limited by DiffCSP++ beating it on BiDB JSD_G without controlled explanation. Impact (60) — preprint with no released code/weights. Weighted: 0.20*80+0.25*70+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.10*60 = 71.75.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Generative models for inorganic crystals have advanced rapidly for bulk (triperiodic) materials, but a large and scientifically important class of materials — 2D monolayers, bilayers, thin-film semiconductors, and catalytic surfaces — is diperiodic: periodic in two dimensions and aperiodic in the third. These systems are governed by one of 80 discrete layer groups, not the 230 space groups that existing models assume. Crucially, 18 of the 80 layer groups do not injectively map to any space group, so simply projecting layer group symmetries onto space groups introduces systematic errors. Layer group symmetry directly controls valley-contrasting physics, superconductivity, ferroelectricity, and topological band structure in these materials. Data-driven design has been hampered further by the scarcity of experimentally known 2D materials (~10² experimentally confirmed vs. ~10³–10⁴ computationally predicted) and by the absence of datasets, metrics, and models that respect layer group symmetry. Key Idea The central insight is that the SGEquiDiff framework — which builds space-group-equivariant diffusion by symmetrizing a base score network via group averaging — can be extended to layer groups if two subtle issues are resolved: (1) the noise covariance must be anisotropic, using separate variances for periodic and aperiodic directions because layer group distributions do not become uniform in the aperiodic direction as noise grows; and (2) the equivariance proof from Chang et al. [15] assumes the rotation matrix R_g is orthogonal in fractional coordinates, which fails for hexagonal groups — requiring a detour through Cartesian space for score computation. Combining this corrected diffusion with layer-group-aware Wyckoff embeddings and lattice generation under group constraints yields a model whose likelihood is exactly G-invariant for any space or layer group G. Methodology SLayerGen represents each crystal as a tuple (G, L, W, A, X) and factorizes the joint distribution as p(G) × p(L|G) × p(W,A|L,G) × p(X|W,A,L,G). Stage 1 samples G from the empirical training distribution. Stage 2 generates the 6 lattice parameters (a,b,c,α,β,γ) via a coarse-to-fine autoregressive MLP with two resolution levels and 100 categories each, with group-specific constraints enforced by logit masking at both training and inference; the aperiodic c-axis length is masked out since layer groups do not constrain it. Stage 3 uses a transformer to autoregressively generate Wyckoff positions and elements, ordered lexicographically, with a learned stop token for implicit atom count; logits for 0D Wyckoff points are masked if atoms already exist there. Stage 4 uses noise-conditional score matching on atomic fractional coordinates with a G-invariant group-wrapped normal (GWN) prior, symmetrized via group averaging (Eq. 1), with anisotropic noise covariance Σ_t = diag(σ²_{t,xy} I₂ ⊕ σ²_{t,z} I₁). For hexagonal groups, scores are computed in Cartesian coordinates and converted back. Layer group and Wyckoff position embeddings concatenate lattice centering, crystal family, point group, chirality/centrosymmetry flags, and Fourier features of ASU centroid/vertex positions (65 + 212 = 277 total features), extended from SGEquiDiff's space group embeddings to enable correlation learning across di- and triperiodic groups. Critical design decisions: (a) exact ASU enumeration by intersecting Wyckoff shapes with ITC-defined boundaries, correcting two errors in the International Tables for layer groups 7 and 46; (b) anisotropic noise schedule motivated by the physical distinction between periodic and aperiodic dimensions; (c) center-of-mass subtraction along z before score computation to build translation-invariant distributions along the aperiodic axis. Key Results SLayerGen was evaluated against DiffCSP (12.3M params), DiffCSP++ (not stated), MatterGen (44.6M params), and SGEquiDiff (5.5M params) on four DFT-filtered diperiodic datasets: 2DMatPedia (2,696 monolayers), C2DB (8,502 monolayers), BiDB (2,641 bilayers), and Alex2D (30,082 materials). A budget of 10,000 generated structures per model was used, with MLIP-relaxation of 1,000 valid structures for stability assessment. On 2DMatPedia (Table 1): SLayerGen achieves JSD_G = 0.185 vs. SLayerGen3D (space-group variant) at 0.252, and stability rate 43.8% (vs. MatterGen 41.5%, SGEquiDiff 42.7%). S.U.N. template rate: SLayerGen 7.0% vs. MatterGen 3.2%, DiffCSP++ 2.9%. On C2DB (Table 2): SLayerGen achieves the best JSD_G = 0.183 and best stability 60.0%, with S.U.N. template 6.8%. On BiDB (Table 3): SLayerGen achieves best JSD_G = 0.209, stability 93.5% (highest), S.U.N. template 7.6% (best). On Alex2D (Table 4): With temperature tempering (T_{G,W}=2.0), SLayerGen achieves best S.U.N. template 4.4% at modest stability cost. On MP20 bulk crystals (Table 5): SLayerGen3D achieves 85.2% composition validity, 82.4% structure validity, and 89.5 S.U.N. structure rate, comparable to SGEquiDiff; joint training (Alex2D + MP20) yields higher U.N. rates. Layer group JSD improvement over SLayerGen3D is consistent across all four datasets, confirming that explicit layer group modeling is the operative factor. The paper also demonstrates that dynamical phonon stability constraints (Γ-point stability) significantly reduce reported stability rates compared to prior evaluations that ignored them — a calibration contribution to the field. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Genuinely novel: the first unified generative model covering all 80 layer groups and 230 space groups; the anisotropic GWN for aperiodic dimensions; the enumeration of exact Wyckoff shapes for all 80 layer groups with two ITC error corrections; and the curated stability-filtered diperiodic datasets with MLIP-validated phonon stability. The transformer and diffusion components are direct extensions of SGEquiDiff; the novelty lies in the symmetry infrastructure and its application to a problem domain previously unaddressed. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present but coarse: the SLayerGen3D variant (identical architecture, space group treatment of diperiodic data) isolates the layer group modeling effect, and the comparison is consistent across all datasets. However, no ablation separates the anisotropic noise covariance fix from the corrected ASU definitions from the new Wyckoff embeddings — it is not possible to determine which subcomponent drives the JSD_G improvement. Error bars and multiple random seeds are not reported; significance tests are absent. The phonon stability filtering methodology is validated by computing F1 scores for Orb-v3 on Materials Project convex hull data (Table 6; F1 = 0.914/0.903/0.825 on three datasets vs. dummy classifier F1 = 0.723/0.726/0.480), which is a credible sanity check. Code and model weights are not stated to be publicly released. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.08262v1, 7 May 2026), not yet peer reviewed. The ITC error corrections for layer groups 7 and 46 are validated by projecting 1,000 uniform random points and verifying each orbit lands within the corrected ASU exactly once — a sound validation procedure. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on a single A40 NVIDIA GPU with batch sizes of 78–164 samples; training datasets are at most ~30,000 materials, all within reach of academic compute. - Efficient: Generation throughput is 78–164 samples/batch (A40), slower than DiffCSP (247 s/batch) but comparable to MatterGen (1,308 s/batch), with SGEquiDiff at 82 s/batch as the fastest space-group-aware baseline; SLayerGen's 81–136 s/batch is competitive. - Effective: MLIP-relaxed stability rates reach 43.8–93.5% depending on dataset, and the model generates structures with novel Wyckoff templates not seen in training at rates of 4.4–11.3% (S.U.N. template), supporting the claim that it generalizes beyond memorization. - Scalable: Tested on datasets up to 30,082 materials (Alex2D); no experiments at 10× scale; the joint Alex2D + MP20 training (137,833 + 45,231 materials) is the largest setting tested. Scaling behavior with model size or data volume is not studied. The work delivers a trained model and evaluation pipeline in paper form; code and weights are not stated to be released. Strengths - The identification and correction of two errors in the International Tables of Crystallography (layer groups 7 and 46) is a standalone contribution that benefits any group computing layer group ASUs. - The anisotropic noise covariance design is physically well-motivated and the equivariance proof for the induced score function (Sec. 7.4) is presented rigorously, not just claimed. - Phonon stability validation via Orb-v3 F1 scores (Table 6) is a meaningful methodological advance over prior evaluations that used only energy-above-hull thresholds; the finding that dynamical stability constraints significantly reduce apparent stability rates recalibrates the field's benchmarks. - The modular architecture allows inference-time tempering of layer group and Wyckoff position distributions independently, providing a practical handle for exploring underrepresented symmetries without retraining. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) MLIP predictions (Orb-v3) are used as proxies for DFT, introducing approximation error; (2) diperiodic stability metrics ignore substrate effects, magnetism, and disorder; (3) the effect of joint training on model scaling and architecture design is unexplored; (4) future work is needed on symmetry-dependent properties, handling broken symmetries, and transfer learning across materials classes. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No component-level ablation: the paper does not isolate whether the JSD_G improvement comes from the new Wyckoff embeddings, the anisotropic noise schedule, the corrected ASUs, or all three in combination. This makes it difficult to know which innovation to carry forward in future work. - The S.U.N. template metric rewards novelty relative to training data, but no analysis confirms that novel templates correspond to physically realizable structure types — the connection between template novelty and materials utility is assumed, not demonstrated. - Evaluation uses structures padded to a fixed c = 25Å, which is an artifact introduced by the model (not a physical property); StructureMatcher is sensitive to this padding, and the authors acknowledge this but do not quantify the distortion it introduces in structure novelty counts. - All four diperiodic datasets (2DMatPedia, C2DB, Alex2D, BiDB) are computationally derived via DFT from a shared pool of experimentally known structures; the model has no way to learn from the ~10² truly experimentally observed 2D materials, and generalization to genuinely novel chemistries (not elemental substitutions of known prototypes) is undemonstrated. - DiffCSP++ outperforms SLayerGen on the BiDB JSD_G metric; the authors attribute this to DiffCSP++ leveraging ground-truth Wyckoff combinations from training data — but this attribution is not tested by an experiment that controls for Wyckoff template coverage. Bottom Line SLayerGen is a technically sound and well-scoped extension of the SGEquiDiff framework to 2D materials that fills a genuine gap — correct layer group symmetry enforcement — and the ASU error corrections and phonon stability calibration are standalone contributions worth the read for anyone working on 2D crystal generation or symmetry-constrained generative modeling.
Citations 2605.08262 PDF
#47 — Innovative (73)

Proteo-R1: Reasoning Foundation Models for De Novo Protein Design

Fang Wu, Weihao Xuan, Heli Qi, Hanqun Cao, Heng-Jui Chang, Zeqi Zhou, Haokai Zhao, Ma Jian, Carl Ma, Yu-Chi Cheng, Kuan Pang, Xiangru Tang, Zehong Wang, Guanlue Li, Hanchen Wang, Kejun Ying, Pan Lu, Chiho Im, Seungju Han, Peng Xia, Tinson Xu, Yinxi Li, Deyao Zhu, Pheng-Ann Heng, Naoto Yokoya, Masashi Sugiyama, Li Erran Li, Jure Leskovec, Yejin Choi · arxiv.org · Jure Leskovec - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Proteo-R1, a dual-expert framework that couples a multimodal LLM (understanding expert) with an AF3-style diffusion model (generation expert) for antibody CDR co-design, communicating between them via sparse, residue-level hard anchor constraints rather than soft textual conditioning. - Why it's important: Existing diffusion-based protein design models are non-deliberative — they cannot explicitly reason about which residues are functionally critical, limiting interpretability, controllability, and the reuse of biochemical knowledge across design tasks. - How it does it: The understanding expert identifies key interaction residues and predicts their amino-acid identities, then injects these decisions into the diffusion model's embedding space as fixed anchor embeddings that remain clamped throughout the denoising trajectory, decoupling reasoning from geometry while preserving the generator's inductive biases. - The good: Proteo-R1 achieves the lowest intra-chain and inter-chain steric clashes (0.50% / 0.14%) and best backbone dihedral divergence among all baselines in simultaneous multi-CDR redesign, and the oracle anchor ablation confirms the bottleneck is reasoning quality rather than generation capacity — a clear signal that the architecture is sound. - The missing: Evaluation is entirely computational on SAbDab/RAbD; no wet-lab binding or affinity measurements confirm that the structurally superior designs are biologically active, and generalization to non-antibody design contexts claimed in the abstract is undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on controllable protein design or LLM–diffusion integration; the anchor-injection interface is a transferable design pattern, and the three-stage curriculum details are unusually well-documented for reproducibility.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (75) supported by leakage-controlled CDR-masked refolding, three-stage curriculum ablations across 11 task families, and the oracle-anchor ablation that diagnoses the bottleneck — but no error bars on stochastic-diffusion structural tables. Novelty (75) — the sparse residue-aligned hard-anchor interface bridging LLM reasoning and AF3-style diffusion is a genuine architectural contribution. Non-Obviousness (70), Completeness (70) limited by no wet-lab validation and asserted-but-untested generalization to non-antibodies. Resilience (65) — the low CDR-H3 AAR claim leans on the structural metrics without binding evidence. Impact (75) — code/demo released and the interface is model-agnostic (UniMoMo swap). Weighted: 0.20*75+0.25*75+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.10*75 = 71.75.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Computational de novo protein design, and antibody CDR redesign in particular, is dominated by diffusion/flow-matching models that directly synthesize atomic coordinates and amino-acid identities through a continuous denoising trajectory. These models treat all residues uniformly; design intent (e.g., which residues should form a charged anchor or hydrophobic hotspot) is encoded only implicitly in model parameters. This entanglement of reasoning and generation limits interpretability — designers cannot inspect or override design decisions — and prevents systematic reuse of biochemical priors. Human structural biologists routinely first identify key interaction residues and then optimize geometry subject to those commitments; no purely generative framework replicates this two-phase workflow in a learned, end-to-end manner. Key Idea: The central trick is converting the LLM's discrete, high-level reasoning outputs (which CDR residue positions are interaction anchors, and what amino-acid identity each should have) into hard constraints that are enforced at the representation level inside the diffusion model. Rather than using soft textual embeddings or chain-of-thought tokens as indirect guidance, Proteo-R1 replaces the standard unknown-token embedding at each identified anchor position with a fused vector that additively combines the anchor's amino-acid identity embedding and a linearly projected LLM hidden state. Non-anchor CDR residues remain masked and are fully resolved by the diffusion process. This makes reasoning causally upstream of generation while leaving the generator's architecture unchanged. Methodology: Proteo-R1 comprises two expert modules. The understanding expert E_und is built on Qwen-3-4B-Instruct augmented with frozen ESM-2 sequence embeddings and truncated AF3-style structural features (extracted via CDR-masked inpainting to prevent native-geometry leakage). A multimodal fusion MLP integrates sequence and structural representations at the residue level. E_und outputs: (i) the subset of CDR positions identified as key interaction anchors, (ii) predicted amino-acid identities at those positions, and (iii) final-layer LLM hidden states encoding reasoning context. The generation expert E_gen is an AF3-style diffusion model trained on the MFDesign formulation; it receives anchor assignments via two pathways — identity clamping at the sequence level and additive embedding injection at the representation level (Algorithm 2, BUILDANCHORS). Training proceeds through three stages: Stage I freezes the LLM backbone and trains only projection layers using PDB assemblies with schema-completion and captioning objectives; Stage II unfreezes the LLM through a four-phase curriculum (residue grounding → pairwise geometry → compositional consistency → interface localization) with replay buffers to prevent catastrophic forgetting; Stage III jointly trains E_und and E_gen end-to-end on SAbDab CDR redesign, where gradients from the diffusion objective propagate back through the anchor interface to shape the understanding expert's representations. The joint objective is L_total = L_gen + λ_und · L_und, with λ_und = 0.1. Key design decisions: (1) structural features are extracted only from CDR-masked refolded structures, never from native CDR coordinates — this prevents geometry leakage into the reasoning module; (2) only the sparse anchor embeddings enter E_gen, preserving the diffusion model's equivariance and inductive biases; (3) the three-stage curriculum with a 5% replay rate prevents catastrophic forgetting of earlier structural skills when introducing harder objectives. Key Results: On simultaneous multi-CDR redesign (SAbDab test set, Table 1), Proteo-R1 achieves the lowest per-CDR Cα RMSD in 5/6 loop regions (H1: 1.33Å, H2: 1.13Å, L2: 0.85Å) and the lowest steric clash rates (Clash_in 0.50%, Clash_out 0.14%) against six baselines (DiffAb, dyMEAN, HTP, IgGM, AbX, MFDesign). On CDR-H3-only redesign (RAbD benchmark, Table 2), Proteo-R1 achieves the highest DockQ (0.801), lDDT (0.9693), TM-score (0.9816), and lowest RMSD (2.46Å) among all generative baselines, though its AAR (10.75%) is the lowest — substantially below DGENet (42.67%) and dyMEAN. The authors argue correctly that low AAR in a de novo redesign setting reflects alternative valid solutions rather than poor quality, supported by the strong structural metrics; nonetheless this claim is not experimentally validated with binding assays. Structure-sequence consistency analysis (Table 3) shows Proteo-R1 has consistently small or positive Δ = IF-AAR − AAR across CDRs, whereas all baselines exhibit large negative Δ (e.g., MFDesign H3 Δ = −45.31), confirming that Proteo-R1-generated geometries are intrinsically more compatible with realizable sequences. Generated sequences score at or below native perplexity under IgLM, AbLang, and IgT5 antibody language models (Table 4). Compatibility with UniMoMo (a different diffusion backbone, Table 5) demonstrates that Proteo-R1's reasoning expert is model-agnostic: Proteo-R1 + UniMoMo achieves RMSD 0.83Å and IMP 67.79% versus UniMoMo standalone at 65.00%. The oracle anchor ablation (Table 1, bottom row) shows that ground-truth hotspot residues improve IMP from 56.58% to 62.25% and substantially reduce steric clashes, confirming that anchor accuracy is the primary bottleneck. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The explicit decoupling of LLM-based molecular reasoning from diffusion-based generation via sparse, residue-aligned hard anchor constraints is genuinely novel. The three-stage curriculum strategy for jointly training an LLM and a diffusion model through a narrow discrete interface is also a non-trivial contribution. Individual components — multimodal LLM alignment, AF3-style diffusion, antibody CDR redesign, chain-of-thought reasoning — are existing ideas; the novelty is in the interface design and training paradigm that makes them jointly functional. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: Stage I modality ablations (Table 9) confirm complementarity of structure and sequence tokens; Stage II curriculum ablations (Table 12) track accuracy across 11 task families through training; Stage III ablations (Tables 14–15) isolate the contributions of CoT supervision (Thinking) and replay (Playback). The oracle anchor upper-bound analysis is a particularly credible methodological inclusion. No error bars or significance tests appear in the main structural evaluation tables (Tables 1–3), which is a notable gap given the stochastic nature of diffusion sampling. Code, data, and demos are stated as publicly available at https://smiles724.github.io/r1/. Data splits are based on SAbDab structure release dates relative to MFDesign pretraining cutoff, preventing leakage; CDR-H3 clustering at 50% sequence identity via MMseqs2 prevents near-duplicate test contamination. All supervision signals in Stages I–II are deterministically derived from atomic coordinates, eliminating experimental annotation noise. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates a complete training and inference pipeline. - Practical: Training requires concurrent GPU resources for a Qwen-3-4B LLM plus an AF3-style diffusion model; inference at test time runs on standard research hardware, with the understanding expert and generation expert executed sequentially. The paper does not report GPU hours or specific hardware requirements. - Efficient: The anchor interface adds minimal overhead to generation — only the sparse embedding injection step — and the understanding expert runs once per design. The three-stage training is compute-intensive (10K + 20K + 10K steps across stages), but the marginal inference cost over the baseline diffusion model is low. - Effective: The evidence shows Proteo-R1 produces structurally superior and more geometrically realistic antibody designs than baselines on computational metrics; no wet-lab data confirms binding or neutralization activity, so effectiveness at the ultimate task is unverified. - Scalable: Tested on conventional antibody complexes in SAbDab and the 60-complex RAbD benchmark; neither generalization to longer CDR loops, to multi-chain assemblies beyond heavy/light/antigen, nor to other protein classes (claimed as a future direction) has been demonstrated at scale. A software artifact (code + demos) is released. Strengths: - The sparse residue-aligned anchor interface is a clean, principled design that enforces hard constraints without modifying the generative model's architecture, making it model-agnostic (demonstrated by swapping in UniMoMo). - The three-stage curriculum is well-engineered with replay buffers that empirically prevent catastrophic forgetting across 11 task families, and the curriculum ablations support each design choice. - The CDR-masked refolding scheme as a leakage-control mechanism is a careful methodological choice that makes all structural reasoning truly predictive rather than retrospective. - The oracle anchor ablation gives a meaningful upper bound and cleanly diagnoses that future gains should target the understanding expert's hotspot prediction, not the generation architecture. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge: (1) no wet-lab experimental validation — all evaluation is computational; (2) anchor identification accuracy is the primary bottleneck (established by oracle ablation); (3) the system is currently instantiated and evaluated only on antibody CDR redesign, with generalization to other protein design tasks left as future work; (4) biosecurity risk of misuse for harmful biological agents. These are stated directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Beyond the authors' own caveats: (1) The low CDR-H3 AAR (10.75%) is attributed to exploration of alternative valid sequences, but without binding affinity experiments this claim is unverified — it could equally reflect designs that are structurally plausible but immunologically inactive. (2) No error bars appear in the structural evaluation tables; with stochastic diffusion sampling, confidence intervals are needed to distinguish systematic improvement from variance. (3) The understanding expert uses a 4B-parameter LLM; whether larger models improve hotspot identification accuracy (the stated bottleneck) is not investigated. (4) All training and evaluation data come from PDB/SAbDab crystallographic structures; performance on AlphaFold2-predicted or cryo-EM structures, which dominate the practical design context, is untested. (5) The claim that the framework generalizes to non-antibody contexts (peptides, enzymes) is entirely asserted with no experiment to support it. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on controllable antibody design gains a concrete, implementable architecture and a well-documented training recipe; the anchor-injection interface is transferable to other LLM–diffusion hybrid systems, making this worth reading for that contribution alone, though wet-lab validation remains absent.
Recommended 2605.02937 PDF
#48 — Innovative (72)

FERMI: Exploiting Relations for Membership Inference Against Tabular Diffusion Models

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FERMI, a feature-mapping framework that distills relational membership signals from multi-table auxiliary data into the single-table feature space to mount stronger membership inference attacks against tabular diffusion models at inference time using only a single target-table row. - Why it's important: Real sensitive tabular data lives in relational databases, yet all standard MIA evaluations assume a single-table setting, causing practitioners to substantially underestimate the privacy risk of deployed tabular diffusion models. - How it does it: A neural mapper trained with MSE reconstruction loss plus CORAL covariance alignment projects single-table loss-fingerprint features toward their relational counterparts learned from shadow models, transferring the stronger relational membership signal without requiring relational access at query time. - The good: Under the Full-Relational Side Information setting, FERMI improves TPR@0.1FPR by up to 53% relative over the single-table baseline in white-box, and in black-box recovers 55% of the AUC gap (0.687→0.725 vs. merged-upper-bound 0.755) using only the released synthetic data. - The missing: The paper provides no evaluation against differentially private tabular diffusion models — the most relevant practical defense — leaving open the question of whether FERMI survives the defense practitioners would actually deploy. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in synthetic data privacy, tabular generative models, or relational privacy auditing; the TabSyn finding alone — that latent-space bottlenecks provide near-complete structural resistance to relational augmentation — has immediate implications for architecture selection in privacy-sensitive deployments.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is strong — first formalization of training-inference asymmetry in multi-relational MIA plus the actionable TabSyn architectural finding. Rigor (75) solid with the identity-mapper ablation as a clean control, but small 200-record challenge sets produce noisy TPR@0.01 estimates and no code release. Non-Obviousness (75) — the relational-context-in-training-only insight is non-obvious. Completeness (65) and Resilience (65) reduced because there is no DP defense evaluation and black-box is a single architecture-dataset pair. Impact (70) reflects paper-form framework with no software release. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*70 = 71.5 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Sensitive tabular records in healthcare, finance, and social science reside in relational databases where an individual's information spans many linked tables connected by foreign keys. Organizations increasingly train per-table diffusion models to generate synthetic versions of these records for privacy-preserving data sharing. Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are the standard empirical tool for auditing whether a generative model leaks training data, yet every existing MIA against tabular diffusion models assumes a single-table setting — the adversary sees only the target table's attribute vector. This ignores that a realistic adversary may possess rich auxiliary relational data (from prior releases, related-institution leaks, or public databases) during the attack training phase, even though at inference time they observe only a single candidate row from the target table without access to that row's parent, child, or sibling records. No prior work formalizes or addresses this training-inference asymmetry in the multi-relational MIA setting, and the MIDST challenge (the only public benchmark for tabular diffusion MIAs with relational tracks) saw no winning submission exploit related-table information. Key Idea Querying a diffusion model on joined (merged) multi-table representations produces a substantially wider gap in per-record denoising loss between member and non-member records than querying on the single-table representation alone, because the model internalizes not just individual attribute statistics but their relational conditioning context. This richer membership signal can be harvested from shadow models during attack training even though it is unavailable at inference time: a neural mapper trained on paired (single-table features, multi-table features) examples from shadow models learns to project single-table loss fingerprints into a geometry that mimics the relational feature space, injecting an inductive bias that concentrates discriminative signal at the same small-timestep regime where relational features are most discriminative. At inference time the adversary applies the learned mapper to single-table features alone, recovering a substantial fraction of the relational signal without access to any related tables. Methodology FERMI follows the standard shadow-model paradigm and proceeds in three stages. (1) Paired Feature Extraction: for each shadow record x in target table T_j, two loss-fingerprint feature vectors are extracted — Φ_single(x) from a diffusion model trained on T_j alone, and Φ_multi(x̃) from a model trained on the merged table x̃ = x ⋈ x_p (child joined with parent attributes). Both use the loss-fingerprint extractor Φ(x_0) = [ℓ_{θ_j}(x_0, t, ε)]_{t∈T, ε∈E} ∈ ℝ^d, probing at |T| timesteps with |E| noise samples each; for TabDDPM, d = 7 × 500 = 3,500 per record. (2) Mapping: a neural mapper f_map (residual MLP, 3 blocks, hidden dim 128, layer norm) is trained on tuples (Φ_single(x), Φ_multi(x̃), y) across 10 shadow models, minimizing L_map = E[||f_map(Φ_single) − Φ_multi||²₂] + λ_coral · (1/4d²)||C_map − C_multi||²_F, combining pointwise MSE reconstruction with CORAL covariance alignment (λ_coral = 0.01) to match not just values but the distributional geometry of the relational feature space. (3) Membership Prediction: an attack classifier f_att (4-layer MLP, hidden dim 128, BN, dropout 0.2, binary cross-entropy) is trained on mapped features. The mapper and classifier are jointly fine-tuned end-to-end with L_total = λ_map L_map + λ_cls L_cls (λ_map = 0.05, 500 epochs, Adam). Two auxiliary knowledge settings are evaluated: Parent-Context Side Information (PCSI, target table plus parent tables only) and Full-Relational Side Information (FRSI, all linked tables). Critical design decisions: (a) CORAL alignment is well-justified — pointwise MSE alone may not align the covariance structure, which governs which dimensions a bounded-capacity downstream classifier can exploit; (b) using shadow auxiliary partitions for joins makes the merge tractable even on million-row datasets; (c) joint fine-tuning (rather than pipeline training) allows the mapper to specialize toward membership-informative directions rather than generic reconstruction. Key Results Single-table baselines (Table 1) establish that MIA susceptibility is architecture-dependent: TabDDPM is the most vulnerable (AUC 0.753–0.949 across three datasets); TabDiff varies considerably (AUC 0.503–0.814); TabSyn achieves near-random AUC (0.494–0.529) on all datasets and attack variants, suggesting its VAE encoder acts as a privacy bottleneck. Under PCSI (Table 2): the Merged (upper bound) attack on TabDDPM Instacart achieves AUC 0.967, TPR@0.1 = 0.996, TPR@0.01 = 0.806 — a dramatic jump over the single-table AUC 0.949. FERMI on the same setting: AUC 0.961, TPR@0.1 = 0.892 (+10.7 pp absolute over baseline 0.806), TPR@0.01 = 0.658 (+59.6 pp over baseline estimated from context). On Berka TabDiff, FERMI TPR@0.1 rises from single-table 0.258 to 0.378 (+46% relative). Under FRSI (Table 3): the Merged attack on TabDDPM Instacart reaches AUC 0.997, TPR@0.01 = 0.994 — near-perfect identification of members at 1% FPR. FERMI FRSI: AUC 0.963, TPR@0.1 = 0.910. On Berka TabDiff FRSI, FERMI TPR@0.1 = 0.394 vs. single-table baseline 0.258, a ~53% relative gain. In black-box (Table 4, TabDDPM California): single-table AUC 0.687, FERMI 0.725, Merged 0.755; FERMI recovers 55% of the AUC gap and 51% of the TPR@0.1 gap. TabSyn resists all relational attack variants across all settings, with AUC uniformly near 0.5. The ablation confirming the mapper contributes signal rather than capacity (identity-mapper variant produces AUC 0.893, TPR@0.1 0.656, TPR@0.01 0.380 — statistically indistinguishable from the single-table baseline) is the strongest piece of evidence that the framework's gains are mechanistically genuine. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the formalization of the training-inference asymmetry in multi-relational MIA — no prior work distinguished what relational context is available at attack-training time from what is available at inference time — combined with the domain-adaptation approach to bridging this gap. The individual components (shadow-model paradigm, loss fingerprinting, CORAL domain adaptation) are existing ideas; their composition in the multi-relational MIA context is new. The empirical finding that TabSyn's latent encoding provides near-complete structural resistance to relational augmentation — a finding that persists even under the full-relational oracle setting — is a genuinely actionable architectural result not previously documented. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and targeted: the identity-mapper ablation (Appendix F) isolates that gains come from relational supervision signal, not added parameters or training budget — a specific and convincing control. The loss-visualization ablation (Figure 2) provides intuitive mechanistic support. Results are averaged over 5 target models with 10 shadow models each; standard deviations are reported throughout. However, challenge sets of only 200 records per model (100 member/100 non-member) produce noticeably noisy TPR@0.01 estimates, with standard deviations often exceeding 0.04–0.09. No formal significance tests are reported. The paper includes formal theoretical guarantees: a non-degradation theorem (FERMI optimal attack class ≥ single-table) and a necessary condition for useful relational supervision (conditional mutual information > 0 between the single-table features and the multi-table features given membership). Remark 1 honestly acknowledges that finite-sample training can produce a mapper that degrades performance when relational features are adversarial to child-record membership — a meaningful caveat. No code repository or model weights are mentioned in the paper; reproducibility is limited by the absence of a public release. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper designs, implements, and evaluates an attack framework experimentally. - Practical: All experiments run on a single NVIDIA L40 GPU with 16 CPU cores; no specialized hardware required, though shadow training for 10 models × 3 architectures × 3 datasets is compute-intensive. - Efficient: Feature extraction requires 500 noise samples per probing point per record (7 timesteps for TabDDPM/TabSyn, 7 for TabDiff), yielding 3,500–7,000 dimensional vectors; this is substantially more expensive than a single forward pass but tractable on moderate hardware. Training times are not reported. - Effective: FERMI demonstrably solves the stated problem for data-space diffusion architectures (TabDDPM, TabDiff); it explicitly does not improve over single-table attacks for latent-space architectures (TabSyn), and the paper is transparent about this scope condition. - Scalable: Datasets reach up to 1.69M rows in the target table but challenge sets are capped at 200 records; no experiments at 10× shadow training data scale. The claim that the framework extends to larger schemas is asserted but untested. The work delivers a paper-form attack framework with algorithm descriptions and hyperparameters sufficient for reimplementation, but no software artifact is released. Strengths - Formalizes a real and previously overlooked threat model: the training-inference asymmetry in multi-relational MIA is a practically motivated distinction that changes what privacy auditors should measure, and FERMI is the first framework built explicitly around it. - Honest and informative scope characterization: the paper pre-identifies when FERMI will fail (when the merged attack does not outperform the single-table attack), provides an empirical check (compare Merged vs. Direct Single before deploying the framework), and does not overstate transferability. - The identity-mapper ablation is a strong, specific control that rules out capacity-based explanations for the gains — a level of rigor that many attack papers omit. - The TabSyn finding is architecturally actionable: it establishes that the VAE latent bottleneck provides structural privacy resistance that persists even under full-relational oracle access, giving practitioners a concrete rationale for choosing latent-space diffusion architectures in privacy-sensitive deployments. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that FERMI cannot improve attack performance when the target architecture provides no meaningful relational membership signal (TabSyn), and leave latent-space–specific attacks to future work. They acknowledge that the Merged attack is a natural upper bound that FERMI only partially closes, that black-box experiments are limited to a single architecture-dataset pair (TabDDPM on California), and that Remark 1 identifies conditions under which the mapper can degrade finite-sample performance. These are stated with appropriate directness rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No evaluation against differentially private tabular diffusion models — DP-SGD or DP-Adam with calibrated noise budget is the standard practical defense, and whether FERMI's relational signal survives DP noise is the most policy-relevant open question, entirely absent from the paper. - Challenge sets of 200 records per model are small; TPR@0.01 estimates with standard deviations of 0.05–0.09 are unreliable for practical risk characterization, where decisions hinge on the low-FPR regime. - All three datasets are established ML benchmarks (California census microdata, Instacart market basket, Berka financial transactions) rather than proprietary or clinical records; the gap between benchmark-scale risk estimates and production deployment risk is unaddressed. - The black-box evaluation is restricted to TabDDPM on California; the claim that FERMI generalizes to black-box settings for TabDiff or multi-dataset settings is asserted from one data point. - No discussion of adaptive defenses: a model trainer who knows FERMI exists could train with randomized relational context augmentation or relational differential privacy to degrade the mapper's signal — this attack-defense equilibrium is not explored. Bottom Line Practitioners auditing privacy of relational tabular synthesis pipelines should read this paper — it exposes a concrete and previously unmeasured threat (relational context in attack training), provides a practical framework for exploiting it, and delivers the actionable architectural finding that latent-space diffusion (TabSyn) resists relational augmentation in a way that data-space models do not.
Search Results 2605.11527 PDF
#49 — Innovative (72)

Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust Learning for Debiased Knowledge Tracing

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TSDR (Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust), a model-agnostic training framework that wraps any Knowledge Tracing backbone with a doubly robust estimator and a theoretically derived temporal smoothness regularizer to correct selection bias from MNAR interaction data. - Why it's important: Standard KT models train on selectively observed interactions — students skip questions they find too hard or too easy — producing biased mastery estimates that adaptive tutoring systems then amplify in a compounding feedback loop, causing downstream recommendations to diverge from actual student knowledge. - How it does it: Jointly co-trains a propensity model and an error imputation model alongside the KT backbone using a DR loss; a temporal smoothness constraint on the imputation model's latent trajectory reduces the Rademacher complexity of the imputation function class, formally tightening the generalization bound's variance term. - The good: Applied to 6 SOTA KT backbones across 9 diverse datasets, TSDR consistently improves AUC, ACC, and RMSE — with gains up to +5.06% AUC on AKT (Prob dataset) and +4.76% on SparseKT (ASSISTments17) — and controlled synthetic MNAR experiments (γ ∈ [0, 0.999]) confirm that the improvements track bias severity. - The missing: The propensity model's accuracy against ground-truth observation probabilities is never empirically validated, even in the synthetic setting where true propensities are known, leaving the "double robustness" guarantee empirically unverified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on causal/debiased sequential modeling or educational ML; the theoretical grounding and plug-and-play architecture make it directly actionable for practitioners building adaptive tutoring systems.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (78) is the strongest dimension — 9 datasets × 6 backbones, 5-fold student-stratified CV, error bars reported, full theoretical proofs (Theorems 3–4 via Dudley's entropy integral), comprehensive ablation isolating each design choice, and public code at github.com/plzhan/TSDR. Non-Obviousness (75) — the chain from temporal smoothness → path length → covering number → Rademacher complexity → DR variance is genuinely non-obvious. Novelty (70) — synthesis of DR + temporal smoothness for KT with a derived bound. Completeness (70) and Resilience (70) solid but the propensity model is never validated against ground-truth even in the synthetic setting where it is known — a notable gap in the DR guarantee's empirical verification. Impact (70) — public repo, plug-and-play across SOTA backbones. Composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*70 = 72.35 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Knowledge Tracing (KT) systems model a student's evolving knowledge state from interaction histories to predict future performance on unseen questions, serving as the core of intelligent tutoring systems and adaptive learning platforms. A fundamental problem is that educational interaction data is Missing Not At Random (MNAR): students strategically skip questions perceived as too hard or too boring, and platform recommendation policies filter which questions are presented, creating systematic gaps in the observed data. Standard KT models apply naive empirical risk minimization over observed interactions, which conflates genuine knowledge states with observation patterns. This produces skewed proficiency estimates — overestimating knowledge on easy-skipping students, underestimating on hard-skipping students — and since recommendations are tailored to these biased estimates via the Zone of Proximal Development, the feedback loop reinforces and amplifies the bias over time. Prior work on KT debiasing addresses response noise and causal confounders in what is recorded, but largely ignores the systemic selection bias in why interactions are recorded at all. Key Idea The core insight is that in the sequential KT setting, the generalization error of a doubly robust estimator decomposes into a bias term (controlled by the product of propensity and imputation errors, which vanishes if either model is accurate) and a variance term that scales with the squared imputation errors divided by estimated propensities, Σ(δ_{t,c}/p̂_{t,c})², accumulated over the interaction sequence. This variance term drives training instability because imputation errors in noisy educational logs compound along the sequence. By imposing a temporal smoothness constraint — penalizing the squared L2 distance between consecutive latent states of the imputation model, Σ||h_t^imp - h_{t-1}^imp||² — the path length of the imputation trajectory in latent space is restricted, which formally reduces the Covering Number of the imputation function class, which in turn reduces the Rademacher complexity and tightens the generalization bound's variance term. This is not a heuristic regularizer: Theorems 3 and 4 establish the causal chain from L_smooth → path length → covering number → Rademacher complexity → variance reduction via Dudley's entropy integral and the chaining technique. Methodology TSDR jointly trains three components in an alternating 3-step procedure per mini-batch. (1) Propensity Model (ψ): a sigmoid linear layer over the KT backbone's hidden state h_t, modeling observation probability P(o_{t+1,c}=1 | h_t, c) as a multivariate Bernoulli over all concepts, trained via BCE treating unobserved concepts as implicit negatives. (2) Imputation Model (ω): a separate encoder of identical architecture to the KT backbone producing imputation-specific latent states h_t^imp, from which an error predictor outputs counterfactual errors ê_{t+1,c} for all concepts c. Trained with IPS-weighted MSE as base loss L_base plus temporal smoothness L_TS = λ/(T-1) Σ||h_t^imp - h_{t-1}^imp||²; total imputation loss L_imp = L_base + L_TS. (3) KT Backbone (θ, φ): trained with the DR loss L_DR = 1/N Σ[ê_{t+1,c} + (o_{t+1,c}/p̂_{t+1,c})(e_{t+1,c} - ê_{t+1,c})], which uses imputed errors as a baseline estimate and corrects for bias on observed interactions via IPS-weighted residuals. Three critical design decisions: applying temporal smoothness to the imputation branch rather than the KT backbone (ablation shows direct smoothing of KT backbone consistently hurts); joint co-optimization of auxiliary models with the backbone rather than pre-training then freezing (ablation shows joint learning uniformly improves AUC); and keeping the auxiliary modules training-only so inference runs only the KT backbone without overhead. Key Results Evaluated on 9 public benchmarks spanning diverse domains and scales: Spanish (182 users, 578K interactions), ASSISTments17 (1,708 users, 934K interactions), Slepemapy (5K users, 625K), Algebra05 (571 users, 607K), Prob/Linux/Database/Comp from PTADisc (512–5,488 users, 42K–990K), and EdNet (5K users, 789K interactions). Baselines are 6 current SOTA KT models: DKT (LSTM), AKT (context-aware Transformer), simpleKT, FoLiBiKT (forgetting-aware attention), SparseKT (k-sparse attention), DisKT (disentangled causal model). TSDR improves original AUC in the large majority of dataset-backbone combinations: representative gains include AKT +5.06% on Prob, SparseKT +4.76% on ASSISTments17. Gains are most pronounced on open-ended platforms with student autonomy (Prob, ASSISTments17, Slepemapy) and smallest on EdNet (~1% AUC), which uses mandatory bundle completion enforcing near-random missingness. Calibration (RMSE) improves consistently even when AUC gains are marginal — e.g., SparseKT on Linux: 1.70% RMSE reduction with near-zero AUC gain. Synthetic MNAR experiments with controllable bias degree γ ∈ {0.0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 0.999} show TSDR benefits all backbones across the entire spectrum; at γ=0.0 (no bias), TSDR does not degrade performance, attributed to regularization effects. Hyperparameter λ is stable in [0.3, 1.0] on the Prob dataset across all 6 backbones. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The DR estimator (IPW + imputation), joint learning paradigm, and temporal regularization are individually established in recommendation systems and time-series contexts, but their synthesis for the KT sequential setting — and specifically the derivation that temporal smoothness in the imputation model's latent trajectory formally controls the DR generalization bound's variance term via Rademacher complexity — constitutes a genuine novel theoretical contribution. The plug-and-play backbone-agnostic architecture is practically novel for the KT field, where debiasing has previously required architectural changes. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation (Table 5) tests all combinations of: DR-only, DR+joint learning, DR+TS on imputation, DR+TS on KT backbone, and the full variant across all 6 backbones — rigorously attributing improvements to each design choice. Theorems are proved in Appendices E-F using Azuma-Hoeffding for martingale difference sequences (sequential data) and Dudley's entropy integral for Rademacher complexity; proof sketches are in the main body, full derivations in the appendix. Results use 5-fold student-stratified cross-validation with early stopping (15 epochs no-improvement). Error bars (±) are reported for all metrics. Source code is publicly available at https://github.com/plzhan/TSDR. Methodological concern: the propensity model is never validated against ground-truth observation probabilities, even in the synthetic setting where γ parameterizes true observation probability — this leaves the key assumption of the DR guarantee (one of the two models must be accurate) empirically unverified. No significance tests beyond numerical reporting. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper builds, trains, and evaluates a training framework applied to existing KT architectures. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU using standard KT backbone architectures (LSTM and Transformer variants). - Efficient: Per-epoch training overhead is moderate (+0.21s for DKT, +0.78–1.68s for attention backbones); memory overhead is more significant for attention models (up to +8,908 MB for AKT due to intermediate activations during joint optimization), though zero additional inference cost since auxiliary modules are training-only. - Effective: Evidence is solid — consistently positive across 54 dataset-backbone combinations (9×6), with gains correlated to MNAR severity in controlled synthetic experiments, not just coincident with architectural improvements. - Scalable: Tested on datasets up to ~990K interactions (Database); the additional memory from imputation encoder plus joint optimization may be prohibitive at 10× scale for attention-based backbones; no stress tests beyond current benchmark scale. Delivers: source code repository implementing the training framework as a backbone wrapper. Strengths - The temporal smoothness constraint has a complete theoretical justification — the causal chain from L_smooth to variance reduction is established through Theorems 3 and 4, not asserted as intuition. - Model-agnostic plug-and-play design is validated across architecturally diverse backbones (LSTM through sparse and disentangled Transformers), demonstrating that the benefit is not backbone-specific. - The synthetic MNAR simulation provides the only ground-truth counterfactual evaluation in the paper, enabling verification that the method reduces bias rather than merely improving predictive accuracy through regularization effects. - Table 5's ablation cleanly isolates the contribution of each component, specifically showing that temporal smoothness belongs on the imputation branch (not the KT backbone) — a non-obvious design insight supported by empirical evidence. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that joint optimization of auxiliary modules increases training overhead, though they note this is confined to training and does not affect inference efficiency. No other limitations are explicitly stated in the conclusion; the paper does not confront the cases where TSDR provides minimal gains (EdNet) as a limitation, attributing it to dataset characteristics instead. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The propensity model assumes a multivariate Bernoulli with conditional independence across concepts given h_t, ignoring inter-concept observation dependencies (e.g., students who skip all math questions); this misspecification is not analyzed and could systematically bias propensity estimates. - In the synthetic experiments where the true MNAR mechanism is known (γ parameterizes exact skip probability), the paper never reports how closely estimated propensities p̂ match true propensities p — the most direct validation of the DR guarantee's core assumption. - No comparison to a baseline that applies the standard DR estimator from recommendation systems (Wang et al. 2019) without temporal smoothness to KT, making it hard to isolate how much of the gain comes from DR itself vs. the temporal smoothness innovation. - The λ sensitivity analysis covers only the Prob dataset; whether the stable region λ ∈ [0.3, 1.0] generalizes to other datasets (especially sparser or noisier ones) is unvalidated. - The theoretical guarantees assume bounded imputation errors and Lipschitz-continuous function classes; these assumptions are not verified for the specific neural architectures used in experiments. Bottom Line This preprint delivers a well-motivated and theoretically grounded plug-and-play debiasing framework with consistent empirical validation across diverse KT architectures; researchers studying causal/debiased learning for sequential educational data should read it, while practitioners should note that the largest gains occur on platforms with genuine student autonomy rather than structured curricula.
Search Results 2605.05958 PDF
#50 — Innovative (72)

Position: LLM Inference Should Be Evaluated as Energy-to-Token Production

Xiang Liu, Shimiao Yuan, Zhenheng Tang, Peijie Dong, Kaiyong Zhao, Qiang Wang, Bo Li, Xiaowen Chu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
