The 7-metric battery relies heavily on LLM calls for scoring (Detection F1, Fuzzing F1, Surprisal AUROC, LLM-Judge) — a potential self-grading circularity when the same model class serves as both refiner and evaluator. No human evaluation of absolute explanation accuracy is reported. The safety auditing case study withholds the feature identifier and intervention recipe, making that result non-reproducible by design. Deployability (SYSTEMS): This is a SYSTEMS paper — a built multi-agent tool evaluated by deployment on real SAE features. - Practical: Runs on standard LLM API access (GPT-4o-mini, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude 4 Sonnet) with transformer-lens and sae-lens for SAE loading; no specialized hardware beyond what is needed to serve the target model. - Efficient: 34× token overhead over one-shot baseline; $0.006 per feature with GPT-4o-mini, $0.124 with frontier GPT-5.4 — cost is manageable for up to low thousands of features but becomes expensive at Neuronpedia scale (millions of features). - Effective: Evidence is strong for the refinement component (63–83% win rate) and for discovery (ARI 0.315, validated across 8 language and non-linguistic categories); the causal steering safety demonstration shows functional intervention, though only qualitatively for a single feature. - Scalable: Tested up to 1,600 features per run; the paper projects scaling to large SAE repositories as future work but provides no experiment beyond this. Cost scales linearly with feature count at 13 LLM calls each. The system delivers an open-source Python software artifact. Strengths: - The proposal-test-revise loop grounded in real model activations is a principled scientific workflow that moves mechanistic interpretability from correlational labeling toward falsifiable experimentation, with every hypothesis, test case, metric score, and revision logged as an auditable trace. - Polysemanticity detection is practically important and methodologically sound: rather than forcing a single label, the system flags and reports all semantically distinct hypotheses achieving comparable multi-metric support, turning a common failure mode into a detectable output. - Generalizing to raw MLP neurons in weight-sparse transformers without any SAE (Appendix A.9) demonstrates representational agnosticism and independently validates findings that previously required approximately one researcher-day of manual circuit analysis. - The cost analysis (Tables A3–A4) is unusually transparent and practically useful — establishing that iterative refinement of 1,000 features costs under $6–$125 depending on model tier removes a key adoption barrier. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: the refinement loop does not guarantee monotonic convergence; hypothesis quality depends on the base LLM's ability to generate informative test cases, which degrades for abstract or context-dependent features; FeatureFinder requires user-provided prompt sets with group labels and fully unsupervised discovery remains future work; and the safety auditing demonstration withholds the feature identifier and intervention recipe, framing it as an auditing illustration rather than a reproducible experiment. These are stated directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant gap is the absence of any comparison against Paulo et al. (2025) as a competing pipeline — the paper adapts three of their metrics but never benchmarks against their method, leaving the question open of whether simpler iterative improvements within one-shot autointerp achieve comparable gains. The 7-metric battery uses LLM calls for five of seven metrics; the same model family serving as both hypothesis generator and evaluator creates potential self-grading circularity that is not addressed. Absolute clustering ARI values are low (0.315), meaning the majority of prompts are incorrectly clustered — the paper demonstrates k-NN beats alternatives but does not establish that this absolute level is sufficient for reliable discovery in practice. The refinement win-rate estimate for Claude 4 Sonnet (83.3%, n=60 features) is fragile: with this sample size, a 95% confidence interval spans roughly ±10 percentage points. Causal steering (the most safety-relevant claim) is demonstrated on one feature with qualitative outputs; no quantitative measurement of refusal suppression magnitude or behavioral specificity is provided. Bottom Line: A mechanistic interpretability researcher or AI safety practitioner working with Gemma-2-scale SAEs gains a directly usable, open-source automated pipeline that demonstrably outperforms one-shot auto-interpretation, detects polysemanticity, and generalizes beyond SAEs — worth reading despite narrow evaluation scope and the absence of comparisons against recent competing interpretability pipelines.
Recommended 2605.01555 PDF
#56 — Innovative (72)

GazeVLM: Active Vision via Internal Attention Control for Multimodal Reasoning

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GazeVLM, a 4B-parameter Vision-Language Model that performs active visual foveation by emitting special <LOOK> tokens that dynamically modulate the causal attention mask over pre-encoded visual features, enabling the model to shift focus between global and local image regions without re-encoding image patches. - Why it's important: Existing VLM reasoning approaches either process vision passively (allowing text tokens to progressively dominate visual attention, causing hallucinations) or use external zoom-in tools that inflate the context window by ~1,325 tokens per trace on high-resolution benchmarks, degrading efficiency and fragmenting spatial coherence. - How it does it: A plateau-shaped Gaussian suppression bias β_i(b) is additively injected into the LLM decoder's pre-softmax attention logits for text-to-vision queries, zeroing penalty inside a predicted bounding box while exponentially dampening tokens outside it; this bias acts as a training-time regularizer and is disabled at inference once the behavior is internalized. - The good: At inference, disabling the explicit bias outperforms keeping it active (+3.9% on MathVista, +2.6% on MMStar), demonstrating that the gaze routing policy is genuinely internalized into model weights rather than being a test-time crutch — a compelling behavioral internalization result. - The missing: The evaluation dataset used for SFT warm-start (GQA, ChartQA, PlotQA, InfoVQA) overlaps substantially with the ChartQA evaluation benchmark, raising a Goodhart's Law concern that is not explicitly addressed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on multimodal reasoning efficiency, visual grounding, or attention-steering; the mechanism is technically clean and the ablation structure is strong, though the high-resolution benchmark improvements (+3.9/+4.4) are meaningful but not transformative.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) plateau-Gaussian logit bias as training-time regularizer for foveation is genuinely novel. Rigor (70) well-structured 2x2 ablation, zero-shot suppression sweep with ±1σ over 10 trials, but ChartQA train/eval overlap unflagged, no code release. Non-Obviousness (80) behavioral internalization (bias-off > bias-on at inference) is a surprising and crisp finding. Completeness (75) authors honestly acknowledge sub-pixel limits, over-gaze bias, and dual-use. Resilience (70) zero-shot sweep validates mechanism independently of training. Impact (65) 4.98x fewer tokens than DeepEyes, but no public weights. Composite: 0.20*72+0.25*70+0.15*80+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.10*65 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: As VLMs engage in extended chain-of-thought reasoning, textual tokens increasingly dominate causal attention, progressively underweighting the original visual features. This causes over-reliance on linguistic priors and hallucination of visual content. Existing remedies are unsatisfactory: grounded reasoning (outputting bounding box coordinates alongside answers) enforces a superficial text-image link but does not change internal attention during generation. External zoom-in tools (V*, Pixel Reasoner, Ground-R1, DeepEyes) crop and re-encode image patches, adding up to ~1,325 additional visual tokens per reasoning trace, inflating context windows and degrading inference speed. Neither approach gives the model active, metacognitive control over where it looks within an already-encoded scene. Key Idea: The core insight is that spatial selective attention — foveal fixation — can be implemented as an additive logit bias on a transformer's pre-softmax attention scores over already-encoded visual tokens, rather than by feeding new image tokens into the context. By constraining this bias to affect only text-to-vision attention queries and injecting it as a Gaussian plateau (zero suppression inside the target bounding box, increasing suppression outside proportional to squared distance from the box edge), the model experiences attention routing as a differentiable training signal. Crucially, the bias serves as a training-time regularizer that forces the model's weights to internalize the routing behavior, making explicit bias injection unnecessary at test time. Methodology: GazeVLM follows a three-component pipeline. Architecture: built on Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct, the model extends the vocabulary with <LOOK at="text" bbox=[x1,y1,x2,y2]> and </LOOK> special tokens. Each visual token v_i is assigned a normalized 2D coordinate p_i in [0,1]^2 corresponding to its receptive field. When a <LOOK> token is emitted, a plateau suppression bias β_i(b) = −α_s · D²(p_i, b)/(2σ²) is computed per visual token, where D(p_i, b) is Euclidean distance to the nearest bounding box edge (zero inside the box). This bias is added to s(q_t, k_i) for text query tokens q_t only (indicator 1_text(q_t) = 1), leaving vision-to-vision attention unaffected. Multiple sequential <LOOK> operations accumulate via element-wise maximum over historical bias states. Training stage 1: SFT on curated gaze-reasoning traces (generated by Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B on GQA, ChartQA, PlotQA, InfoVQA) with standard cross-entropy, activating the suppression bias during the forward pass. Training stage 2: GRPO with a composite reward R(c) = R_correct + R_format + R_bbox + R_overlap + R_excess + R_len, rewarding answer correctness, valid token format, geometrically valid bounding boxes, non-redundant gaze, and brevity. Key design decisions: (1) plateau shape (zero inside the box) rather than full Gaussian centered on the box — justified by preserving uniform visibility within the target region; (2) text-to-vision-only bias to avoid corrupting the visual feature representations themselves; (3) disabling bias at inference — validated by ablation showing internalized routing outperforms hard-coded test-time bias. Key Results: GazeVLM (4B parameters) achieves 73.3 on MathVista, 89.2 on ChartQA, 69.0 on MMStar, 85.4 on CV-Bench, 83.4 on HRBench-4k, and 78.0 on HRBench-8k. Compared to its base (Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct), gains are +1.6, +1.5, +1.7, +2.0, +3.9, +4.4 respectively. Against the passive grounding baseline (same data + GRPO, no attention bias), the gaze mechanism adds up to +5.2% on MathVista and +2.6% on MMStar. Against DeepEyes-7B (an RL-trained agentic VLM with external zoom-in tool) on HRBench-4k, GazeVLM-4B achieves +8.3% accuracy while generating 4.98× fewer tokens per trace (265.98 vs. 1,325.33). GazeVLM uses 1.19 active-vision interventions per trace vs. DeepEyes's 1.81. Zero-shot suppression strength sweep (using oracle bounding boxes on the unmodified base model) demonstrates correctness rising from 0% (α_s=0) to a near-optimal plateau at α_s=4, empirically validating the mechanism independent of training. Baselines include competitive RL-trained VLMs (Vision-R1-7B, Pixel-Reasoner-7B, DeepEyes-7B, Ground-R1-7B) and closed-source models (OpenAI-o1, GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5). GazeVLM at 4B competes with or beats most 7B RL-trained models on high-resolution tasks. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The specific contribution — using a differentiable, spatially structured logit bias on pre-encoded visual tokens as both a foveation mechanism and a training-time behavioral regularizer, then disabling it at inference — is genuinely novel. Attention steering (e.g., ASCD) and visual grounding (e.g., Argus) are prior art, but neither combines the plateau suppression formulation, multi-gaze accumulation, GRPO training loop, and behavioral-internalization evaluation in this way. The general framework of token-logit manipulation for attention control is not new. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are well-structured: Table 2 isolates gaze bias at training vs. inference, and SFT vs. GRPO stages, across five benchmarks. The behavioral internalization finding (bias-off > bias-on at inference) is specifically ablated. The zero-shot suppression strength sweep (Figure 3) is a clean mechanistic validation independent of the full training pipeline. Error bars are absent from the main results table; the zero-shot sweep reports ±1σ across 10 independent trials. No code or model weights release is announced. The ChartQA training/evaluation overlap is not flagged. Data generation details are deferred to Appendix A.1, which was not fully reviewed but is referenced in the main text. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on a 4B-parameter model (Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct base); compatible with any hardware that can serve a 4B VLM, with no requirement for external cropping infrastructure. - Efficient: Generates 4.98× fewer output tokens per trace than DeepEyes on HRBench-4k (265.98 vs. 1,325.33); the suppression bias is a lightweight additive logit operation with negligible per-token overhead. - Effective: Solves the stated problem of attention dilution on high-resolution tasks, with +3.9/+4.4 on HRBench-4k/8k over base; the qualitative attention heatmap comparison is compelling for a single example but not systematically evaluated. - Scalable: Tested at 4B parameters on standard VQA benchmarks; behavior at larger model scales (7B+) or on resolution beyond 8k is not evaluated. No claim about scaling is made beyond the tested configurations. The work delivers a trained model checkpoint (not publicly released at submission) and an architecture modification; no software library or standalone tool is provided. Strengths: - The behavioral internalization result — that disabling the explicit bias at inference yields superior performance — is the paper's strongest intellectual contribution and provides mechanistic evidence that GRPO genuinely embeds spatial attention routing into model weights, not just behavioral output formatting. - The zero-shot suppression sweep (Figure 3) is a clean, surgical experiment: injecting oracle bounding boxes into an unmodified base model and sweeping α_s from 0 to 20 demonstrates that the plateau suppression mechanism alone, independent of training, already unlocks high-resolution reasoning on a model that scores 0% without it. - The ablation design (Table 2) properly disentangles four dimensions (SFT/GRPO × train-with-gaze/infer-with-gaze), providing cleaner causal attribution than most VLM reasoning papers. - Computational efficiency comparison (Table 3) uses a concrete and relevant metric (tokens/trace and active-vision calls/trace) against a directly comparable agentic baseline, rather than abstract FLOPs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state three limitations: (1) the mechanism cannot recover sub-pixel detail when targets occupy only a few pixels at native encoding resolution, since no re-encoding occurs — a hybrid fallback policy is identified as a natural extension; (2) the curated SFT dataset retains only successful <LOOK> traces, biasing the policy toward over-gazing even when a global view would suffice; (3) dual-use concerns around the gaze trace interpretability feature, specifically surveillance and biometric tracking applications. These are frank and substantive acknowledgments. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The ChartQA training set is one of the four sources for SFT warm-start data generation, and ChartQA is also used as an evaluation benchmark; the paper does not address whether the SFT traces were filtered to avoid evaluation-set contamination. The passive grounding baseline (GazeVLM w/o gaze bias) already outperforms some 7B RL-trained baselines (e.g., DeepEyes 74.4 vs. 72.6 on HRBench-8k), suggesting that data curation quality is a large driver of the results — the marginal contribution of the attention mechanism alone is +1.0 to +5.2 points depending on benchmark, which is meaningful but smaller than the data-quality effect. No human evaluation or qualitative analysis of failure modes (beyond the single Figure 2 example) is provided. Comparison to V* [36], cited as the most resolution-capable external tool, is absent from Table 1. The MMStar result (69.0 vs. 67.3 for base) is the smallest gain and is actually lower than Qwen2.5-VL-7B (69.0 = tied), suggesting 4B is at its ceiling on abstract reasoning tasks; this is not discussed. Bottom Line: Practitioners working on efficient high-resolution multimodal reasoning should read this paper for its clean mechanistic approach and the behavioral-internalization result, though the absolute performance numbers at 4B require context: data curation does much of the heavy lifting, and the gaze mechanism's marginal benefit is benchmark-dependent.
Search Results 2605.07817 PDF
#57 — Innovative (72)

Retrieve, Integrate, and Synthesize: Spatial-Semantic Grounded Latent Visual Reasoning

Jin Cui, Xinyue Long, Xunyong Zhang, Yadong Zhang, Chuanchang Su, Jingye Gan, Boran Zhao, Pengju Ren · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RIS (Retrieve, Integrate, and Synthesize), a three-phase training framework that anchors MLLM latent reasoning tokens to spatial bounding-box evidence and semantic region descriptions to produce interpretable, causally effective latent visual reasoning trajectories. - Why it's important: Existing latent visual reasoning methods (LVR, Monet, CoVT) suffer from manifold divergence, trajectory collapse, and answer bypassing — meaning their latent tokens carry little actual visual information and are ignored by the answer decoding process, defeating the purpose of in-context visual reasoning. - How it does it: RIS jointly imposes bounding-box regression losses (ℓ1 + GIoU) and cosine semantic alignment losses on supervised latent slots via lightweight side-head decoders, uses a progressive attention bottleneck mask to force answer tokens to route through latent tokens, and introduces elaborated "full answer" transition tokens to bridge the high-entropy latent manifold back to vocabulary-aligned decoding. - The good: RIS achieves consistent improvements over all open-source and latent reasoning baselines across five benchmarks, with particularly large gains on localization-heavy tasks: +11.01 on BLINK Object Localization and +3.55 on MMVP over Monet, using the same Qwen2.5-VL-7B backbone and only 5 latent tokens. - The missing: The supervision dataset (GLSD) derives entirely from GQA train-split images with 2–4 step chains (avg 2.67 steps), and all evaluation is on static single-image VQA; whether the grounding mechanism generalizes to video, multi-image, or open-ended generation is claimed but untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on latent-space MLLM reasoning or grounded fine-tuning — the geometric manifold analysis of why existing latent methods fail is independently valuable, and the progressive bottleneck mechanism is a technically concrete solution to a documented problem.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) is a strength — ablations with error bars, mechanistic diagnostics (entropy, trajectory diversity, PCA), five benchmarks, well-controlled Base+GLSD baseline. Novelty (72) — progressive attention bottleneck plus side-head spatial-semantic supervision is a concrete and well-motivated new recipe, but built on known components. Non-Obviousness (75) — the three-failure-mode diagnosis is a real insight. Completeness (72) and Resilience (68) — author limitations are candid, but teacher-distillation confound is only partially controlled and benchmark gains are modest on half the tasks. Impact (65) reduced because no code/weights release is announced and only 7B scale tested. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*65 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation MLLMs compress continuous visual features into discrete text tokens before any reasoning occurs, creating a lossy bottleneck for fine-grained perceptual tasks (precise spatial layouts, object relationships, high-resolution details). Recent latent visual reasoning methods attempt to bypass this by reasoning in continuous hidden states, but a causal mediation study (cited as [14]) and the authors' own geometric analysis show that latent tokens in methods like LVR and Monet are weakly grounded in the visual input and exert limited causal influence on final answers. The authors identify three concrete failure modes: (1) manifold divergence — fine-tuned latent states drift away from the pretrained vocabulary-aligned manifold into regions incompatible with pretrained reasoning circuits; (2) trajectory collapse — latent tokens across different samples converge to nearly identical, instance-agnostic representations; (3) answer bypassing — answer tokens allocate the majority of attention directly to image and text tokens, ignoring latent tokens altogether. These three pathologies explain why forming an expressive but detached latent manifold does not suffice for effective visual reasoning. Key Idea The core insight is that latent visual reasoning must develop as a compatible extension of the pretrained reasoning manifold rather than a separate visual manifold. Latent tokens should simultaneously know where to look (spatial grounding via bounding boxes), what is seen (semantic anchoring via region descriptions), be forced into use (progressive attention bottleneck preventing bypass), and smoothly return to vocabulary space (transition tokens that gradually reduce representation entropy from latent to answer tokens). Together, these constraints keep latent space within reach of pretrained reasoning circuits while injecting spatially grounded visual evidence. Methodology The pipeline operates in three training phases on a 7B backbone (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) with K=5 fixed latent tokens. The prerequisite is GLSD (Grounded Latent Supervision Dataset), a 96k-sample dataset derived from GQA training data: an MLLM (Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B) decomposes each QA pair into 2–4 grounded reasoning steps, each containing an operation tag, a bounding-box prediction, and a region semantic description; Grounding DINO then verifies and calibrates the predicted boxes. Phase 1 (Explicit Grounding Initialization): Fine-tune the backbone with standard NTP loss on the full textual grounded reasoning trace T concatenated with full answer A_s and final answer A. This establishes a text-space grounded reasoning prior. Phase 2a (Side-Head Grounding): Freeze the backbone; train two lightweight side heads on the hidden state z_t at each supervised latent slot. The Visual Grounding Decoder f_reg predicts bounding-box coordinates with ℒ_reg = ℓ1 + λ_GIoU × ℒ_GIoU. The Semantic Alignment Decoder f_desc projects z_t to match the pre-computed text embedding of the region description: ℒ_desc = cosine distance. The Phase-2a loss is ℒ_Phase2a = λ_r ℒ_reg + λ_d ℒ_desc. Phase 2b (Progressive Latent Internalization): Unfreeze backbone; apply a Coconut-style curriculum replacing the first s textual reasoning steps with corresponding latent tokens C_{≤s}, while retaining remaining textual steps T_{>s} and full answer A_s for next-token prediction. Side-head losses are applied only to replaced supervised slots. Combined loss: ℒ_ans + α ℒ_cot + λ_r ℒ_reg + λ_d ℒ_desc. Phase 3 (Bottlenecked Latent Integration): All reasoning steps are now latent. A Progressive Attention Mask anneals masking probability ρ from 0.3 to 1.0 via Bernoulli sampling over the causal attention matrix, forcing answer tokens to condition exclusively on latent tokens C rather than raw image/text inputs. Loss: ℒ_ans + λ_r ℒ_reg + λ_d ℒ_desc. The "full answer" A_s (an elaborated natural-language sentence bridging latent output to the short final answer) serves as Manifold Transition Tokens — dense NTP supervision that gradually walks the representation from high-entropy latent states to low-entropy vocabulary-aligned decoding, preventing the abrupt entropy collapse that causes representation mismatch. Total training: ~20k update steps, 26.5–33 hours on 4×A100 80GB. An optional Phase-4 applies VLPO (Visual-latent Policy Optimization from Monet) using 3.2k–6.4k GLSD samples and accuracy + format rewards. Key Results All results use Qwen2.5-VL-7B as the shared backbone. Baselines include GPT-4o (proprietary), vanilla SFT on GLSD (Base+GLSD), and latent reasoning methods LVR, Monet, CoVT. On V* (guided visual search benchmark): RIS 83.75% vs. Monet 81.90%, LVR 80.60%, CoVT 79.10%, base 76.65%, GPT-4o 65.15%. On HRBench4K (high-resolution perception): RIS overall 73.23% vs. Monet 69.97%; but CoVT achieves 85.50% FSP subcategory (RIS: 86.33 FSP, Monet: 85.50 FSP — competitive). On HRBench8K: RIS 68.52% vs. Monet 67.01%, CoVT 68.40% — marginal differences. On MMVP: RIS 73.55 vs. Monet 70.00, CoVT 58.70. On BLINK: RIS 60.60 vs. Monet 56.70, CoVT 57.40. BLINK subcategories: Spatial Reasoning 89.60 (+2.79 over base), Object Localization 54.63 (+11.01 over base, +9.01 over Monet), Counting 72.02 (+3.72). RIS+VLPO improves BLINK to 60.95 (+5.66 over Monet). Ablations (Figure 5a) show the progressive attention mask produces the largest performance drop when removed, followed by bounding-box supervision, transition tokens, and description supervision — all components are empirically necessary. Cross-sample latent token cosine similarity is substantially lower for RIS than LVR and Monet, confirming diversity of learned representations. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The spatial-semantic side-head supervision applied directly to latent token hidden states, combined with the progressive attention bottleneck mask as an explicit anti-bypass mechanism, constitutes a technically novel training recipe for grounded latent reasoning. The GLSD dataset is a new resource. The geometric manifold analysis distinguishing three failure modes of existing latent methods is a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual components (GIoU loss, curriculum internalization, contrastive semantic alignment) are known; the specific combination applied to latent token training with the bottleneck design is original. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation study covers all four major components with error bars reflecting standard deviation across repeated training runs — this is a genuine strength. The attention entropy and trajectory diversity analyses (Figures 4a–d) provide interpretable evidence that RIS achieves the intended latent behavior rather than just improving benchmark numbers. However, code and data are not stated to be publicly released (not stated). The supervision traces in GLSD are generated by Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B, a 235B model — this introduces a teacher quality confound: gains could partly reflect distillation from a much larger model rather than the grounding mechanism itself; the Base+GLSD SFT baseline partially controls for this (it improves the base from 76.65% to 78.25% on V*), and RIS adds a further 5.5pp, suggesting the mechanism itself matters. The paper is an arXiv preprint (v1, May 8, 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Requires 4×A100 80GB GPUs for training (commercially available but high-end); inference adds only 5 latent tokens to the standard MLLM forward pass, imposing negligible overhead. - Efficient: Total training cost is 26.5–33 hours on 4 A100s at 7B scale; Phase 2b dominates at 16–20 hours and scales linearly with latent budget K — manageable but not cheap. - Effective: Evidence across 5 diverse benchmarks (including BLINK, designed to test perceptual capabilities MLLMs often miss) supports that grounding translates to measurable accuracy gains, not just internal representation quality; the link from grounded latent tokens to final answer accuracy is validated by the bottleneck ablation. - Scalable: Tested only at 7B scale with K=5 tokens; performance does not improve monotonically with K (degrades beyond training trace length, especially on HRBench8K and MMVP), and no experiments at 13B+ scales or with longer reasoning chains are reported. The paper delivers a training recipe and a new dataset (GLSD), but no public code repository or pretrained model weights are announced. Strengths - The geometric manifold analysis (PCA visualization, layer-wise parameter shift, attention distribution) provides a principled and reproducible diagnostic framework for evaluating latent reasoning methods beyond accuracy numbers — genuinely useful for the field independent of RIS. - The progressive attention bottleneck mask is a specific, well-justified mechanism targeting the precisely identified "latent bypassing" failure mode, and its ablation confirms it is the single most important component. - GLSD construction (MLLM trace generation + Grounding DINO calibration) provides a scalable recipe that others can apply to other source datasets beyond GQA. - The entropy trajectory analysis (Figure 4c) showing that supervised latent tokens maintain high entropy while transition tokens gradually reduce it constitutes interpretable mechanistic evidence that the design works as intended. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) multiple interacting hyperparameters and training schedules without principled automatic selection; (2) fixed latent token budget K, which cannot adapt to input complexity; (3) evaluation restricted to standard single-image VQA benchmarks, with video, multi-image, and open-ended generation left to future work. These limitations are addressed directly in Appendix A with candor, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The teacher-distillation confound is partially controlled but not eliminated: the Base+GLSD SFT baseline uses the same GLSD traces as RIS but without latent tokens. Since Base+GLSD already improves substantially over base on some metrics, decomposing "grounding mechanism" from "richer trace supervision" requires a more controlled experiment (e.g., training on equivalently detailed non-spatial descriptions). - The 2D PCA manifold visualization (Figure 1a) is a projection of high-dimensional hidden states. The paper does not report the explained variance ratio, so the visual claim that LVR/Monet manifolds are "separated" from the vocabulary manifold could be partially an artifact of the projection plane chosen. - No baseline tests a simpler approach: adding 5 explicit visual region attention operations in text space (e.g., selecting and describing regions as text, without latent tokens). The comparison to Base+GLSD SFT is the closest proxy, but it doesn't have the iterative multi-region structure that RIS imposes. - Evaluation benchmarks (V*, HRBench, MMVP, BLINK) are all single-image, short-answer tasks. The "synthesis" function of unsupervised latent tokens (claimed to aggregate evidence across steps) is most visible on BLINK but is not tested on tasks requiring longer reasoning chains or open-ended answers, where it should matter most. - RIS+VLPO does not consistently outperform RIS, suggesting the RL component is unstable. This is acknowledged but the instability's source is not diagnosed. Bottom Line A practitioner working on grounded multimodal reasoning or latent token training will gain concrete, implementable ideas from RIS — particularly the progressive attention bottleneck and the manifold diagnostic framework — though the evidence base is limited to a single 7B backbone and standard short-answer VQA benchmarks, and the gains are modest on half the tested benchmarks.
Search Results 2605.07106 PDF
#58 — Innovative (72)

Computational Lesions in Multilingual Language Models Separate Shared and Language-specific Brain Alignment

Yang Cui, Jingyuan Sun, Yizheng Sun, Yifan Wang, Yunhao Zhang, Jixing Li, Shaonan Wang, Hongpeng Zhou, John Hale, Chengqing Zong, Goran Nenadic · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a computational lesion framework applied to six multilingual LLMs that causally dissociates shared versus language-specific parameter subsets driving fMRI brain-model alignment across English, Chinese, and French native speakers. - Why it's important: Whether the brain's language network implements a common computational algorithm across languages or recruits language-specific mechanisms is foundational to multilingual neuroscience, and existing correlational methods cannot answer this causally. - How it does it: Gradient×weight importance scores derived from language-specific fine-tuning identify a compact shared "core" (top 1% of cross-language important parameters) and per-language subsets, which are then zeroed to create lesioned models whose voxel-wise fMRI encoding performance is compared against intact models. - The good: Core ablation produces a convergent triple collapse — representational geometry compresses (Silhouette Coefficient drops from 0.4277 to 0.0594 in Qwen2.5-7B), syntactic probing degrades across all three languages, and whole-brain encoding correlation falls 60.32% — all from removing just 1% of parameters, replicated across six models from three architectures. - The missing: Core ablation causes catastrophic perplexity inflation (Qwen2.5-7B: 6.75 → 3,792,672.75), making it impossible to distinguish selective removal of brain-relevant computation from generic representational collapse; dose-response or matched-performance controls are absent. - Read it or not: Researchers at the intersection of multilingual NLP and cognitive neuroscience should read it for its causal lesion logic, which advances beyond correlational layer-wise comparisons, though the confound between core lesion severity and general competence destruction remains the central unresolved problem.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — causal lesion design across multilingual LLMs for fMRI alignment is a fresh combination, with a real new empirical finding about a sparse cross-language core. Rigor (70) — six models, FDR correction, public code/data, and a random-ablation control, but the core-lesion confound (perplexity exploding ~560,000×) is not addressed by matched-degradation controls and effect sizes are reported as single means without CIs. Non-Obviousness (80) — the triple-collapse convergence and typology-graded cross-language impairment are surprising. Completeness (70) and Resilience (60) are pulled down by the unresolved competence-vs-specificity confound the authors themselves flag. Impact (70) for a reusable analysis pipeline. Weighted: 0.20·75 + 0.25·70 + 0.15·80 + 0.15·70 + 0.15·60 + 0.10·70 = 71.5 → 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Neuroimaging has identified a fronto-temporo-parietal language network active across many languages, but shared anatomy does not imply shared computation — the same regions can support different operations over different grammatical structures. The question of whether multilingual comprehension relies on a common computational substrate or recruits language-specific mechanisms is unanswerable by standard neuroimaging alone. Prior brain-model alignment work has been largely correlational (comparing intact representations across model layers) and monolingual (English-centric), leaving open whether the model components that drive alignment are shared or selective. Native speakers of typologically distant languages such as Mandarin Chinese and French present particularly sharp test cases, since their grammatical and orthographic demands diverge substantially from the English-dominated training distributions of most models tested previously. Key Idea Causal necessity is testable by disruption rather than correlation: identify parameter subsets important for each language using gradient×weight importance scores accumulated during language-specific fine-tuning, zero the top 1% of the cross-language "core" versus per-language subsets, then measure whether fMRI predictivity fails globally (implicating shared computation) or selectively (implicating language-specific computation). This converts brain-model alignment from a descriptive encoding score into a failure-mode test tied to specific, distributed parameter circuits. Methodology Three-stage pipeline. (1) Parameter importance identification: six multilingual LLMs (LLaMA2-7B/13B, Qwen2.5-7B/14B, Mistral-8B/12B) are fine-tuned separately on English, Chinese, and French literary corpora (Jane Eyre / Pride and Prejudice; Dream of the Red Chamber / Romance of the Three Kingdoms; L'Étranger / Les Misérables) using autoregressive next-token prediction. Importance score IL(θi) = |θi| × Σt|∂LL/∂θi(t)| (weight magnitude × accumulated absolute gradient). Core importance sums scores across all three languages; language-specific importance is the per-language rank percentile minus the maximum of the other two languages' percentiles. Top 1% of core parameters and top 1% of each language-specific subset are zeroed (set to zero), uniformly per transformer layer and component. (2) Neural encoding: final-layer contextualized embeddings from intact and lesioned LLMs are averaged per TR (2s), shifted 4s for hemodynamic lag, and used to fit voxel-wise ridge regression models (9-fold cross-validation, one fold per acquisition run) predicting BOLD time series per participant. Performance = Pearson r between predicted and observed BOLD. (3) Statistical analysis: voxel-wise paired t-tests (Fisher-transformed r, intact vs. lesioned), FDR < 0.01. A Language Processing Index (LPI) normalizes per-language t-maps within each model and computes a contrast score (target language vs. mean of others), then averages across all six models for architecture-robust localization. Critical design decisions: uniform proportional ablation across all layers avoids concentrating damage at a single layer (well-justified relative to single-layer ablation); using final-layer embeddings only rather than a layer-optimal approach is not compared against alternatives; literary fine-tuning corpora differ in domain from the fMRI stimulus (The Little Prince), a mismatch not discussed. Key Results Intact multilingual LLMs reliably predict BOLD responses in the canonical language network across all three languages and all six architectures, with Pearson r up to ~0.12–0.14 in left temporal ROIs (FDR < 0.01). Increasing model size from ~7B to ~14B parameters does not yield systematic improvement in neural predictivity. Core ablation reduces whole-brain mean encoding correlation by 60.32% relative to intact models (reported as a mean across all models; within-model variance not stated). In Qwen2.5-7B, Silhouette Coefficient of language-separated embedding clusters drops from 0.4277 (intact) to 0.0594 (core-lesioned), and perplexity on WikiText-2 increases from 6.75 to 3,792,672.75. Syntactic probing (sentence length, tree depth, top constituents, coordination inversion) drops across all three languages after core ablation, with largest drops on structural tasks. Language-specific lesions produce a dissociation: cross-language embedding separation is preserved (UMAP clusters remain distinct), but voxel-wise encoding drops most for matched-language participants — a clear diagonal in the 3×3 (lesion language × participant language) t-map matrix. English-French specific ablations cross-impair each other more strongly than Chinese-specific ablations affect French/English participants, consistent with Indo-European typological proximity. A random-ablation control (same 1% budget, randomly selected parameters) is included and produces smaller effects, partially ruling out generic parameter-removal artifacts. All effects concentrate in canonical language-selective regions (anterior and posterior temporal, inferior frontal). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Applying gradient-guided computational lesions across multilingual LLMs to causally dissociate shared versus language-specific components of fMRI brain-model alignment is novel. The specific finding — that a compact, distributed core (~1% of parameters) accounts for the majority of cross-language brain-model alignment while language-specific subsets contribute smaller, language-matched and typologically graded effects — constitutes a new empirical result. Underlying components (voxel-wise ridge regression encoding, gradient-weight importance, the LPPC-fMRI dataset) are established; the novelty is the causal dissociation design and its application to a multilingual neural encoding framework. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly available (github.com/yang-C23/Shared-Unique-Neural-Architecture-for-Language); fMRI data are available via OpenNeuro (ds003643). A random-ablation control is included. FDR correction (p < 0.01) is applied to all voxel-wise tests. Results replicate across six models from three families with different tokenizers and attention mechanisms — a genuine methodological strength. However, the headline 60.32% reduction figure is reported as a single mean with no confidence interval or model-level breakdown. The 1% ablation threshold is fixed and untested; no dose-response curve is provided. Syntactic probing datasets for French and Chinese are machine-translated from English SentEval without reported validation or back-translation checks; Chinese annotations (syntactic tree depth, coordination) depend on segmentation choices not documented. Fine-tuning corpora are literary texts in a different genre and era from the fMRI stimulus, introducing a domain mismatch that could bias which parameters are flagged as important. Effect sizes for the language-specific lesion dissociation (diagonal vs. off-diagonal contrast) are not reported quantitatively. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The ablation and encoding pipeline runs on open-source model weights; full-parameter fine-tuning (required for importance estimation) used EuroHPC HPC resources and is not runnable on consumer hardware for 14B models. - Efficient: No compute or wall-time estimates are reported; the ablation itself is parameter zeroing and adds negligible overhead, but six rounds of full-parameter fine-tuning per model family is expensive. - Effective: The lesion framework demonstrates structured, anatomically specific reductions in fMRI predictivity, though the core lesion effect conflates specific information removal with general model collapse; evidence for causal specificity is strong for the language-specific lesions, weaker for the core. - Scalable: Only 7B–14B models are tested; the paper explicitly declines to extrapolate beyond this range and notes no systematic size effect within it; behavior at 70B+ is unknown. The paper delivers a reusable analysis pipeline and code; ablated model weights are not explicitly stated as released. Strengths - The causal lesion design — comparing intact versus lesioned models on the same stimulus within each language group — eliminates cross-stimulus confounds that plague cross-language fMRI comparisons, making the within-language impairment contrasts clean. - Convergence across six architecturally heterogeneous models (three families, two scales, different tokenizers, MHA vs. GQA) substantially reduces the risk that findings are artifacts of a specific design choice. - The "triple collapse" under core ablation — simultaneous failure of representational geometry, linguistic probing, and fMRI predictivity from the same 1% intervention — provides triangulating convergent evidence that is more compelling than any single measure alone. - The graded cross-language impairment pattern for language-specific lesions (English-French > Chinese cross-impairment) independently recapitulates a known typological distance gradient, providing external validity for the language-specific circuit claim without requiring additional experiments. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly and honestly confront: (1) core ablation causes extreme perplexity inflation, so reduced neural predictivity could partly reflect global representational corruption rather than selective removal of brain-relevant computation — they recommend dose-response and matched-performance controls as future work; (2) "core" and "language-specific" are operational constructs tied to importance under specific fine-tuning objectives, not guaranteed architecturally pure; (3) the single-narrative, three-language design entangles language differences with translation and prosody properties; (4) the linear final-layer encoding model may miss nonlinear correspondences or layer-specific dynamics; (5) scanner heterogeneity (GE for English/Chinese, Siemens for French) coincides with French showing weaker alignment in some ROIs. This level of self-criticism is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 60.32% core lesion effect is uninterpretable without matched-degradation controls: ablating the same number of non-core parameters to achieve equivalent perplexity inflation would test whether the effect tracks structural specificity rather than competence loss. The current random-ablation control does not match perplexity, making it insufficient. - The 1% threshold is arbitrary; without a dose-response curve, it is unknown whether the effect is linear, threshold-gated, or whether 0.1% suffices — a question directly relevant to the "sparsity principle" conclusion. - Domain mismatch between importance estimation corpora (19th-century literary novels, classical Chinese texts) and the fMRI stimulus (The Little Prince, a 20th-century novella) is unacknowledged; parameters flagged as important may reflect genre- or register-specific processing, not language-general computation. - Machine-translated probing datasets for French and Chinese lack quality validation; Chinese syntactic probes are especially suspect given that tree depth and coordination are sensitive to segmentation conventions. - The study covers only three languages, two of which (English, French) are typologically close Indo-European languages; generalization of the "shared backbone with embedded specializations" conclusion to morphologically rich, agglutinative, or tonal languages beyond Mandarin is not established. - The language-specific lesion dissociation (diagonal vs. off-diagonal) is shown qualitatively in t-maps but the quantitative effect size of the matched vs. mismatched contrast is never reported, making it impossible to assess practical significance. Bottom Line A neuroscientist or computational linguist working on brain-language alignment will find the causal lesion logic and cross-architecture convergence genuinely valuable, but the catastrophic core-lesion confound — collapsing a model's language ability by factor ~560,000 in perplexity and then attributing reduced brain predictivity to specific information loss — is the paper's central unsolved problem, and the conclusion that a sparse core is causally necessary for alignment should be read as a strong hypothesis requiring the matched-degradation controls the authors themselves identify.
Recommended 2604.10627 PDF
#59 — Innovative (72)

cotomi Act: Learning to Automate Work by Watching You

Masafumi Oyamada, Kunihiro Takeoka, Kosuke Akimoto, Ryoma Obara, Masafumi Enomoto, Haochen Zhang, Daichi Haraguchi, Takuya Tamura · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces cotomi Act, a browser-based computer-using agent that pairs a context-efficient execution scaffold (achieving 80.4% on the 179-task WebArena human-evaluation subset) with a behavior-to-knowledge pipeline that passively converts user browsing into shared, editable organizational artifacts. - Why it's important: Enterprise browser agents fail as co-workers not because they can't execute tasks but because they lack tacit organizational knowledge — who approves what, what jargon means, what is already in progress — forcing constant clarification requests or risky silent guessing. - How it does it: Verbal-diff-based DOM change descriptions replace stale full accessibility trees in both current observation and execution history, while an agentic ETL pipeline segments and abstracts raw browsing events into structured wiki pages, task boards, and timelines that the agent retrieves on demand. - The good: 80.4% on the WebArena 179-task human-evaluation subset using only answer-format clarification hints — no site-specific UI/navigation hints that all prior top systems rely on — exceeds the reported 78.2% human baseline with manual re-verification of automatic scores. - The missing: The behavioral knowledge evaluation uses clean, successful agent-generated trajectories as a proxy for real user browsing; the pipeline's robustness to the failures, interruptions, and exploratory detours of actual users is entirely unvalidated. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers building enterprise-facing browser agents should read it; the verbal-diff observation design and the bidirectional shared workspace architecture are actionable findings, though the behavioral knowledge claims require a real-deployment follow-up.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (80) drives the score — a deployed browser-extension system, open-sourced WebArena-Mod harness, and concrete deployment-relevant design patterns; the verbal-diff scaffold and shared-workspace architecture are directly actionable. Rigor (65) is solid for execution (39-config ablation × 3 runs, manual score re-verification) but weak for the behavioral pipeline (no kappa, only 2 annotators, no RAG baseline, successful agent trajectories used as user-behavior proxy). Novelty (70) is meaningful — bidirectional human-editable shared workspace from passive observation is genuinely new. Non-Obviousness (70) supported by the coverage×format interaction finding. Completeness (60) and Resilience (60) limited by undisclosed production LLM, 4.7pp human-subset gap, and unvalidated real-user deployment. Weighted composite: 0.15*70+0.15*65+0.10*70+0.15*60+0.15*60+0.30*80 ≈ 69, rounded up for the strong central engineering contribution.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Browser agents deployed in enterprise settings face two compounding problems: (1) long-horizon web tasks generate context bloat that degrades LLM reasoning quality over many steps, and (2) every organization operates on tacit knowledge — unwritten approval chains, local jargon, pending work — that generic agents cannot access. RAG over enterprise document repositories does not solve this because the most relevant knowledge is never written down. The consequence is that agents must either interrupt users with constant clarifications or silently guess and risk irreversible actions. The state-of-the-art agent before this work reached 71.6% on WebArena, still below the 78.2% human baseline, confirming that execution reliability alone is unresolved. No prior system combines reliable execution with continuous organizational learning from observed user behavior. Key Idea The central insight is that two distinct problems — context bloat during execution and missing organizational knowledge — both benefit from differential representations of change rather than full-state snapshots. For execution, replacing full accessibility trees with natural-language verbal diffs of DOM changes ("New element: [42] button 'Submit'") gives the largest single accuracy gain (+12.7pp) while reducing history tokens 3.8×, because the agent needs to know what changed between steps, not the raw page at every step. For organizational knowledge, the analogous insight is that an LLM-based agentic ETL pipeline can abstract noisy raw browsing events into compact, semantically coherent procedural artifacts that are more useful than either raw traces or rigid rule-generated summaries, and that these artifacts should be first-class human-editable documents rather than opaque agent-internal memory. Methodology The execution scaffold follows a ReAct-style loop with four engineered mechanisms. (1) Adaptive lazy observation: by default sends only viewport-visible elements to the model, with the full accessibility tree fetched on demand; reduces end-to-end task time from 471s to 184s at comparable accuracy. (2) Verbal-diff history compression: stale full-observation history is replaced with the same verbal diff format used for current observation, cutting per-step tokens from 23.9K to 6.3K with +1.1pp accuracy. (3) Coarse-grained action space: element-level actions (e.g., scrollInto(element)) replace pixel-level operations to reduce step count and context accumulation. (4) Test-time scaling: best-of-N majority-voted action selection (N≤5) at each step, combined with model-delegated task decomposition into parallel sub-agents when multiple independent sub-goals are detected. These four choices are validated through a 39-configuration ablation on WebArena-Verified (82 tasks × 3 runs, Gemma-4-31B-IT). The behavior-to-knowledge pipeline operates in two phases: capture (site-level actions, tab transitions, periodic screenshots, diff-based deduplication) and agentic ETL abstraction (segment raw events into episodes by inactivity timeout → summarize each segment into structured records → aggregate into wiki pages, task items, and activity timelines). The agentic ETL design is compared against a fixed-rule pipeline via blind annotation by 2 annotators over 50 browsing sessions; the agentic variant is reported to produce more accurate and coherent artifacts. The shared workspace exposes three artifact types (activity timeline, task board, wiki) queryable by the agent via a search_workspace tool invoked on demand during ReAct reasoning. A PII-masking stage redacts personally identifiable information before artifacts are written. Key Results On WebArena (179-task human-evaluation subset): cotomi Act achieves 80.4% with test-time scaling (base scaffold alone: 76.5%), versus the reported human baseline of 78.2% and prior best agent OpAgent at 74.9% (both using site-specific hints; cotomi Act uses only answer-format hints). Full-benchmark scores: 75.7% (+TTS) and 74.4% (base). The 4.7pp gap between human-subset and full-benchmark performance suggests the 179-task human-evaluation subset is easier than the full 812-task benchmark. On behavioral knowledge (WorkArena-L1, 6 categories, 20 seeds per template): task success improves up to +10pp over the 51% zero-coverage baseline as behavioral knowledge accumulates, across all three artifact formats (trajectory, script, insight). Raw trajectories degrade at low coverage (–2pp at 17%) before becoming the strongest format at full coverage; scripts and insights are stable throughout. In the service-catalog domain specifically, access to 48 procedural scripts raises agent success from 75.0% to 99.2% (mean over 6 random orderings), confirming direct causal impact of the knowledge pipeline on task execution. ETL quality comparison: agentic vs. fixed-rule, 2 annotators, 50 browsing sessions — agentic wins, but no quantitative agreement metrics are reported. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The behavior-to-knowledge pipeline with a bidirectional human-editable shared workspace is the primary novel contribution — prior agent memory systems keep knowledge internal to the agent and do not learn from user browsing. The verbal-diff observation and history design is a novel instantiation of change-based representation (building on prior token-efficiency work) that delivers the largest single ablation gain. Achieving super-human WebArena performance with format-only hints (no site-specific guidance) is a new empirical result for the field. The execution scaffold components individually draw on known ideas (ReAct, best-of-N sampling, task decomposition); the contribution is their joint engineering for long-horizon web tasks. Rigor & Reproducibility The execution scaffold ablation is thorough: 39 configurations × 82 tasks × 3 runs with a fixed model (Gemma-4-31B-IT), producing the accuracy–token-cost Pareto chart in Figure 2. Manual re-verification of WebArena automatic judgments by 3 annotators is a genuine methodological strength. The WebArena-Mod evaluation harness is publicly released (github.com/nec-research-labs/WebArena-Mod). The behavioral knowledge evaluation has important limitations: it uses successful agent trajectories as a proxy for user behavior, explicitly acknowledged; the ETL quality comparison quantifies no inter-rater agreement metric; WorkArena-L1 navigation/menu tasks are excluded without reporting what fraction that removes or how results would change if included. Error bars are absent from Figure 4 (6 orderings per coverage point is a modest randomization). The cotomi Act system itself is a commercial NEC product and is not open-sourced; the specific production LLM powering the scaffold is not disclosed, so results may not generalize to other models. No comparison against a strong RAG baseline over enterprise documents appears, which is the most direct alternative approach. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs as a browser extension with backend services; no specialized hardware is required, though a capable LLM (model unspecified for production) is needed; deployment on existing enterprise infrastructure is plausible. - Efficient: Lazy observation cuts task time 2.6× (471s → 184s); verbal diff compression reduces history tokens 3.8×; best-of-N with N≤5 adds inference overhead that the paper does not quantify in wall-clock terms. - Effective: Evidence on WebArena (execution) and WorkArena-L1 (knowledge) shows strong proxy results, but no real-user deployment data exists; the behavioral knowledge pipeline has not been tested on genuinely noisy human traces. - Scalable: Tested on WebArena (812 tasks, 5 domains) and WorkArena-L1 (6 categories over a ServiceNow instance); enterprise-scale deployment with many users, large knowledge bases, or cross-domain knowledge is not evaluated and not claimed. The paper delivers a browser extension + backend system (commercial, not open-sourced) and the WebArena-Mod evaluation harness (open-sourced); no model weights or training code are released. Strengths - The verbal-diff observation design produces the largest single accuracy gain in the ablation (+12.7pp) while also being the mechanism that enables history compression; the insight that natural-language change descriptions outperform standard positional diff formats is well-supported and broadly applicable to any web agent context management problem. - Achieving 80.4% on the WebArena human-evaluation subset with only format-level hints — when all prior top systems use site-specific UI/navigation hints — is a strong and fairly compared result, with manual score verification that addresses a known weakness of the automatic evaluator. - The shared workspace design as "boundary objects" (artifacts interpretable by both user and agent on their own terms) solves a real alignment problem: agent inferences from observation alone are inevitably incomplete, and exposing artifacts for human curation provides a continuous correction loop without requiring explicit synchronization. - The coverage × format interaction finding (trajectories degrade at low coverage, stabilize at high; scripts and insights are stable throughout) is a practical and non-obvious design guidance result for anyone building knowledge distillation pipelines for agents. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that the behavioral knowledge evaluation uses successful agent trajectories as a proxy for real user behavior and that real traces contain failures, interruptions, and exploratory detours whose effect on the pipeline has not been tested. They acknowledge that the WorkArena-L1 study represents a controlled approximation requiring a full deployment study to validate. They note that raw trajectories at low coverage degrade performance and use this to motivate the abstraction pipeline. These acknowledgments are direct and credibility-positive for a 7-page demo/systems paper. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The production LLM powering cotomi Act is never disclosed; the ablation uses Gemma-4-31B-IT, but WebArena results are reported for an unnamed production model, making it impossible to assess reproducibility or whether the scaffold design is model-specific. - The WebArena human-evaluation subset (179 tasks) yields 80.4% while the full benchmark yields 75.7% — a 4.7pp gap suggesting the subset is not representative; claiming to exceed the human baseline should note this selection effect prominently. - The ETL quality comparison ("more accurate and coherent") between agentic and fixed-rule pipelines has no quantitative metric, no kappa statistic, and only 2 annotators — this is insufficient evidence to support a design decision at a systems level. - No comparison against a RAG baseline retrieves documents from a static enterprise knowledge base; this is the most natural alternative to the behavioral pipeline and its omission is a missing baseline. - The PII-masking stage is described in a single sentence with no evaluation of false-negative leakage rate, which matters for enterprise deployment. - WorkArena-L1 navigation/menu tasks are excluded from the behavioral knowledge evaluation; since these presumably require less procedural scripting, the reported gains may overstate the pipeline's benefit for the full task distribution. Bottom Line A practitioner building enterprise browser agents gains concrete, actionable design guidance from the verbal-diff scaffold ablation and the shared workspace architecture — the behavioral pipeline's value in real deployments remains unproven, but the problem framing and system design are the most coherent treatment of organizational knowledge for web agents published to date.
Citations 2605.03231 PDF
#60 — Innovative (72)

Differentiable parameter-less co-clustering using graph neural networks

link.springer.com · cluto - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces τANGO-CC, a fully differentiable, parameter-less co-clustering framework that uses graph neural networks to jointly optimize row and column cluster assignments in large-scale co-occurrence matrices without requiring the number of clusters as input. - Why it's important: All competitive parameter-less co-clustering methods (CCOT, PB-τCC) fail or run out of memory on datasets with more than ~10 million entries, leaving practitioners without scalable options for real-world document-term or user-item matrices. - How it does it: The data matrix is cast as a bipartite graph; GNN message-passing learns soft cluster embeddings that are jointly optimized end-to-end by differentiating through a smooth approximation of Goodman-Kruskal's τ association measure summed across all GNN layers. - The good: τANGO-CC is the only evaluated method that successfully completes on the ml-32 M dataset (200,948 rows × 84,432 columns, ~32 M nonzero entries), achieving τ ≈ 0.25 where all competitors either run out of 512 GB RAM or exceed 24 hours. - The missing: The embedding dimension d is an upper bound on the number of clusters and must be set a priori, which partially undermines the parameter-free claim; no sensitivity analysis over d is reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners working on scalable unsupervised learning on sparse co-occurrence matrices; those already satisfied with PB-τCC on small/medium data can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (78) strong — peer-reviewed DMKD, open-source code, 5-seed averaging, informative ablations on initialization and multi-layer loss. Novelty (70) — first parameter-less differentiable τ-optimizing co-clustering at scale, components individually known. Non-Obviousness (65). Completeness (68) — scalability validated only via internal metrics on unlabeled large data, no d-sensitivity study. Impact (78) — open-source, only method completing 32M-entry MovieLens, immediately useful. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*78 = 72.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 68 Resilience 72 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Co-clustering simultaneously partitions rows and columns of a data matrix, exploiting the reciprocal similarity between objects (e.g., users, documents) and features (e.g., movies, words). Applications include collaborative filtering, text mining, and bioinformatics. Prior parameter-less methods — the only category that avoids requiring the cluster counts as input — split between optimal transport (CCOT) and Goodman-Kruskal τ optimization (PB-τCC). Both exhibit super-linear scaling with matrix dimensions and exhaust memory or time budgets on matrices beyond ~10^7 entries. Parameter-requiring methods (BCOT, DeepCC) inherit the same limitation or require tuning cluster counts. The gap is a scalable, parameter-free approach that preserves the principled τ-based quality criterion. Key Idea Cast the co-clustering problem as end-to-end differentiable optimization over a bipartite graph: represent each object and feature as a node, encode co-occurrence weights as edges, use a GNN to compute soft cluster assignments as probability simplex vectors, and minimize (negative) Goodman-Kruskal's τ computed directly from the soft assignment matrices. Because τ is differentiable with respect to the soft assignments, and the GNN is differentiable with respect to its parameters and the initial embeddings, the entire pipeline is trainable with standard backpropagation — no discrete search, no enumeration. Methodology The input matrix M ∈ ℝ^{n×p} is converted to a bipartite graph G = (O ∪ F, E) where edges correspond to nonzero entries. Object and feature nodes are initialized with learnable d-dimensional embeddings h^(0). A stack of L GNN layers (GCN, GAT, or GIN, compared experimentally) performs message passing to produce per-node embeddings at each layer. At each layer ℓ, embeddings are shifted to be non-negative and softmax-normalized to obtain soft cluster assignment matrices P_O^(ℓ) ∈ ℝ^{n×d} and P_F^(ℓ) ∈ ℝ^{p×d} (d serves as the maximum cluster count). The relaxed joint distribution is R̃^(ℓ) = P_F^(ℓ)ᵀ M P_O^(ℓ) ∈ ℝ^{d×d}. The loss at each layer is L_τ^(ℓ) = −(α · τ_{X|Y}(R̃^(ℓ)) + (1−α) · τ_{Y|X}(R̃^(ℓ))), where α is dynamically reweighted by the relative magnitudes of the two τ directions to balance row and column quality. The total loss sums over all layers, a multi-layer supervision strategy analogous to early-exit networks. Hard assignments are extracted post-training via argmax. Three design decisions stand out: (1) summing loss across all layers rather than only the last layer is well-justified by the ablation in Table 5, consistently improving ARI and NMI; (2) PCA/SVD initialization is shown to improve external metric alignment vs. random initialization, though random initialization better captures intrinsic structure per τ; (3) the embedding dimension d is a user-set upper bound that implicitly constrains the cluster count — this is acknowledged but not fully resolved. Key Results Seven datasets spanning 10^5 to 10^10 matrix entries are used (cstr, tr41, classic3, k1b, ml-10 M, ml-20 M, ml-32 M). Baselines: CCOT, PB-τCC (both parameter-free τ-based), BCOT, DeepCC (parameter-requiring), and DMoN (GNN graph clustering). On small/medium datasets (cstr through k1b), τANGO-CC variants match or exceed all baselines; on cstr, τANGO_{GAT} achieves ARI = 0.76 ± 0.03 vs. PB-τCC's 0.76 ± 0.04 (essentially tied), and on classic3 τANGO_{GIN} achieves NMI = 0.93 ± 0.00 matching PB-τCC's 0.92 ± 0.01. The decisive result is at scale: CCOT and PB-τCC fail (OOM or timeout) on ml-10 M and above; τANGO-CC completes all three large datasets, recording τ_μ = 0.25 ± 0.01 on ml-32 M. Running time is competitive with PB-τCC on small datasets and substantially faster than CCOT on all datasets; PB-τCC remains faster on small data, but diverges steeply. Memory complexity for sparse data is O(|M_{≠0}| + L(n+p+d)d) vs. O(np + p²) for CCOT — a crucial advantage for the large sparse MovieLens matrices. The GIN variant slightly outperforms GCN and GAT on several benchmarks. No surprising counter-intuitive results are reported. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is the end-to-end differentiable formulation that directly optimizes Goodman-Kruskal's τ through GNN-based soft assignments on a bipartite graph representation of the co-clustering problem, achieving parameter-free operation at scale. The individual components (GNNs, τ as a quality measure, differentiable clustering, bipartite graph representations) are not new; the integration into a scalable, fully differentiable, parameter-less co-clustering framework is. Rigor & Reproducibility Two ablation studies are present and informative: initialization strategy (Table 4, PCA vs. SVD vs. random) and multi-layer loss vs. last-layer-only (Table 5). Five seeds are used with mean ± std reported throughout. No significance tests (e.g., paired t-tests) are reported. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/spideralessio/TANGO-CC, and data availability is confirmed. The paper is peer-reviewed (accepted DMKD, received October 2025, accepted April 2026). One methodological concern: the largest datasets (ml-10 M through ml-32 M) have no ground-truth labels, so external metrics (ARI, NMI) cannot be computed for them — scalability is validated only via internal metrics (τ, MI, MSR) and runtime, which could mask degenerate solutions. On small datasets where ground truth exists, the evaluation is thorough. The embedding dimension d = 16 is fixed across all experiments without a sensitivity analysis, which is a gap given that d bounds the cluster count. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 with 512 GB RAM CPU; training on ml-32 M completes within the 24-hour window, making it feasible on workstation-class hardware. - Efficient: Sparse memory complexity O(|M_{≠0}| + L(n+p+d)d) is substantially better than CCOT's O(np + p²); the GNN itself adds O(L(n+p+d)d) overhead, which is small for typical d=16. - Effective: On labeled benchmarks, achieves competitive or best ARI/NMI; on unlabeled large-scale data, achieves positive τ scores, but the absence of ground truth means effectiveness at scale is inferred, not directly measured. - Scalable: Tested up to ~200K rows × 84K columns with 32 M nonzero entries — a genuine stress-scale result. Scaling to 10× this size (billions of entries) is not tested and no complexity argument rules out GPU memory as a bottleneck. The paper delivers a software artifact (open-source GitHub repository with reproducible scripts). Strengths - Scalability to 32 M nonzero entries where all competitors fail is a concrete and practically significant result, not a marginal improvement. - Multi-layer loss supervision (summing τ across all GNN layers) is a well-motivated and empirically validated design choice that improves performance over last-layer-only training without adding parameters. - The framework supports three GNN backbones (GCN, GAT, GIN) and two initialization strategies, giving practitioners flexibility and a systematic comparison of architectural choices. - The use of Goodman-Kruskal's τ as the differentiable objective is principled: it is parameter-free by design (does not assume a fixed cluster count), and its connection to established co-clustering theory (Battaglia et al. 2024) is properly grounded. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that the embedding dimension d acts as an upper bound on the number of clusters and, while this does not break parameter-freeness (the exact count is inferred), it does influence the clustering outcome. They identify relaxing or automatically setting d as a direction for future work. This is stated plainly in the conclusion, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No sensitivity analysis on d: all experiments fix d=16 with 4 layers. Since d is the implicit cluster-count bound, practitioners need to know how sensitive results are to this choice before trusting the method on new datasets. - Large-scale evaluation uses no ground truth: the three MovieLens datasets report only internal metrics. A practitioner cannot verify that the algorithm is finding semantically meaningful co-clusters at scale rather than optimizing τ trivially (e.g., one large cluster). - Convergence and training time are not reported per dataset, only total runtime. It is unclear how much of the wall-clock time is GNN training vs. initialization vs. final assignment extraction. - The GIN backbone modestly outperforms GCN and GAT in several settings but no explanation or theoretical justification is offered for this — a missed opportunity given that GIN's 1-WL expressiveness is a well-understood property. - DMoN is included as a baseline but the paper correctly notes it targets graph clustering, not co-clustering; a more relevant comparison to subspace co-clustering methods (SC³) would strengthen the positioning. Bottom Line A practitioner dealing with large sparse co-occurrence matrices (document-term, user-item) who needs parameter-free co-clustering should read this paper — it is currently the only method that demonstrably scales past 10 million nonzero entries while maintaining principled τ-based quality, and the open-source implementation makes adoption straightforward.
Search Results
#61 — Innovative (72)

scHG: A supercell framework with high-order graph learning enables scalable multi-omics analysis

journals.plos.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces scHG, a two-stage graph-based framework that aggregates single cells into cross-omics-consistent "supercells" via second-order co-occurrence neighbors, then clusters those supercells with a modality-weighted Laplacian optimization to achieve scalable multi-omics clustering. - Why it's important: Existing graph-based multi-omics clustering methods carry O(n³) complexity, making them infeasible for datasets with tens of thousands of cells, and their coarse resolution routinely masks rare but biologically meaningful subpopulations such as dendritic cells and NK-like B cells. - How it does it: Cells are linked into supercells only when their local neighborhoods are persistently shared across all omics modalities (second-order co-occurrence), low-coherence peripheral cells are probabilistically pruned by degree centrality, and then a block coordinate descent optimizer alternately updates modality weights and supercell cluster assignments over the reduced graph. - The good: On the 30,672-cell Bmcite dataset all six primary baselines crash due to memory exhaustion, while scHG completes in ~18 minutes achieving ARI = 0.6732; on a 100,000-cell simulated dataset it achieves ARI = 0.9967, NMI = 0.9927. - The missing: The 100,000-cell scalability test is a single synthetic dataset with no competing baselines (all fail to run), so the atlas-scale claim rests entirely on simulated evidence with no real-world counterpart. - Read it or not: Worth reading for computational biologists building pipelines for CITE-seq or multiome data above 10,000 cells, especially for rare-cell discovery; less compelling for ML methodologists since Seurat WNN matches or beats it on the standard PBMC10× benchmark.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) strong — code + data + README released, deterministic across 20 runs, thorough 4-component ablation across 6 datasets, baselines run with ground-truth k while scHG estimates it (conservative). Novelty (72) — cross-omics second-order co-occurrence as supercell-defining criterion is a genuinely new and biologically motivated design. Non-Obviousness (70) — rare-cell rescue at supercell resolution is a real new empirical finding (cDC2, NK-like B cells). Completeness (68) — 100K-cell scalability only on a single synthetic dataset, Seurat WNN edge on PBMC10× deprioritized in presentation. Impact (78) — deployable on consumer laptop, immediately useful for CITE-seq pipelines >10K cells. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*78 = 72.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 68 Resilience 68 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Single-cell multi-omics profiling (RNA, surface proteins via ADT, chromatin accessibility via ATAC) provides complementary views of cellular identity, but integrating these heterogeneous layers for joint clustering poses two unsolved challenges. First, graph-based multi-view methods that capture higher-order cell-cell structure typically require eigen-decomposition or large matrix multiplications, incurring O(n³) complexity that renders them infeasible for datasets with more than ~10,000 cells. Second, rare cell populations — cDC2-like antigen-presenting cells, NK-like B cells — are outnumbered by dominant populations and their expression signals are diluted at conventional cluster resolution; no existing method explicitly enforces cross-omics neighborhood concordance as a criterion for defining an intermediate representation unit. The practical consequence is that standard pipelines on datasets such as the 30,672-cell Bmcite corpus exhaust memory and fail entirely, and rare populations remain invisible without prior labels. Key Idea The core insight is a redefinition of the intermediate aggregation unit: instead of compressing cells by single-omic similarity (as in MetaCell or SEACells), scHG defines a supercell as a group of cells whose local neighborhoods are stably co-occurring across all omics modalities simultaneously, captured by a second-order co-occurrence matrix that requires cell j to lie within cell i's α-nearest neighborhood and to share at least β neighbors with i in each omic layer. Cells that are topologically peripheral within each candidate component are probabilistically discarded via degree-centrality scoring before clustering. This cross-omics consistency criterion means that weak but coherent multi-modal signals — precisely those emitted by rare populations — are preserved as coherent supercell units rather than averaged into surrounding dominant populations, while the dramatic reduction in effective sample size (from n cells to S ≪ n supercells) eliminates the scalability bottleneck. Methodology Stage 1 (Supercell formation): For each of V omics layers, a Pearson correlation coefficient matrix PCC(v) is computed among all n cells. The γ nearest neighbors form a proximity-weighted similarity matrix A(v) (Eq. 6).
