A second-order co-occurrence matrix N(v) is binary: n_ij(v) = 1 iff j is within the α-nearest neighborhood of i and the two neighborhoods share ≥ β cells (Eq. 11). A cross-omics consistency matrix M fuses all layers: m_ij = 1 iff the count of omics supporting co-occurrence exceeds ⌊V/2⌋ (Eq. 12). Connected components of the graph induced by M form supercell candidates; degree-centrality-based probabilistic pruning (Eqs. 13–15) stochastically removes cells with normalized degree below the component consensus, yielding final supercell set {SC(s)}. Default hyperparameters: γ = 10 or 20 (feature-dimensionality adaptive), α = 5, β = 2. Stage 2 (Supercell clustering): The objective minimizes modality-weighted inter-cluster similarity over supercells: min_{E,ω} ∑_v ω(v) ∑_p ||L(v)e_p||₁, subject to a simplex constraint on inverse-weights and binary cluster indicators E ∈ {0,1}^{S×C} (Eq. 1/19), where L(v) is the supercell-level Laplacian of the aggregated similarity matrix AS(v). Block coordinate descent (BCD) alternates between: (a) closed-form update of modality weights ω(v) proportional to inverse square root of per-omic cluster cost (Eq. 20); and (b) greedy row-wise update of E by evaluating objective change for reassigning each supercell (Eqs. 22–26). Initialized with the parameter-free FINCH algorithm. Cluster count estimated without supervision via per-omic elbow-criterion v-means with modality-weighted consensus (Eq. 29). Total complexity O(n + |ε| + S²VT) ≈ O(n + |ε| + S²) for V ≪ S. Critical design decisions: (1) PCC angle-aware metric over Euclidean, justified by scale-invariance and validated in ablation with NMI gains up to +16.3%; (2) second-order co-occurrence for topology capture, supported by Recall_sc = 75.97% supercell purity; (3) probabilistic rather than deterministic pruning, justified empirically but lacking theoretical grounding. Key Results Benchmarked on six datasets (n = 77 to 30,672) against six primary baselines (GBS, scAI, SMSC, CGD, scMNMF, GSTRPCA) plus five additional baselines (MOFA+, Seurat WNN, Harmony+clustering, MultiVI, scVI). Primary metrics: ARI and NMI. PBMC10× (6,661 cells, 7 types): scHG ARI = 0.8774 / NMI = 0.8230 vs. CGD best at 0.8078 / 0.7458 among primary baselines; Seurat WNN edges it out at ARI = 0.8826 / NMI = 0.8314 in the additional comparison. PBMC_Inhouse (1,182 cells, 6 types): scHG ARI = 0.7029 / NMI = 0.7992 (best among all); MultiVI NMI = 0.8069 in the additional set. Bmcite (30,672 cells, 27 types): ARI = 0.6732 / NMI = 0.6917 — the only method to complete; all primary and additional baselines crash with memory errors. mESC (77 cells, 2 types): scHG ARI = 0.9373, best overall. PBMC_Cao (8,185 cells, 9 types): scHG ARI = 0.5299 / NMI = 0.5972 (best across all tested methods). Sim (500 cells, 4 types): ARI = 0.9646 (third; GBS and SMSC both reach 0.9881). Runtime on PBMC10×: scHG 13.8s vs. CGD 1,154s, scAI 14,772s (Table 5). On 100,000-cell simulated data: ARI = 0.9967, NMI = 0.9927; no baselines complete. Ablation gains for angle + pruning over Euclidean-only baseline: NMI +16.32% (PBMC10×), +10.53% (PBMC_Inhouse), +1.87% (Bmcite). Randomness analysis: zero variance across 20 runs on all datasets. Biological validation: Supercell 62 identified as cDC2-like (CD1C, CD1E, FCER1A, CLEC10A, HLA-DPA1 enriched), confirmed across GO, KEGG, Reactome, and MSigDB; signal lost when supercell excluded. Supercell 363 identifies an NK-like B cell (CD56+TIGIT+ in B-cell cluster), consistent with published NK-B cell literature. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The core novelty is the definition of supercells by cross-omics second-order co-occurrence neighborhood concordance — prior supercell methods (MetaCell, SEACells, PAGA) use single-omic similarity or task-specific heuristics and do not enforce cross-modality neighborhood consistency as a defining criterion. The modality-weighted BCD optimizer on supercell-level Laplacians combines known components (Laplacian-based clustering, BCD, PCC similarity) in a formulation tailored to the multi-omics supercell structure. The rare-cell detection capability is a genuinely new empirical finding: cDC2-like and NK-like B cell signals masked at the cluster level become significant at the supercell level. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and thorough: Table 9 reports 8 metrics × 6 datasets for 4 component combinations (Euclidean vs. angle-aware × no pruning vs. pruning), isolating each contribution. Zero variance across 20 independent runs (Table 10) confirms determinism despite stochastic pruning. No confidence intervals or significance tests are reported for the primary benchmark comparisons in Tables 3–4 and 7–8. Code (MATLAB + Python), datasets, and a step-by-step README are publicly released (github.com/anchor-hue/scHG; zenodo.org/records/18240578). All experiments run on a single consumer laptop (AMD Ryzen 7 4800H, 16 GB RAM, MATLAB R2022a); while this hardware level supports the practical-deployment claim, it is unusual for a benchmarking study to use non-standardized personal hardware, and runtime comparisons carry implicit platform assumptions. No concern about benchmark gaming: scHG estimates cluster numbers without ground-truth labels while several baselines receive the true count as input, making the accuracy comparisons conservative for scHG. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Yes — all experiments run on a 16 GB consumer laptop; a Python version is provided alongside the primary MATLAB implementation, and a step-by-step README with sample data is included. - Efficient: On large datasets scHG is the fastest method tested, completing PBMC10× (6,661 cells) in 13.8 seconds vs. hours for scAI; memory consumption is moderate and comparable to baselines that complete, while several baselines fail entirely on the 30,672-cell Bmcite dataset. - Effective: Achieves best or second-best ARI/NMI on five of six benchmarks and identifies biologically validated rare populations (cDC2, NK-like B cells) that cluster-level analysis misses; Seurat WNN slightly outperforms it on the headline PBMC10× dataset. - Scalable: Successfully processes a 100,000-cell simulated dataset (ARI = 0.9967), but this is a single synthetic benchmark with no real-world atlas-scale validation; the authors acknowledge that neighborhood graph construction and sparse matrix fusion will become demanding at millions-of-cells scale. Software artifact: MATLAB implementation and Python wrapper released at github.com/anchor-hue/scHG and github.com/anchor-hue/scHG_Python. Strengths - The cross-omics second-order co-occurrence criterion for supercell formation is a principled and biologically motivated design that directly targets the rare-cell masking problem, distinguishing scHG clearly from MetaCell, SEACells, and PAGA. - Ablation analysis is genuinely informative: isolating angle-aware metric and probabilistic pruning across 8 metrics and 6 datasets quantifies the marginal contribution of each component rather than treating the system as a black box. - Biological validation is multi-layered: GO, KEGG, Reactome, and MSigDB enrichment analyses converge on the same interpretation for Supercell 62 (cDC2), and the NK-like B cell finding is supported by positional context and published literature reference. - Practical scalability advantage is demonstrated compellingly on Bmcite, where the method works while all alternatives fail, not merely runs faster. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that atlas-scale datasets (hundreds of thousands to millions of cells) may push neighborhood graph construction, second-order co-occurrence computation, and sparse matrix fusion beyond practical limits on standard hardware, and propose GPU parallelization and distributed/mini-batch implementations as future work. They also flag that Supercell 363 (NK-like B cells) could in principle reflect technical confounders such as doublets or ambient RNA contamination, and explicitly recommend orthogonal validation (manual gating, independent reference mapping) before treating this as a confirmed rare population. These are direct, credibility-positive acknowledgments. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 100,000-cell scalability demonstration uses only a single simulated dataset; no real-world data at that scale is tested, and no competing method completes, so the scalability advantage at that regime is asserted rather than measured comparatively. - Seurat WNN outperforms scHG on PBMC10× (ARI 0.8826 vs. 0.8774; NMI 0.8314 vs. 0.8230) and MultiVI outperforms it on PBMC_Inhouse NMI (0.8069 vs. 0.7992); these results appear only in the Section 2.11 "additional baselines" discussion rather than being foregrounded in the main comparison, creating a mild presentation bias. - The mESC dataset (n = 77, 2 cell types) is too small to meaningfully stress any clustering method; results there neither support nor challenge the paper's claims. - The Python version's performance parity with the MATLAB implementation is asserted but not benchmarked; practitioners adopting the Python release cannot verify equivalence from the paper. - Runtime comparisons are conducted on the same physical machine for all methods but MATLAB R2022a has known performance differences across algorithms in ways not controlled for; true wall-clock fairness would require profiling at the operation level. - Supercell count S is an emergent output of the construction process and differs across methods, confounding runtime comparisons in ways the paper does not analyze. Bottom Line Practitioners building multi-omics clustering pipelines for CITE-seq or multiome data above 10,000 cells will find scHG directly useful — it runs where alternatives fail, delivers competitive or best-in-class clustering accuracy, exposes biologically meaningful rare populations, and ships reproducible code — but researchers seeking atlas-scale (1M+) or definitive superiority over Seurat WNN on standard benchmarks will need to wait for further validation.
Citations
#62 — Innovative (72)

TFM-Retouche: A Lightweight Input-Space Adapter for Tabular Foundation Models

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TFM-Retouche, a lightweight gated residual adapter that applies a small learned correction in input space to align downstream tabular data with the inductive biases of a frozen tabular foundation model (TFM), without modifying any pretrained weights. - Why it's important: Weight-space fine-tuning and LoRA-style PEFT of modern TFMs have empirically mixed or negative effects on already-strong zero-shot models, yet zero-shot ICL forgoes per-task accuracy and calibration gains that targeted adaptation can capture. - How it does it: A gated near-identity residual g_φ(x) = (1−α)⊙x + α⊙δ(x) is trained end-to-end through the frozen backbone with α initialized at 0.02, plus a post-training identity guard that per-fold routes inference back to the unmodified TFM whenever the adapter fails to improve held-out validation performance. - The good: TabICLv2-Retouche (T+E) reaches Elo 1651 on TabArena-Lite, +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base (1595), tops the 51-dataset leaderboard, and dominates the training-time Pareto frontier at 243s/1K samples — all under a 20× smaller HPO budget than every tuned competitor. - The missing: Only TabICLv2 is evaluated as the backbone; the ablation matrix reveals that the identity guard alone (without any learned transformation) contributes more pairwise wins (65–35 random-adapter-with-guard vs. trained-adapter-without-guard) than end-to-end optimization, leaving the mechanism of the lift underexplained. - Read it or not: Worth reading for tabular ML practitioners and TFM researchers — it provides the cleanest iso-backbone evidence to date that input-space residual nudging outperforms LoRA and full fine-tuning on modern TFMs, though readers wanting backbone-agnostic validation will find that missing.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (80) is the strongest dimension — the pairwise ablation matrix and iso-backbone, iso-HPO head-to-head against LoRA/SFT/BETA is unusually clean. However, the discovery that the identity guard contributes more wins than the trained transformation (Non-Obv 68) leaves the core mechanism underexplained, and single-backbone evaluation (only TabICLv2) caps Resilience (70) and Novelty (62) — combining residual adapters, gated connections, and near-identity init is a sound but incremental synthesis. Completeness (75) is good with honest disclosure of hardware and HPO asymmetries, though the 60× inference overhead is never confronted. Practical Impact (72) reflects promised but unreleased code. Weighted ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Tabular foundation models (TFMs) predict zero-shot through in-context learning over frozen weights, encoding a transferable synthetic prior that often rivals tuned tree ensembles on small-to-medium datasets. The residual gap is that per-task structure — domain feature semantics, monotonicities, dominant pairwise interactions — is not always captured by synthetic generators, so zero-shot ICL leaves accuracy and calibration on the table. Existing adaptation routes require either full fine-tuning (backprop through every backbone parameter, expensive) or PEFT methods such as LoRA (architecture-tied, requires per-model choices about which projections to wrap). Two recent empirical studies (Rubachev et al. 2025; Tanna et al. 2026) report mixed or negative effects of weight-space adaptation on already-strong TFMs, with the interpretation that gradient updates erode the synthetic-prior inductive bias. The prior input-space method BETA (Liu & Ye 2025) replaces rather than nudges the input with a fixed-width encoder designed for first-generation TabPFN's narrow capacity constraints, which destructs information on more capable modern TFMs. The problem is therefore to deliver per-task alignment cheaply, safely, and without requiring access to the TFM's internal architecture. Key Idea Adapt the input, not the weights, via a near-identity residual: g_φ(x) = (1−α)⊙x + α⊙δ(x), initialized at α=0.02 so the adapter starts as an approximate passthrough and the frozen TFM sees inputs near-identical to those it was pretrained on. The trainable gate α allows the adapter to suppress its correction channel-wise wherever it hurts, the inner block δ learns a small structured correction, and a post-training identity guard at the fold level selectively deploys the adapter only where it demonstrably improves held-out performance — making adaptation strictly opt-in per dataset per fold. Methodology The pipeline has five stages. (1) Preprocessing: numeric features are median-imputed and standardized; categorical features are ordinal-encoded and standardized to a common scale; datasets with d > 500 features get a fixed-rank trainable linear projection (orthogonally initialized) before the adapter. (2) Gated adapter: g_φ(x) = (1−α)⊙x + α⊙δ(x) where α ∈ ℝ^d is a per-channel learnable gate initialized at 0.02 (analogous to ReZero/LayerScale). Two inner-block variants for δ are studied: DCNv2 cross block (default), which applies L residual layers of Hadamard-product multiplicative interactions x_{l+1} = x_0 ⊙ (W_l x_l + b_l) + x_l with W_l = U_l V_l^T low-rank, modeling explicit polynomial feature interactions; and residual MLP block, which applies bottlenecked nonlinear projections x_{l+1} = x_l + W_l^2 σ(W_l^1 x_l + b_l^1) + b_l^2. Both use O(2dh) parameters per layer at low-rank ratio r. Critical design decisions: (i) near-identity initialization — justified as necessary to preserve the frozen TFM's pretrained inductive bias at the start of training; (ii) per-channel gate vs. scalar gate — scalar is a lower-capacity regularizer, but a controlled comparison is deferred; (iii) dimension-preserving residual — motivated by the shift from capacity-constrained first-generation TFMs to alignment-constrained modern TFMs, where replacing the input (as BETA does) destroys information the backbone can use. (3) End-to-end training through frozen backbone: AdamW or Muon optimizer with separate parameter groups for matrix weights, biases, and α; cross-entropy with label smoothing for classification, MSE for regression; multi-cycle cosine LR schedule; random train/query context split per epoch matching the ICL setting of the backbone. (4) Identity guard: after training, the guard scores the adapter path f_θ(g_φ(X)) vs. the raw TFM f_θ(X^raw) on the held-out AutoGluon fold using the deployment metric (1−AUC for binary, log loss for multiclass, MSE for regression); if the adapter does not improve by 0.5% tolerance, inference routes around the adapter. (5) Evaluation follows the TabArena-Lite protocol (Erickson et al. 2025): 8-fold bagged cross-validation, 51 datasets (binary/multiclass/regression), 748–150,000 samples, 5–1,777 features; HPO uses 11 configurations (1 default + 10 random draws), a 20× smaller budget than the 200-trial TabArena standard. Key Results On TabArena-Lite (51 datasets, 64-method universe, bootstrap 95% CIs): TabICLv2-Retouche (T+E) achieves Elo 1651 (±63/+85), +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base (1595), rank 1. TabICLv2-Retouche (T) achieves Elo 1632, rank 2, +37 over base. TabICLv2-Retouche (D) (default config, no HPO) achieves Elo 1608, rank 5, +13. The next non-Retouche entries are TabPFN-2.6 (D) at Elo 1627 and RealTabPFN-2.5 (T+E) at 1614. Training time for T+E is 243s/1K samples vs. RealTabPFN-2.5 (T+E) at 2060s and RealMLP (T+E) at 2792s. Inference time 22s/1K (T+E) vs. 0.35s/1K for frozen TabICLv2 (D). Critical-difference diagram (Friedman + Nemenyi post-hoc, α=0.05): TabICLv2-Retouche (T+E) achieves lowest mean rank (10.2) and sits in the top significance group; pairwise differences within this group (including TabPFN-2.6-D, RealTabPFN-2.5-T+E, TabDPT-T+E) are not statistically significant. Head-to-head against three iso-backbone adaptation baselines on 36 classification datasets (2 excluded for baseline OOM): Retouche wins 58–42 over frozen base; LoRA loses 39–61 to base; full SFT loses 39–61 to base; BETA loses 8–92. TALENT benchmark (170 classification datasets, default config only): TabICLv2-Retouche mean rank 3.83 vs. TabICLv2 base 4.05; 540 wins vs. 428 losses across 1,020 paired metric-dataset cells. Identity guard fires (routes to frozen TFM) on 32.3% of all runs; regression tasks show 14.4% mean fallback rate, binary classification 29.2%, multiclass 28.1%. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The gated near-identity input-space residual adapter for TFMs is not previously studied in this formulation — existing input-space work (BETA) replaces inputs with a fixed-width encoding rather than nudging them, and weight-space work (LoRA, full SFT) operates on backbone parameters. The identity guard as a per-fit deployment-time gating mechanism is also new in the TFM adaptation context. Individually, residual adapters, gated connections, near-identity initialization (ReZero/LayerScale), and DCNv2 cross blocks are established techniques; the contribution is their combination with a principled alignment framing and empirical demonstration that this approach dominates weight-space adaptation on modern TFMs. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are systematic and informative: the pairwise win-rate matrix (Figure 8) covers block type (cross, MLP, identity/random), gate variants (α_init+0.5, α=1, no-guard), and the random-adapter training ablation, allowing clean attribution of each design decision. The counterintuitive finding that the identity guard contributes more per-task wins than end-to-end training ([cross, random] beats [cross, no-guard] 65–35) is highlighted and supported. The head-to-head against LoRA, full SFT, and BETA uses an iso-backbone, iso-HPO-budget, iso-bagging protocol (Appendix F) across the same 11 configurations and 8-fold bagging, which is methodologically sound. Hardware asymmetry (Retouche on A10/A100; published baselines on H100) and HPO budget asymmetry (10 trials vs. 200) are both explicitly disclosed and treated as factors biasing against Retouche. Error bars (95% bootstrap CIs) are provided for all Elo scores. Code is promised as open-source but no URL is given in the preprint — reproducibility cannot be verified at submission. Evaluation is on a public benchmark (TabArena-Lite), not an author-private dataset, and the per-dataset HPO protocol does not directly optimize the aggregate leaderboard metric. This is a preprint. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on single NVIDIA A10 (24 GB) or A100 (80 GB); two of 51 TabArena tasks required A100 for the gradient-path baselines (LoRA, SFT) but not for Retouche itself; a fixed-rank projection handles d > 500 inputs within the backbone's input budget. - Efficient: Adds only 10²–10⁵ trainable parameters per dataset while leaving all backbone parameters frozen; training time 243s/1K samples for the T+E variant is 8–11× faster than RealTabPFN-2.5 or RealMLP T+E; inference time 22s/1K (T+E) is a ~60× overhead vs. frozen TabICLv2 default (0.35s/1K), driven by the bagged ensemble, not the adapter itself. - Effective: The identity guard activates on 67.7% of runs, confirming the adapter genuinely helps in the majority of cases; the ablation confirms the lift is real (random adapter with guard beats trained adapter without guard), meaning the guard is both load-bearing and correctly calibrated. - Scalable: Tested on datasets up to 150,000 samples and 1,777 features; no stress-scale evaluation beyond this is reported. TALENT adds breadth (170 datasets) but not scale. Claim of generalizability to other TFM backbones is not tested. The paper promises an open-source library release but provides no code URL. Strengths - The ablation pairwise win-rate matrix (Figure 8) is genuinely informative: it delivers a clean empirical decomposition showing the identity guard is the dominant contributor (65–35 over trained-but-unguarded adapter), the trainable gated residual is the second contributor (51–49 tie when gate frozen at 1), and removing both is catastrophic (8–16% vs. all opponents) — a level of attribution rare in tabular ML papers. - The iso-backbone, iso-HPO, iso-bagging head-to-head (Appendix F) demonstrating that LoRA and full SFT lose to the frozen base (39–61) while Retouche beats it (58–42) constitutes the strongest available evidence that weight-space adaptation actively hurts modern TFMs, not just fails to help. - The cross-block weight inspection (Appendix D) shows that 2 of the 15 largest learned feature interactions on the Taiwanese bankruptcy dataset recover known pairs from classical bankruptcy-prediction literature (Ohlson 1980, Altman 1968), providing a concrete interpretability example tied to domain knowledge. - The default-config (D) variant achieves Elo 1608 (+13 over base) with zero HPO cost, making the contribution accessible to practitioners who cannot run hyperparameter search. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) only TabICLv2 is benchmarked as a backbone — TabPFN-2.6 is excluded due to licensing, and extensions to other TFMs are left to future work; (2) a single shared HPO search space is used across classification and regression, even though HPO that helps one task type can hurt the other; (3) wall-clock times are measured on A10/A100 while published baselines used H100s, systematically biasing time comparisons against Retouche; (4) deliberate simplicity excludes PLR encodings, BatchEnsemble, and BETA-style bootstrap bagging at inference, which are expected orthogonal improvements. The authors characterize all results as lower bounds on what the framework can deliver under matched conditions, which is a credible framing. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The identity guard's dominance over end-to-end training raises an unasked question: would a well-calibrated selective predictor that deploys the frozen TFM vs. a simple per-task calibrated baseline (without any learned adapter) achieve comparable gains purely through selective deployment? This baseline is not evaluated and could partially explain the lift. - The "architecture-agnostic" claim is structurally validated but empirically untested for any backbone other than TabICLv2; the generalization to TabPFN-2.5/2.6, Mitra, LimiX, ConTextTab, or TabDPT is entirely unverified. - The TALENT benchmark evaluation (Appendix K) uses only the default configuration (not T+E), reports mean rank improvement from 4.05 to 3.83 — a much smaller effect than the +56 Elo headline — and uses only per-dataset paired wins rather than a full leaderboard protocol, making direct comparison with the main results difficult. - Regression tasks show the largest gains and lowest fallback rates; the paper does not investigate whether this reflects TabICLv2's known relative weakness on regression or a generalizable property of input-space adaptation, which matters for practitioners choosing when to deploy the adapter. - The 60× inference time overhead of the T+E ensemble (22s/1K vs. 0.35s/1K for frozen TabICLv2-D) is never discussed in the limitations section, despite being potentially prohibitive for latency-sensitive applications. Bottom Line Practitioners using TabICLv2 for tabular prediction gain a concrete, well-ablated, low-risk per-task lift from TFM-Retouche — and the paper simultaneously delivers the cleanest available empirical evidence that input-space residual adaptation beats weight-space fine-tuning on modern TFMs — but the single-backbone evaluation and the discovery that the identity guard does more work than the learned transformation leave the paper's core mechanism underexplained and its backbone-agnostic claims unsubstantiated.
Search Results 2605.06047 PDF
#63 — Innovative (72)

Edit-Based Refinement for Parallel Masked Diffusion Language Models

Houxing Ren, Mingjie Zhan, Zimu Lu, Ke Wang, Yunqiao Yang, Haotian Hou, Junting Pan, Hongsheng Li · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ME-DLM, an edit-based refinement framework that augments masked diffusion language models with a post-generation editing phase that applies minimal localized corrections (replacement, deletion, insertion) to fix sequence-level inconsistencies introduced by parallel multi-token decoding. - Why it's important: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) degrade sharply under aggressive parallel decoding because token-level training objectives model each position's marginal distribution independently, producing locally plausible but globally inconsistent outputs — a fundamental bottleneck limiting the practical efficiency advantage of diffusion over autoregressive generation. - How it does it: After standard mask diffusion produces a coarse draft, a second diffusion process predicts minimal edit operations conditioned on the full sequence; training supervision is derived from edit distance between model-generated drafts and ground-truth, providing a deterministic signal under a fixed canonicalization scheme, with the same shared model weights used for both phases. - The good: At 1/8 the total diffusion budget, ME-DLM Stage 3 built on LLaDA-8B achieves +11.6 points on HumanEval (25.0 vs. 13.4) and +33.6 points on GSM8K (63.8 vs. 30.2) over the Stage-2 baseline, with negligible inference overhead (<0.01s per case latency change). - The missing: All experiments use a single base architecture (LLaDA-8B) and Stage-3 training data overlaps with evaluation benchmarks (AM-DeepSeek-R1-0528-Distilled contains math/code distributions similar to GSM8K and HumanEval); no comparison against autoregressive models at matched wall-clock inference time. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers working on diffusion language models or parallel decoding efficiency should read this — it cleanly diagnoses a fundamental failure mode and delivers a practical, low-overhead fix with strong multi-benchmark evidence.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — clean new method (edit-based refinement for MDLMs) with non-trivial mechanism for inducing joint consistency. Rigor (70) — strong ablations, code released, but single-architecture (LLaDA-8B), no error bars, likely train/eval distribution overlap (AM-DeepSeek-R1-Distilled vs GSM8K/HumanEval) is a Goodhart concern. Non-Obviousness (75) — the edit-operator-induced coupling is a genuine insight. Completeness (70) and Resilience (70) — limitations acknowledged briefly, but no AR wall-clock comparison. Impact (80) — code+model released, plug-in fix. Composite: 0.20*70+0.25*70+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.15*70+0.10*80 = 71.75 → 72.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Masked diffusion language models generate text by iteratively unmasking token positions in parallel, enabling substantially faster decoding than left-to-right autoregressive models. However, when multiple tokens are generated simultaneously — e.g., 4 or 8 per step — quality degrades sharply even when all training sequences are valid. The root cause is a mismatch between marginal token prediction and joint sequence consistency: MDLMs are trained with token-level cross-entropy objectives that model each position's marginal distribution p(x_{0,i} | x_t) independently. During parallel decoding, tokens are selected simultaneously based on these marginals, implicitly assuming conditional independence across positions. This produces sequences that are locally plausible but globally inconsistent — mixing operands and results from different valid training examples (e.g., "2+2=5" by selecting "2" from one training sequence and "5" from another). Sequential or single-token decoding sidesteps this failure because left-to-right conditioning enforces inter-token dependencies, but this defeats the efficiency purpose of diffusion. Key Idea The central technical insight is a two-stage separation of the generation process: use standard mask diffusion to produce a coarse but semantically approximately correct draft, then reinterpret subsequent diffusion steps as an edit process that applies minimal localized corrections (replacement, deletion, insertion) conditioned on the entire current sequence state. Critically, even though edit predictions are factorized token-wise at the model level, their deterministic application through a shared edit operator A couples the decisions across positions — inducing sequence-level consistency through the application mechanics rather than through the prediction distribution itself. Training supervision for this edit phase is derived from edit distance between model-generated noisy drafts and ground truth, providing a deterministic signal through canonical shortest-edit-script selection. Methodology The pipeline has three sequential training stages, all using shared parameters on LLaDA-8B-Base. Stage 1 trains on Nemotron-Pretraining-SFT-v1 with standard masked diffusion, additionally predicting the next token at each position to enable edit modeling. Stage 2 fine-tunes on AM-DeepSeek-R1-0528-Distilled with the standard masked diffusion objective, producing the baseline. Stage 3 interleaves standard mask diffusion training with edit-based diffusion training: n parallel unmasking steps produce a noisy draft x^(n), then m edit-diffusion steps are applied with supervision computed from minimum edit scripts between x^(m) and ground truth x*, mapped to per-token (c_i, n_i) pairs where c_i is the replacement/deletion token (augmented vocabulary includes [DEL]) and n_i is the insertion token. The edit application operator A processes predictions left-to-right in a single pass: if c_i = [DEL], the token is removed; otherwise c_i replaces x_i^(t), and if n_i ≠ c_{i+1}, the predicted n_i is inserted immediately after position i. A curriculum over diffusion depth m starts at m=0 and gradually increases to expose the model to noisier intermediate states. At inference, 3/4 of the total step budget is allocated to mask diffusion and 1/4 to edit diffusion, with edit steps capped at 32. Three critical design decisions: (1) using model-generated intermediate states rather than rule-based noise, because random corruption doesn't match the structural error distribution of parallel decoding; (2) canonical edit-distance training signal for deterministic supervision despite multiple equivalent shortest scripts; (3) fixed step-budget split validated to be robust across α ∈ {0.25, 0.5, 0.75}. Key Results Evaluated on HumanEval, HumanEval+, MBPP, MBPP+ (code generation, Pass@1), GSM8K, MATH-500 (math, accuracy), MMLU, ARC-C, TruthfulQA (generalization) under fixed total diffusion step budgets (1/1, 1/2, 1/4, 1/8 of full steps). Compared against LLaDA-Instruct, ME-DLM Stage 2, Soft Mask, and EvoToken. At budget 1/1: ME-DLM Stage 3 achieves 57.9 HumanEval vs. 44.5 LLaDA-Instruct (+13.4), 84.8 GSM8K vs. 71.9 (+12.9), average 60.0 vs. 45.3 (+14.7); competitive with Soft Mask (57.8 HumanEval, 84.0 GSM8K). At budget 1/8: ME-DLM Stage 3 achieves 25.0 HumanEval vs. 12.2 (+12.8), 63.8 GSM8K vs. 50.3 (+13.5), average 32.6 vs. 20.9 (+11.7) — and +11.6 HumanEval / +33.6 GSM8K over Stage 2 specifically. Gains consistently grow as budget decreases, confirming that edit refinement is most valuable under aggressive parallelism. Generalization (Table 5, budget 1/8): MMLU improves from 21.7 to 56.2, ARC-C from 26.0 to 69.2, TruthfulQA from 19.5 to 46.9. Inference overhead is negligible: per-case latency changes by ≤0.01s across edit-step allocations; throughput stable at ~42 tokens/sec (512 steps) and ~340 tokens/sec (64 steps). Baselines Soft Mask and EvoToken are recent (2025–2026), making comparisons meaningful. The paper is peer-reviewed at ICML 2026. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine contribution is the edit-based diffusion formulation for masked diffusion LMs: training a diffusion process to predict discrete edit operations (replace/delete/insert) via edit-distance supervision over model-generated drafts, with the insight that a deterministic edit-application operator induces sequence-level consistency from factorized token predictions. Individual components — edit distance, coarse-to-fine generation, masked diffusion training, parallel token prediction — are all prior art. What is non-obvious and non-trivial is the specific coupling of edit-application mechanics to address the marginal/joint mismatch under a fixed diffusion budget, and the training recipe requiring model-generated (not rule-based) intermediate states. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly released at https://github.com/renhouxing/ME-DLM. Ablations cover refinement-state construction (Table 6: mask-only vs. rule-based noise vs. full Stage 3), step allocation (Figures 3–4 and Table 4), parameter sensitivity (Table 7: α ∈ {0.25, 0.5, 0.75}, β ∈ {0, 0.5}), convergence of edit diffusion (Table 3), and inference efficiency (Table 4). No error bars or significance tests are reported — single-run evaluation is the norm in LLM papers but limits statistical confidence. Training compute is stated (64 H800 GPUs, Stage 3 ~60h). One methodological concern: Stage 3 trains on AM-DeepSeek-R1-0528-Distilled, which is a distilled reasoning dataset likely overlapping in distribution with GSM8K and HumanEval; data contamination analysis is absent. A second concern: all experiments use LLaDA-8B exclusively; no cross-architecture or cross-scale validation. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Inference runs on standard GPU hardware (H800 or equivalent); the edit refinement step adds only parallel forward passes with no additional model parameters, making deployment straightforward. - Efficient: Edit diffusion introduces ≤0.01s per-case latency overhead and maintains constant throughput (~42 tok/s at 512 steps, ~340 tok/s at 64 steps) across all tested mask/edit allocations, measured on H800 GPUs. - Effective: Consistent gains across code generation (HumanEval, MBPP), mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH-500), and general knowledge (MMLU, ARC-C, TruthfulQA) under all tested budgets confirm the method addresses real generation failures rather than proxy metrics. - Scalable: Tested only at 8B parameters on one architecture; the edit-step cap of 32 is fixed and not ablated at higher ceilings; no evidence provided for behavior at 70B+ or with other MDLM families. The work delivers a software artifact (GitHub code release) and a trained model (ME-DLM Stage 3). Strengths - The marginal/joint mismatch diagnosis is formalized clearly and illustrated with a concrete arithmetic example ("2+2=5"), making the failure mode precise and independently verifiable rather than just an empirical observation. - Edit-distance training supervision is deterministic under canonicalization, requiring no reward model, preference data, or verifier — a principled lightweight solution that avoids the complexity of RLHF-style post-training. - The ablation demonstrating that model-generated intermediate states are strictly necessary (rule-based noise performs worse than even mask-only states on math tasks) reveals a non-obvious finding about the importance of matching the training distribution to the actual error distribution of parallel decoding. - The negligible inference overhead is demonstrated with rigorous wall-clock and throughput measurements rather than just FLOP estimates, making practical deployment claims credible. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The conclusion mentions exploring training-free or lightweight alternatives to enforce sequence-level consistency as future work, implicitly acknowledging the non-trivial training cost of Stage 3 (60 GPU-hours on 64 H800s). The impact statement notes the contribution is methodological with no new capabilities beyond generation quality improvement. The authors do not explicitly discuss failure modes, sensitivity to the base model quality, or behavior outside the tested task domains. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All experiments use LLaDA-8B exclusively; no evidence that the three-stage training pipeline transfers to other MDLM architectures (Dream-7B, Seed-Diffusion) or different parameter scales, making the generality claim unverified. - No comparison against autoregressive models at matched wall-clock inference time — the central practical question of whether MDLM+editing is competitive with GPT-style models at fixed latency budget goes unanswered. - Training data (AM-DeepSeek-R1-0528-Distilled) likely overlaps in distribution with GSM8K and HumanEval; data contamination is not characterized, raising Goodhart's Law concerns for the largest reported gains. - The edit-step ceiling of 32 is fixed without ablation beyond this cap; whether performance saturates, improves, or degrades with more edit steps at larger budgets is unknown. - Evaluation uses pass@1 only for code tasks; no pass@k or sampling temperature analysis, leaving open whether edit refinement improves output diversity or only greedy-decoding quality. Bottom Line Practitioners building or evaluating masked diffusion language models should read this paper — it delivers a principled, low-overhead fix to the most pressing quality degradation failure mode in parallel multi-token generation, with consistent and substantial gains across diverse benchmarks at the regime (low step budgets) where the problem is worst.
Recommended 2605.09603 PDF
#64 — Innovative (72)

Warp-as-History: Generalizable Camera-Controlled Video Generation from One Training Video (34 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Warp-as-History, a camera-control method that converts a target camera trajectory into a camera-warped pseudo-history and injects it through a pretrained video diffusion model's native visual-history pathway, enabling camera-following without camera-specific modules or large-scale annotated training data. - Why it's important: Existing camera-controlled video generation methods require post-training on tens of thousands to hundreds of thousands of camera-annotated videos, making them expensive to adapt; Warp-as-History achieves comparable performance with a single annotated training video, drastically lowering the resource barrier. - How it does it: Camera-induced warps are positionally aligned to target-frame RoPE indices and filtered by a visibility mask before being inserted into the model's existing history token stream, exploiting the pretrained history-continuation behavior as a latent camera-control channel. - The good: After LoRA finetuning on one video (~1 hour on a single A800 GPU), the method achieves best-in-class FID/FVD and DOVER visual quality on DAVIS (68.18/57.95 FID/FVD, DOVER 0.448) while maintaining camera-following accuracy competitive with baselines trained on ~90K–632K clips. - The missing: The one-shot source video (car-roundabout) was selected via a retrospective sweep and is acknowledged to be high-performing but not the best overall, meaning the headline result is partially post-hoc; scaling beyond one video shows non-monotonic improvement, leaving the few-shot regime poorly understood. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on efficient video generation control or on exploiting latent capabilities of pretrained video diffusion models; less urgent for practitioners who already have large camera-annotated datasets and are using full-scale training pipelines.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (82) is the strongest dimension — the insight that a frozen history-conditioning pathway latently encodes camera-following when warps are RoPE-aligned to target frames is genuinely surprising and not predicted by prior work. Novelty (78) — routing warps as pseudo-history with target-frame positional alignment is a clean new control interface. Rigor (62) is the main drag: the headline one-shot result depends on retrospective selection of car-roundabout from an 11-clip sweep, baselines are reproduced from published outputs rather than re-run, no variance estimates, non-monotonic few-shot scaling, and the Helios backbone is itself an unpeer-reviewed preprint. Completeness (72) — limitations are stated directly without minimization. Resilience (65) — the source-selection bias and uneven camera-error performance vs. Gen3C are real vulnerabilities. Practical Impact (72) — code+LoRA released, 1-hour single-A800 finetuning is a meaningful efficiency win. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*72 = 71.6 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Camera-controlled video generation — generating a video that follows a prescribed viewpoint trajectory — currently requires either (a) post-training on large camera-annotated corpora via dedicated camera encoders, control branches, or attention/positional-encoding modifications, or (b) test-time optimization and denoising-time guidance that adds inference latency. Neither approach is cheap. The underlying question is whether an existing history-conditioned video generation model already possesses latent camera-following behavior that can be exposed through a minimal, architecture-neutral interface, avoiding new modules or large annotated datasets entirely. Key Idea: The core insight is that a pretrained video diffusion model's visual-history pathway — designed to condition future frame generation on observed past frames — can also interpret camera-induced geometric evidence if warped frames are presented as pseudo-history aligned to target-frame temporal positions, with unreliable (invisible) warp tokens dropped to mimic naturally incomplete histories. Instead of teaching the model what a camera trajectory means, the method teaches it to read the trajectory as if it were already-observed prior footage from the target viewpoint. Methodology: The pipeline has three components. First, camera-warped pseudo-history construction: given a target camera trajectory C = (c₁,...,cT), an off-the-shelf reconstruction model (π³) renders the scene at each target viewpoint, producing a warp video W_C. The warp is packed into history tokens using the backbone's native history construction operator H. Second, target-frame positional alignment: rather than assigning warp tokens their natural (past) RoPE positions, each warp token receives the RoPE index of its corresponding target (future) frame, causing the model to treat the warp as spatially aligned to the frame being denoised rather than as generic past context. This is the critical design decision that immediately activates zero-shot camera-following; ablations confirm it is necessary. Third, visible-token selection: a warp validity mask M_C is computed and tokens with insufficient support are dropped from the history stream, leaving disocclusion and newly visible regions to the model's generative prior. The frozen-model interface produces measurable zero-shot camera-following; lightweight LoRA (rank 32, α=32) trained for 1000 iterations on one separate camera-annotated video stabilizes behavior without test-time optimization. The LoRA update is applied only in the first (lowest-resolution) Helios stage; higher-resolution refinement stages are unchanged. The training loss is the standard video generation objective. No new camera branches, guidance losses, or sampling-time constraints are introduced. Key Results: On WorldScore (static scenes), Camera Control rises from 26.42 (text-only Helios-Distilled) to 61.32 (zero-shot) and 62.00 (one-shot), 132%/134% relative gains; Subjective Quality rises from 47.37 to 54.83 with one-shot finetuning. On DAVIS (dynamic videos with foreground motion), Warp-as-History achieves FID/FVD of 68.18/57.95 and DOVER of 0.448, both best across all compared methods (Gen3C trained on ~90K videos: FID/FVD 72.71/64.98, DOVER 0.326; Voyager: 90.68/80.28, 0.371; ViewCrafter: 76.54/65.76, 0.507). Camera adherence metrics on DAVIS: R-Err 2.97, T-Err 0.0942 (Gen3C leads with R-Err 2.24, T-Err 0.0663). On RE10K (static real estate, out-of-distribution for the one-shot source), FID/FVD of 22.11/17.92 (best), DOVER 0.442 (best), though camera metrics trail Gen3C. Ablations (Table 5) confirm that removing positional alignment (NoAlign) increases R-Err from 3.41 to 7.33 and T-Err from 0.1336 to 0.1343; removing visible-token drop (NoVisDrop) degrades VisLPIPS from 0.274 to 0.341 in zero-shot. Few-shot sensitivity (Table 6): the clearest gain is zero-to-one video; adding 3–12 videos yields non-monotonic changes, with PSNR ranging 15.04–15.89 vs. 16.02 for one video. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The technical move of routing warps through a pretrained history pathway with target-frame positional alignment is genuinely novel as a camera-control interface — prior work uses warps as rendering targets, guidance signals, or explicit conditioning inputs, not as pseudo-history evidence. The empirical finding that a frozen history-conditioned model exhibits measurable zero-shot camera-following when presented with aligned warp history is independently noteworthy and not an obvious prediction from prior literature. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative (Table 5, Figure 6), isolating the contribution of positional alignment and visible-token selection. The training/test split is explicitly disjoint. However, the primary one-shot source video (car-roundabout) was selected via a retrospective sweep across 11 DAVIS clips (Appendix D, Tables 9–11) and is acknowledged as high-performing but not the best; this introduces selection bias into the headline results. The paper does not report multiple runs or variance estimates. Code is publicly available at github.com/yyfz/Warp-as-History, and a project page is provided. The backbone (Helios) is a preprint (arXiv:2603.04379, 2026), not yet peer-reviewed, meaning the base model's capabilities are not independently validated. The paper is explicitly labeled as a preprint. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A800 GPU; finetuning takes ~1 hour and inference requires the warp preprocessing step plus the standard video sampler. - Efficient: Warp-as-History adds 4.62–7.81s end-to-end overhead per 33-frame chunk vs. the native sampler (15.83s baseline), with the overhead dominated by the longer transformer sequence from warp tokens; visible-token dropping reduces this meaningfully (7.81s at 86% visibility vs. 4.62s at 47%). - Effective: Evidence shows the method solves camera-following in realistic dynamic video (DAVIS) at quality levels meeting or exceeding large-scale trained baselines on visual quality metrics; camera adherence is competitive but does not uniformly lead. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 33-frame chunks with the standard Helios rollout; long-video generation relies on the backbone's autoregressive rollout, and camera error accumulation over long sequences is not analyzed. The paper does not test at larger spatial resolution. The software artifact is the LoRA weights plus the inference wrapper; no standalone library is released. Strengths: - The identification of the history-conditioning pathway as a camera-control channel is a conceptually clean and practically consequential insight: it requires no architectural changes, enabling zero-shot behavior from a frozen pretrained model, which the paper convincingly demonstrates. - The one-training-video result is remarkable for its resource efficiency: 1 video → 4 clips vs. 90K–632K clips for strong baselines, with competitive camera adherence and superior visual quality on DAVIS. - The ablation study is well-constructed and directly supports the two key design decisions (positional alignment and visible-token selection), with both qualitative and quantitative evidence. - The paper is transparent about the diagnostic framing of one-shot finetuning — treating it as evidence that the behavior is latently pretrained rather than newly learned — which is an honest and testable scientific claim. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) dependence on reconstruction model quality — warp construction inherits geometry estimation errors, visibility errors, and disocclusion artifacts; (2) the history interface itself adds runtime overhead (4.62–7.81s per chunk); (3) generalization is bounded by the backbone's pretrained capacity to preserve dynamics and complete unobserved content; (4) LoRA can stabilize but not fully overcome backbone limitations. These are stated directly in Appendix F without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The retrospective source-video selection (car-roundabout chosen after sweeping 11 candidates) is a real methodological concern: the headline one-shot result reflects a partially optimized configuration, not a blind protocol, and the non-monotonic few-shot scaling makes it unclear whether a practitioner choosing a different source video would reproduce the paper's gains reliably. The baselines (Gen3C, Voyager, ViewCrafter) are reproduced from their published outputs rather than re-run under a common protocol, making subtle evaluation mismatches possible. The comparison with HyWorldPlay (Table 2) uses a different evaluation protocol (VBench on 30-second trajectories) from the main WorldScore table, preventing direct comparison across all baselines. Camera adherence on DAVIS trails Gen3C on R-Err and T-Err despite stronger visual quality, and the paper does not analyze when camera control fails or what scene properties predict failure. Long-video behavior beyond the first 33-frame chunk is not analyzed. Bottom Line: Practitioners working on camera-controlled video generation should read this paper: it demonstrates a resource-efficient, architecture-neutral control interface with a genuine conceptual contribution and competitive empirical results, though the source-video selection bias and non-monotonic few-shot scaling warrant caution before treating the one-shot claim as a robust general recipe.

#65 — Innovative (71)

Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning (66 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SDAR (Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning), a training method that combines GRPO-based reinforcement learning with token-level gated On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) for multi-turn LLM agents, using a sigmoid gate to selectively amplify positive teacher endorsements and attenuate unreliable negative signals. - Why it's important: Naively combining RL with OPSD for multi-turn agents causes catastrophic training instability — KL divergence surges and task performance collapses — because compounding trajectory drift makes privileged teacher guidance increasingly unreliable over turns; this work directly addresses that failure mode. - How it does it: The method converts the teacher-student log-probability gap per token into a bounded [0,1] sigmoid gate (detached from the gradient graph), which modulates the OPSD loss token-by-token so that only teacher-endorsed tokens receive strong distillation pressure while negative-gap tokens are softly suppressed. - The good: Even with random (zero task-awareness) skill retrieval, SDAR still outperforms the GRPO baseline (+1.9/+1.6/+1.0 on ALFWorld/WebShop-Score/WebShop-Acc), demonstrating the gain stems from the gating mechanism rather than retrieval fidelity. - The missing: All three benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop, Search-QA) are relatively narrow in action-space complexity and horizon length compared to real-world agentic deployments; the method is not evaluated on GUI, coding, or long-horizon open-world tasks where multi-turn drift would be most severe. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners training multi-turn LLM agents with RL who want a principled, plug-in auxiliary distillation objective; the ablations and theoretical propositions are tight and the robustness analysis is unusually honest.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) is the strongest dimension — every design choice (gating type, β, λ, KL direction) is ablated, and the random-retrieval robustness experiment cleanly isolates the gating mechanism as the source of gains; missing error bars on the 128-task WebShop validation set is the principal weakness. Novelty (70) — detached sigmoid gating of per-token OPSD with five supporting propositions is a genuinely new mechanism. Non-Obviousness (75) — diagnosing asymmetric trust in privileged guidance as a failure mode distinct from drift instability is a non-obvious analytical contribution. Completeness (65) and Resilience (65) limited by narrow benchmark scope (short horizons, restricted action spaces) relative to the multi-turn-drift motivation. Practical Impact (75) — public code on GitHub and consistent multi-scale gains. Composite ≈ 0.20·70 + 0.25·75 + 0.15·75 + 0.15·65 + 0.15·65 + 0.10·75 = 71.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Post-training LLM agents with reinforcement learning gives coarse trajectory-level supervision: a single reward signal covers dozens of sequential decisions, providing no per-token credit assignment. On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) fills this gap by extracting dense token-level guidance from a privileged teacher branch (the same policy augmented with reference answers or retrieved skills not available at inference time). However, two problems arise in multi-turn agentic settings. First, once the student policy drifts from the teacher trajectory, token-level supervision becomes misaligned — the KL divergence between teacher and student explodes across turns (measured empirically: per-turn KL surges and task performance collapses to near-zero on Search-QA for Qwen3-1.7B). Second, the privileged teacher's guidance is asymmetrically trustworthy: when the teacher assigns higher probability than the student (positive gap), it endorses behavior the student nearly generates, a reliable signal; when it assigns lower probability (negative gap), the cause could be bad student behavior but also could be irrelevant or redundant retrieved skills, skill grounding failures, or multi-turn drift amplification. On Qwen2.5-3B, over 50% of tokens have negative teacher-student gaps, making undiscriminating distillation actively harmful. Key Idea: The core trick is to wrap the per-token teacher-student log-probability gap Δ_t = log π_T(y_t | s_t⁺) − log π_θ(y_t | s_t) in a logistic sigmoid gate g_t = σ(β·Δ_t), detach it from the gradient graph, and use it to weight the per-token OPSD loss. This single operation implements an asymmetric curriculum automatically: positive-gap tokens (teacher more confident than student) receive amplified distillation; negative-gap tokens receive soft attenuation proportional to the discrepancy magnitude, rather than either full trust or full suppression. The gate is self-paced — no schedule, threshold, or external curriculum is needed. Methodology: SDAR operates as a two-component joint training objective: L(θ) = L_GRPO(θ) + λ_SDAR · L_SDAR(θ), where L_GRPO is the standard clipped surrogate policy gradient with group-relative advantages (GRPO), and λ_SDAR = 0.01. The GRPO component is left completely unmodified, preserving advantage unbiasedness. The SDAR component computes, for each sampled token y_t in each rollout, the detached gap Δ_t and gate g_t = σ(5·Δ_t) (β=5 chosen by ablation), then applies the per-token loss ℓ_t^SDAR = g_t · (log π_θ^+(y_t | s_t⁺) − log π_θ(y_t | s_t)). The aggregate L_SDAR is a masked average over valid response tokens. Three gating variants are introduced and ablated: entropy gating (g_t = σ(β·h_t), targeting uncertain positions), gap gating (g_t = σ(β·Δ_t), the default), and soft-OR gating (g_t = σ(β·[1−(1−h_t)(1−Δ_t)]), combining both signals). Gap gating consistently wins. The reverse-KL direction for the distillation objective is justified by its mode-seeking property: it naturally down-weights tokens where teacher probability is low, complementing the gating mechanism; forward-KL (mode-covering) and JSD perform measurably worse. Skills are retrieved from SkillBank via UCB (multi-armed bandit over historical task rewards), keyword matching, full retrieval, or random selection — all four are tested. Key Results: On ALFWorld (3,827 task instances, text-based embodied agent), SDAR achieves 84.4% success on Qwen2.5-3B vs. GRPO's 75.0% (+9.4%), 85.9% on Qwen2.5-7B vs. 80.9% (+5.0%), and 53.9% on Qwen3-1.7B vs. 46.1% (+7.8%). On Search-QA (NQ, TriviaQA, PopQA in-domain; HotpotQA, 2Wiki, MuSiQue, Bamboogle out-of-domain), SDAR achieves 68.0% (3B) and 82.8% (7B) average accuracy vs. 63.3% and 72.6% for GRPO. On WebShop (128 tasks, web shopping), Score/Acc: 53.9/58.6 (3B) and 89.4/82.8 (7B) vs. 80.9/63.3 and 87.4/77.3 for GRPO. Compared to hybrid baselines Skill-SD and RLSD, SDAR consistently outperforms or ties across all three model scales and three benchmarks. GRPO+OPSD (naïve combination) catastrophically fails on Qwen3-1.7B (32.0 vs. 46.1 for GRPO alone). Skill-GRPO, which injects skills only at inference, drops sharply without skills (60.2 → 80.5 on ALFWorld-3B), while SDAR without any inference skills matches or exceeds Skill-GRPO with skills (84.4 vs. 80.5), demonstrating genuine knowledge internalization. Random retrieval still outperforms pure GRPO, confirming the gate filters retrieval noise. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The specific contribution is the detached sigmoid gating mechanism for selectively weighting per-token OPSD signals within a joint RL+distillation objective. The identification of asymmetric trust in privileged guidance as a distinct failure mode (separate from multi-turn drift instability) is a genuine analytical contribution. The combination of GRPO + OPSD is not novel; the insight that the raw OPSD loss must be subordinated and token-selectively gated — rather than simply added with a fixed weight — is the genuine novelty. The theoretical analysis (5 propositions covering equivalence to weighted log-likelihood, gradient boundedness, monotonicity, gradient explosion prevention from detachment) is clean and directly motivates each design choice. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for all critical design choices: gating strategy (entropy vs. gap vs. soft-OR, Fig. 6), sharpness β ∈ {0,1,5,10} (Fig. 7), distillation weight λ ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1} (Fig. 8), and distillation objective type (reverse-KL, forward-KL, JSD, Fig. 9). Each ablation is run on at least one model scale with training curves reported. The robustness analysis across four skill retrieval qualities (UCB, KM, Full, Random) directly addresses the main practical concern about retrieval dependency. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/ZJU-REAL/SDAR. No error bars or significance tests are reported for the main Table 1 results — this is a meaningful gap given the small WebShop validation set (128 tasks). Training runs 150 steps on 8 H800 GPUs; the paper does not report total compute or wall-clock time. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.15155, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on 8 H800 GPUs for 150 training steps on 3B–7B models; the compute requirement is within reach of academic labs and mid-tier industry teams. - Efficient: SDAR adds one teacher forward pass per rollout token (same model, different context) and a scalar gate computation per token; the paper does not quantify the wall-clock overhead vs. GRPO, which is a gap. - Effective: Evidence shows it solves the specific instability problem (GRPO+OPSD collapses; SDAR does not) and achieves consistent multi-benchmark gains; the internalization evidence (matching skill-augmented inference without inference skills) is concrete. - Scalable: Tested at 1.7B–7B parameter scales; no experiments at 30B+ or with longer-horizon tasks (>50 turns) where cumulative drift would be more severe; scalability is claimed but not measured. The work delivers a training algorithm with public code; no pretrained checkpoints are mentioned as released. Strengths: - The diagnostic analysis of the two failure modes (multi-turn OPSD instability and asymmetric privileged guidance) is concrete and empirically grounded — the mean negative-gap frequency (~50%+) motivates the asymmetric treatment directly. - The sigmoid gate is theoretically clean: Propositions 1–5 collectively show it yields bounded gradients, preserves gradient direction (only scales magnitude), and avoids self-referential instability from non-detachment. - The robustness experiment (random retrieval still beats GRPO) credibly isolates the gating mechanism as the source of gains, not retrieval quality. - Testing across three model families and three model scales (Qwen2.5-3B/7B, Qwen3-1.7B) with consistent results strengthens the claim of general applicability. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not include a dedicated limitations section, but they acknowledge: (1) SDAR degrades gracefully with retrieval quality (UCB > KM > Full > Random, with Random still above GRPO); (2) TCOD addresses multi-turn instability via curriculum learning but relies on rigid temporal schedules, which SDAR avoids. They do not explicitly discuss the compute overhead of the teacher forward pass, the sensitivity to skill library quality thresholds, or failure cases. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No error bars or significance tests on Table 1 despite WebShop's small 128-task validation set, making the WebShop-Acc gains (e.g., 58.6 vs. 50.8) statistically uncertain. - Benchmark scope is narrow: ALFWorld is text-based with finite action templates; WebShop and Search-QA have restricted observation spaces. No evaluation on GUI automation, long-horizon coding agents, or open-world tasks where multi-turn drift and retrieval noise would be most extreme. - The teacher forward pass overhead is not quantified; for large-scale training this could matter significantly. - All three benchmarks use relatively short horizon lengths; the paper's core motivation (compounding multi-turn drift) is most severe at 50+ turns, not tested. - The hyperparameter sensitivity analysis shows λ=0.1 causes severe degradation, suggesting the method requires careful tuning in new domains. Bottom Line: Any practitioner training multi-turn LLM agents with GRPO-style RL should read this paper — the gating mechanism is a principled, low-overhead fix to a real and well-documented instability, the ablations justify each design choice, and the code is public.
Recommended 2605.15155 PDF
#66 — Innovative (71)

SearchSkill: Teaching LLMs to Use Search Tools with Evolving Skill Banks

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SearchSkill, a framework that teaches LLMs to issue higher-quality search queries by conditioning each retrieval action on an explicitly selected, reusable skill card drawn from an evolving SkillBank, trained via two-stage supervised fine-tuning. - Why it's important: Existing search-augmented LLM systems train models to use a retriever but devote little capacity to how they formulate queries; in multi-hop settings, naive whole-question queries waste retrieval budget, inject noise, and cascade errors across reasoning steps. - How it does it: At each interaction step the model first emits a skill-selection tag (routing to a card such as bridge-entity-search or temporal-anchor-carry-forward), reads the corresponding card content, then emits a search query or final answer conditioned on that card — the SkillBank itself is grown iteratively through failure-driven add/refine operations before SFT. - The good: SearchSkill achieves the best macro-average exact match across seven benchmarks on both Qwen2.5-7B (42.34 EM) and Qwen2.5-3B (36.94 EM), and the frozen SkillBank also improves three proprietary closed-source models (GPT-4o-mini, Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite, Claude Haiku 4.5) without any fine-tuning, confirming that the skill routing is a transferable structured prior rather than a model-specific artifact. - The missing: All retrieval uses a fixed local E5-based retriever returning top-3 passages; the method's benefit when the retriever itself is live (web search) or much larger is not evaluated, leaving the most practically relevant deployment scenario untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on retrieval-augmented reasoning and tool-augmented LLM training — it offers a concrete, lightweight alternative to costly RL-based search agents, with practical ablations; practitioners building on web-search agents should note the local-retriever evaluation gap.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — factorizing each retrieval turn into explicit skill-selection + skill-conditioned execution over an evolving SkillBank is a clean, mechanistically motivated contribution distinct from RL-based search agents. Rigor (68) is above average: well-designed ablations (empty bank, no-card, seed replacement) and mechanistic query diagnostics (copy rate, atomic-hop rate, Correct@≤3) tie gains to the proposed mechanism, but single-run results, no significance tests, arXiv preprint, and dependence on GPT-5.4 as teacher are real concerns. Non-Obviousness (72) is supported by the surprising closed-source transfer result. Completeness (68) and Resilience (62) drop because the fixed local E5 top-3 retriever is the only retrieval setting tested — the live-web setting motivating the work is untested. Practical Impact (78) is elevated by releasable LoRA adapters and a transferable text SkillBank that improves three proprietary models without fine-tuning. Composite ≈ 0.20·70 + 0.25·68 + 0.15·72 + 0.15·68 + 0.15·62 + 0.10·78 = 70.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Open-domain and multi-hop question answering requires LLMs to interact with external retrievers across multiple turns, following bridge entities, verifying intermediate answers, and composing partial evidence. Current methods — RAG, Search-R1, ZeroSearch — optimize whether a model searches, but not how it formulates the search query. In multi-hop settings, models frequently copy the full question as the first query, issue broad queries that retrieve irrelevant passages, or fail to decompose the problem into atomic retrieval subgoals. This raises latency (more retrieval calls), injects irrelevant context, and causes error cascades in later reasoning steps. The problem is therefore not retrieval access but retrieval query discipline. Key Idea The core insight is to factorize each search interaction turn into two sub-steps — skill selection and skill-conditioned execution — so that the choice of retrieval strategy becomes an explicit, trainable discrete variable rather than an implicit side effect of generation. A compact SkillBank of named, human-readable skill cards (e.g., bridge-entity-search, verbatim-evidence-span, temporal-anchor-carry-forward) encodes reusable retrieval policies as structured text; the model reads the selected card before emitting its query. Because the bank is external and frozen at inference time, it can be updated independently of the model weights and shared across model families. Methodology The pipeline has three phases. (1) Stage-wise SkillBank evolution: beginning from a seed bank B0 of six skills covering bridge search, comparison, temporal extraction, conflict checking, evidence span, and answer grounding, the bank is expanded through four curriculum rounds (B1 from NQ/TriviaQA, B2 from HotpotQA, B3 from 2WikiMultiHopQA, B4 from MuSiQue). Each round uses GPT-5.4 to propose add/refine operations from failure patterns in coverage-stratified training packets; accepted updates are merged into the next bank version. The final B4 bank contains approximately 20 named skills in four functional categories (direct lookup, bridge/chain, comparison/join, grounding/verification). (2) Teacher trajectory synthesis: GPT-5.4 acts as teacher under the fixed bank, rolling out the select-read-act protocol on stratified training pools drawn from five QA datasets totaling ~35,000 examples; canonical trajectories that satisfy correctness, legal skill usage, and retrieval consistency filters form a pool of 3,017 SFT training records plus 16 held-out. (3) Two-stage SFT: Stage I trains the target backbone (Qwen2.5-7B or 3B, base or instruct) to learn the execution format (skill tag + search or answer); Stage II rewrites each action turn into a skill-selection turn + card-context turn + original action turn, training the model to route through the SkillBank index. The loss L_exec is token-level cross-entropy over skill-tagged action turns; L_bank adds a joint selection+execution objective. LoRA (r=16, α=32) is used throughout on attention projection modules. A lightweight GRPO post-SFT stage using reward r = r_EM + λ_e·r_evi − λ_d·r_dup is explored as an auxiliary study. Critical design decisions: (a) separating skill discovery from policy optimization is justified to prevent the policy from discovering shortcuts that bypass the routing discipline; (b) curriculum ordering (single-hop before multi-hop) is justified to stabilize simpler skills before introducing long-hop decomposition; (c) the retriever is fixed at a local E5 top-3 system, which keeps comparisons fair across methods but limits external validity. Key Results Evaluation uses seven benchmarks: NQ, TriviaQA, PopQA (single-hop) and HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, MuSiQue, Bamboogle (multi-hop), all scored by exact match after answer normalization. Baselines include direct inference, CoT, RAG, Search-o1, ZeroSearch-Base, and SearchR1 (GRPO-trained). SEARCHSKILL-SFT-Instruct on Qwen2.5-7B achieves 41.60 macro-average EM, versus 38.68 for SearchR1-Instruct (GRPO) — a +3.66 point gain over the strongest GRPO baseline with no online rollout cost. On Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, SEARCHSKILL reaches 36.94 vs. SearchR1's 33.14 (+3.80). The gap is largest on multi-hop tasks: on 2WikiMultiHopQA, SEARCHSKILL-Instruct scores 43.00 (7B) and 35.50 (3B) against SearchR1's 40.00 and 29.00 — gains of +3.00 and +6.50. Query diagnostics on four multi-hop benchmarks confirm the mechanism: first-query copy rate drops from 93.5% (SearchR1) to 76.0% (SEARCHSKILL); atomic hop-query rate rises from 47.7% to 50.0%; average searches per example fall from 4.12 to 2.98; fraction correct within ≤3 searches rises from 21.8% to 24.4%. The frozen B4 bank, when prepended to search-only prompts for GPT-4o-mini, Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite, and Claude Haiku 4.5, improves exact match on all four multi-hop benchmarks for all three models (gains ranging roughly +4 to +22 points, with Bamboogle showing the largest shifts). GRPO post-SFT yields modest additional EM gains (e.g., 7B-Instruct macro rises from 0.342 SFT to a slightly higher value) but substantially improves execution-quality metrics: evidence-conditioned rate rises from 70.3% to 81.2%, evidence-hit rate from 62.5% to 87.5%, grounded-answer rate from 75.7% to 97.9% on corrected examples. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel element is the factorization of each search interaction turn into an explicit skill-selection step driven by an evolving, externally maintained SkillBank — this is distinct from prior work that treats search as a monolithic action. The SkillBank evolution protocol (failure-driven add/refine through curriculum stages) is also a new instantiation of skill-bank ideas specifically adapted to retrieval. The two-stage SFT recipe (execution format first, then bank routing) is a straightforward consequence of the factorization. Applying SFT to search-tool use rather than RL is not new (RAG, Toolformer), but applying it with an explicit bank-conditioning protocol is. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper includes multiple well-designed ablations: empty bank (skill ids absent + card content absent), no-card (ids present but card text stripped), and seed-bank replacement (evolved B4 replaced by B0). Each ablation isolates a distinct claim: (a) the SkillBank ids contribute routing structure; (b) card content provides additional retrieval guidance beyond the name; (c) iterative evolution beyond the seed bank matters. These are credible and show non-catastrophic but consistent drops, which is an honest result — the SFT itself teaches a basic search prior even without the bank. No error bars or significance tests are reported across seeds; all results are from single training runs. Code and data are stated to be available at a linked repository (URL provided in abstract). The retriever is a fixed local E5 model — this controls comparison but means all numbers are not directly comparable to papers using live web search or BM25. GPT-5.4 is used as the teacher model for both trajectory synthesis and SkillBank evolution, which introduces a dependency on a proprietary model not available to most researchers; reproducing the teacher synthesis step requires access to GPT-5.4 API. The paper is an arXiv preprint (v2, May 2026); it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a trained pipeline with an external SkillBank artifact). - Practical: Fine-tuning runs on 4×H20 GPUs using LoRA; inference requires only the fine-tuned 3B or 7B model plus a fixed E5 retriever and a text SkillBank index — feasible on commodity hardware. - Efficient: SFT wall-clock time is approximately 1.21 hours (7B-Instruct, two stages combined); GRPO adds ~10 hours per run; the SkillBank itself is a small text file, adding negligible inference overhead beyond an additional generation turn per interaction step. - Effective: On the local E5 retrieval environment, evidence shows consistent EM gains over all baselines on all seven benchmarks; query diagnostic metrics confirm the underlying query-quality mechanism rather than only a surface accuracy gain. - Scalable: The method is tested only with 3B and 7B models and a local top-3 E5 retriever; behavior at larger model scales (70B+), live web search, or significantly larger/noisier corpora is not evaluated and not claimed. The work delivers a trained LoRA adapter, a text SkillBank (B4), and trajectory data as releasable artifacts. Strengths - The factorization of search into skill-selection + skill-conditioned execution is a clean, principled design that addresses a real failure mode (broad copied queries) with a mechanistically interpretable fix. - Closed-source transfer experiments (Figure 6) are a strong validation that the SkillBank encodes a model-agnostic retrieval prior, not just a fine-tuned surface behavior — this is the most surprising and practically useful result in the paper. - Ablations (empty bank, no-card, seed replacement) are well-structured and honestly show that gains are additive across components rather than attributable to a single factor. - The query diagnostic metrics (copy rate, atomic-hop rate, search count, Correct@≤3) provide mechanistic evidence linking the method to its stated motivation rather than treating EM as the sole signal. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge two limitations (Appendix B): (1) the SkillBank is evolved from a finite, coverage-oriented training pool, so the bank may miss skills for new domains, tools, or rare reasoning forms — extending to continual skill discovery is flagged as future work; (2) GRPO can disturb stable search behavior on questions the SFT model already solves correctly, and better RL algorithms aligned to the SkillBank action space are needed. The authors confront these directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The retriever is fixed at a local E5 top-3 model throughout all experiments. This is a major external-validity gap: the stated motivation (reducing retrieval budget waste in open-domain search) is most relevant in live web-search settings where retrieval cost is real, but no experiment uses web search or a BM25 baseline, making it impossible to assess whether skill-conditioned querying helps when the retriever is stronger or noisier. - GPT-5.4 is used as the teacher for both SkillBank evolution and trajectory synthesis. This creates a capability asymmetry: the trajectories reflect the implicit reasoning of GPT-5.4 under the SkillBank interface. The paper does not ablate teacher quality (e.g., what happens with a weaker teacher), so it is unclear whether the method works for SkillBank bootstrapping without access to a frontier proprietary model. - Bamboogle results show an anomalous pattern: removing the SkillBank increases search count by 30.4% and sometimes improves EM (SearchSkill scores lower than ZeroSearch-Base on Bamboogle in the 7B block at 39.20 vs. 36.80, though SearchSkill-Instruct wins). This "Bamboogle exception" is discussed briefly but the underlying cause — short heterogeneous questions benefiting from unconstrained search — is not fully resolved. - The SFT training pool is only 3,017 records; it is not shown whether performance saturates at this scale or continues to improve with more trajectories, which matters for practitioners considering the method. - No comparison is made against other skill-based or modular search decomposition methods (e.g., IRCoT, which also decomposes multi-hop retrieval into sequential sub-queries) beyond the baselines listed.
