#72 — Innovative (68)

The Anatomy of Silent Data Corruption: GPU Error Pattern Study and Modeling Guidance

Chung-Hsuan Tung, Yanxiang Huang, Nirmal Saxena, Philip Shirvani, Saurabh Hukerikar, Twinkle Jain, Abhishek Tyagi, Sanjay Gongalore · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper characterizes the statistical properties of silent data corruption (SDC) in a production-class data-center GPU through 3 million simulator-hours of gate-level stuck-at fault injection across 63 CUDA micro-benchmarks, producing hardware-unit-attributed distributions over corruption types, bit-flip patterns, and warp-aligned spatial periodicity. - Why it's important: Existing high-level fault injection tools (SASSIFI, NVBitFI, PyTorchFI) assume uniform single-bit flips and random address dispersion — assumptions this work shows are empirically wrong for production GPUs, meaning resilience evaluations of LLM training infrastructure built on these tools are systematically misleading. - How it does it: Gate-level stuck-at fault injection on a synthesized production GPU netlist, followed by bitwise XOR comparison of corrupted vs. golden memory dumps, with corruption addresses analyzed modulo the warp size (W=32) to reveal spatial structure. - The good: The finding that single-bit flips constitute <40% of GPU bit-flip corruptions — versus 72–98% in CPUs — and that NaN/±INF account for only 1.01% of SDC outcomes directly invalidates the dominant assumptions in all major software-level FI tools. - The missing: The study uses a reduced 2-SM configuration of an anonymized GPU and evaluates only micro-benchmarks, not actual LLM training kernels, leaving the mapping from micro-benchmark SDC patterns to real training workload behavior unvalidated. - Read it or not: Essential for hardware reliability researchers and ML infrastructure engineers building SDC detectors or resilience evaluators for GPU clusters; less urgent for those working at the model or algorithm level.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) — first gate-level SDC characterization on a production-class GPU with hardware-unit attribution; the three quantitative findings directly invalidate dominant FI tool assumptions. Rigor (65) limited — preprint, no confidence intervals on distributions, no code/data release, SAF model unvalidated against cell-aware faults, anonymized 2-SM reduced configuration. Non-Obviousness (70) — the warp-aligned periodicity and NaN/INF rarity are surprising. Completeness (55) and Resilience (60) pulled down by no validation of the proposed FI template, no real LLM kernel evaluation, and no comparison against field SDC observations from Google/Meta. Impact (65) — actionable findings but no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*65 = 65.25; rounding to 66-68 given the strong novelty of the empirical finding → 68.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Large-scale GPU clusters training LLMs encounter SDCs at non-trivial aggregate rates: 1.4% of unexpected interruptions during Llama 3 training, weekly/biweekly occurrences in Gemini training. SDCs produce incorrect computation without any hardware error signal, making detection expensive — dual modular redundancy (2× overhead) for detection, triple modular redundancy (3× overhead) for correction. Suspicion-based heuristics monitoring NaN, ±INF, or loss spikes suffer from delayed detection and unreliable rollback. Software-level fault injection tools meant to evaluate resilience assume uniform random or single-bit flip models, but these are empirically ungrounded. Prior gate-level FI work exists only for simplified open-source accelerators (NVDLA), not production-class data-center GPUs, leaving the actual structural properties of GPU SDC uncharacterized. Key Idea By conducting gate-level stuck-at fault injection on a synthesized production GPU netlist and attributing results to specific hardware units, the paper shows that GPU SDC has three structured properties — hardware-unit-dependent corruption type distributions, non-uniform multi-bit flip profiles with LSB-to-MSB decreasing probability, and warp-aligned spatial periodicity — none of which are captured by existing high-level FI tools. The key move is treating warp-aligned address periodicity as a first-class measurement target, not an afterthought. Methodology The campaign has three components. First, fault model: single stuck-at faults (SAF) are injected into a synthesized gate-level netlist of a production-class data-center GPU in a reduced 2-SM configuration. Faults are uniformly sampled from all SM logic and filtered for stimulus reachability, reducing candidates from ~6,925 to ~1,507 per benchmark (78.2% reduction). Stuck-at-0 and stuck-at-1 are assigned randomly, producing an approximately balanced distribution. Second, test vectors: 63 CUDA micro-benchmarks cover addition, multiplication, FMA, and GEMM across ALU and tensor cores with UINT8/UINT32 and FP8/FP16/FP32/BF16/TF32 data types, using Mersenne-Twister, universal test patterns, and LFSR inputs. Third, analysis: each SDC outcome yields a corrupted memory dump; bitwise XOR against the golden output extracts corruption type (nullified, NaN/±INF, non-special bit-flip), per-bit-position flip probability per hardware unit and data type, multi-bit flip count distribution, and spatial periodicity via address modulo warp size W=32. Critical design decisions: the SAF model is justified by the argument that error patterns are primarily a function of logic network topology and that most localized fault types propagate through the same sensitized paths — this is plausible but not experimentally verified against cell-aware faults. The 2-SM reduced configuration is an explicit cost-tractability trade-off. Hardware-unit attribution is the key enabling choice for the unit-specific distributions. Key Results Across all benchmark-fault pairs: 25K SDC cases and 28K DUEs/hangs. Corruption type breakdown across 600 million observed corruptions: nullification = 50.68%, non-special bit-flips = 48.31%, NaN = 0.39%, +INF = 0.49%, -INF = 0.13% (NaN/±INF jointly 1.01%). Nullification is 50.17× more likely than NaN/±INF, meaning software checks for NaN/INF catch at most ~1% of SDCs. Single-bit flips constitute <40% of non-special corruptions at any hardware unit peak, versus 72–98% for CPUs. Multi-bit flip counts form a concentrated mode at intermediate values rather than a tail. Bit-flip probability decreases from LSB to MSB; FP exponent bits (bits 23–30 for FP32, bits 10–14 for FP16) are least affected, consistent with simpler exponent logic relative to mantissa. SDC addresses exhibit periodicity at granularities 2, 4, 8, and 16 modulo warp size W=32, varying by hardware unit, data type, and corruption category. Control-path faults (CudaCoreControl1/2) corrupt 20–75% of an SM's output space per fault and skew heavily toward nullification; compute-unit faults (TensorCore, ALU) show lower per-fault corruption rates but higher total fault counts due to area dominance. No baseline comparison with downstream ML workload impact is provided — results are at the micro-benchmark level only. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuine novelty is the first quantitative characterization of SDC patterns at gate-level fidelity on a production-class (though anonymized and reduced) data-center GPU, with hardware-unit-level attribution of corruption type, bit-flip structure, and spatial periodicity. The 63-benchmark suite and distribution-aware injection template are secondary contributions. The injection methodology (SAF + XOR comparison) is standard in the reliability field; the novelty lies in applying it at this scale to a production GPU architecture and extracting structured, actionable statistics. Rigor & Reproducibility 3 million simulator-hours establishes substantial empirical scale. The reachability filter is methodologically sound but introduces potential sampling bias — faults unreachable by micro-benchmarks may have qualitatively different SDC patterns in production workloads. No statistical confidence intervals or significance tests accompany the extracted distributions. The GPU architecture is anonymized — the exact device is not identifiable, and the 2-SM configuration is explicitly not a full chip. No public code, data, or model release is mentioned. Authors acknowledge the SAF model limitation but do not experimentally validate it against cell-aware or physically-aware faults. The SAF-vs.-cell-aware comparison is argued topologically but not empirically verified. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.04213v1); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The gate-level simulation campaign required a commercial EDA tool and a synthesized production-GPU netlist — not reproducible by most academic groups; the resulting software injection template is implementable on commodity hardware. - Efficient: The characterization campaign cost 3 million simulator-hours; the software injection template derived from it adds negligible compute overhead to any existing FI framework. - Effective: The paper characterizes SDC patterns with quantitative precision but does not validate the injection template against any downstream resilience evaluation — the template's fidelity relative to gate-level ground truth for real workloads is unestablished. - Scalable: Results are from a 2-SM reduced model; extrapolation to 10,000+-SM production deployments, including inter-SM effects, L2/HBM-level corruptions, and system-level timing, is asserted but not tested. The paper delivers a descriptive injection template (Fig. 8) and a micro-benchmark suite; no software artifact or code release is stated. Strengths - First gate-level SDC characterization on a production-class GPU with hardware-unit attribution: prior gate-level work was limited to open-source simplified accelerators (NVDLA), making this a meaningfully higher-fidelity dataset. - The three core quantitative findings — NaN/±INF at 1.01%, single-bit flips <40%, warp-aligned periodicity — are specific, reproducible claims that directly contradict the assumptions embedded in every major FI tool in the field. - Hardware-unit breakdown reveals a practically important non-uniformity: control-path faults produce high-impact, zero-biased corruptions while compute-unit faults produce subtler but more numerous non-special bit-flips — a distinction that informs where to place detection logic. - Scale and coverage: 63 benchmarks × 8 hardware unit categories × multiple data types × 25K SDC cases provides statistical credibility for the extracted distributions. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the SAF model is used instead of more accurate bridging, cell-aware, or physically-aware region faults, justified by EDA tool constraints and topological argument; (2) the reduced 2-SM configuration is not a full chip; (3) FP8 results are excluded from per-format analysis due to limited samples and higher variance; (4) product identifiers are omitted, limiting reproducibility. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No validation of the injection template: the paper proposes a distribution-aware software FI template (Fig. 8) but never demonstrates that it produces SDC impact predictions matching gate-level results for any downstream task — the template's fidelity is entirely unvalidated. - All evaluation uses elementary micro-benchmarks; actual LLM training kernels (transformer attention, layer normalization, Adam optimizer steps) are never tested, leaving the mapping from micro-benchmark SDC distributions to real training workload corruption behavior an open assumption. - No comparison against field observations from Google [2], Meta [3], or Alibaba [7] — the extracted patterns cannot be validated against real-world SDC incidents, so the gap between the 2-SM synthesized model and deployed hardware remains unquantified. - The 78.2% reachability filter may introduce systematic sampling bias: faults that micro-benchmarks cannot reach may represent a qualitatively different failure mode that surfaces only in complex real workloads. - The paper does not address inter-SM or multi-GPU correlation, which is relevant for the cluster-level SDC problem motivating the work. Bottom Line This paper delivers the strongest quantitative empirical foundation to date for understanding GPU SDC structure at gate-level fidelity, and the core findings — NaN/INF rarity, multi-bit flip dominance, warp-aligned periodicity — are concrete enough to directly improve every major FI tool in the field; it is required reading for anyone building GPU resilience infrastructure, with the caveat that the gap from micro-benchmark characterization to real ML workload impact validation remains entirely unaddressed.

#73 — Innovative (68)

XtraMAC: An Efficient MAC Architecture for Mixed-Precision LLM Inference on FPGA

Feng Yu, Hongshi Tan, Yao Chen, Weng-Fai Wong, Bingsheng He · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces XtraMAC, a datatype-adaptive FPGA MAC architecture that unifies integer, floating-point, and mixed-precision multiply-accumulate operations within a single shared DSP datapath with cycle-level runtime datatype switching. - Why it's important: Mixed-precision LLM quantization (INT4×BF16, FP8×BF16, etc.) forces FPGA MAC designs to waste 60–90% of DSP capacity through operand upcasting or spatial/temporal replication, directly limiting the parallelism and throughput achievable on FPGA inference accelerators. - How it does it: Decomposes all MAC formats into a shared integer mantissa product on the DSP multiplier with lightweight peripheral sign/exponent logic, enabling multi-lane DSP packing and a 4-stage pipeline with constant latency=4 and initiation interval=1 regardless of active datatype. - The good: On AMD Xilinx Alveo U55c, XtraMAC reduces per-operation LUT, FF, and DSP usage by 27–51% versus the vendor Floating-Point Operator and delivers 1.4–2.0× compute density improvement, while achieving 1.2× lower GEMV latency and 1.9× higher energy efficiency than an NVIDIA H100 in bandwidth-bound decode-stage workloads. - The missing: End-to-end LLM inference comparisons against the H100 rely on an analytical simulation framework rather than measured execution; the GPU comparison is restricted to the bandwidth-bound regime where FPGAs are structurally advantaged, and compute-bound (large-batch) results are absent. - Read it or not: Essential reading for FPGA architects designing LLM inference accelerators; practitioners focused on GPU or ASIC paths can safely skip.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (75) anchors the score — open-source RTL on a commercially available Alveo U55c with standard Vivado/Vitis toolchain makes this immediately deployable for FPGA practitioners. Rigor (70) is strong: per-component ablations across 50+ datatype combinations, post-place-and-route (not just synthesis) GEMV results, deterministic synthesis flow, and explicit acknowledgment of the limited TATAA-fapp comparison scope. Novelty (65) and Non-Obv (65) are meaningful — the formalization of INT/FP/INT×FP as a shared mantissa product with lane-packed runtime switching is a clean technical move, though bit-packing primitives are known from integer-only contexts. Resilience (60) is capped by the GPU comparison being restricted to the bandwidth-bound regime where FPGAs are structurally favored, and end-to-end LLM claims relying on an analytical model rather than measurement. Weighted: 0.15*65+0.15*70+0.10*65+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.30*75 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Mixed-precision quantization schemes such as AWQ, GPTQ, and SmoothQuant assign different numeric formats to different LLM components — integer weights paired with floating-point activations, or heterogeneous MoE experts — resulting in a single forward pass that traverses multiple MAC datatypes (e.g., INT4×BF16 in projection layers and BF16×BF16 in attention). FPGAs are attractive for customized, low-latency inference but existing FPGA MAC architectures address this heterogeneity poorly. Operand upcasting to a fixed high-precision datapath (exemplified by the AMD Xilinx Floating-Point Operator) leaves on average 67.3% of DSP bit-space unused, achieving only 32.4% average DSP utilization. Spatial replication of per-datatype pipelines drops effective DSP utilization to 26.7%. Temporal sharing (TATAA) achieves 71.1% in INT8 mode but collapses to 8.9% for BF16 MAC because each BF16 operation must be decomposed into four sequential INT8 micro-operations, capping BF16 throughput at one quarter of INT8. No existing FPGA design supports cycle-level switching between heterogeneous INT and FP formats within a unified datapath. Key Idea The core insight is that integer, floating-point, and mixed-precision (INT×FP) multiplication all reduce to a single operation: computing an integer mantissa product on the DSP multiplier, while sign and exponent are handled as lightweight peripheral logic. For FP×FP, the DSP computes mantissa product m_x·m_y; for INT×FP, the integer operand is reinterpreted as a two's-complement magnitude (zero exponent bias), yielding the same mantissa-product computation. This shared multiplication datapath allows multiple low-precision lanes to be packed into the DSP's bit-width simultaneously using non-overlapping stride offsets, and confines all datatype-specific behavior to lightweight mapping (Stage 1) and post-compute (Stage 2) modules — so the DSP multiplier itself is fully shared and runtime switching requires only propagating a datatype control signal through matched delay slices, with no stalls, flushes, or reconfiguration. Methodology XtraMAC implements a four-stage fully pipelined datapath. Stage 1 (Operand Interpretation and Bit-Mapping) instantiates N parallel mapping submodules statically; the active datatype selects one pair via multiplexer. Floating-point submodules extract sign, exponent, and mantissa and pack mantissa bits at precomputed DSP-port offsets S_i, t_j; integer submodules perform two's-complement sign extraction and pack magnitude bits. The result is a pair of bit-packed vectors A_DSP = Σ(a_i ≪ s_i), B_DSP = Σ(b_j ≪ t_j) satisfying the non-overlap condition S ≥ W_lane + G. Stage 2 (Multiplier and Post-Compute) performs a single wide integer multiplication P_DSP = A_DSP × B_DSP; a post-compute module uses per-lane deterministic shift-and-mask extraction P_{i,j} = (P_DSP ≫ (s_i+t_j)) & (2^S − 1) to recover all lane products in parallel; FP lanes apply leading-zero-count normalization and exponent reconstruction. Stage 3 (Accumulation) uses decoupled INT and FP adder paths — INT uses a bank of two's-complement adders, FP uses exponent alignment, mantissa add/subtract, and renormalization — selected by the datatype signal; the decoupling avoids the superlinear LUT cost of a unified barrel shifter (C_shifter ≈ β_sh · w_fp · log2(w_fp)). Stage 4 (Output Selection) concatenates P lane results. Special-value handling (FTZ, DAZ, NaN propagation, saturation overflow) is implemented via flags forwarded through matched delay slices so exception processing never disrupts pipeline timing. Critical design decisions: (a) decoupled INT/FP accumulation justified by quantified cost analysis of barrel shifters; (b) non-overlapping lane packing guaranteed by the stride constraint with guard bits; (c) per-stage configurable pipeline depth allowing frequency-latency tradeoffs at synthesis time without altering II=1. Key Results All synthesis and implementation results use AMD Xilinx Alveo U55c with Vivado/Vitis 2022.2. Versus the AMD Xilinx Floating-Point Operator v7.1 (Table IV), XtraMAC reduces per-operation LUT usage by 29–35%, FF usage by 44–50%, and DSP usage by 50% across INT8×BF16, FP4×BF16, FP8×BF16, and FP8×FP16 configurations, achieving 1.4–2.0× compute density improvement. Maximum frequency is on average 22% lower than the vendor IP (462 MHz vs. approximately 590 MHz) due to routing congestion from 2× parallelism, but all configurations exceed 400 MHz; the 2× parallelism delivers approximately 1.56× higher effective throughput per DSP. Versus TATAA (2025) in runtime datatype switching evaluation (Table V), XtraMAC reduces per-operation LUT by 59.7%, FF by 72.5%, and DSP by 93.8%. Scalability: LUT, FF, and DSP scale linearly with XtraMAC count up to 1024 instances at 300 MHz; moderate frequency degradation to 250–270 MHz occurs at 1920 instances due to HBM routing congestion. Mixed-precision GEMV versus NVIDIA H100 with CUTLASS (Table VII): 1.2× lower latency and 1.9× higher energy efficiency on 1×4096×4096 and 1×4096×12288 at batch size 1 (bandwidth-bound), with XtraMAC sustaining approximately 74% effective HBM utilization. Analytical LLM simulation for Qwen-3-8B, Llama-3.1-8B-FP8, GPT-oss-20B at batch 32 (compute-bound) shows 1.5–1.8× end-to-end decode latency improvement versus the FP IP baseline. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The mathematical unification of INT, FP, and INT×FP multiplication into a shared integer mantissa product with peripheral sign/exponent handling is a genuinely novel formalization; bit-lane packing of multiple low-precision operands within a single DSP is known from integer-only contexts, but extending it to heterogeneous INT/FP mixed-precision with cycle-level runtime switching in a shared pipeline is new. The four-stage RTL architecture, FPGA implementation, and open-source release are the primary deliverable. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: per-component resource breakdown by mapping/arithmetic/adder logic across all four runtime-switching configurations (Fig. 7) isolates where sharing occurs and where it does not; incremental scalability study (Fig. 8) adds datatype support one format at a time and tracks LUT, FF, frequency. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported, which is appropriate since FPGA synthesis is deterministic. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/Xtra-Computing/XtraMAC. Methodological flags: (1) The TATAA comparison in Table V evaluates only the fapp subunits (BF16 adder and multiplier), not the full TATAA design, which the paper footnotes acknowledge — this likely understates TATAA's actual overhead and inflates XtraMAC's apparent advantage. (2) End-to-end LLM latency comparisons (Fig. 14) use an analytical simulation framework [7] rather than measured execution, which limits confidence in the 1.5–1.8× improvement claims. (3) GPU comparison is restricted to batch sizes 1, 8, 32 at context 512; large-batch compute-dominated results where H100 Tensor Cores are structurally favored are absent. Deployability (4-property check) SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on AMD Xilinx Alveo U55c, a commercially available PCIe accelerator card, using the standard Vivado 2022.2/Vitis 2022.2 toolchain; RTL is open-sourced. - Efficient: Consumes 1 DSP per instance with 27–51% lower LUT/FF than vendor IP; 22% lower max frequency due to 2× parallelism routing overhead, but delivers approximately 1.56× higher effective throughput per DSP after accounting for frequency gap. - Effective: Demonstrated on actual synthesized and post-place-and-route GEMV kernels achieving 1.2× latency and 1.9× energy versus H100 in the bandwidth-bound decode regime; end-to-end LLM inference benefits are shown only via analytical simulation, not measured. - Scalable: Linear resource scaling confirmed to 1024 XtraMAC instances at 300 MHz on U55c; tested on one FPGA platform only; cross-platform portability to Intel/Altera or AMD Versal not evaluated. The work delivers a synthesizable RTL library (open-sourced). Strengths - The processing-pattern formulation — proving formally that all INT, FP, and INT×FP MACs reduce to a shared integer mantissa product — provides principled theoretical grounding rather than an ad hoc engineering approach, making the design generalizable. - Achieving latency=4, II=1 uniformly across all supported datatypes makes XtraMAC a true drop-in replacement for conventional scalar MAC units in GEMV pipelines with no scheduling, interface, or timing changes required. - Resource characterization is thorough: per-operation utilization across 50+ datatype combinations (Fig. 6), component-level breakdown (Fig. 7), and post-implementation (not just synthesis) results for the full GEMV kernel. - Open-source RTL release at synthesis time makes the contribution reproducible and immediately deployable. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The 22% frequency reduction versus vendor IP from routing congestion at 2× parallelism is explicitly acknowledged and quantified. Frequency degradation to 250–270 MHz at 1920 instances from HBM routing is noted. The TATAA comparison footnotes that only the fapp units are measured. The analytical nature of the LLM simulation framework is stated in the evaluation setup. Authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The GPU comparison (Table VII, Fig. 14) is exclusively in the bandwidth-bound decode regime (batch 1–32, context 512). In the prefill phase or at large batch sizes where arithmetic throughput rather than memory bandwidth limits performance, H100 Tensor Cores at 989 TFLOPS (BF16) would be expected to decisively outperform; these results are not shown. - The TATAA comparison understates the baseline's cost by evaluating only its fapp subunits. A full-design comparison using the same normalization methodology as Table IV would more fairly characterize the competitive landscape. - Only one FPGA platform is tested; portability and frequency behavior on Intel Agilex, AMD Versal, or smaller edge FPGAs are not addressed. - FlexiBit [34] is the most closely related configurable-precision work mentioned in related work but is absent from hardware comparisons; the paper justifies this by noting FlexiBit targets ASIC rather than FPGA, but the omission leaves the FPGA mixed-precision design space incompletely benchmarked. - The claim of 1.5–1.8× end-to-end LLM inference improvement at batch 32 rests entirely on an analytical model; no real workload measurement at the LLM level is presented. Bottom Line Practitioners building mixed-precision LLM inference accelerators on FPGAs should read this paper: it delivers a principled, open-source MAC primitive with strong hardware results and a clean theoretical basis that substantially outperforms the vendor baseline and TATAA under the heterogeneous datatype demands of modern quantized models.
Search Results 2605.06052 PDF
#74 — Innovative (68)

Coral: Cost-Efficient Multi-LLM Serving over Heterogeneous Cloud GPUs

Yixuan Mei, Zikun Li, Zixuan Chen, Shiqi Pan, Mengdi Wu, Xupeng Miao, Zhihao Jia, K. V. Rashmi · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Coral, a system that jointly optimizes resource allocation and model placement for serving multiple LLMs concurrently on heterogeneous cloud GPU pools at minimum cost under per-model latency SLOs. - Why it's important: Organizations must serve dozens of LLMs simultaneously on public cloud GPUs, and per-model greedy strategies cause contention over scarce hardware, leaving cheaper mid-tier GPUs underutilized despite better cost efficiency per unit of compute or memory. - How it does it: A lossless two-stage decomposition exploits the observation that the throughput-optimal placement on a node set depends only on that set — not on how the rest of the cluster is allocated — enabling placements to be pre-computed offline as reusable "Serving Templates" and cached, so the online resource allocator need only solve a lightweight ILP over templates rather than rerunning placement search from scratch. - The good: In the extended 6-model, 20-GPU-configuration setup under scarce resource availability, Coral reduces hourly cost by 1.25× over Cauchy while delivering 2.39× higher goodput, and under imbalanced large-model demand reduces cost 2.79× over Homo — concrete evidence that joint cross-model optimization resolves contention that per-model greedy strategies cannot. - The missing: All large-scale results (6 models, 3 regions, 100–300 GPUs) are simulation-only, and simulator fidelity is validated against real hardware for only one model (Phi4 14B), leaving the accuracy of extended-setup numbers unverified for MoE architectures such as GPT-OSS 120B. - Read it or not: Essential for ML infrastructure researchers and cloud serving platform engineers; the decomposition technique is clean and deployable, but practitioners should treat extended-setup numbers with caution until real-hardware validation at scale is reported.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (70) drives the score along with Novelty (75) — the lossless two-stage decomposition (offline Serving Templates + online ILP) is a genuine algorithmic contribution and the system is built end-to-end (53K LoC). Rigor (55) is the main concern: headline 3.14×/7.64× numbers come from simulation validated on only one dense model (Phi4 14B), with no error bars and single-run epochs; the conspicuously absent BOute comparison from the same group weakens completeness. Non-Obviousness (70) — substructure observation is clean. Completeness (60) and Resilience (60) reduced by the simulation-to-MoE generalization gap. No code release. Composite: 0.15·75 + 0.15·55 + 0.10·70 + 0.15·60 + 0.15·60 + 0.30·70 = 65, rounded with slight upward weighting for the strong real-hardware core setup → 68.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Organizations serving LLMs at production scale must handle many models concurrently — Perplexity serves 46 models, Microsoft Office 365 uses 7 LLMs from 3 families. Public cloud GPU pools span multiple hardware generations (H100, A100, L40S, L4, A10G) with varying availability and cost; mid-tier GPUs such as L40S offer comparable or better cost efficiency per TFLOP or per GB than top-tier H100 but are treated as second-class resources by existing systems. Existing allocation systems (SkyServe, SageServe) optimize replica counts assuming internally homogeneous replicas, while model placement systems (Helix, HexGen) optimize placement on a fixed, given heterogeneous node set. Neither dimension can be pruned independently — the throughput a model achieves and whether it satisfies its latency SLO depend on how its layers are partitioned across nodes, yet the optimal partition can only be determined once the node set is fixed. Joint brute-force search over both dimensions is exponential in both the number of nodes and the number of layer-partitioning choices, making it intractable: Helix requires four hours to find a placement for a single model on 24 nodes even without a latency SLO constraint. Key Idea: The core insight is a substructure in the joint optimization: given a model, its latency SLO, and a fixed node combination G', the throughput-optimal model placement Ψ*(G') depends only on G' itself, independent of how the remaining cluster is allocated to other models. This means placements can be pre-computed for every plausible node combination and cached offline as Serving Templates; the online resource allocator then selects which templates to instantiate across regions, converting an exponential joint search into a fast ILP over a pre-built library. Methodology: Coral formalizes multi-LLM serving as: minimize total provisioning cost (sum over models, replicas, and nodes) subject to per-model aggregate throughput ≥ T_m under latency SLO ℓ_m. The serving strategy is a hybrid pipeline-parallel and data-parallel layout; each replica is a pipeline with S stages, each stage holding j consecutive layers assigned to one or more nodes that act as data-parallel replicas within the stage. Tensor and expert parallelism are restricted to within a node (where GPUs are homogeneous and high-bandwidth interconnects exist) because inter-node TP/EP require tight all-reduce or all-to-all communication that is sensitive to heterogeneous link speeds. Offline Serving Template Generator: For each (model m, SLO ℓ) pair, enumerate node combinations up to N_max=6 nodes and total GPU memory ≤ ρ=12× model size. For each combination G', solve an ILP with binary variables x_{sj} (stage s holds j layers) and y_{sk} (node k assigned to stage s). Throughput is bounded by the slowest stage: T(τ) ≤ Σ_{j,k} z_{sjk} · T̂_j(g_k), where z_{sjk}=x_{sj}·y_{sk} is linearized via auxiliary binary variables. Each GPU's per-stage throughput T̂_j(g_k) is obtained from one-time offline profiling. The ILP is solved across all stage counts S ∈ [1, |G'|]; the best candidate is the Serving Template. Template generation is parallelized on a c8i instance (384 cores), completing in minutes to hours per model. The library yields thousands to tens of thousands of templates per (m, ℓ) pair. Online Resource Allocator: Given current availability A_r(c) and per-node prices p_r(c) per region r and GPU configuration c, the allocator solves a second ILP over integer variables v_r(τ_i^m) (how many instances of template τ_i^m to deploy in region r). Constraints enforce that node consumption does not exceed availability and that aggregate throughput meets T_m for each model. The objective adds an initialization penalty I_r(τ_i^m) ≥ (v_r − v'_r) · p(τ_i^m) · K for newly added instances (K = node_init_time / reconfiguration_interval) to discourage unnecessary churn. Because the optimal solution is sparse (typically a few dozen active templates from millions of variables), Gurobi solves this ILP in under 1 minute on average even with hundreds of thousands of variables. Three critical design decisions: (1) PP+DP only across nodes — justified because TP/EP bottleneck at the slowest device in heterogeneous mixes; (2) pruning at N_max=6, ρ=12 — validated by sensitivity analysis showing cost efficiency plateaus at this setting; (3) templates do not span regions — justified by inter-region latency being prohibitive for pipeline-parallel inference. Key Results: - Core setup (3 models: Qwen3 32B, GPT-OSS 20B, Phi4 14B; 12 GPU configurations on L40S/L4/A10G; 2 regions; 20–40 GPUs; real hardware): Coral reduces average hourly cost by 1.62× over Homo and 1.60× over Cauchy; online ILP solves in 0.24 seconds on average. - Extended setup (6 models adding Qwen3 235B, GPT-OSS 120B, Llama3 70B; 20 GPU configurations; 3 regions; 100–300 GPUs; simulation only): Coral reduces cost by 3.14× over Homo and 2.66× over Cauchy; average ILP solve time 9.68 seconds. Qwen3 235B alone accounts for ~60% of total cost; Coral achieves 4.93× reduction over Homo and 4.08× over Cauchy on this model. - Scarce resource availability (extended): Coral is 1.52× more expensive than Homo in raw provisioning cost, but delivers 7.64× higher goodput (Homo fails to meet throughput demand); reduces cost 1.25× over Cauchy with 2.39× better goodput. - Large-Heavy imbalanced demand (extended): Coral reduces cost 2.79× over Homo and 3.47× over Cauchy. - Comparison with Helix (single Llama3 70B on Helix's High GPU-Heterogeneity Cluster of 64 GPUs; both using Helix's simulator): Coral reduces cost 21% and improves throughput 26% over Helix while satisfying both prefill (2090 ms) and decode (730 ms) SLOs. - Simulator fidelity: prefill and decode latency CDFs for Phi4 14B match real hardware to within 5.6% and 7.2% on average. No other model is validated against real hardware. - Baselines (Homo and Cauchy) run within Coral's own runtime for fairness; heterogeneity-aware placement systems (Helix, HexGen) are compared separately because they do not include resource allocation. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW FORMULATION. The joint formulation of resource allocation and model placement under per-model latency SLOs for concurrent multi-LLM serving is new — no prior system co-optimizes both dimensions simultaneously. The lossless two-stage decomposition into offline Serving Templates and fast online ILP is a genuine algorithmic contribution, not a heuristic approximation. Intra-replica heterogeneous pipeline + data parallelism (mixing GPU types within a single replica across pipeline stages) is a new capability absent from all compared prior systems. The end-to-end Coral runtime (53K LoC) operationalizes all components. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are limited to sensitivity analysis of pruning parameters (N_max, ρ) in Sec. 6.7, which validates the plateau behavior but does not ablate individual system components (e.g., what happens without the initialization penalty, or with homogeneous-only templates). No error bars or variance statistics are reported; each condition runs for 30 minutes with five 6-minute epochs, and only single-run trajectories are shown. Code and model weights are not publicly released; the system is described as 53K LoC of Python and C++. The most important methodological concern: the extended-setup results — which yield the headline 3.14× and 7.64× figures — are simulation-only, and simulator fidelity is validated against real hardware for one model (Phi4 14B). MoE models such as GPT-OSS 120B have different execution profiles (sparse expert activation, all-to-all token exchange) that may introduce larger simulation errors. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.04357v1, 5 May 2026) and has not been peer reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (built end-to-end system evaluated by deployment on real hardware and simulation). - Practical: Runs entirely on commercially available cloud GPU instances (AWS US-East-2, GCP US-Central-1, AP-Northeast-2) using standard GPU types; no exotic hardware required. - Efficient: Online ILP solves in 0.24–41 seconds for 3–6 models across up to 20 GPU configurations, well within a 6-minute reconfiguration window; offline template generation is a one-time cost taking minutes to hours per (model, SLO) pair on a 384-core CPU instance. - Effective: Real-hardware experiments (core setup, 3 models, 20–40 GPUs) confirm 1.6× cost reduction; extended-setup results showing 3.14× and 7.64× figures rely on a simulator validated to ~7% accuracy for one model. - Scalable: Paper argues online ILP sparsity enables scaling to thousands of nodes, and the offline stage is trivially parallelizable; the largest real-hardware experiment uses 20–40 GPUs and the largest simulated experiment uses 100–300 GPUs — no experiment at 10×–100× scale is reported. The work delivers an end-to-end software system (53K LoC) but does not release code or binaries. Strengths: - The lossless decomposition into offline Serving Templates and online ILP is a principled, non-obvious algorithmic contribution: the substructure observation (placement optimality depends only on the local node set) is clean and enables fast online adaptation without approximation loss. - Intra-replica heterogeneous placement — mixing GPU types within a single pipeline replica — is a genuine capability gap in prior systems; it drives the largest cost savings on prefill-heavy models like Qwen3 235B (4.93× over Homo). - The high-fidelity simulator (5K LoC, calibrated from per-GPU offline profiling) enables cost-effective evaluation at 100–300 GPU scales with quantified and disclosed accuracy bounds, which is honest engineering practice. - The joint optimization demonstrably resolves cross-model resource contention that per-model greedy strategies cannot: under scarce availability, Homo and Cauchy fail to satisfy throughput demand while Coral succeeds by redirecting GPUs across models (Sec. 6.4). Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state that large-scale experiments run in simulation due to cost constraints and validate the simulator on Phi4 14B only. They acknowledge that the Serving Template enumeration is restricted to a principled subset (N_max=6, ρ=12) rather than the full combinatorial space, justified by sensitivity analysis showing negligible performance impact. They note that offline template generation takes hours for larger models. They do not confront limitations around the initialization penalty hyperparameter K or around single-run evaluation without variance reporting. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The most direct competitor, BOute (Jiang et al. arXiv:2602.10729, 2026 — same research group at CMU), also addresses joint cost-efficient LLM serving with heterogeneous GPUs via multi-objective Bayesian optimization; its omission from the comparison is conspicuous and leaves the relative performance of the two approaches unknown. - Simulator fidelity validated for Phi4 14B (dense, 40 layers, no MoE, no hybrid attention) does not generalize by inspection to GPT-OSS 120B (MoE) or Qwen3 235B (MoE); expert-parallel all-to-all communication patterns under heterogeneous node mixes may introduce substantially larger simulation errors than the reported 5.6–7.2%. - The initialization penalty hyperparameter K (node init time / reconfiguration interval) controls churn behavior, but its sensitivity is never ablated; a poorly calibrated K could cause excessive thrashing or prevent beneficial reconfigurations. - All experiments assume uniform 10 req/s arrival rate across models; the imbalanced demand study addresses skewness but not burstiness or long-tail request length distributions, which are dominant cost drivers in production LLM serving. - No evaluation of how Coral handles model updates, new GPU types being added to the pool mid-operation, or SLO changes — all require regenerating parts of the Serving Template Library, with undisclosed latency. - The paper restricts inter-node parallelism to PP+DP, which may limit throughput for very large models (Qwen3 235B) on small heterogeneous node sets where pipeline stages are deep and stage imbalance may be unavoidable; this trade-off is discussed qualitatively but never quantified against a TP-enabled baseline. Bottom Line: A practitioner building multi-tenant LLM serving infrastructure on heterogeneous cloud GPUs should read this paper — the Serving Template decomposition is a deployable algorithmic contribution with well-validated cost reductions on real hardware, but extended-setup headline numbers should be treated as simulation estimates until confirmed at scale.
Recommended 2605.04357 PDF
#75 — Innovative (68)

EnergyLens: Interpretable Closed-Form Energy Models for Multimodal LLM Inference Serving

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EnergyLens, a 12-parameter closed-form energy model derived via symbolic regression that predicts LLM inference energy consumption and selects energy-optimal parallelism configurations across diverse model architectures, modalities, and GPU vendors. - Why it's important: Latency-based energy proxies diverge from energy optima in over 20% of configurations with penalties up to 79.8%, and existing ML surrogates require hundreds of profiling samples and cannot generalize across hardware — leaving data center operators without a reliable, low-overhead tool for energy-optimal deployment. - How it does it: Symbolic regression acts as a structure-discovery tool over profiling data, finding recurring mathematical motifs (token-load/parallelism ratio, batch-size saturation) that are consolidated into a single formula decoupling tensor parallelism (TP) and pipeline parallelism (PP) contributions and separating prefill from decode energy; 12 scalar parameters are then fitted from as few as 50 measurements via L-BFGS-B. - The good: Across 940 evaluation scenarios, EnergyLens achieves 88.2% Top-1 configuration selection accuracy versus 60.9% for the closest prior analytical baseline (MAVERIQ), while reaching 13.0% MAPE at n=50 samples compared to 49.0% for Random Forest at the same budget. - The missing: All evaluation is confined to models of at most 9B parameters with TP/PP degrees up to 4; whether the formula structure and sample-efficiency claims hold for 70B+ deployments — where energy optimization is operationally most critical — is not tested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and data center operators targeting energy-efficient LLM deployment; the empirical findings on TP/PP energy divergence and 4-bit quantization overhead are actionable even without adopting the full modeling framework.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (70) — actionable framework with promised code, 88.2% Top-1 selection accuracy across 940 scenarios on real multi-GPU hardware, but no released artifact at submission and untested at the 70B+ scale where energy optimization matters most. Rigor (60) is held back by point estimates without confidence intervals in the main tables and a non-reproducible symbolic-regression consolidation step (library, grammar, human judgment all undisclosed). Novelty (65) — using SR as structure-discovery rather than prediction is a clever methodological twist, and the empirical findings (TP/PP energy divergence, 4-bit quantization energy overhead) are non-obvious. Completeness (70) — authors directly confront the additive-template limitation, MoE/video gaps, and lack of dynamic scheduler integration. Resilience (65) — the 9B-parameter ceiling is the main unacknowledged extrapolation risk. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*70 = 66.5 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Data centers serving LLM inference must choose among hundreds of deployment configurations spanning tensor parallelism degree, pipeline parallelism degree, batch size, and quantization scheme. Two dominant practices fail: using predicted latency as an energy proxy (which misidentifies the energy-optimal configuration in more than 20% of cases, with energy penalties up to 79.8% relative to the true optimum) and using black-box ML surrogates (Random Forest, gradient-boosted trees) that require 500+ profiling samples to reach ~12% MAPE and must be retrained from scratch for each new model-hardware pair. The gap is that no existing method is simultaneously interpretable, data-efficient (~50 samples), and generalizable across GPU vendors, model families (dense, MoE, SSM), and input modalities (text, image, video) without structural modification. Key Idea The core insight is that LLM inference energy exhibits consistent mathematical structure across model families, modalities, and hardware that symbolic regression can uncover from profiling data — specifically, a formula in which energy decomposes additively into prefill and decode terms, each expressed as a token-load-over-parallelism ratio with batch-size saturation encoded in the denominator, and with TP and PP entering via independent power-law exponents. Once this structure is fixed, only 12 scalar parameters need to be fitted for any new model-hardware pair, rather than learning a new model topology. Methodology The pipeline has five stages. First, a profiling harness sweeps heavy configurations (TP ∈ {1,2,4}, PP ∈ {1,2,4}, max batched tokens ∈ {1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384}) and light configurations (max output tokens ∈ {64, 128, 256, 512}) across NVIDIA A100, Intel GPU Max 1550, and AMD MI100, using the vLLM serving engine on models including Mistral-7B, Qwen2-VL-7B, Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, NVIDIA Nemotron-v2-9B, and Mamba-130M under text, image+text, and video+text workloads. Second, symbolic regression is run independently on each model/modality combination to discover recurring formula motifs. Third, these motifs are consolidated into the unified closed-form formula (Eq. 4): E = αp · (total_input_tokens / (batch_size^βp + εp)) · dTP^γ1p · dPP^γ2p + αd · (max_tokens / (batch_size^βd + εd)) · dTP^γ1d · dPP^γ2d + δ1·dTP + δ2·dPP, where the twelve parameters (αp, αd, βp, βd, εp, εd, γ1p, γ2p, γ1d, γ2d, δ1, δ2) carry explicit physical meaning. Fourth, these parameters are fitted to ~50 profiling measurements via L-BFGS-B. Fifth, the formula is evaluated analytically over the full configuration space to select the energy-optimal deployment. Three critical design decisions: (1) independent TP and PP exponents — well-justified by empirical evidence that TP reduces per-GPU computation while PP introduces pipeline-bubble idle power, producing qualitatively different energy signatures; (2) additive prefill/decode decomposition — reasonably justified by the known compute-bound vs. memory-bound bottleneck distinction between the two phases; (3) random sampling of n=50 profiling points outperforms Latin Hypercube Sampling — justified by the observation that the energy surface is steepest at small batch sizes, and random draws naturally over-represent this high-gradient region (13.8%±1.3% vs. 25.9%±1.7% MAPE on Mistral-7B over 10 seeds). Key Results On Mistral-7B (NVIDIA A100), EnergyLens achieves 13.0% MAPE / R²=0.977 at n=50 vs. Random Forest 49.0% MAPE / R²=0.560 and Gradient Boosting 38.0% MAPE / R²=0.483 at the same budget; at n=500, Gradient Boosting catches up to 13.2% MAPE while EnergyLens reaches 11.3%, confirming the sample-efficiency claim but showing that the accuracy advantage narrows at larger budgets. Across 11 hardware-model-modality combinations at n=50 (Table V), EnergyLens achieves the lowest MAPE in 9/11 cases, ranging from 12.8% (LLaVA text) to 50.9% (LLaVA video-chat); exceptions are Qwen2-VL video (33.7% vs. 28.2% for Random Forest) and LLaVA video (50.9% vs. 49.5% for MAVERIQ), with modest gaps. Across 940 total scenarios (Table VI), EnergyLens achieves 95.8% pairwise ranking accuracy vs. MAVERIQ's 91.5%, Spearman ρ=0.951 vs. 0.896, 88.2% Top-1 configuration selection accuracy vs. 60.9%, and mean energy regret of 7.0% vs. 12.1%. Four counter-intuitive empirical findings stand out: (1) pipeline parallelism reduces energy for dense transformers even when it provides no latency benefit, by enabling lower GPU power states between micro-batches; (2) a non-monotone energy spike occurs at (TP=2, PP=1) for all large models — this configuration is simultaneously faster and less energy-efficient than adjacent PP configurations; (3) 4-bit quantization increases energy above BF16 baseline at low parallelism due to dequantization overhead; (4) batch-size saturation occurs before batch size 50, after which further batching yields negligible per-inference energy savings. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuinely novel contribution is using symbolic regression as a structure-discovery tool (not a predictor) to derive an interpretable closed-form energy formula that decouples TP/PP and separates prefill/decode — no prior work has applied this combination to multi-GPU inference energy modeling. The empirical findings (TP/PP energy divergence, 4-bit quantization energy overhead, batch saturation boundary) are also non-obvious contributions with direct practical implications. The individual components — symbolic regression, energy profiling, LLM inference parallelism analysis — are not new, and applying them to this domain constitutes a meaningful but not fundamental synthesis. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for sampling strategy (LHS vs. random, 10 seeds) and profiling budget (n=50 vs. n=500). Error bars are reported only for the LHS comparison; Tables IV, V, and VI report point estimates without confidence intervals or standard deviations across seeds, weakening confidence in the generalizability claims. No significance tests are reported. Code and datasets are promised as open-source on GitHub but have not been released at the time of this preprint (the paper uses future tense: "We will release"). The most significant reproducibility gap is that the symbolic regression step is underspecified: the library, primitive set, grammar, and iteration budget are not disclosed, and the process by which discovered motifs are "consolidated" into Eq. 4 involves undisclosed human judgment, making this step non-reproducible from the paper alone. MAVERIQ is adapted for energy by re-fitting its parameters to minimize energy MAPE via gradient descent — a fair and well-described comparison methodology. The paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2605.10556v2) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper builds and evaluates a deployed profiling and modeling framework. - Practical: Runs entirely on commercially available hardware (A100, MI100, Intel Max 1550); profiling requires ~50 inference jobs, executable on any multi-GPU cluster with vLLM. - Efficient: The 12-parameter fitting via L-BFGS-B and subsequent formula evaluation are computationally negligible; the dominant cost is profiling (~50 measurements), which the paper claims completes in minutes but does not quantify precisely. - Effective: 88.2% Top-1 selection accuracy and 7.0% mean energy regret across 940 scenarios provide strong evidence that EnergyLens identifies near-optimal configurations in practice; however, the gap on video multimodal workloads (50.9% MAPE for LLaVA-video) limits effectiveness for those deployment targets. - Scalable: Tested only on models up to 9B parameters with TP/PP ≤ 4; the claim of structural transferability to larger models or higher parallelism degrees is asserted but not experimentally validated — a significant gap given that energy optimization is most critical at 70B+ scale. The paper does not deliver a released software artifact at submission time, stopping at the paper and a GitHub promise. Strengths - Using symbolic regression as a structure-discovery tool to derive a physically interpretable formula, rather than using it as a predictor, is a genuinely clever methodological choice that yields a model whose parameters map directly to measurable physical quantities (TP exponent, PP idle-power coefficient, batch saturation rate). - The empirical profiling study is thorough for its scale, covering three GPU vendors, five model architectures, and four modalities with enough configurations (940 scenarios) to provide a stable evaluation signal; the counter-intuitive findings (PP reduces energy without reducing latency; 4-bit quantization can increase energy) are concrete and actionable. - The 10× sample-efficiency advantage over ensemble ML methods at comparable MAPE is a practically significant result for operators who need to profile new hardware quickly. - The robustness analysis showing that EnergyLens maintains 88.3–99.3% pairwise accuracy without per-configuration power telemetry — while MAVERIQ degrades to near-random on some workloads — directly addresses a real production constraint. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge three limitations: (1) the additive prefill/decode formula template misses multiplicative TP×PP×sequence-length interactions, which reduces accuracy on MoE and long-context video workloads; (2) domain-specific features (expert routing frequency, frame-encoding cost) are absent and could close the accuracy gap on these workloads; (3) EnergyLens currently performs static configuration selection and lacks integration with dynamic schedulers like vLLM's continuous batching. The authors confront these limitations directly and frame them as concrete future work, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The model scale ceiling (9B parameters) is the most serious unacknowledged gap: LLM operators most in need of energy optimization run 70B–400B parameter models across 8–64 GPUs, where TP and PP degree ranges and their energy interactions may differ fundamentally from the small-model regime studied. - The symbolic regression consolidation step is not reproducible from the paper: the specific SR library, search grammar, primitive operators, and the process of human-guided motif consolidation are not described, making the method's key novelty partially a black box despite the paper's emphasis on interpretability. - The paper conflates "batch size" (concurrent requests) with "max batched tokens" (a vLLM config parameter) across different sections without consistently defining which quantity drives the saturation curve in Figure 5, introducing potential confusion about what the formula's batch-size exponent actually captures. - At n=500, Gradient Boosting (13.2% MAPE) is competitive with EnergyLens (11.3%), so the sample-efficiency narrative, while valid, is weaker for operators willing to invest more profiling time — an acknowledged but underdiscussed trade-off. - Wall-clock profiling cost for the 50-measurement regime is described only qualitatively ("typically completes in minutes"); a practitioner needs to know whether "minutes" means 5 or 500 before adopting the framework. Bottom Line A practitioner managing multi-GPU LLM inference deployments gains immediate, actionable insight from EnergyLens — particularly the TP/PP energy divergence empirics and the quantization-parallelism interaction finding — and the formula derivation methodology is a credible template for energy modeling on new hardware, though the unreleased code and underspecified symbolic regression step leave reproducibility as the main obstacle to adoption.
Search Results 2605.10556 PDF
#76 — Innovative (68)

Uncovering Intra-expert Activation Sparsity for Efficient Mixture-of-Expert Model Execution

arxiv.org · Ion Stoica - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates that substantial intra-expert activation sparsity (up to 90%) is readily available in existing pre-trained MoE LLMs without any model modification, and integrates this sparsity into vLLM to achieve up to 2.5× MoE-layer and 1.2× end-to-end inference speedup. - Why it's important: Inter-expert sparsity has hit a practical ceiling due to expert collapse and load imbalance during training; intra-expert sparsity offers a complementary, training-free path to further reduce MoE inference cost as model scale grows. - How it does it: At inference time, each expert's neurons are ranked by absolute activation output magnitude and the lowest-scoring fraction is zeroed out using a threshold-based O(n) masking kernel, with the resulting sparse indices driving a fused gather-based up-down projection implemented in Triton. - The good: On Qwen3.5-122B-A10B, fewer than 2% of active routed expert neurons suffice to retain model capacity when shared experts are fully activated—a striking empirical finding that quantifies the parameter inefficiency of large MoE FFN layers. - The missing: All accuracy evaluations use five multiple-choice benchmarks (ARC, HellaSwag, Winogrande, TruthfulQA); no generation-quality metrics (perplexity, math reasoning, code) are reported, leaving open whether the 5% accuracy budget is consumed uniformly or concentrated on tasks that matter most in practice. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers integrating MoE inference into production, and for researchers studying activation sparsity in large-scale transformers; practitioners focused on generation quality or reasoning tasks should treat the accuracy claims with caution until broader evaluations appear.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (80) drives the score — production-grade vLLM integration respecting HIP/CUDA Graph constraints, with 1.5-2.5× MoE-layer and 1.1-1.2× end-to-end speedup on commodity hardware across three GPU vendors. Novelty (60) — extending known activation-sparsity phenomena to frontier MoE scale is a useful empirical finding but methodologically a straightforward adaptation. Rigor (55) materially reduced: evaluation limited to five multiple-choice benchmarks with no generation/reasoning/perplexity metrics, no per-component speedup ablation, preprint. Non-Obviousness (60) — shared-vs-routed expert sensitivity is a concrete actionable insight. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) limited by narrow benchmark coverage and absent quantization comparison. Weighted: 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*60 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*80 = 67.5 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large MoE LLMs rely on inter-expert sparsity—routing each token to only a small subset of experts—to reduce compute relative to dense models. However, increasing inter-expert sparsity requires adding more experts, which exacerbates expert collapse, load imbalance, and representation collapse during training, creating a fundamental bottleneck. A complementary and underexplored question is whether the neurons within each activated expert are themselves fully utilized. If large fractions of intra-expert neurons are near-zero for any given token, skipping their computations could reduce inference cost without retraining or architecture changes. This gap is especially pressing as MoE models scale to hundreds of billions of parameters and production inference systems such as vLLM implement sophisticated but purely inter-expert optimizations. Key Idea: The core insight is that the FFN activation outputs of MoE experts follow a long-tailed distribution (54.85% of outputs are zero or near-zero in the measured case), and that thresholding neurons by their absolute activation value—without any training-time adaptation—yields high sparsity that is safe to exploit at inference time. Rather than learning to be sparse, existing pre-trained models already are sparse at this granularity, and the sparsity increases with model scale, making the technique more useful precisely where inference cost is highest. Methodology: The study proceeds in two parts. First, a characterization study evaluates eight off-the-shelf MoE models (1B–400B parameters: Granite-1B-A400M, OLMoE-1B-7B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, GPT-OSS-20B, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, Qwen3.5-122B-A10B, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, Llama-4-Maverick) by sorting each expert's post-activation outputs and zeroing the lowest-scoring neurons at varying sparsity levels. Accuracy is measured on five benchmarks (ARC-Challenge, ARC-Easy, HellaSwag, Winogrande, TruthfulQA-mc2) via lm-eval-harness with a vLLM backend. The "sparsity cutoff" is defined as the maximum total activation sparsity at which the model retains 95% of baseline average benchmark score. Separate analyses examine shared vs. routed expert neurons and inter-expert neuron budget allocation by router weight (three ratio configurations: 1:1:1, 3:2:1, 8:4:1). Second, vLLM integration adds a sparse execution codepath for routed experts only (shared experts are left dense, as they run in parallel and have high per-neuron accuracy impact). Three key engineering components are: (1) a dense gate projection reusing the existing BMM kernel to compute activations, (2) a custom Triton threshold-masking kernel that generates a packed active-neuron index buffer in O(n) using a per-model lookup table (vs. O(n log n) top-k), and (3) a fused sparse up-down projection Triton kernel using batched vector gather of active weight rows. Compatibility with HIP Graph and CUDA Graph is maintained by padding the active-neuron index buffer to full size and launching Triton kernels with a fixed 64-neuron tile granularity; tiles consisting entirely of padding exit early. A profiled tipping-point batch size switches between sparse and dense paths at runtime. Critical design decisions: (a) excluding shared experts from sparsification (justified by accuracy sensitivity analysis), (b) using threshold-based masking rather than top-k (O(n) vs O(n log n), empirically near-lossless), (c) not budgeting neurons across experts by router weight (analysis showed ≤2 pp improvement, outside scope). Key Results: Across 8 models, the 95%-accuracy sparsity cutoff ranges from 26.5% (Granite-1B-A400M) to 90.8% (Llama-4-Maverick), with larger models consistently achieving higher cutoffs—extending prior work that only covered models up to 30B parameters. For Qwen3.5-122B-A10B, the 99%-accuracy sparsity cutoff reaches 87.4% total activation sparsity. In MoE-layer benchmarks on WikiText-2 samples, the sparse Triton codepath achieves 1.5–2.5× speedup over dense vLLM on Qwen3.5-35B-A3B and Qwen3.5-122B-A10B across AMD MI355X, NVIDIA H200, and NVIDIA RTX4090 at 85–87% target sparsity, with peak speedup at batch sizes 16–128. Speedup degrades at small batch sizes (kernel overhead dominates) and large batch sizes (compute-bound regime favors dense BMM). Resource-constrained hardware (RTX4090) shows higher speedup than high-bandwidth hardware (MI355X), consistent with the memory-bound interpretation. Table 2 confirms the threshold-based approach achieves target total activation sparsity within ±3 percentage points and maintains 95% baseline accuracy up to 85% target sparsity on Qwen3.5-35B-A3B. End-to-end Qwen3.5-35B-A3B inference on H200 reaches 1.2× speedup and 1.1× on MI355X (Table 3), with Amdahl's law limiting gains since the MoE layer accounts for ~45% of total execution time and the dense gate projection cannot be sparsified. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The empirical finding that high intra-expert activation sparsity exists and scales with model size in off-the-shelf pretrained MoE LLMs—without any training modification—is genuinely novel and extends known sparsity phenomena (ReLU models, dense LLMs) to modern SwiGLU-based MoE architectures at frontier scale. The vLLM integration that maintains HIP Graph/CUDA Graph compatibility while delivering real speedups is a meaningful engineering contribution. The threshold-based masking approach is a straightforward adaptation of prior activation-sparsity methods; the novelty is the scale of empirical validation and production-grade integration. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations isolate which component of the sparse codepath (threshold kernel vs. fused up-down kernel) contributes most to speedup. Speedup numbers are averaged over 100 runs with 96% confidence intervals reported in Figure 6, which is adequate. No error bars are reported for accuracy evaluations. Accuracy benchmarks are five multiple-choice tasks—no generation benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, MATH, HumanEval, perplexity) are included, leaving a gap between reported accuracy retention and practical quality degradation on harder tasks. The threshold lookup table is computed per model at engine startup, and the approach is validated only on Qwen3.5 variants and indirectly confirmed for other models through the sparsity characterization. Full source code is included in Appendix B and reportedly in supplementary materials; no public repository URL is stated in the main text. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.08575v1); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on commercially available GPUs (RTX4090, H200) and AMD MI355X without specialized hardware, integrating as a plugin into an existing production inference engine (vLLM). - Efficient: The sparse codepath adds a constant overhead from the always-dense gate projection (limiting theoretical maximum speedup to ~3× on up-down projections) and static kernel launch costs, but achieves 1.5–2.5× MoE-layer speedup in the latency-bound decode regime at 85%+ sparsity. - Effective: Evidence shows the method retains 95% of baseline benchmark accuracy at the model's sparsity cutoff, and layer-level speedups translate to 1.1–1.2× end-to-end speedup; effectiveness on generation quality tasks is not demonstrated. - Scalable: The approach is empirically validated up to 400B-parameter models; no stress-scale testing (e.g., extremely long sequences, very high-concurrency serving) is reported, and the batch-size switching heuristic may require per-deployment tuning. A software artifact (vLLM patch + Triton kernels) is delivered. Strengths: - Validates intra-expert activation sparsity at frontier scale (up to 400B parameters) across eight architecturally diverse models, establishing the phenomenon as broadly real rather than architecture-specific. - Demonstrates that the sparsity is training-free and immediately exploitable in off-the-shelf checkpoints—no fine-tuning pipeline required. - Delivers a production-compatible implementation that respects HIP Graph and CUDA Graph constraints, a non-trivial engineering constraint that prior research-level baselines ignored. - The counterintuitive finding that shared expert neurons have disproportionately high per-neuron accuracy impact (justifying their exclusion from sparsification) is a concrete, actionable insight for system design. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge that the gate projection cannot be sparsified under the current design (capping theoretical speedup at ~3×), that the sparse execution path underperforms at large batch sizes requiring a fallback to dense execution, and that the neuron budget allocation across experts (by router weight) was not incorporated due to marginal gains. They note that grouped (block-level) sparsity is a promising future direction to improve hardware utilization. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The accuracy evaluation relies exclusively on five multiple-choice classification benchmarks, none of which test open-ended generation, mathematical reasoning, or code synthesis—tasks where subtle degradation from neuron masking could be more consequential. The 95% accuracy retention threshold is applied to the mean benchmark score, which could hide task-specific accuracy collapse. No comparison is made to quantization (e.g., W4A16, W8A8) as an alternative inference-time efficiency method, which is the dominant deployed technique and would contextualize the speedup claims. The dynamic batch-size switching threshold is profiled at startup but its sensitivity to input distribution shifts (e.g., varying sequence lengths, prefill-heavy vs. decode-heavy traffic) is not analyzed. The claim that the technique "lays the groundwork for future efficient LLM research" is not backed by evidence. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying MoE inference on memory-constrained or latency-sensitive hardware gains a concrete, immediately applicable technique with measured speedups and a working vLLM integration, but should independently validate accuracy retention on their specific task distribution before relying on the multiple-choice benchmark results.
Recommended 2605.08575 PDF
#77 — Innovative (68)

Combining On-Policy Optimization and Distillation for Long-Context Reasoning in Large Language Models

Miguel Moura Ramos, Duarte M. Alves, André F. T. Martins · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces dGRPO (Distilled Group Relative Policy Optimization), a post-training method that augments on-policy GRPO with dense token-level teacher guidance, plus LONGBLOCKS, a 193K-sample multilingual synthetic long-context dataset, forming a two-stage recipe for long-context alignment of LLMs. - Why it's important: Existing post-training methods for long-context fail in complementary ways — off-policy methods suffer from exposure bias over long horizons while on-policy RL with sparse rewards is unstable — leaving practitioners without a reliable recipe for long-context alignment. - How it does it: dGRPO jointly optimizes sparse outcome-based GRPO rewards and a reverse-KL on-policy distillation term toward a stronger teacher (Qwen3-32B) along the model's own sampled trajectories, providing dense token-level regularization that stabilizes reward optimization. - The good: RULER performance at 128K tokens improves from 11.0 to 44.5 (a 4× gain) while short-context benchmarks are preserved or slightly improved, and the final 1.7B model is competitive with 3B–4B baselines including a YaRN-based context-extension variant. - The missing: The entire empirical study is conducted at 1.7B parameters; no evidence is provided that the recipe scales to 7B, 14B, or 70B models, where reward-hacking dynamics and teacher-capacity gaps may differ substantially. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on post-training alignment for long-context LLMs — the recipe is concrete, reproducible, and the ablations are unusually thorough; skip it if you need scale evidence beyond 1.7B.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) is solid — thorough β sweep, teacher source, initialization, and data-mix ablations with 5-run averages and reported stddev; in-family LM-judge and Qwen3-generated training data are mild Goodhart concerns. Novelty (65) — straightforward but well-motivated linear combination of GRPO+OPD. Non-Obviousness (60) — the combination is reasonable rather than surprising. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) limited by single-scale (1.7B-only) evaluation that does not test the central scaling claim. Practical Impact (70) reflects model + dataset on HF. Composite ≈ 0.20·65 + 0.25·75 + 0.15·60 + 0.15·65 + 0.15·60 + 0.10·70 = 66.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Real-world applications requiring LLMs to reason over code repositories, multi-document collections, and long conversations expose a fundamental limitation: post-training methods that work well at short context fail to generalize. Off-policy SFT and knowledge distillation suffer from exposure bias — they optimize only on expert-generated prefixes, so the model never learns to recover from its own long-horizon errors. On-policy GRPO addresses exposure bias but relies on sparse trajectory-level rewards that produce noisy gradient estimates, unstable training, and degradation of short-context skills when applied at long context. On-policy distillation (OPD) provides dense token-level guidance on model-generated rollouts but cannot directly optimize arbitrary reward signals. The gap is a principled method that combines on-policy reward optimization with dense supervision, applied to a sufficiently diverse long-context training set. Key Idea The core insight is that GRPO's sparse reward signal and OPD's dense teacher signal are complementary rather than competing: by adding a reverse-KL divergence term between the student policy and a stronger teacher model (evaluated along the student's own sampled trajectories) directly to the GRPO objective, the teacher provides per-token learning signal that stabilizes credit assignment over long sequences while the GRPO term preserves the ability to optimize non-differentiable task rewards. The teacher's KL term acts as a learned regularizer tied to the student's current distribution, avoiding the train-inference mismatch of standard KD. Methodology The training pipeline has two stages. Stage 1 is an off-policy SFT warm-up on a 10/90 mix of short-context (Nemotron-Post-Training-Dataset-v2) and long-context (LONGBLOCKS) data, trained for 2 epochs with AdamW (lr=1×10⁻⁵, batch size 4M tokens, bfloat16, sequence packing). This stage stabilizes rollout coherence before on-policy RL. Stage 2 applies dGRPO for 1 epoch on the same data mixture. The dGRPO objective is: J_dGRPO(θ) = E[GRPO_objective − β·D_KL(π_θ(·|p,o_{<t}) ∥ π_teacher(·|p,o_{<t}))], where β=0.5 (swept over {0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.4, 0.5}), and the KL is computed over student-sampled completions. Three critical design decisions: (1) setting β_ref=0 (eliminating the KL term to the SFT reference policy, following prior reasoning-model work), well-justified by evidence that reasoning models can safely diverge from their initialization; (2) using Qwen3-32B as the external teacher rather than self-distillation, justified empirically by Figure 5 showing self-distillation is consistently inferior; (3) the 10/90 short/long token ratio, justified by ablation in Appendix C.4 showing long-context performance is sensitive to this ratio while short-context is not. Rewards are verifiable (SymPy for math, unit tests for coding) or LM-judge-based (Qwen3-4B as binary judge for chat and long-context tasks). LONGBLOCKS is constructed by prompting Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking to generate QA pairs from raw documents, then filtering with an LLM-as-judge step that rejects locally answerable, weakly grounded, or malformed examples. The base model is Qwen3-1.7B extended to 128K context by fixing the RoPE base frequency to 1×10⁶ without modifying positional parameterization. Key Results All evaluations use lm-eval-harness with temperature 0.6, top-p=0.95, top-k=20, 32,768 max generation tokens, averaged over 5 runs. On RULER (128K context), average score improves from 64.5 to 74.8 (Qwen3-1.7B base → ours); the 128K-specific score improves from 11.0 to 44.5. On LongBench the model improves from 34.4 to 39.6; on ∞Bench from 6.6 to 22.7. Short-context benchmarks show preservation or slight gains: GSM8K 89.8→89.8, MATH-500 60.4→65.3, HumanEval 67.7→69.5. The final model is competitive with Llama-3.2-3B, Gemma-3-4B, and Qwen3-1.7B+YaRN while using a 1.7B parameter base. The dGRPO ablation (Figure 4) shows GRPO alone produces noisy reward curves and lower long-context accuracy, OPD produces stable but smaller gains, and dGRPO achieves the highest reward and best long-context performance. The β=0 (GRPO-only) case is strictly dominated across all β>0 settings tested. One notable finding: KD cold-start provides only marginal gains over SFT (e.g., RULER 28.4 vs 28.9) at higher computational cost, making SFT the preferred initialization. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (dGRPO) + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (LONGBLOCKS). The combination of on-policy reward optimization with on-policy distillation in a single objective is genuinely novel for long-context post-training; individual components (GRPO from Shao et al. 2024, OPD from Agarwal et al. 2024) are established, and the integration follows a straightforward linear combination, but its application to the long-context stability problem and the systematic recipe are new contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough and directly address the main design choices: β sweep, teacher source (external vs. self), SFT vs. KD cold start, and short/long data ratio. Each benchmark is run 5 times and standard deviations are reported in Appendix C.6; deviations are broadly comparable between the base model and the proposed method, confirming the training procedure does not inflate variance. LONGBLOCKS data generation prompts are fully reproduced in Appendix A. The LM-judge prompt for reward computation is shown in Appendix B. Methodological concern: the LM-judge reward (Qwen3-4B) is the same model family as the base model (Qwen3), and LONGBLOCKS is generated by Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking and verified by Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking — creating potential in-family circular quality validation. Code and trained models are stated to be available on Hugging Face (no explicit URL provided in the paper text, but footnote 1 indicates Hugging Face). This is a preprint under review.
