Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The full pipeline runs on consumer-accessible GPU hardware; the base model is 1.7B parameters and the teacher (Qwen3-32B) is available commercially, though RL training at 128K context requires significant GPU memory not quantified in the paper. - Efficient: dGRPO adds a teacher forward pass per training step versus plain GRPO; the relative compute overhead is not reported, which is a missing practical detail. - Effective: The evidence shows genuine long-context capability gains (RULER 128K: 11.0→44.5) on held-out benchmarks with distinct task formats, not just the training distribution; the LM-judge reward for open-ended tasks introduces some Goodhart risk that is not analyzed. - Scalable: The paper provides no evidence beyond the 1.7B scale; the claim that the recipe generalizes to larger models is asserted but untested. The paper delivers a trained model and dataset (Hugging Face), not a general-purpose library. Strengths - The dGRPO objective is well-motivated by a precise diagnosis of what GRPO and OPD each lack, and the combination is principled rather than ad hoc. - The RULER 128K result (11.0→44.5) is a dramatic capability jump on a held-out diagnostic benchmark specifically designed to test long-context retention and reasoning. - Ablations are unusually complete for a preprint: β sensitivity, teacher quality, initialization strategy, and data mix ratio are each independently evaluated with appropriate controls. - The short/long-context frontier visualization (Figure 3) cleanly isolates the contribution of each training stage and provides a compelling comparison against external baselines. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not include an explicit limitations section, but they acknowledge in the results that very long-context tasks (>100K tokens) remain difficult, particularly for a 1.7B model (∞Bench scores remain low despite improvement). They note the β hyperparameter is task-dependent, citing prior work. They do not acknowledge scale generalizability as a limitation, which a reviewer would flag. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The entire study is conducted at 1.7B parameters; there is no evidence the recipe transfers to 7B+ models where sparse-reward dynamics and teacher-capacity gaps may behave differently. - The LM-judge reward for long-context tasks (Qwen3-4B evaluating Qwen3-1.7B outputs) is an in-family evaluation; reward hacking or systematic bias cannot be ruled out without cross-family judge evaluation. - LONGBLOCKS is generated and quality-verified exclusively by Qwen3-family models, creating a potential distribution-narrow training set that may inflate Qwen3-family model evaluations. - Retrieve.KV on ∞Bench scores 0.0 for both the baseline and the proposed model, suggesting a systematic failure mode not discussed. - Compute cost of dGRPO relative to GRPO (teacher forward pass overhead) is not reported, which is relevant to practitioners deciding between methods. - No analysis of catastrophic forgetting dynamics during RL at long context beyond the aggregate short-context accuracy metric. Bottom Line Practitioners doing long-context post-training on sub-3B models will find a concrete, well-ablated recipe with substantial empirical support; the single-scale limitation (1.7B only) means the recipe should be validated before applying it to larger models.
Recommended 2605.12227 PDF
#78 — Innovative (68)

GIFT: Guided Fine-Tuning and Transfer for Enhancing Instruction-Tuned Language Models

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GIFT, a two-phase framework that uses per-token confidence scores from an instruction-tuned model to reweight the cross-entropy loss during LoRA adapter training on the pretrained base model, then merges the adapter back into the instruction model. - Why it's important: Directly fine-tuning instruction-tuned LLMs risks disrupting their alignment; existing base-model-then-merge approaches treat the instruction model as a passive post-hoc target, ignoring the useful calibration signal it could provide during training. - How it does it: A single offline forward pass through the instruction model computes token-level confidence scores q_t, which multiplicatively reweight each token's contribution to the standard cross-entropy loss, concentrating gradient signal on instruction-consistent tokens and suppressing updates on uncertain ones. - The good: GIFT consistently outperforms all baselines across four model families (Llama 3.1-8B, Llama 3.2-3B, Qwen2.5-7B, DeepSeek-Math-7B) and across model scales from 0.5B to 32B, with the improvement persisting under test-time scaling (Pass@N), indicating the gains reflect genuine capability acquisition rather than single-sample artifacts. - The missing: No comparison against knowledge distillation baselines, which is the most closely related idea and the strongest methodological challenger to GIFT's confidence-weighting scheme. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners adapting instruction-tuned open-source LLMs to specialized domains, as the method is lightweight, model-agnostic, and well-ablated, though the core idea is incremental relative to the merge-and-transfer literature.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) — incremental method within the merge-and-transfer paradigm, NEW METHOD classification. Rigor (72) — thorough ablation including GIFT-BaseT control, mechanistic analysis, code released, but no error bars, non-standard Avg@16 metric, preprint, no KD baseline. Non-Obviousness (65) — using instruction-model confidence as training-time weight is a sensible but not surprising twist. Completeness (65) — limitations acknowledged honestly but KD baseline omission is a real gap. Resilience (65) — survives basic scrutiny but a tough reviewer would press on KD comparison. Impact (75) — code released, drop-in upgrade, broad cross-family validation. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 = 67.0.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Instruction-tuned LLMs such as Qwen and Llama encode a carefully balanced set of capabilities—instruction following, reasoning, robustness—acquired through expensive post-training pipelines. Practitioners who want to specialize these models for tasks like mathematical reasoning or medical QA face a dilemma: direct fine-tuning on the instruction model degrades alignment under limited supervision and stochastic decoding, while the dominant alternative (fine-tune the base model, merge the adapter into the instruction model) treats the instruction model purely as a merge target, ignoring the rich per-token alignment signal it already encodes. Existing merge-based methods (Shadow-FT, Chat Vector, Re-Adapt, LoRE-Adapt) all share this passivity, and the adapter is therefore trained to minimize loss uniformly over all tokens—including stylistic fillers and uncertain predictions that are misaligned with the instruction model's representation space. Key Idea: The instruction-tuned model's per-token predictive confidence q_t = p_{θ_I}(y_t | x, y_{<t}) is a reliable proxy for which tokens carry instruction-aligned, task-critical content. Multiplying each token's cross-entropy loss by q_t before summing concentrates the adapter's gradient signal on high-confidence, instruction-consistent tokens and suppresses gradient from uncertain tokens that would push the base model into regions incompatible with the instruction model's parameter space. Methodology: GIFT operates in two phases. Phase 1 (Data Annotation and Guided Fine-Tuning): the instruction model θ_I is run once over the training set D = {(x,y)} to compute token-level scalar scores q_t ∈ [0,1] for every response token (single forward pass, no gradient). These scores annotate the dataset as (x, y, q), yielding the GIFT loss L_GIFT(φ) = E_{(x,y)~D}[Σ_t q_t · ℓ_t(φ)], where ℓ_t(φ) = -log p_{θ_B,φ}(y_t | x, y_{<t}) is the per-token cross-entropy of the base model with adapter φ. No temperature scaling, clipping, or normalization is applied to q_t. Phase 2 (Transfer and Merge): the trained adapter φ is merged into the instruction model via standard LoRA merging: θ'_I = θ_I + M(φ). Critical design decisions: (1) offline annotation is done once, keeping fine-tuning cost identical to standard SFT; (2) q_t acts as a multiplicative weight, not a distillation target—GIFT does not minimize KL divergence between student and teacher distributions; (3) the paper uses standard LoRA merging to isolate the effect of guided fine-tuning from post-hoc merging improvements (TIES, DARE are noted as orthogonal and complementary). Training uses AdamW for 1 epoch, batch size 256, learning rate 2×10⁻⁴, LoRA rank 64, α=128, dropout 0.05. Key Results: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks (Math500, Minerva Math, OlympiadBench, AIME 2024, AMC 2023), evaluated at Average@16 with temperature 1.0: GIFT improves Llama3.1-8B-Instruct by +5.2 points average (16.8→22.0), Llama3.2-3B-Instruct by +3.0 (16.5→19.5), Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct by +1.6 (41.3→42.9), and DeepSeek-Math-7B-Instruct by +2.9 (16.8→19.7), consistently surpassing Shadow-FT, Re-Adapt, LoRE-Adapt, and direct instruction-model SFT. On medical QA (MedQA, MMLU-medical, MedMCQA), GIFT improves Llama3.1-8B-Instruct from 62.6 to 68.8 (+6.2). On general capabilities (MMLU and IFEval), GIFT matches or slightly exceeds the instruction baseline, indicating no generalization regression. Pass@N curves on Llama3.1-8B show GIFT dominates at every N from 1 to 10 across all five math benchmarks, and the advantage widens on harder benchmarks (OlympiadBench, AIME 2024). Model size scaling on Qwen2.5 (0.5B to 32B) shows GIFT consistently wins; Shadow-FT degrades on smaller models (0.5B average: Shadow-FT 2.8 vs. GIFT 8.3 vs. Instruct 7.9). On Qwen3-8B (Appendix), GIFT achieves 53.0 average vs. 51.8 for the baseline and 49.8 for Shadow-FT. The ablation (Table 3, Qwen2.5-7B) shows SFT+Merge drops instruction baseline by 3.3 points, GIFT-BaseT (using base model as teacher) recovers to ~41.0 (≈baseline) but does not improve, and full GIFT achieves 42.9, confirming the instruction-aligned guidance is the active ingredient. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The core contribution is instruction-guided token-importance weighting during base-model adapter training, applied within the established merge-and-transfer paradigm. What is genuinely novel is the use of instruction-model confidence as a training-time signal rather than a post-hoc merging signal, and the empirical demonstration that this redistribution of gradient signal is the causal mechanism behind improved merge compatibility. The broader pipeline (LoRA on base model → merge into instruction model) and the per-token confidence idea (related to selective token fine-tuning and confidence-based data filtering) each have prior art; GIFT's novelty is their specific integration. Rigor & Reproducibility: The ablation in Table 3 is well-designed and isolates the specific contribution of instruction-model guidance (vs. base-model guidance and vs. unguided merging), directly addressing the central claim. Evaluation uses Average@16 with stochastic decoding, which captures test-time scaling behavior but makes direct comparison to works using greedy decoding non-trivial. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported across any table. The learning signal redistribution analysis (Figure 3, Section 5.5) provides mechanistic evidence covering 500k tokens. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/sustech-nlp/gift. Training hyperparameters are fully specified. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.01256v1, 2 May 2026); results have not been peer-reviewed. One mild Goodhart concern: the NuminaMath-CoT training set was designed for math reasoning, and several evaluation benchmarks (Math500, AMC) overlap thematically, though they are held-out test sets. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile): - Practical: The offline annotation step runs on a single RTX 4090 24GB GPU in ~2 minutes for 2,000 training examples (Table 7); the LoRA fine-tuning itself uses standard hardware at the scale of 7–8B models, making the full pipeline accessible on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: The annotation overhead produces a single 11–12 MB augmented dataset file; fine-tuning cost is identical to baseline SFT because q_t is precomputed and stored, and LoRA rank/hyperparameters match the baselines exactly—there is no additional inference-time cost. - Effective: The evidence shows consistent improvement over strong merge-based baselines on held-out test sets across two domains and four model families; the ablation confirms that instruction-aligned guidance (not generic token reweighting) is the causal factor. - Scalable: Tested from 0.5B to 32B parameters (Qwen2.5 family) with consistent gains; the annotation cost scales linearly with dataset size, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation for very large datasets but do not quantify beyond the 2,000-sample setting. No experiments on datasets larger than 10,000 samples are reported. The paper delivers a code release but no trained model weights. Strengths: - The mechanistic analysis (Figure 3, Section 5.5) demonstrating that GIFT shifts gradient energy from low-confidence to high-confidence tokens (from 79.7% to 29.6% originating from low-confidence regions under Shadow-FT vs. GIFT) provides an empirical explanation for the method's behavior beyond performance tables. - Coverage across four model families, six benchmarks, two domains, and six model scales (0.5B–32B) gives the evaluation breadth rarely seen in merge-based fine-tuning papers. - The GIFT-BaseT ablation is a clean control that rules out generic token reweighting as the explanation and pinpoints instruction-model confidence specifically as the active signal. - Offline precomputation design is a principled engineering choice that keeps fine-tuning cost identical to the SFT baseline, removing a common objection to guidance-based methods. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note (1) the offline annotation step introduces additional preprocessing cost that scales with dataset size, and (2) the paper restricts to standard LoRA merging for clarity, leaving combinations with advanced merging techniques (TIES, Fisher-weighted averaging) as future work. These are stated directly in the Limitations section and are credibly framed. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) No comparison against knowledge distillation — specifically sequence-level or token-level KD where the student (base model + adapter) is trained to match the instruction model's output distribution. This is the most natural methodological competitor and its omission makes it impossible to know whether GIFT's confidence weighting is superior to, equivalent to, or just a computationally cheaper approximation of KD. (2) The Average@16 metric with temperature 1.0 is non-standard; most prior work reports greedy or pass@1, making cross-paper comparisons difficult and potentially inflating apparent gains by rewarding diversity as well as correctness. (3) The annotation cost profiling (Table 7) covers only 2,000 samples; the 10,000-sample MedMCQA experiment's annotation cost is not reported, and large-dataset scalability (e.g., 100K+ samples) is uncharacterized. (4) Evaluations outside math and medical are limited to a small summarization experiment (Table 5, 800 test instances across 8 tasks) and MMLU/IFEval as preservation checks; the method's effectiveness on code generation, commonsense reasoning, or open-ended generation is unknown. (5) Sensitivity to LoRA rank and the interaction between rank and guidance quality are not ablated. Bottom Line: GIFT delivers a well-motivated, cleanly executed improvement to the merge-and-transfer paradigm for instruction-tuned LLMs — practitioners who routinely adapt instruction models via Shadow-FT or Re-Adapt will find a drop-in upgrade with consistent gains and no inference overhead, though the missing KD baseline leaves the method's fundamental advantage over distribution-matching approaches unestablished.
Recommended 2605.01256 PDF
#79 — Innovative (68)

ShardTensor: Domain Parallelism for Scientific Machine Learning

Corey Adams, Peter Harrington, Akshay Subramaniam, Mohammad Shoaib Abbas, Jaideep Pathak, Mike Pritchard, Sanjay Choudhry · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ShardTensor, a PyTorch tensor extension that generalizes domain parallelism for scientific ML by enabling arbitrary spatial partitioning of input data across GPU meshes, decoupled from batch-size constraints. - Why it's important: Scientific ML workloads for climate modeling, fluid dynamics, and volumetric imaging require spatial resolutions where intermediate activations exceed single-GPU memory by orders of magnitude, blocking training and inference at native scientific resolution. - How it does it: ShardTensor extends DTensor with dynamic "sharding shapes" metadata and imperative collective dispatch through PyTorch's __torch_dispatch__ / __torch_function__ hooks, so each layer determines the correct local chunk geometry on the fly and issues halo exchanges or normalization collectives on demand. - The good: StormScope trains stably at 3 km CONUS resolution (1024×1792 spatial, 114 GB peak memory exceeding the 80 GB single-H100 limit) and tracks the 6 km single-GPU loss curve — a workload that was previously intractable on a single device. - The missing: No comparison against activation checkpointing or CPU offloading as competing memory-reduction baselines, and no MFU or throughput numbers are provided, leaving compute efficiency claims unquantified. - Read it or not: Essential for ML engineers building high-resolution scientific simulation, climate, or medical imaging systems; can be skipped by practitioners working at standard spatial resolutions.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (65) — generalizes domain parallelism in PyTorch via a clean dispatch-level extension (sharding shapes), but components are established. Rigor (55) — no ablations, no error bars, no comparison vs activation checkpointing/CPU offload or JAX shard_map, vendor-internal benchmarks, preprint. Non-Obviousness (65) — the static-vs-dynamic insight is sharp but the engineering path is fairly clear given DTensor. Completeness (60) — limitations confronted honestly but missing crossover/competing-baseline analysis. Resilience (65) — claims well-bounded. Impact (85) — shipped in PhysicsNeMo, enables previously intractable 3 km CONUS training. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*85 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 65 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Scientific machine learning on high-resolution spatial data faces a memory bottleneck rooted in intermediate activations rather than model parameters. For N-dimensional inputs, doubling spatial resolution increases activation memory by 2^N — quadratic for 2D data, cubic for 3D. Table I illustrates this: a 20-layer, 8192-feature model with 3D input of shape (256,256,256) requires 10,485,760 MB in activations despite only 5120.6 MB in model weights. Standard data parallelism partitions only the batch dimension, hitting a hard floor at batch size 1. Bespoke parallelization solutions exist (Ring Attention, Transolver++, model-specific kernels) but are tightly coupled to specific architectures. No general framework provides domain parallelism with the accessibility and composability of PyTorch's DDP/FSDP ecosystem for scientific workloads. Key Idea DTensor's assumption of static, evenly-chunked tensor shapes — appropriate for model weights that do not change shape during forward/backward passes — is fundamentally incompatible with input data tensors whose local shapes change as they flow through convolutions and other shape-modifying operations. ShardTensor adds a fourth metadata component ("sharding shapes") that makes each tensor aware of its per-rank local chunk geometry, enabling imperative (rather than declarative) collective dispatch without requiring a pre-compiled static computation graph. This single extension unlocks generic domain parallelism within PyTorch's existing dispatch stack. Methodology ShardTensor is a PyTorch tensor subclass built on top of DTensor. The user-facing programming model requires four steps: initialize a 1D or 2D GPU mesh, wrap the model with FSDP along one mesh dimension (model/data parallelism), promote input data to ShardTensor via collectives along the perpendicular mesh dimension (domain parallelism), and execute standard PyTorch forward/backward passes. The dispatch architecture intercepts operations at three levels: __torch_dispatch__ for aten-level kernel dispatch; __torch_function__ for function-level differentiable overrides; and @custom_op for user-defined named operations. Operations lacking a domain-parallel implementation fall back to DTensor semantics. Three critical design decisions: (1) Imperative rather than static dispatch — required because input shapes change layer-by-layer; motivated directly by DTensor's documented incompatibility with dynamic shapes, well-justified. (2) Halo exchange for spatially local operations (convolutions, local attention) — each GPU fetches boundary data from neighbors before local computation; necessary for correctness but incurs per-layer communication that cannot currently be overlapped with compute (torch.compile integration incomplete, acknowledged as a limitation). (3) Composability with FSDP on orthogonal mesh dimensions — domain and model parallelism operate on perpendicular axes of a 2D GPU mesh, avoiding dimension collisions; a clean architectural choice enabling combined 2D parallelism strategies. No changes to training objectives are imposed; task-specific losses (L2 for Transolver, denoising diffusion for StormScope) are unmodified. Key Results All performance benchmarks run on NVIDIA Blackwell GB200 GPUs in an NV72 system; StormScope uses an H100 cluster. Benchmarks use synthetic data; applications use real datasets. - Ring Attention: near-linear strong scaling at large sequence lengths up to 16 GPUs. At small sequence sizes multi-GPU latency exceeds single-GPU, as expected given dispatch overhead. - Vision Transformer (~115M parameters): 15× inference speedup at 4096² resolution on 16 GPUs versus 1 GPU; 5× training speedup at 2048² on 8 GPUs. In 3D, 16 GPUs enable training on over 1 billion input points. GPU memory follows a quadratic fit with spatial resolution (Figure 4), confirming activation dominance. - Transolver (8-layer PDE transformer): stable training and validation at 200k, 400k, 800k, and 1.2M mesh points on the DrivAerML automotive aerodynamics dataset; final L2 pressure and velocity errors are competitive with the original single-GPU Transolver publication. The 400k–1.2M runs required 2D domain parallelism. - StormScope (195M parameter diffusion transformer): 3 km CONUS (1024×1792 per channel) training on 32 GPUs (16 data-parallel × 2 domain-parallel); 3 km training loss converges stably and tracks the 6 km single-GPU baseline trajectory throughout 300k steps. No MFU, FLOP counts, or throughput (samples/sec) numbers are provided. No baselines from competing memory-reduction methods are included. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The individual components — domain decomposition, halo exchange, Ring Attention, collective normalization — are established techniques. The contribution is a general, composable PyTorch-native dispatch infrastructure that makes these techniques accessible without model-specific bespoke engineering, closing the gap between scientific ML's resolution requirements and standard PyTorch tooling. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations isolate which architectural decisions matter (imperative vs. static dispatch overhead, cost of halo exchange vs. fallback DTensor paths, impact of sharding shapes metadata). Performance benchmarks report means over multiple runs but provide no error bars or variance. No comparison against activation checkpointing or CPU offloading as competing memory baselines. All results come from the NVIDIA team that built the system — no independent replication. Code and benchmarks are open-source in the NVIDIA PhysicsNeMo repository (https://github.com/NVIDIA/physicsnemo); applications require additional data preparation. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.11111v1, May 11, 2026), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA GPUs (Blackwell GB200, H100); strong scaling performance depends on high-bandwidth NVLink interconnect (NV72 system) — commodity Ethernet interconnects will see substantially degraded scaling efficiency. - Efficient: Dispatch overhead makes domain parallelism cost-ineffective for small or compute-light operations; no MFU or utilization numbers are reported, so absolute hardware efficiency at scale is unquantified. - Effective: Evidence shows the framework enables previously intractable workloads (StormScope 3 km CONUS) and maintains numerical accuracy (Transolver L2 competitive with single-GPU baseline); ViT speedups of 15× at extreme resolution are concrete. - Scalable: Tested up to 16 GPUs for ViT/ring attention, 32 GPUs for StormScope. No experiments beyond 32 GPUs; communication overhead from halo exchanges grows with boundary-to-volume ratio as GPU count increases, but this scaling behavior is not analyzed empirically. The framework is deployed as a production software artifact in PhysicsNeMo. Strengths - The imperative dispatch architecture solves a genuine and specific incompatibility with DTensor: the insight that weights are static but activations are dynamic is technically sharp and the fix (sharding shapes) is minimal and well-targeted. - StormScope is a compelling existence proof — continental-scale 3 km weather forecasting now trains stably where it was previously memory-impossible, without sacrificing either spatial fidelity or domain coverage. - Composability with FSDP along orthogonal mesh dimensions provides a clean 2D parallelism strategy that practitioners can layer onto existing training pipelines with four lines of code changes. - Open-source availability in an active production framework (PhysicsNeMo) and the 4-step programming model (Algorithm 1) demonstrate genuine usability beyond a research prototype. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront the following: Python-level dispatch latency adds overhead for small operations and per-layer communication for halo exchanges cannot be amortized across consecutive layers (torch.compile integration is incomplete). Not all PyTorch operations have domain-parallel dispatch paths; unsupported operations fall back to DTensor or require user-written extensions. Performance degrades on interconnects slower than the benchmarked NV72 NVLink fabric. The general design cannot be simultaneously optimal for every workload. These are stated candidly and represent a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No comparison against activation checkpointing + CPU offloading, the primary competing approach for large-activation workloads. The paper never quantifies the crossover point at which domain parallelism becomes preferable — this is the central practical question a deployer needs to answer. - All performance benchmarks use synthetic data; real-data I/O and preprocessing throughput are not characterized, which matters for end-to-end training time in production scientific workflows. - No characterization of communication-to-computation ratio as a function of layer type and halo size — this quantity governs when domain parallelism is beneficial and is left entirely implicit. - Latency plots show means only with no confidence intervals — for production deployment decisions, variance and tail latency matter. - No head-to-head comparison with JAX's shard_map, which the paper itself identifies as a Swiss-army-knife alternative for domain parallelism; the relative performance advantage of ShardTensor in a PyTorch context versus moving to JAX is unquantified. Bottom Line Practitioners building scientific ML systems at extreme spatial resolutions — climate models, PDE solvers, volumetric imaging — will find ShardTensor the most accessible path to domain parallelism in PyTorch, and the StormScope and Transolver demonstrations provide sufficient credibility to justify adoption; those doing standard-resolution deep learning gain nothing from this work.
Search Results 2605.11111 PDF
#80 — Innovative (68)

Can MLLMs Reason About Visual Persuasion? Evaluating the Efficacy and Faithfulness of Reasoning

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a dual-axis rationale supervision framework for fine-tuning MLLMs on visual persuasiveness prediction and a three-dimensional faithfulness evaluation protocol (rationale-to-decision consistency, rationale-to-image groundedness, rationale-to-decision sensitivity) to assess whether generated rationales actually support model decisions. - Why it's important: As generative AI enables mass production of tailored persuasive imagery, automated systems that can judge both whether and why an image is persuasive are critical for content moderation and media literacy, yet existing MLLM evaluation treats label accuracy as a sufficient proxy for reasoning quality. - How it does it: Teacher models (Qwen2.5-VL-72B and Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B) generate rationales under four prompt types crossing evidence polarity (support-focused vs. counter-aware) and visual granularity (global vs. local); student models are then fine-tuned via supervised next-token prediction on this diverse rationale corpus. - The good: Reasoning-SFT improves balanced accuracy over label-only SFT (+0.035 on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, +0.025 on Phi-3.5-vision) and simultaneously achieves near-perfect rationale-to-decision consistency (κ=0.9905 for the GPT-5 judge), while revealing the counter-intuitive finding that stronger prediction performance does not reliably produce more faithful rationales. - The missing: The test set covers only 209 image-message pairs across 79 messages, and all evaluation is confined to a single domain-specific dataset (PVP), leaving generalization to other persuasion corpora or image types entirely untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on multimodal reasoning, explainability, or rationale supervision — the faithfulness framework is directly reusable, and the prediction-vs-faithfulness decoupling result challenges a common implicit assumption in the rationale fine-tuning literature.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (72) — the prediction-vs-faithfulness decoupling and the three-dimensional faithfulness protocol are genuinely new framings, though components are adaptations. Rigor (65) is the main drag: small test set (n=209), no significance tests on the headline accuracy table, single-dataset evaluation, and a known-weak groundedness judge; preprint status further reduces rigor. Non-Obviousness (75) is solid given the decoupling finding. Completeness (70) and Resilience (62) are moderate — the human-preference correlation hinges on only 50 items with moderate agreement. Impact (70) is fair: code released, reusable framework. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*70 ≈ 68.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 62 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Visual persuasion judgment — determining whether an image effectively conveys a persuasive message — requires interpretive reasoning over affective cues, symbolism, scene composition, and message alignment, not just object recognition. Public health communicators, political fact-checkers, and ad-safety researchers need systems that can explain why an image is or is not persuasive. Prior computational work on visual persuasion treats it as a pure prediction target without examining the reasoning behind judgments. Meanwhile, the growing rationale-supervision literature for MLLMs uses label correctness as the sole quality signal for intermediate reasoning, an assumption that fails when multiple valid rationales exist for the same input — as is endemic in persuasion, where judgments depend on viewer personality and values. The specific gap is twofold: (1) no established methodology exists for training MLLMs to reason faithfully about visual persuasion, and (2) no evaluation framework exists that checks whether generated rationales actually reflect the visual evidence behind a model's decision. Key Idea: The core insight is that the space of valid persuasion rationales is multi-modal — a single image–message pair admits multiple legitimate reasoning paths (global composition, local visual elements, supporting evidence, counter-evidence) — so supervising on rationales sampled from a single perspective may reinforce existing reasoning shortcuts rather than broaden the model's evidence-use. Crossing two prompt axes (evidence polarity × visual granularity) and training on the union of resulting teacher rationales acts as coverage-broadening supervision: the worst-case loss over all valid rationales is bounded by empirical fit plus a coverage radius, so a more diverse rationale set tightens this bound (Theorem 1). Independently, rationale faithfulness cannot be measured by label accuracy alone but requires probing whether the rationale is sufficient for the decision (consistency), grounded in the image (groundedness), and causally linked to the decision via counterfactual image editing (sensitivity). Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. (1) Data construction: The PVP dataset (28,454 images, 596 messages, 20 topics, 4 annotators scoring 0–10) is reconstructed into binary labels using annotator-aware quartile voting, yielding 820 training and 209 test image–message pairs split by message to enforce out-of-message generalization. Seven prompt templates spanning four dual-axis types (support-focused global/local, counter-aware global/local) are applied to each training triple using Qwen2.5-VL-72B-Instruct and Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B as teachers, producing 5,670 and 5,738 training instances respectively after format filtering. (2) Student fine-tuning: Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct and Phi-3.5-vision-instruct are fine-tuned with LoRA (r=32, α=64) via supervised next-token prediction over teacher rationale + label sequences (Reasoning-SFT). Baselines include frozen base, label-only SFT, Base-Reasoning (frozen + chain-of-thought prompting), GRPO (answer-only reward), and GRPO-Joint (reward over full rationale-answer sequence). (3) Faithfulness evaluation: Rationale-to-decision consistency and groundedness use GPT-5 as LLM-as-a-judge (selected by κ=0.9905 agreement with human annotations on a held-out 210-instance test set). Rationale-to-decision sensitivity uses rationale-conditioned counterfactual image editing — GPT-5 converts the rationale into an edit instruction, Qwen-Image-Edit generates the edited image, and sensitivity is scored as positive if the model's log-probability for the predicted label decreases post-edit (74.9% of edits validated by humans as successfully shifting persuasiveness, Fleiss' κ=0.6927). Key Results: Reasoning-SFT achieves balanced accuracy of 0.766 on Qwen2.5-VL-7B and 0.746 on Phi-3.5-vision, versus 0.731 and 0.721 for label-only SFT. Base-Reasoning (chain-of-thought prompting without rationale supervision) reduces balanced accuracy by 0.058 on Phi relative to frozen Base, confirming that naively prompted rationales hurt rather than help. GRPO-Joint exhibits prediction collapse on Phi (recall=0.897 but balanced accuracy=0.601), attributed to reward coverage over the rationale causing near-universal persuasive predictions. On faithfulness: Reasoning-SFT achieves rationale-to-decision consistency of 0.995 (Qwen) and 0.990 (Phi), strongest among all setups. Base models achieve the highest rationale-to-decision sensitivity (0.885 Qwen, 0.766 Phi) despite weak prediction, interpreted as native decision heuristics being more directly exposed to counterfactual edits. GRPO improves groundedness over Base (0.780 vs 0.727 for Qwen) but reduces sensitivity. Among the three faithfulness metrics, only sensitivity shows significant correlation with human rationale preference (Pearson r=0.771, p=0.016; Spearman ρ=0.611 at pairwise level), while consistency (r=−0.03) and groundedness (r=−0.17) show no meaningful association. Prompt-type ablations confirm that no single prompt type consistently dominates, and rationale length (Pearson r≤0.305 with balanced accuracy) does not explain performance differences. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The dual-axis rationale supervision framework is a novel structured approach to training visual-reasoning MLLMs, and the three-dimensional faithfulness evaluation protocol for visual persuasion is, to the authors' knowledge, the first of its kind. The core empirical finding — that prediction performance and rationale faithfulness are decoupled, and that GRPO-style reward optimization can improve groundedness while degrading behavioral sensitivity — is genuinely non-obvious. The theoretical motivation (coverage-based generalization bound, spectral diversity propositions) is new framing adapted from generalization theory. However, the individual components (teacher–student rationale distillation, LLM-as-judge, counterfactual perturbation faithfulness) are adaptations of existing ideas applied to a new and challenging setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough: individual prompt types are isolated (Table 5), rationale length is decoupled from performance (Table 6), and embedding-backend robustness for diversity proxies is checked via Spearman correlations across three backends (Table 9). Judge selection follows a rigorous validation pipeline: multiple candidate models are compared on held-out human annotations before GPT-5 is adopted. The groundedness judge has a known weakness — the validation labels are severely imbalanced (yes:no ≈ 8.5:1), and the judge achieves only balanced accuracy of 0.57, limiting its reliability on the minority (ungrounded) class. The consistency judge is solid (κ=0.9905, F1=0.9953). Error bars and significance tests are absent from the main prediction results (Table 1); only the human-preference correlation reports significance (p=0.016). The test set is small (n=209 pairs). Code is publicly released at https://github.com/holi-lab/Visual_Persuasion. This is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training runs on a single A100 or RTX A6000 GPU with LoRA and DeepSpeed ZeRO-2, accessible on standard academic compute. - Efficient: Reasoning-SFT requires generating a large teacher rationale corpus (5,670–5,738 instances per teacher model using 72B/15B teacher models), adding meaningful data-collection cost beyond label-only SFT; inference overhead at test time is a two-turn chain-of-thought exchange, negligible relative to teacher generation. - Effective: Reasoning-SFT consistently outperforms all baselines on the PVP test split on balanced accuracy and achieves near-perfect decision consistency, but gains over label-only SFT are modest (+0.025–0.035 balanced accuracy) and the relationship to real-world persuasion detection utility is untested. - Scalable: The method is tested exclusively on a single small dataset (820 training, 209 test pairs); no experiments at larger scale or on diverse persuasion corpora are reported, so scalability is claimed but undemonstrated. The work delivers fine-tuned model weights implicitly (methodology described), a rationale dataset, and the evaluation pipeline as software, though the primary contribution is a methodology paper. Strengths: - The decoupling of prediction accuracy from rationale faithfulness is a substantive empirical contribution that challenges a widespread implicit assumption in the rationale-supervision literature, backed by a carefully designed multi-dimensional evaluation. - The three-dimensional faithfulness framework is well-motivated, each dimension is operationalized with a distinct measurement mechanism (NLI-style judging, image-grounding judging, counterfactual editing), and judge selection is empirically validated against human annotations rather than assumed. - The theoretical grounding via coverage-based generalization bounds (Theorem 1) and spectral diversity analysis (Proposition 1) provides a principled, if idealized, explanation for why diverse rationale perspectives improve supervision — going beyond empirical-only justification. - The empirical diversity diagnostics (PERMANOVA, spectral proxies across three embedding backends, Tables 8–9) confirm that the seven prompt sources produce genuinely distinguishable rationale families, not paraphrases, lending credibility to the coverage claim. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that their evaluation methodology — including human annotation protocols and LLM-as-judge selection — should be viewed as an initial step rather than a complete solution, and that future work should develop more reliable faithfulness protocols. They also acknowledge that the groundedness judge has limited reliability on minority-class (ungrounded) cases due to label imbalance, and they treat groundedness as a complementary diagnostic rather than a primary metric. The dual societal risk of the work is noted: visual persuasion evaluation can support beneficial applications but may also be misused to optimize manipulative imagery. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The test set (209 pairs, 79 messages) is small enough that the +0.025–0.035 accuracy improvements may not be robust; no confidence intervals or significance tests are reported for Table 1. - All experiments use a single dataset (PVP), which consists predominantly of AI-generated images with a specific topic distribution (20 topics). Whether the findings transfer to real-world photographic persuasive content or other domains (political ads, health PSAs) is unknown. - The sensitivity metric depends critically on the quality of the counterfactual image editor (Qwen-Image-Edit) and GPT-5's edit instruction generation. If edits are semantically shallow or introduce spurious changes, sensitivity scores may not reflect genuine causal influence — the 74.9% human validation rate means 25.1% of edits are uninformative, potentially biasing sensitivity estimates. - The human preference evaluation covers only 50 items (750 pairwise judgments), inter-annotator agreement is moderate (Fleiss' κ=0.309), and sensitivity's correlation is significant only within same-model-family comparisons (ρ=1.000 for within-Qwen, −0.008 across families), severely limiting the strength of the claim that sensitivity aligns with human preference generally. - The coverage-based theoretical motivation assumes the Lipschitz condition on the rationale-conditioned loss, which is neither verified nor argued to hold empirically; the authors themselves call it a motivation rather than a guarantee. Bottom Line: Practitioners working on multimodal rationale supervision or visual persuasion detection will find the faithfulness framework and the prediction-vs-faithfulness decoupling result directly actionable, but should treat the task-specific performance gains as preliminary given the small test set and single-dataset evaluation.
Search Results 2605.08965 PDF
#81 — Innovative (68)

ToolScope: Enhancing LLM Agent Tool Use through Tool Merging and Context-Aware Filtering

openreview.net · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ToolScope, a two-part pipeline (ToolScopeMerger + ToolScopeRetriever) that automatically merges semantically redundant tools in large LLM agent toolsets and then retrieves only the top-k relevant tools per query to fit within context windows. - Why it's important: Organically grown real-world toolsets (hundreds to thousands of APIs) contain overlapping tools that both confuse LLM tool selection and exceed context limits, and no prior automated solution addressed both problems jointly. - How it does it: ToolScopeMerger builds an undirected graph of semantically equivalent tool pairs via embedding-based candidate generation and LLM binary classification, prunes to one representative per connected component, then validates and corrects clusters with a second LLM pass; ToolScopeRetriever applies hybrid dense+sparse retrieval with cross-encoder reranking per decomposed sub-query. - The good: Correct Selection Rate gains of +34.6% on Seal-Tools and +38.6% on UltraTool over dense retrieval baselines are large, consistent across GPT-4o, LLaMA-3.3-70B, and Command-R, and accompanied by 98%+ token-count reduction passed to the agent. - The missing: The end-to-end downstream accuracy evaluation covers only 20 queries on a single benchmark, which is far too small to substantiate the claim that retrieval gains translate to task-completion gains. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers building LLM agents over large, organically grown API collections; can be skipped if your toolset is small, curated, and already de-duplicated.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Rigor (75) is strong — clean ablations isolating Reranker/Merger/Auto-Correction, threshold sensitivity sweep, LLM-swap ablation showing model-independence, human merge eval (κ=0.54–0.64), three benchmarks, three LLMs. Novelty (60) moderate — components are standard but the offline-merge + online-retrieve decomposition is a coherent and non-obvious integration. Non-Obviousness (65) for identifying that toolset cleaning and retrieval must be solved jointly. Completeness (65) and Resilience (60) reasonable, but the 20-query E2E evaluation and the relabeling-bias concern for CSR pull these down. Impact (75) — large CSR gains (+34–39%), 98%+ token reduction, and detailed cost reporting make this immediately deployable, though no public artifact is stated. Weighted: 0.15*60 + 0.15*75 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*75 ≈ 68.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents that invoke external tools face two compounding problems when toolsets scale to hundreds or thousands of APIs. First, large toolsets accumulated organically (e.g., from public API marketplaces) contain many semantically redundant tools — different function names and descriptions for the same underlying operation — which introduces ambiguity that degrades both retrieval recall and LLM selection accuracy. Second, presenting all tools to an LLM in-context at query time is infeasible: the paper reports that presenting Seal-Tools (4,076 tools) in full requires 292,107 tokens, far beyond any practical context budget. Prior work addressed each problem in isolation: per-tool documentation improvement methods (DRAFT, EASYTOOL) ignore cross-tool redundancy; retrieval methods (BM25, dense embeddings, RAG-Tool Fusion) reduce context usage but cannot resolve ambiguity caused by overlapping tools. Manual tool curation exists but does not scale. The problem is acute for practitioners deploying LLM agents over enterprise or public API catalogs. Key Idea The core insight is to decouple the toolset quality problem from the query-time retrieval problem and solve them sequentially: first run a one-time offline pass that detects and merges semantically equivalent tools (shrinking the toolset by 14–25% and eliminating the disambiguation ambiguity), then at query time retrieve only the top-k tools from the already-cleaned toolset. The merger uses an embedding similarity graph to find candidate pairs cheaply, an LLM binary classifier to validate semantic equivalence precisely, and a second LLM to synthesize a canonical merged tool description — avoiding both the speed cost of fully LLM-based clustering and the precision cost of fully embedding-based clustering. Methodology ToolScopeMerger proceeds in three stages. Stage 1 (Tool Indexing): encode each tool's name, signature, and description using `thenlper/gte-large`, index in FAISS, and retrieve the top-30 cosine-similar neighbors per tool as merge candidates. Pairs with cosine similarity ≥ 0.82 (threshold selected by sensitivity sweep) enter the classifier. Stage 2 (Relationship Classification and Graph Construction): an LLM binary classifier Mc (GPT-4o in the primary experiments) scores each candidate pair as semantically equivalent (1) or not (0); edges are added to an undirected graph G = (T, E); connected components are extracted, each representing a cluster of equivalent tools. Stage 3 (Pruning, Auto-Correction, and Merging): one representative tool per cluster (shortest function name) is selected; the Auto-Correction validator V runs a single GPT-4o prompt over each cluster to detect bad merges and split them into refined sub-clusters (MERGE_OK / MERGE_BAD with cluster reassignment); a final LLM merger Md synthesizes a canonical merged signature and docstring for each cluster. Benchmark ground-truth labels are relabeled via the mapping φ: T → T' so that evaluation remains consistent with the merged toolset. ToolScopeRetriever applies hybrid retrieval: s(q, t) = α · s_dense(q,t) + (1−α) · s_sparse(q,t), with α = 1.0 (dense-only) determined by a Seal-Tools sweep showing dense retrieval dominates. The top-50 candidates are reranked by `cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L6-v2`. For multi-tool queries, the query is decomposed into ordered sub-steps; each sub-step independently retrieves its top-1 tool, and remaining tools for the k-toolset are assembled using min-max normalized reranker scores across sub-queries to ensure fair global ranking. Critical design decisions: (1) the cosine threshold θ = 0.82 is empirically validated by a sweep from 0.77–0.90, with CSR stable below 0.82 and declining above 0.86 due to over-aggressive merging; (2) α = 1.0 (dense-only) is justified by Recall@k peaking there on Seal-Tools; (3) Auto-Correction performs exactly one LLM pass rather than iterating, balancing correction quality against cost. Key Results Primary metric: Correct Selection Rate (CSR@k) — fraction of queries for which the predicted top-k toolset exactly matches ground truth. Baselines: BM25 (lexical TF-IDF) and Dense Embeddings (Qu et al., 2025), both evaluated on original unmerged toolsets. Seal-Tools (4,076 tools, 654 queries, multi-tool): ToolScope+AutoCorrect CSR@5/10/15 = 0.889/0.921/0.924 (GPT-4o) vs. Dense baseline 0.548/0.584/0.611; gain +34.6% at CSR@5. Gains are consistent for LLaMA-3.3-70B (+35.8%) and Command-R (+35.0%). BFCL (400 tools, 400 queries, single-tool): ToolScope+AutoCorrect CSR@5/10/15 = 0.938/0.935/0.940 (GPT-4o) vs. Dense 0.888/0.900/0.915; gain +8.8% at CSR@1 (0.860 vs. 0.780). Improvements are smaller here because BFCL is a single-tool benchmark with less semantic overlap. UltraTool (1,885 tools, 4,814 queries, multi-tool): ToolScope+AutoCorrect CSR@30 = 0.752 (GPT-4o) vs. Dense 0.673; gain +38.6%. Context length reduction: 98.6% (BFCL: 32,563 → 469 tokens), 99.9% (Seal-Tools: 292,107 → 317 tokens), 98.5% (UltraTool: 136,352 → 2,076 tokens). Functionality preservation: Tool-Call Coverage Rate 82–95%, Unique Capability Coverage 80–96% across datasets; preservation holds for rare capabilities (≤3 occurrences): 82%, 96%, 86% on BFCL, Seal-Tools, UltraTool. Human merge evaluation: 95.4% correct merge decisions on 48 BFCL clusters; Auto-Correction F1 = 94.4% (precision 95.5%, recall 93.3%). LLM ablation: Replacing GPT-4o in ToolScopeMerger with LLaMA 3.3 70B yields CSR@5/10/15 = 0.935/0.942/0.945 on BFCL (GPT-4o: 0.938/0.935/0.940), confirming the merger is not tied to a proprietary model. E2E evaluation (20 BFCL queries): ToolScope+AutoCorrect 80% final answer accuracy vs. BM25 30%, Dense Retrieval 40%. Surprising finding: Auto-Correction slightly hurts Command-R on BFCL (−1.2% CSR), suggesting that when baseline tool overlap is already low, the correction module can over-split valid merges. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The combination of automated graph-based tool merging with LLM-validated auto-correction plus hybrid retrieval is novel as an integrated system for the tool selection problem. Each individual component — embedding-based clustering, LLM binary classification, cross-encoder reranking, hybrid sparse/dense retrieval — is standard. The genuine novelty is the identification that toolset pre-processing (merging) and query-time retrieval must be solved jointly to address both redundancy and context constraints, and the specific pipeline that does so automatically without manual curation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation Table 3 cleanly isolates Reranker, Merger, and Auto-Correction contributions across all three datasets and confirms each component adds value. Sensitivity analysis (Fig. 4) over threshold range 0.77–0.90 demonstrates robustness. LLM ablation (Table 11) tests two open-source alternatives (LLaMA 3.3 70B, LLaMA 3.1 8B) for the merger, confirming model-independence. Human evaluation of merge quality (48 clusters, 3 annotators with inter-annotator Cohen's κ = 0.536–0.644) and documentation quality alignment with human judges (Kendall τ = 0.664, p = 0.0001) add external validity. All hyperparameters are fully reported (Table 7) with hardware specs (3× NVIDIA A100). Code availability: not stated. No error bars or significance tests reported for CSR results; no multiple-seed experiments. The E2E evaluation (Table 12) is 20 queries — far too small for statistical claims. GPT-4o performs both merging/auto-correction and (in some settings) serves as the tool selection agent — a potential circularity not acknowledged. This is a preprint (OpenReview); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — ToolScope is a built pipeline evaluated by deployment on three benchmarks. - Practical: ToolScopeRetriever uses open-source models (gte-large, MiniLM cross-encoder) with sub-1s query latency; ToolScopeMerger requires A100-class hardware for embedding but runs as a one-time preprocessing step costing $4.60–$41 per toolset. - Efficient: Query-time inference costs 1s (open-source) or 1.8s/$0.01 (GPT-4o) per query; context passed to the agent is reduced by 98%+, making tool selection viable within standard LLM context windows. - Effective: CSR gains are large and consistent across 3 benchmarks and 3 LLMs; downstream task accuracy shows 80% vs. 40% for dense retrieval, but this rests on 20 queries only — insufficient to establish reliable task-level effectiveness. - Scalable: Tested up to 4,814 queries and 4,076 tools; the paper acknowledges multi-index retrieval as needed future work for larger scales; behavior at 100K+ tool settings (full API marketplaces) is untested and unclaimed. No software artifact is stated as publicly released. Strengths - The offline/online decomposition (one-time merging, query-time retrieval) is architecturally correct for production deployment and cleanly separates compute budgets. - CSR gains of +34.6% and +38.6% on Seal-Tools and UltraTool are large, reproducible across three heterogeneous LLMs (proprietary and open-source), and accompanied by detailed ablations that attribute gains to specific components. - Per-tool and per-dataset compute cost reporting (Table H, Appendix J) enables practitioners to make adoption decisions without running their own cost experiments. - Auto-Correction adds genuine value beyond naive graph clustering: by catching and correcting false-positive merges (e.g., `final_velocity` vs. `calculate_final_speed`), it addresses a real failure mode at low marginal cost (one additional LLM prompt per cluster). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state: retrieval relies only on names, signatures, and descriptions — richer metadata (use cases, domains, I/O schemas) could strengthen ranking in very large toolsets; multi-index retrieval may improve scalability; evaluation with reasoning-oriented models could affect Auto-Correction quality; ToolScope is not suitable for deployment in medical, legal, or financial settings where tool selection errors cause significant harm; LLM-generated merging is probabilistic and subject to hallucination, bias, and misclassification of overlapping tools. Authors confront these limitations directly and do not minimize them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The E2E evaluation (20 queries, Table 12) is the paper's primary evidence that CSR gains translate to task-level gains, but 20 queries yields confidence intervals wide enough to encompass no improvement; this claim needs an order-of-magnitude more queries. - Baselines (BM25, Dense) are evaluated on original unmerged toolsets, while ToolScope is evaluated on merged ones. A fairer ablation would apply the same retrieval pipeline (decomposition + reranking) to the unmerged toolset, isolating the merger's contribution from the retrieval improvements; Table 3 does this partially but not with the same baselines used in Table 2. - Ground-truth relabeling (Eq. 3) replaces merged tool clusters with a single canonical label in the benchmark — this can inflate CSR if merging is too aggressive, because a query previously requiring two distinct tools now only needs one. The paper does not analyze whether this labeling effect biases the metric. - Auto-Correction uses GPT-4o exclusively; the LLM ablation (Appendix L) only swaps the graph-merger model, not the Auto-Correction validator — the claim of model-independence does not fully extend to the correction stage. - Functionality preservation (TCCR, UCC) measures argument-set containment, not behavioral equivalence; two tools with identical signatures but different semantics would pass this check, leaving undetected capability loss. Bottom Line Practitioners building LLM agents over large, redundancy-prone toolsets will find ToolScope immediately deployable and cost-effective — the retrieval gains are large and the ablations are credible — but the 20-query end-to-end evaluation is too small to reliably confirm that selection accuracy translates to downstream task success, leaving the most important claim inadequately supported.
Citations
#82 — Innovative (68)

Spherical Mixture Integration for Latent Embedding Alignment across Multi-Source Feature Spaces

arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SMILE (Spherical Mixture Integration for Latent Embeddings), a probabilistic framework that jointly aligns heterogeneous multi-source EHR embeddings into a shared latent space while simultaneously recovering synonym clusters via a mixture of von Mises-Fisher distributions. - Why it's important: Multi-institutional EHR studies cannot be conducted by naively pooling data because each institution uses partially overlapping, incompatible clinical code vocabularies, and existing multi-view methods require aligned feature spaces that do not exist in this setting. - How it does it: Four coupled model components — a multi-source latent factor model, a vMF spherical mixture for synonym groups, and two logistic models for auxiliary relational knowledge graph pairs — are optimized jointly via a composite quasi-likelihood with an alternating EM algorithm. - The good: On real MGB/VA EHR data (7,448 features, 4 embedding sources), SMILE lifts relatedness AUC from 0.7762 to 0.9455 (22% relative gain over the strongest baseline, SLIDE) and improves clustering AMI by 67–185% across code categories relative to all competing methods. - The missing: The paper's theoretical guarantees require initialization "in a neighborhood of the truth" — a local convergence condition that is standard but non-constructive; the paper does not prove that the proposed warm-start initialization actually satisfies this condition under realistic data regimes. - Read it or not: Worth reading for statisticians and clinical informatics researchers building multi-institutional EHR pipelines; the clustering gains are large enough to matter in practice, but the absence of released code and an untested ablation separating model design from auxiliary data access will frustrate practitioners trying to adopt or replicate.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Strong contribution but pulled down by Completeness (62) — no ablation isolating model design from auxiliary relational data access, GPT-4 silver labels contaminate both training and evaluation, K=1900 chosen by SVD-friendly metric. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (72) solid — joint vMF + multi-source factor model with non-asymptotic theory in a new regime. Rigor (68) — three theorems but local-convergence condition unproven for proposed init, no code. Impact (55) — strong domain method but no software artifact and unproven scalability past ~7K features. The 67–185% AMI gains are large enough to matter.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 62 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multi-institutional EHR studies pool data from health systems that independently develop partially overlapping clinical code vocabularies — ICD codes mapped to PheCodes, medications to RxNorm, labs to LOINC, plus institution-specific local codes (e.g., VA-specific lab groupings). Two interdependent obstacles prevent direct integration: (1) synonymy — multiple codes across institutions may encode the same clinical concept but are represented as distinct high-dimensional features, and (2) feature-space misalignment — each institution's code vocabulary defines its own embedding space. Restricting analysis to shared features discards information; ad hoc aggregation introduces measurement error; and manual ontology mappings are incomplete. Privacy constraints prohibit sharing patient-level records, so institutions exchange only summary embedding representations (PMI-SVD co-occurrence embeddings or pretrained language model representations). Existing multi-view integration methods (JIVE, AJIVE, SLIDE) decompose variation into shared and view-specific structure but require a common aligned feature coordinate system, which is unavailable when vocabularies only partially overlap. No prior unified framework jointly addresses representation alignment and synonym recovery under partial vocabulary overlap and many-to-one correspondences. Key Idea The key technical insight is that synonym groups can be directly encoded in the geometry of the latent space by modeling the shared representations as draws from a mixture of von Mises-Fisher distributions on the unit hypersphere — each cluster is a tight directional concentration around a concept mean direction — while simultaneously fitting a multi-source linear projection from heterogeneous institutional embeddings to this shared sphere. Coupling both tasks in a single composite quasi-likelihood objective means that relational supervision from auxiliary knowledge graphs propagates through the latent geometry to sharpen both alignment and clustering, rather than the two tasks pulling against each other when solved sequentially. Methodology SMILE specifies a joint generative model with four components. (1) Multi-source latent factor model: U_i^(l) = W_l V_i + E_i^(l), where V_i ∈ ℝ^r (unit-norm) is the shared latent representation, W_l ∈ ℝ^{r_l × r} is a source-specific loading matrix, and E_i^(l) is sub-Gaussian noise; this projects source-specific embeddings of dimension up to 1500 into a shared r-dimensional space (r=60 in the real experiment). (2) vMF mixture: V_i ~ f_r(x; μ_{z_i}, κ) with K clusters, unit-norm mean directions {μ_k}, and common concentration κ; cluster membership z_i encodes synonym group assignment. (3) Similarity logistic model: P(δ_{ij}^(S)=1 | μ_{z_i}, μ_{z_j}) is a logistic function of the inner product of cluster means, encoding coarse concept-level synonymy signals. (4) Relatedness logistic model: P(δ_{ij}^(R)=1 | V_i, V_j) uses a bilinear form V_i^T R V_j to capture fine-grained functional associations (e.g., disease–treatment pairs) across cluster boundaries. Estimation minimizes a weighted sum of four normalized losses: ℓ_lr + w_{vmf} ℓ_{vmf} + w_{sim} ℓ_{sim} + w_{rel} ℓ_{rel}, solved via an alternating block procedure that alternates between EM updates for vMF cluster parameters (fixing embeddings) and gradient-based refinement of embeddings and loading matrices (fixing cluster parameters). Prior probabilities {π_{ik}} softly encode partial ontology knowledge. Three critical design choices: (a) composite quasi-likelihood avoids specifying the full joint distribution across heterogeneous data types while remaining tractable; (b) soft prior probabilities allow partial ontology knowledge to supervise without imposing hard constraints; (c) initialization via matrix factorization then spherical k-means to satisfy the well-initialized assumption required by the theory. Key Results Simulation: n=1000 features, K∈{50,100} clusters, L∈{2,3,4} sources, r=6, 100 Monte Carlo runs. SMILE achieves relative accuracy of latent embeddings ~0.70 vs. ~0.60 for AJIVE/SVD; AMI ~0.75–0.85 vs. ~0.45–0.50 for the best competing method. SMILE dominates all settings; gains increase with more sources and higher vMF concentration. Competing methods (SVD, AJIVE, SLIDE) require imputed embeddings for the partial-overlap setting, which disadvantages them. Real data (MGB + VA, 7,448 total features: 1,864 PheCode, 2,678 RxNorm, 998 LOINC, 1,814 VA OtherLab, 94 ShortName; L=4 embedding sources: MGB-PMI 1500-dim, VA-PMI 1500-dim, SapBERT 768-dim, BGE 1024-dim; r=60; K=1,900 clusters; ~6% of code pairs labeled as relational pairs): Similarity AUC test: SMILE=0.8878 vs. SLIDE=0.8828 (marginal). Relatedness AUC test: SMILE=0.9455 vs. SLIDE=0.7762 (22% relative gain — the largest improvement). Clustering AMI: PheCode SMILE=0.3994 vs. AJIVE=0.2395 (+67%); RxNorm SMILE=0.1703 vs. AJIVE=0.0936 (+82%); Lab SMILE=0.1597 vs. SLIDE=0.0718 (+122%); VA local lab SMILE=0.1659 vs. SLIDE=0.0582 (+185%). Spearman correlation with GPT-4 disease relevance rankings: SMILE=0.4407 vs. SVD=0.4283. Baselines are strong and current; the comparison is held out on a clean 20% test split. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The novelty is the joint generative framework coupling multi-source latent factor modeling with vMF synonym clustering and bilinear relational supervision in a single composite quasi-likelihood, with non-asymptotic theory for the EHR-specific regime (high cluster count, small cluster sizes, partial vocabulary overlap). Individual components — vMF mixtures, latent factor models, logistic relational models — are each standard; the contribution is their principled integration and the theoretical analysis of their combined estimation behavior in a previously uncharacterized regime. Rigor & Reproducibility Three theorems provide non-asymptotic recovery bounds. Theorem 1 bounds average squared error of latent embeddings; the rate is a minimum over three terms from multi-view embeddings, relational pairs, and vMF concentration, formalizing that any informative channel tightens the bound. Theorem 2 bounds cluster mean direction error. Theorem 3 establishes clustering consistency (misclassification rate → 0) given a vanishing proportion of features near the decision boundary margin. All three require initialization in a neighborhood of the truth (Definition 1), which is a standard but local convergence condition for non-convex M-estimators; the authors do not prove that the warm-start initialization reaches this neighborhood. Simulations run 100 Monte Carlo replications with error bars. The real-data analysis uses clean train/val/test splits with test held out until final evaluation and hyperparameters selected on the Pareto frontier of validation AMI and AUC. Code and data are not stated to be publicly released. GPT-4-generated silver-standard labels constitute part of both training supervision and evaluation targets, with the degree of circular contamination unquantified. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on standard hardware; the alternating EM with gradient-based block updates is implementable in any autodiff framework; the real experiment at 7,448 features and 1,900 clusters is computationally feasible. - Efficient: No wall-clock timings are reported. At 6% pair density over 7,448 features, the relational loss evaluates over ~1.65M pairs per iteration, which is manageable but would become a bottleneck for vocabularies an order of magnitude larger; no complexity analysis is provided. - Effective: Strong evidence on the clustering task and relatedness discrimination; the 22% relatedness AUC lift is the most compelling signal. Similarity AUC gains are marginal, suggesting the improvement is concentrated in cross-cluster relational structure rather than within-cluster synonymy alignment. - Scalable: Tested at n=7,448 features; no experiment above this scale. The theory admits n→∞ asymptotics, but 6% relational pair density at 100K+ vocabulary sizes is computationally and data-collection-wise implausible without approximation. No software artifact released. Strengths - Joint formulation closes a methodological gap that all existing multi-view integration methods miss: they require aligned feature spaces, while SMILE handles partial overlap natively without imputation. - Non-asymptotic theory for the high-cluster-number, small-cluster-size, multi-view regime is the first of its kind for EHR harmonization; the three-channel bound formalizes why integrating multiple information sources helps. - Composite quasi-likelihood is a principled solution to the otherwise intractable problem of specifying a full joint distribution across heterogeneous embedding dimensions and binary relational observations. - Real-data clustering gains (67–185% AMI improvement over strong baselines across all code categories) are large enough to be practically consequential, not marginal. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) the local convergence condition (well-initialized assumption) is standard but requires the initialization to be close to the truth — they acknowledge this as a theoretical limitation without proving the condition holds for their proposed initialization; (2) similarity AUC gains are marginal over baselines, partly because the imputation step that baselines require already fuses complementary information; (3) the framework currently applies only to structured codes and does not incorporate narrative clinical note concepts. Authors engage limitations directly and do not minimize them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The primary comparison is structurally tilted: SMILE receives auxiliary relational pairs (from UMLS and GPT-4-generated labels) that no baseline can access. The main paper does not present an ablation of SMILE without relational supervision against baselines, making it impossible to separate the contribution of the joint generative model from the benefit of additional supervision data. The simulation does show SMILE outperforms with only 4% pairs, but the real-data results still conflate model design and data access. - GPT-4-generated silver-standard labels are used in both training supervision and — partly — as evaluation targets. The contamination magnitude is not estimated. - K=1,900 is selected by Silhouette coefficient applied to SVD embeddings, meaning the number of clusters SMILE is evaluated on was chosen to favor SVD's geometry; sensitivity to K is not examined. - Computational cost and scalability to realistic large-scale vocabularies (hundreds of thousands of codes across a national network) are neither analyzed nor demonstrated. - Warm-initialization requirement is invoked as a proof condition but no guarantee is provided that the matrix-factorization + k-means initialization actually satisfies Definition 1 under realistic signal-to-noise conditions. Bottom Line SMILE delivers the most complete and theoretically grounded solution to joint EHR code alignment and synonym recovery to date, and the 67–185% AMI gains on real MGB/VA data are large enough to matter in practice — but practitioners must contend with the absence of released software, unquantified sensitivity to the GPT-4-derived supervision labels, and a comparison design that does not cleanly isolate model from data advantages.

#83 — Innovative (68)

MulTaBench: Benchmarking Multimodal Tabular Learning with Text and Image

Alan Arazi, Eilam Shapira, Shoham Grunblat, Mor Ventura, Elad Hoffer, Gioia Blayer, David Holzmüller, Lennart Purucker, Gaël Varoquaux, Frank Hutter, Roi Reichart · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MulTaBench, a curated benchmark of 40 datasets (20 image-tabular, 20 text-tabular) for Multimodal Tabular Learning (MMTL), selected by an algorithmic pipeline that enforces two formal desiderata — Joint Signal and Task-awareness — to isolate tasks where frozen embeddings genuinely fail. - Why it's important: Existing MMTL benchmarks mix tasks where frozen off-the-shelf embeddings are adequate with tasks where they are not, obscuring whether task-specific representation tuning actually matters and inflating the apparent competitiveness of generic models. - How it does it: A four-condition curation protocol (Unimodal Structured, Unimodal Unstructured, Joint Frozen, Joint TAR) evaluates each candidate dataset across five tabular learners using LoRA-finetuned encoders (DINO-v3-small for images, e5-v2-small for text) as Target-Aware Representations; only datasets where joint TAR beats joint frozen are admitted. - The good: TAR Small (384-dim) outperforms Frozen Large (~1024-dim) on MulTaBench datasets, providing strong evidence that representational capacity alone does not recover task-critical information — task-aware tuning is structurally necessary, not just marginally helpful. - The missing: The curation pipeline is explicitly entangled with the algorithmic solution (LoRA finetuning), so models used in curation cannot be evaluated on the benchmark without selection bias; the paper acknowledges but does not resolve this circularity. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating Multimodal Tabular Foundation Models should read this — it provides the first benchmark that systematically isolates tasks requiring task-specific representations, with concrete empirical support; practitioners satisfied with frozen-embedding pipelines can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. The two-criterion (Joint Signal + Task-awareness) formalization is a genuine conceptual contribution (Novelty 70, Non-Obviousness 70). Rigor (75) is strong with 13+ learners, 95% CIs, robustness across PCA dims, public code/datasets. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) are well-supported by openly stated circularity (curation models can't be fairly evaluated) and acknowledged finetuning overhead. Impact (60) reflects a useful benchmark artifact, though only bimodal in practice despite trimodal framing. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*60 = 70 → calibrated to 68 given LoRA-entanglement caveat.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs) such as TabPFN-2.5 have surpassed gradient-boosted decision trees on standard structured tabular benchmarks, but they handle unstructured modalities — text columns, product images, medical scans — exclusively through frozen, pretrained embeddings produced by external models. In real-world domains such as e-commerce (product listings with images and descriptions), social media (posts with image and text), and clinical medicine (structured EHR plus imaging), the tabular prediction task depends on fine-grained details that general-purpose embedding models discard: exact pixel-level lung anomalies for CheXpert, fur texture for pet-age estimation, specific wording nuances for persuasion prediction. Existing MMTL benchmarks (Multimodal AutoML Benchmark with 18 datasets, TextTabBench with 13, CARTE with 51, MuG with 4) conflate tasks where frozen embeddings suffice with tasks where they fail, because they select datasets based on co-occurrence of modalities rather than predictive necessity. The consequence is that benchmark scores do not distinguish architectural progress from coincidental compatibility with frozen features, and methods that genuinely learn task-aware representations appear only marginally better than simpler baselines. Key Idea The core insight is that a meaningful MMTL benchmark must actively filter for datasets exhibiting two orthogonal properties: (1) Joint Signal — each unstructured modality provides complementary predictive information that the structured features alone cannot capture, verified empirically by requiring that joint-modality performance exceeds the best unimodal baseline; and (2) Task-awareness — the optimal representation of the unstructured modality is task-dependent at a granularity finer than what general-purpose encoders preserve, verified by requiring that LoRA-finetuned (Target-Aware) embeddings outperform frozen embeddings when passed to the same tabular learners. This converts benchmark construction from a manual curation exercise into an algorithmic test. Methodology The curation pipeline evaluates each candidate dataset under four conditions across five tabular learners: LightGBM, CatBoost, TabM, TabPFNv2, and TabPFN-2.5. Text is embedded with e5-v2-small (384-dim); images with DINO-v3-small (384-dim). Target-Aware Representations are produced by finetuning the top 3 transformer layers via LoRA (r=16, α=32, dropout 0.1) on the prediction target using a linear head and AdamW (lr=1e-4 for e5, 1e-3 for DINO), treating this as a preprocessing step decoupled from the downstream tabular learner. Embeddings are PCA-projected to 30 dimensions before tabular learning. Acceptance requires that (1) Joint Frozen beats both unimodal conditions for ≥3 of 5 learners (Joint Signal), and (2) Joint TAR outperforms Joint Frozen for ≥3 of 5 learners (Task-awareness). Training is capped at 10,000 examples per fold; metrics are AUC for classification and R² for regression. Critical design decisions: (a) LoRA finetuning is performed as preprocessing without the structured features — well-justified to avoid data leakage and to maintain independence from the downstream learner, though it also means TAR cannot leverage cross-modal interactions during adaptation; (b) using a single shared e5 model finetuned jointly across all text columns for multi-text datasets — computationally pragmatic but potentially suboptimal for datasets with semantically heterogeneous text fields; (c) fixed hyperparameters across all datasets — deliberately conservative to avoid per-dataset tuning, meaning reported TAR gains are lower bounds. The final benchmark comprises 40 datasets: 20 image-tabular (curated from Kaggle and literature, 5 of 16 candidate datasets from prior work passed, 31% acceptance rate) and 20 text-tabular (20 selected from 56 aggregated across existing benchmarks, ~41% acceptance rate for both criteria). Dataset sizes range from 400 to 114,000 rows with 1 to 245 structured features. The benchmark is hosted on Kaggle with a unified API. Key Results Across all 40 MulTaBench datasets, TAR consistently outperforms frozen embeddings for every tested tabular learner (Figure 4), with 95% confidence intervals non-overlapping for most models.
