TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Granularity Axis, a contrast-based latent direction in LLM hidden states that encodes the social scale of prompted roles — from micro-level individuals to macro-level national/supranational actors — and demonstrates that this axis is the dominant geometric organizer of role representations and is causally manipulable via activation steering. - Why it's important: LLM-based social simulations and multi-agent systems routinely prompt models into diverse social roles, but if a model internally represents all roles via a shared role-playing template rather than distinguishing their social scale, simulations of multi-stakeholder policy debates will collapse to a single representational perspective, invalidating the premise of the simulation. - How it does it: The axis is defined as the vector difference between mean macro-role and mean micro-role hidden states (layers 8–35 in Qwen3-8B), validated by showing it aligns with PC1 of the role representation space at cosine 0.972, and probed causally by adding the axis vector to generated-token activations during decoding. - The good: The monotonic ordering of role projections across all five granularity levels — including intermediate levels the axis was not constructed from — constitutes a non-trivial interpolation result that rules out memorization of the endpoint pair and supports a genuinely graded latent structure. - The missing: The study is limited to two 8B-class instruction-tuned models; there is no test of whether the axis transfers across model scales (e.g., 70B) or model families (e.g., GPT, Gemini), and the behavioral steering effect is modest and model-dependent (Qwen3-8B is near its macro ceiling on generic prompts, so positive steering is invisible without carefully chosen micro-targeted prompts). - Read it or not: Researchers working on LLM interpretability, representation engineering, or validity of LLM social simulation should read this; the framework is clean and the axis-construction pipeline is reusable, but practitioners expecting a reliable deployment-ready social-scale controller will be disappointed by the partial and model-dependent behavioral control.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — applies known contrast-axis methodology to social-scale dimension; the monotonic interpolation across unseen intermediate levels is the genuine new finding. Rigor (75) — extensive ablations (layer, endpoint, holdout, prompt-template), SEM error bars, human annotator calibration, but only 8B models and single primary LLM judge. Non-Obviousness (65) — that role granularity forms a dominant linear axis is interesting but somewhat predicted by Assistant Axis prior work. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) — paper is unusually honest about saturation, model-dependent steering, and the partial-control caveat. Impact (55) — code released but steering is fragile and model-dependent. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*55 = 68.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models are increasingly used as social simulators — prompted to inhabit roles ranging from a worried parent to a central bank governor. The representational validity of these simulations depends on whether models internally distinguish the social scale (granularity) of roles, not merely their surface label. A model that represents all social roles via a shared role-playing template would produce responses where a central bank governor reasons with the horizon and constraints of a worried parent, a failure mode termed "granularity confusion." This matters specifically in multi-agent simulations where nominally distinct macro- and micro-level agents are supposed to deliberate from structurally different positions. The gap prior work leaves is that interpretability research on role-conditioning has focused on persona consistency and stylistic fidelity, not on whether social scale is encoded as an ordered representational primitive rather than scattered topic-specific clusters. Key Idea: The core insight is that social role granularity — from individual to supranational — should manifest as a single ordered direction in activation space, not as unstructured topic clusters. By analogy to the "Assistant Axis" (which tracks movement away from the default assistant persona), the Granularity Axis is constructed as a simple mean contrast: mean(macro-role activations) − mean(micro-role activations). The non-trivial claim is that this two-endpoint contrast vector should recover intermediate levels it was not built from, and that it should be the dominant axis (PC1) of the entire role representation space — not merely one of many directions. Methodology: The pipeline has five stages. (1) Role taxonomy construction: 75 social roles are manually annotated across five granularity levels (15 per level) — Individual, Group/Community, Organization/Meso, Institution/Systemic, Nation/Super-Actor. (2) Response collection: each role is paired with 5 prompt variants (varying instruction style) and 240 shared general-question prompts from the Assistant Axis study, yielding 75×5×240 = 90,000 role-conditioned responses plus 1,200 default-assistant responses (91,200 total) from Qwen3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. (3) Role-level representation: hidden states are mean-pooled over assistant-turn tokens per response, then averaged over the response set per role, yielding one role vector per layer. (4) Axis construction: g^(ℓ) = mean(macro-role vectors) − mean(micro-role vectors) at layer ℓ, using levels {4,5} as macro and {1,2} as micro endpoints. (5) Causal probe via activation steering: during generation, h̃_t = h_t + α·g at the intervention layer, where α ∈ {−4, 0, +4} for main experiments and up to ±36 in the appendix sweep. Validation uses PCA of the 75×d role-vector matrix, checking whether g aligns with the first principal component (cosine similarity and variance explained), and whether projections onto g rise monotonically with level. Behavioral assessment uses gpt-5.4-mini as an LLM judge scoring outputs on a 1–5 granularity_overall rubric, with human annotator calibration (n=72, 3 graduate annotators). Critical design decisions: (a) Averaging over prompt variants rather than treating variants as distinct tasks — justified as a robustness factor and validated by showing all five variants individually remain monotonic; (b) Mean-contrast axis construction rather than a learned linear probe — justified by connection to the Assistant Axis prior work and the goal of interpretability without supervised training; (c) Using layers 8–35 (stable regime) and specifically Layer 18 for main experiments — selected empirically via layer-wise PCA alignment sweep rather than arbitrarily. Key Results: In Qwen3-8B, the Granularity Axis aligns with PC1 at cosine 0.9720 and accounts for 52.57% of role-space variance (Table 1). Spearman correlation of level-mean projections with the five-level ordering is 0.9472, Pearson 0.9414. Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct shows cosine 0.9596 to PC1 with 42.46% variance explained, Spearman 0.9459. Mean projections rise monotonically L1→L5 in both models, though with saturation between L4 and L5 (Qwen: 0.54, 11.11, 17.25, 22.61, 23.26; Llama: −0.88, 1.21, 2.14, 3.21, 3.34). Robustness holds across layers 8–35 (Qwen) and 6–31 (Llama), alternative endpoint definitions (cosine ≥0.93 with PC1 in all variants), prompt holdout (Spearman ≈0.95), and score-filtered subsets. Role holdout is slightly more fragile in Qwen (monotonicity not perfectly preserved). Domain and family controls mostly hold except for health and economy, which fail monotonicity in some settings. For steering: Qwen3-8B on generic prompts shows negligible effect (+0.075 at α=+4, from a near-ceiling baseline of 4.900/5), but on micro-targeted prompts shows +0.500 shift (2.000→2.667, 0% degeneration). Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct shows larger shifts on micro-targeted prompts (+1.167 at α=+4, 2.000→3.167, 8.3% degeneration) but also degeneration under negative steering on generic prompts (42.5% at α=−4). Human annotators agree with the LLM judge direction in all four experimental cells, with Llama micro pairwise rate 0.861 [0.76, 0.92] and Llama generic 0.903 [0.81, 0.95]. Baseline control directions (random vectors, Assistant Axis) do not reproduce the micro-macro shift, ruling out a generic activation-perturbation artifact. Novelty Classification: NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The genuinely novel contribution is demonstrating that social role granularity forms a dominant, ordered linear direction in LLM activation space (not one direction among many) and that it is causally manipulable. The contrast-and-project pipeline is adapted from the Assistant Axis work; the novelty lies in applying it to an ordered social-scale dimension, establishing the ordering property across intermediate levels not used in construction, and providing the causal (steering) probe. The 75-role taxonomy with five ordered granularity levels is a secondary dataset contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are extensive: layer-wise stability, endpoint ablations, held-out prompt/question/role splits, prompt-template variants, score filtering, role-type controls, domain and family subgroup controls. These collectively make the representation result credibly robust. Behavioral steering results are more fragile and the paper is appropriately cautious, framing steering as a partial causal probe rather than a reliable control method, and reporting degeneration rates explicitly. Error bars are reported as SEM over prompts throughout. The judge reliability section compares gpt-5.4-mini with gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview and human annotators, with inter-rater Krippendorff α ≈ 0.61–0.78. Code and data are described as available at "Granularity-Axis" (a repository link is noted in the paper's footnote). This is a preprint; the results have not undergone peer review. The main representational findings appear solid; the steering findings are honest about their limitations. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All experiments run on Qwen3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct with hidden-state extraction and decoding-time vector addition — both feasible on a single consumer GPU. - Efficient: Axis construction requires one forward pass per response across 91,200 responses (a significant one-time cost), but inference-time steering adds only a single vector addition per generated token, with negligible overhead. - Effective: The evidence shows the axis reliably controls representational granularity level; behavioral (output-level) control is real but partial — effective primarily when the prompt leaves headroom (micro-targeted prompts), and with model-dependent stability (Qwen stable, Llama more responsive but prone to degeneration under aggressive negative steering). - Scalable: Only 8B-class models are tested; the paper claims the pipeline generalizes across model families but does not test at 70B or larger scales, so scalability is an untested claim. The work delivers a codebase/data release (Granularity-Axis repository) and a reusable pipeline, but no trained model weights or production tool. Strengths: - The monotonic interpolation result — that the two-endpoint contrast vector recovers ordered intermediate levels — is the methodological crux and is genuinely non-trivial; it distinguishes a graded manifold from a dichotomous endpoint memory. - The paper is unusually transparent about when its claims hold and when they fail: the "Headroom Gate" analysis (§4.2) explicitly explains why Qwen3-8B shows weak steering on generic prompts (near-ceiling baseline) rather than treating all steering results as equivalent. - The human-annotator calibration against LLM-judge scores (Likert ρ ∈ [0.58, 0.79], Krippendorff α ∈ [0.61, 0.78]) grounds the behavioral claims in perceptible, human-validated shifts rather than relying solely on LLM-judged metrics. - The theoretical framing — drawing on Coleman, Schelling, Granovetter, and Bronfenbrenner — motivates the granularity dimension from sociological theory rather than from a purely ad hoc role taxonomy, giving the ordered levels conceptual grounding. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) The contrast axis finds the dominant direction between chosen endpoints, not every direction along which roles differ — correlated dimensions (authority, formality, time horizon) may collapse into one axis, and the paper does not decompose residual structure. (2) Claims are bounded by the 8B-scale instruction-tuned models and 75-role manual taxonomy. (3) Behavioral control is partial and model-dependent: a direction can be representationally robust while behaviorally fragile. (4) The L4–L5 saturation region is acknowledged as a limitation of the current framework. The authors frame these as invitations for multi-axis extensions and broader-scale tests. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The role taxonomy is manually constructed by the researchers and then used as the ground truth for the ordering experiment. The paper does not validate the taxonomy against an independent annotation scheme or social-science gold standard; if experts disagree on which level a "Hospital Administrator" occupies, the level-membership noise propagates into the representation results. - Behavioral evaluation uses a single LLM judge (gpt-5.4-mini) as primary with only a secondary check from gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview; neither judge has been validated against expert human ratings for the specific granularity_overall rubric, and LLM judges are known to exhibit prompt-format sensitivity that could correlate with the steering manipulation. - The paper does not test whether the axis is decomposable from related social dimensions (power/authority, formality, temporal horizon) — the authors acknowledge these correlate but do not show whether residual-orthogonal analysis recovers distinct directions, so the claim that "social scale" specifically is encoded (rather than a composite of correlated features) remains partially unresolved. - All steering experiments use greedy decoding; it is unknown whether the effect size or degeneration rate changes under sampling, which is more typical for role-conditioned generation in deployment. Bottom Line: For researchers in LLM interpretability or the validity of social simulation, this paper delivers a clean, well-validated finding — social role granularity is genuinely encoded as a dominant ordered direction in 8B-class LLM activations — though the practical social-scale control it unlocks remains constrained by model-specific saturation and degeneration thresholds that limit its current applicability.
Recommended 2605.06196 PDF
#90 — Innovative (65)

OLIVIA: Online Learning via Inference-time Action Adaptation for Decision Making in LLM ReAct Agents

Sheldon Yu, Junda Wu, Xintong Li, Nikki Lijing Kuang, Sizhe Zhou, Tong Yu, Jiawei Han, Jingbo Shang, Julian McAuley · arxiv.org · Julian McAuley - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OLIVIA, an inference-time online adaptation framework that augments frozen ReAct-style LLM agents with a lightweight contextual linear bandit decision layer that learns to select better actions from step-level deployment feedback without modifying the backbone model. - Why it's important: LLM agents deployed repeatedly on related tasks accumulate errors silently — the same suboptimal tool choices recur because static prompting cannot learn from interaction history, wasting compute and degrading reliability in production. - How it does it: OLIVIA recognizes that the frozen LLM's output projection over candidate actions is already a linear scorer (s₀(u,a) = wₐᵀu), and replaces the fixed weights with per-action parameters updated online via LinUCB with Sherman–Morrison rank-one updates using the last-layer hidden state as context. - The good: OLIVIA consistently outperforms both static ReAct and the strongest prompt-based adaptation baseline (CLIN) across seven of eight benchmark-model combinations, with gains up to +0.065 F1 on ToolBench/Qwen and +0.071 F1 on BFCL/Qwen over CLIN, converging faster in learning curves. - The missing: The step-level reward signal requires a ground-truth tool multiset at each deployment step, which is a strong supervision assumption unlikely to hold in real production deployments without explicit annotation infrastructure. - Read it or not: Read this if you build or study deployed LLM agents and want a principled, modular alternative to prompt-based continual adaptation — skip it if your deployment setting cannot provide step-level binary feedback.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) is the strongest dimension — the conceptual reframing of a frozen LLM's action-selection head as a linear contextual bandit, with LLM-aligned initialization, is a clean and non-obvious formulation. Rigor (60) — three seeds with shaded bands, informative reward-design and exploration ablations, plus a synthetic linear validation; but no code release, no significance tests, and a documented inconsistency between Table 1 and prose. Non-Obviousness (75) for the bandit-in-the-LLM insight. Completeness (55) and Resilience (55) reduced by the strong ground-truth-tool-multiset supervision assumption, absolute F1 still low (0.21–0.59), and no PEFT/RL baselines. Impact (55) — no released artifact, per-action O(d²) state limits large catalogs. Composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 ≈ 63.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 55 Resilience 55 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents operating as sequential decision makers in tool-use, API orchestration, and enterprise workflow settings must choose among many candidate actions at each step based on task context and evolving trajectory. In repeated deployments — where agents handle streams of related tasks — small action-selection errors compound: the agent repeats unproductive tool calls, burns interaction budget on local mistakes, and never corrects them because prompt-guided policies are static. Existing inference-time adaptation methods (reflection, memory augmentation, retrieval, dynamic in-context examples) operate indirectly through context manipulation and lack an explicit, updatable decision mechanism that can score individual candidate actions, quantify per-action uncertainty, or learn from step-level feedback. This gap leaves deployment-time improvement heuristic and untrackable. Key Idea The key insight is that a frozen LLM's action-selection mechanism is already a linear contextual scorer: at the token position preceding an action decision, the last-layer hidden state u ∈ ℝᵈ encodes the full task and trajectory context, and the output projection head scores each candidate action a as s₀(u,a) = wₐᵀu where wₐ is the action's embedding row. This is exactly the structure of a linear contextual bandit. By replacing the frozen weight wₐ with a learnable parameter θₐ initialized from the LLM's own embedding and updated online from observed step-level rewards, OLIVIA converts the action-selection interface into a principled online learner without touching the LLM's reasoning process, prompts, or backbone parameters. Methodology OLIVIA operates at the action-selection interface of a frozen LLM running the ReAct loop. At each decision step t of episode e, the backbone generates a reasoning trace and produces the last-layer hidden state u_{e,t} = h_LLM(q_e, h_{e,t-1}) ∈ ℝᵈ. OLIVIA maintains per-action sufficient statistics (A_a ∈ ℝᵈˣᵈ, b_a ∈ ℝᵈ), with θ̂_a = A_a⁻¹b_a as the current reward parameter estimate. Actions are scored via LinUCB: UCB(a) = θ̂_aᵀu + α√(uᵀA_a⁻¹u), where the second term is the uncertainty bonus. Three design decisions are central: (1) LLM-aligned initialization — A_a⁽⁰⁾ = I, b_a⁽⁰⁾ = embed(a), so the controller inherits the LLM's semantic priors before any feedback is observed; (2) Step-level binary reward — r_{e,t} = 1[a_{e,t} ∈ G_e^rem], where G_e^rem is the unmatched portion of the ground-truth tool multiset, providing direct credit assignment rather than conflating all decisions via episode-level reward; (3) Sherman–Morrison rank-one updates — each step requires O(d²) computation with no matrix inversion, making updates lightweight. The valid action set shrinks within an episode when a tool has been selected its maximum allowed times, preventing degenerate repetition. The paper instantiates OLIVIA on tool-use benchmarks where hidden states, rewards, and updates couple across trajectory steps because each action changes the subsequent reasoning history. Key Results OLIVIA is evaluated on four tool-use benchmarks (ToolBench, TaskBench, TaskBench-MM, BFCL) with two open-weight backbones (Qwen/Qwen3-4B, Mistral-7B-v0.1). Static baselines are CoT, BM25, and ReAct; online baselines are Bandit (no LLM alignment, no UCB) and CLIN (memory-based continual adaptation). All methods use identical prompts and few-shot demonstrations; results are mean/std over three random seeds with shuffled episode streams. Key F1 numbers (preprint, not peer-reviewed): OLIVIA outperforms CLIN on six of eight benchmark-model combinations — ToolBench/Qwen (0.585 vs 0.553), TaskBench-MM/Qwen (0.178 vs 0.170), BFCL/Qwen (0.366 vs 0.307), ToolBench/Mistral (0.420 vs 0.394), TaskBench/Mistral (0.208 vs 0.193), BFCL/Mistral (0.352 vs 0.324). CLIN edges OLIVIA on TaskBench/Qwen (0.211 vs 0.205). Learning curves show OLIVIA converges faster than CLIN in all settings. A synthetic validation experiment with known linear reward structure confirms OLIVIA recovers the true parameter θ* and achieves lower cumulative regret than weaker baselines. Reward-design ablations show step-level feedback (F1 0.536, regret 8.2) substantially outperforms final-only reward (F1 0.323, regret 56.3); combining both signals (F1 0.536, regret 8.3) yields marginal improvement over step-only. The exploration coefficient α shows a moderate range is best; both under- and over-exploration hurt. Absolute F1 values across all methods remain low (many in the 0.10–0.35 range), reflecting the difficulty of multi-tool selection tasks. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. The genuine novelty is the identification that a frozen LLM's action-selection interface is structurally a linear contextual bandit, enabling principled online adaptation from step-level feedback. The components — LinUCB, hidden-state extraction, Sherman–Morrison updates — are each standard; the contribution is the problem formulation and the modular integration at the action-selection boundary without modifying the LLM. Applying contextual bandits to LLM tool selection has been explored, but the LLM-aligned initialization and the formal argument that the frozen projection head constitutes the bandit's scoring function are new. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: the reward-design study (step vs. final vs. both), the exploration coefficient sweep, and the synthetic linear-reward validation all provide mechanistic evidence. Three random seeds with shuffled episode orders are used, and standard deviations appear in figures (shaded bands). The paper does not report statistical significance tests. Code and model weights are not publicly released — no repository URL is mentioned. A methodological concern: the bandit's linear model assumes the hidden-state representation encodes action quality linearly, which is an approximation. The bandit update rule ignores trajectory dependence (later hidden states depend on earlier actions), treating each step as i.i.d. when they are not. The evaluation streams episodes from a fixed dataset rather than from a live production system, which may overestimate the stationarity of the underlying context distribution. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper builds and evaluates a concrete system on four tool-use benchmarks. - Practical: Runs on commodity open-weight models (4B and 7B parameter) with no gradient computation — the only added cost is O(d²) per-step matrix arithmetic, which is negligible relative to LLM forward passes. - Efficient: The learned state is per-action statistics matrices in ℝᵈˣᵈ; for d = 4096 and, say, 100 candidate tools, this is ~1.6GB of persistent state, which is acceptable but non-trivial and grows with action-space size. - Effective: The evidence shows OLIVIA improves F1 over static ReAct and over CLIN on most benchmark-model pairs, but absolute F1 values remain low (best case 0.585), meaning the approach meaningfully improves a still-imperfect system. - Scalable: Not evaluated beyond ~1,000 episodes or tool sets larger than those in the four benchmarks; the per-action O(d²) state and the assumption that similar decision states recur both face pressure as tool catalogs grow into the thousands. The work delivers a methodology and experimental results; no software artifact or library is released. Strengths - The formalization that a frozen LLM's action-selection interface is a linear contextual bandit is a clean and non-obvious conceptual contribution that enables principled online learning without fine-tuning. - LLM-aligned initialization (bootstrapping from action embeddings) is a practically important design choice that prevents cold-start degradation and is well-motivated and validated by the faster early convergence seen in learning curves. - Step-level binary reward decomposition provides dense, local credit assignment that the ablation study shows is far superior to episode-level reward (F1 0.536 vs 0.323), and the design is grounded in the contextual bandit partial-feedback model. - The synthetic linear-reward validation decouples algorithmic correctness from benchmark noise, providing stronger evidence that OLIVIA actually recovers the underlying reward structure rather than exploiting benchmark artifacts. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that OLIVIA is instantiated only on tool-use benchmarks where the trajectory-dependent coupling between steps is present but manageable, and they note that extending the framework to settings with non-stationary reward distributions or much larger action spaces is an open question. The conclusion concedes that "deployment-time adaptation can be achieved without model fine-tuning" but does not claim parity with fine-tuned approaches. The paper acknowledges the LLM-aligned initialization assumption and the approximation of treating the full LLM as a linear bandit rather than modeling the complete system. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The step-level reward signal r_{e,t} = 1[a_{e,t} ∈ G_e^rem] requires access to the ground-truth tool multiset at each deployment step — a strong supervision assumption. In real deployments, such ground truth is typically unavailable; the paper does not discuss how OLIVIA degrades under noisy, delayed, or partial reward signals. - OLIVIA loses to CLIN on TaskBench/Qwen (0.205 vs 0.211), yet the paper text states OLIVIA reaches "higher final F1 across all four benchmarks" — this is inconsistent with Table 1 and represents a minor overstatement the authors do not flag. - All baselines are prompt-only or memory-based; no comparison against parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods (LoRA, adapter tuning) or offline RL/RLHF baselines is provided, leaving the upper bound on what is achievable with the same feedback signal unestablished. - The per-action O(d²) state assumption becomes expensive with large hidden dimensions and large action catalogs (e.g., thousands of API tools); dimensionality reduction or shared structure across actions is not explored. - Evaluation is confined to tool-selection tasks with discrete, enumerable action sets and ground-truth tool multisets — generalization to open-ended action spaces (web browsing, code execution, natural-language actions) is not addressed. - The paper does not measure wall-clock overhead relative to baseline ReAct in a realistic deployment, making it hard to assess the computational cost of maintaining per-action covariance matrices in practice. Bottom Line OLIVIA offers a principled, modular, and computationally cheap mechanism for improving LLM agent tool selection across repeated deployments, and the clean formalization of the bandit-in-the-LLM insight is worth reading for researchers working on deployed agent systems — but practitioners should be aware that the ground-truth step-level reward requirement is a deployment prerequisite that significantly limits real-world applicability as stated.
Recommended 2605.11169 PDF
#91 — Innovative (65)

TacoMAS: Test-Time Co-Evolution of Topology and Capability in LLM -based Multi-Agent Systems

Chen Xu, Yicheng Hu, Ruizi Wang, Xinyu Lin, Wenjie Wang, Dongrui Liu, Fuli Feng · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TacoMAS, a test-time co-evolution framework that jointly adapts agent capabilities (fast loop, every round) and communication topology (slow loop, every K rounds) within a single LLM-based multi-agent inference instance. - Why it's important: Existing test-time MAS methods adapt only one axis — topology or capability — leaving exploitable complementarity on the table; jointly optimizing both is necessary to escape topology-induced performance plateaus. - How it does it: A two-timescale replicator–mutator process: a meta-judge scores each agent's contribution each round to drive capability refinement via memory updates, while a meta-LLM proposes bounded birth-death and edge-edit operations on the agent graph every K rounds. - The good: TacoMAS achieves +22.8/+5.7/+7.5/+17.3 percentage-point accuracy gains over the strongest per-dataset baseline across four benchmarks, outperforming all 20 baselines on every dataset, with an inference-time scaling curve that continues rising where the best topology-only baseline (SelfOrg) plateaus. - The missing: The meta-LLM (Gemini-2.5-pro) is strictly more capable than the agent backbone (Gemini-2.5-flash-lite), making it impossible to isolate whether gains come from co-evolution mechanics or from the stronger controller model. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers designing adaptive or self-improving multi-agent systems — the evolutionary game-theoretic framing and two-timescale convergence result are genuinely useful conceptual scaffolding, but practitioners should be aware the theoretical assumptions are weakly verified empirically.
Why this rank: Scored Empirical. Novelty (72) — two-timescale co-evolution with replicator-mutator framing is genuinely new for test-time MAS. Rigor (55) pulled down by meta-LLM/agent capability asymmetry confound (Gemini-2.5-pro vs flash-lite), absent error bars on Table 1, and weak Assumption 1 verification (positive-increment probability barely above 0.5). Non-Obviousness (72) fast-slow decoupling earned by theory. Completeness (65) limitations acknowledged. Resilience (55) — the meta-LLM confound and circular assumption-verification mean key gains may not survive controlled ablation. Impact (60) code released, no library, API cost not quantified. Composite ≈ 0.20·72 + 0.25·55 + 0.15·72 + 0.15·65 + 0.15·55 + 0.10·60 = 62.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 65 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) for complex real-world tasks — financial analysis, planning, web retrieval — requires deciding both which roles agents play and how they communicate. Prior training-time approaches fix these choices before deployment, making them brittle to distribution shift at inference. Test-time approaches exist but commit to a single update axis: either topology rewiring (SelfOrg, ChatDev-Puppeteer) or capability updating (CORAL). The core problem is that optimizing one axis while holding the other fixed creates ceilings: a well-tuned capability under a bad topology, or a well-structured topology with undertrained agents, both leave performance on the table. Naïvely updating both at the same rate destabilizes the system, because rapid topology changes make learned capability states stale. No prior work simultaneously and stably adapts both within a single inference instance. Key Idea: Decouple capability and topology evolution onto two timescales — a fast replicator loop that refines agent memory/prompts every round under a fixed topology, and a slow mutator loop that restructures the agent graph every K rounds once capabilities have reached a local plateau. This fast-slow separation, grounded in evolutionary game theory, allows the fast loop to track a quasi-stationary equilibrium under the current structure before the slow loop proposes bounded structural mutations to escape topology-induced plateaus. Methodology: TacoMAS represents the MAS as a directed agent graph G_t = (T_t, Φ_t) where T_t = (V_t, E_t) encodes topology and Φ_t = {φ_{v,t}} encodes per-agent capability states (prompt + memory + tools). Two operators update this graph. The fast capability loop F^C runs every execution round: a meta-judge evaluates each agent's per-round trajectory and produces a numerical contribution score c_{v,t}; a meta-LLM diagnoses agent-specific errors and writes refinement signals into each agent's contextual memory for the next round. Formally, capability update follows a discrete replicator: φ_{v,t+1} ∝ φ_{v,t} exp(η(c_{v,t} − c̄_t)). The slow topology loop F^T fires every K rounds: if the current answer score s_t < threshold τ, the meta-LLM reviews the full trajectory and proposes a structural delta ΔT = (ΔV, ΔE) consisting of birth-death operations on agents and edge additions/removals, subject to budget constraints |ΔV| ≤ B_V and |ΔE| ≤ B_E. Critical design decisions: (1) edit budgets prevent abrupt topological shifts from destroying accumulated capability refinements — justified by the convergence theorem but the specific budget values (B_V = 2 pairs, B_E = 4) are set empirically; (2) K = 2 is chosen as the slow-update interval — ablated in Figure 3(a); (3) initial graph is fixed as a 5-agent centralized template (planner, searcher, calculator, verifier, reflector) shared with the fixed-topology baseline, ensuring improvements are attributable to evolution rather than initialization advantage. Theoretical analysis (Section 4, Appendix A) models the joint process as a replicator–mutator system and constructs a Lyapunov function L(Φ, T) combining topology distance to a locally stable configuration set and fitness gap; Theorem 2 shows E[L(Φ_{t+1}, T_{t+1}) | H_t] ≤ (1-γ)L(Φ_t, T_t) + ε̃ under three assumptions about bounded noise, biased meta-LLM edits, and bounded contribution variance. Key Results: On four benchmarks — finance (SEC filing analysis), browsecomp-plus (multi-hop web retrieval), plancraft (Minecraft feasibility planning), workbench (tool-use workplace tasks) — TacoMAS scores 0.767, 0.745, 0.887, and 0.824 accuracy respectively, versus best per-dataset baselines of 0.539 (SAS), 0.688 (SelfOrg), 0.812 (SwarmAgentic), and 0.645 (ADAS), yielding absolute gains of +22.8, +5.7, +7.5, and +17.3 percentage points. The field of 20 baselines spans fixed-topology (MetaGPT, AFlow, AgentSquare), per-instance graph design (AgentVerse, ARG-Designer, MaAS, MetaAgent, SwarmAgentic, MetaGen, EvolveRouter), and within-instance evolution methods (ChatDev-Puppeteer, SelfOrg, CORAL). All baselines use the same base LLM (Gemini-2.5-flash-lite) and dataset-specific tools. Figure 3(d) shows TacoMAS continues improving accuracy as LLM calls increase from 20 to 140, while SelfOrg saturates around 40 calls — supporting an inference-time scaling claim. Figure 3(a) ablates K: same-speed (K=1) and struct-fixed (K=∞) both underperform the default K=2 schedule. Figure 4 shows consistent pre→post-evolution improvement across Gemini-2.5-flash-lite (0.42→0.77), Claude Haiku-3.5 (0.43→0.73), and GPT-4o-mini (0.44→0.72) as agent backbones, and across Gemini-2.5-pro/Flash/Flash-Lite as meta-LLM backbones. The paper is a preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The core novelty is the two-timescale co-evolution design applied to test-time MAS adaptation — combining fast replicator dynamics with slow bounded structural mutation within a single inference pass. The evolutionary game-theoretic framing of MAS inference is genuinely novel for this community, though the individual components (contextual memory refinement, graph topology editing, replicator dynamics) are each borrowed from existing literature. The empirical finding that jointly adapting both axes dominates either axis alone — with measurable inference-time scaling — is a substantive finding that goes beyond an engineering combination. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover the fast/slow update interval K, initial team size, and backbone model. No error bars or significance tests are reported for the main Table 1 accuracy numbers, which is a meaningful gap given the performance differences (e.g., +5.7pp on browsecomp-plus is modest and could plausibly fall within run variance). The appendix provides assumption verification (Appendix C.3) showing that the mean round-to-round contribution increment is positive across all four benchmarks, but the effect is weak: the probability of a positive increment ranges from 0.44 to 0.51 across benchmarks — barely above chance — providing only nominal support for Assumption 1. Code is available at https://github.com/chenxu2-gif/TacoMAS-MultiAgent. Model weights are not released (the system uses API-accessed LLMs). Benchmarks (finance, browsecomp-plus, plancraft, workbench) are publicly established datasets with independent rubric judges (GPT-4o-mini for rubric evaluation). Evaluation protocol follows Kim et al. (2025), which is an arXiv preprint, not a published benchmark standard. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs entirely via LLM API calls (Gemini, GPT-4o-mini); no specialized hardware required beyond API access, though the meta-LLM defaults to Gemini-2.5-pro which is substantially more expensive than the agent backbone. - Efficient: TacoMAS requires significantly more LLM calls per instance than static baselines (up to 140 calls versus ~20 for simpler methods); the paper shows these additional calls convert to accuracy gains unlike SelfOrg, but absolute cost per instance is not quantified. - Effective: Evidence on four diverse task types is reasonably strong; all-dataset wins over 20 baselines with the same LLM backbone makes cherry-picking unlikely. - Scalable: Tested at standard benchmark scales (hundreds of instances per dataset); behavior at 10× or 100× harder tasks, longer-horizon planning, or much larger agent teams (beyond |V|_max = 20) is not evaluated. The work delivers a software artifact (GitHub repo) but not a library; it is at paper + code form. Strengths: - The two-timescale fast-slow design is theoretically motivated and empirically ablated, not merely asserted. The convergence proof (Theorem 2) and Lyapunov function construction are complete and self-contained in Appendix A. - The baseline comparison is unusually comprehensive for this field (20 baselines, four benchmark domains, same LLM backbone for all), making the result table credible despite the absence of variance estimates. - The inference-time scaling observation — TacoMAS continues improving with compute while topology-only SelfOrg plateaus — is a genuine empirical finding with practical implications for test-time compute allocation. - Evolution trace visualizations (Figures 2, 5–7) provide interpretable evidence that the meta-LLM learns instance-specific role decompositions rather than applying fixed templates. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note in Appendix B that (1) the centralized single meta-controller may bottleneck on highly decomposable or long-horizon tasks where local information summarization exceeds the meta-LLM's capacity; (2) per-instance memory is cleared between queries, preventing reuse of structural discoveries (e.g., recurring effective role decompositions) across related tasks. The authors suggest hierarchical meta-controllers and cross-instance memory as future directions. These are substantive limitations confronted directly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most important uncontrolled variable is the asymmetry between the agent backbone (Gemini-2.5-flash-lite) and the meta-LLM (Gemini-2.5-pro): every topological decision is made by a qualitatively stronger model. No ablation runs all agents AND the meta-LLM on Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, so it is impossible to determine how much of the gain comes from the co-evolution mechanism versus simply having a more capable planner. The assumption verification (Appendix C.3) is circular in structure: it measures the average contribution increment as a proxy for the meta-LLM bias assumption, but the contribution scores are themselves produced by the meta-judge — verifying that the judge's outputs increase in expectation says nothing about whether true task performance increases monotonically. Main Table 1 reports no variance or confidence intervals; given that some gains (browsecomp-plus: +5.7pp) are small and involve hundreds not thousands of instances, statistical significance is an open question. Finally, the paper evaluates exclusively on the same four benchmarks used by the reference evaluation protocol (Kim et al., 2025), which risks benchmark-specific tuning of K, B_V, B_E, and τ. Bottom Line: TacoMAS is the most carefully grounded entry in the rapidly crowding test-time MAS evolution space, and the theoretical fast-slow framing earns its keep — but practitioners should verify the meta-LLM capability gap doesn't account for most of the gain before building on this framework.
Search Results 2605.09539 PDF
#92 — Innovative (65)

TabCF: Distributional Control Function Estimation with Tabular Foundation Models

Geping Chen, Chunlin Li, Tianzhong Yang, Zhengyuan Zhu, Jing Zhou · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TabCF, a two-stage plug-in estimator that wires tabular foundation models (TFMs) into the explicit control function identification formula of Imbens & Newey (2009) to estimate full interventional CDFs — and derived quantiles, means, and Gini indices — under unmeasured confounding with a continuous treatment. - Why it's important: Most existing IV estimators target only mean effects and require expensive per-dataset hyperparameter tuning; practitioners studying distributional consequences of interventions (tail risk, inequality, heterogeneity) have no fast, tuning-light tool that handles continuous treatments with hidden confounders. - How it does it: TabCF treats the first-stage (treatment → instrument) and second-stage (outcome → treatment + control variable) as separate distributional regression tasks solved via TFM in-context learning, forming the control variable as a probability integral transform of the first-stage fitted CDF, then integrating out the control variable to recover the interventional distribution. - The good: Across 6 synthetic settings (2 treatment × 3 outcome models) at n=4000 and 100 seeds, TabCF substantially outperforms DIV and IVQR on interventional quantile MSE across all quantile levels, and runs in under 2 seconds at n=1000 versus 62–81 seconds for the closest ML alternatives. - The missing: All synthetic evaluations use low-dimensional, scalar-instrument setups; there is no experiment beyond n=10,000 and the authors acknowledge performance plateaus (or degrades) at larger n due to TFM context-window limits, leaving scalability to real-world sample sizes unaddressed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for causal inference practitioners who work with small-to-medium tabular datasets and need distributional IV estimates without heavy tuning; less compelling for those dealing with large samples (>10K) or high-dimensional instruments where the method's TFM foundation breaks down.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) — clean composition of Imbens-Newey CF identification with TFM distributional regression, but components are all prior work. Rigor (75) — multiple ablations (explicit vs implicit, cross-fitting, weak-IV), 100 seeds with std devs, code released; preprint so reduce slightly. Non-Obviousness (60) — the explicit plug-in choice is well-motivated but not surprising. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) — limitations openly stated (TFM context-window cap, GPU dependency, copula approximation). Impact (65) — 30-80× speedup is real, code released, but only tested on small-medium scale and continuous treatments. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*65 = 66.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Economists and social scientists estimating distributional causal effects — how an intervention shifts the full outcome distribution, not just the mean — under unmeasured confounding face a fundamental problem: standard IV methods identify only mean effects, while flexible distributional IV methods (DIV, IVQR) require substantial dataset-specific fitting, hyperparameter search, or slow numerical optimization. Meanwhile, recent tabular foundation models (TFMs) like TabPFNv2 and TabICLv2 provide fast, tuning-free in-context distributional prediction for small-to-medium datasets, but existing TFM-based causal methods either assume no hidden confounding or absorb the identification logic into an opaque learned posterior. The gap is a practical, identification-transparent method for distributional causal inference that inherits TFMs' speed and calibration. Key Idea The key insight is that the Imbens-Newey (2009) explicit CF identification formula — which expresses the interventional CDF F_{Y(x)}(y) as an integral over a control variable V = F_{X|Z,W}(X|Z,W) — decomposes naturally into two sequential distributional regression tasks, each of which a pretrained TFM can solve in a single forward pass without gradient-based fitting. The control variable is formed as a probability integral transform (PIT) of the first-stage fitted CDF, making it approximately Uniform(0,1) and independent of the instrument. This explicit approach — estimating each nuisance component in closed form and substituting — avoids the error amplification that occurs when solving implicit equations or optimization problems with imperfect TFM estimates. Methodology TabCF operates in three stages under the triangular structural causal model Y = g(X,W,ε), X = h(Z,W,η) with instrument Z and pretreatment covariates W. Stage U1: regress X on (Z,W) using TFM to obtain F̂_{X|Z,W}, then compute V̂_i = F̂_{X|Z,W}(X_i|Z_i,W_i) — the estimated control variable — via PIT. Stage U2: regress Y on (X,V̂,W) using TFM to obtain F̂_{Y|X,V,W}. Stage U3: estimate the interventional CDF by averaging over V̂ — F̂_{Y(x)}(y) = (1/n)∑F̂_{Y|X,V,W}(y|x,V̂_i,W_i). The identification requires two causal assumptions: (CF1) monotonicity of the treatment structural equation in the disturbance η conditional on W, and (CF2) common support of V conditional on (X=x,W=w). Two critical design decisions: (a) choosing the explicit over implicit CF identification to avoid error amplification — validated empirically against an implicit alternative; (b) using the full sample for both stages rather than cross-fitting — validated to not degrade accuracy while halving runtime. For multivariate outcomes Y=(Y_1,...,Y_K), TabCF applies an x-invariant working copula approximation: marginal interventional distributions are estimated via the univariate procedure, then combined using a fitted copula (Stages M1-M2). The copula assumption is a practical approximation with no nonparametric identification guarantee for the joint interventional law. Key Results On 6 synthetic settings (treatment models T1/T2 × outcome models O1/O2/O3, n ∈ {1000,4000,10000}, 100 seeds): TabCF (both TabPFNv2.5 and TabICLv2 backbones) consistently falls in the best-performing cluster for interventional mean MSE across all settings. The gains are largest in nonlinear and nonadditive outcome settings where linear CF and deep ML baselines (DeepIV, DeepGMM, DIV) incur substantially larger errors. For interventional quantile estimation at n=4000, TabCF achieves near-zero MSE across τ ∈ [0.1,0.9] in most settings while DIV and IVQR show MSEs 4–5× higher in periodic-nonadditive designs. Runtime at n=1000: TabCF runs in 0.89–1.21 seconds (vs. 26.52 for DeepIV, 28.46 for DeepGMM, 62.21–81.15 for DIV/IVQR). For bivariate outcomes at n=2000 and ρ_ε=0.6, TabCF ranks among the best methods on sliced Wasserstein distance across four nonlinear designs. Four real datasets (AJR colonial institutions design, Fulton Fish Market demand, Card education returns, CigarettesSW demand) produce economically interpretable curves qualitatively consistent with known results. The paper does not report ground-truth MSE on real data, only visual plausibility. Cross-fitting leaves accuracy unchanged but roughly triples Stage U1 runtime, justifying the full-sample design. The paper is an arXiv preprint (v1, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the modular coupling of an explicit CF identification formula (previously treated as a theoretical object) with TFM distributional regression as a plug-in estimator, enabling tuning-free distributional IV estimation. The copula extension for multivariate outcomes is a practical engineering contribution. The individual components — control functions, tabular foundation models, PIT-based control variable construction — are all prior work; TabCF's contribution is showing their clean composition is effective and faster than purpose-built distributional IV estimators. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: explicit vs. implicit CF identification (Appendix A.1), full-sample vs. cross-fitting (Appendix A.3), covariate inclusion (Appendix C.1), common-support violations (Appendix C.2), weak-IV robustness (Appendix C.3). Results are averaged over 100 random seeds with standard deviations reported in shaded figures, providing reasonable variance estimates for the synthetic settings. No significance tests are reported. The identification assumptions (CF1 monotonicity, CF2 common support) are formally stated but empirically untestable; the paper appropriately flags this. Code is released at https://github.com/GepingChen/TabCF. The synthetic data-generating processes are fully specified, enabling reproduction. A methodological concern: all synthetic DGPs are drawn from the same distribution family used to motivate the method's design; no held-out structural forms test whether TabCF generalizes beyond the paper's own design choices. The real-data evaluation provides no ground truth, so quantitative performance on realistic settings remains unknown. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (evaluated by running experiments on synthetic and real data). - Practical: Runs on standard NVIDIA A100 GPUs with PyTorch; CPU inference is feasible but substantially slower — the paper notes this explicitly. - Efficient: TabCF at n=1000 takes under 2 seconds on GPU, 30–80× faster than DeepIV/DIV at the same scale; however, runtime and accuracy both degrade for n significantly exceeding TFM context window limits (approximately 10,000 rows based on Appendix Figure 9). - Effective: Achieves near-oracle accuracy on interventional means and quantiles across synthetic nonlinear settings; produces plausible but unvalidated curves on real benchmarks — effectiveness on real data with unknown ground truth is assumed, not measured. - Scalable: Not scalable beyond small-to-medium datasets; the authors explicitly acknowledge performance does not improve with larger n due to context window limits of current TFMs, and subsampling strategies are left for future work. The method delivers a software artifact (GitHub library) alongside the paper. Strengths - The explicit plug-in design elegantly separates causal identification from distributional estimation, producing a modular pipeline where each stage can be diagnosed independently — a practical transparency advantage over black-box amortized causal inference. - The 30–80× speedup over comparable distributional IV methods (DIV, IVQR) at n=1000 while matching or exceeding accuracy is a real and reproducible result, not just a claimed advantage. - The direct empirical comparison of explicit vs. implicit CF identification strategies (Appendix A.1) is a methodological contribution that validates the design choice and would be useful to researchers building other TFM-causal integrations. - The copula extension enabling multivariate interventional distributions with a simple two-stage procedure addresses a practical gap, as most existing TFM-based causal methods handle only univariate outcomes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) GPU dependency and CPU slowness inherited from TFMs; (2) performance plateau or degradation at large n due to context window limits; (3) reliance on untestable IV/CF assumptions (relevance, exclusion, unconfoundedness, monotonicity, common support); (4) no handling of invalid instruments. They propose subsampling strategies and median-IV estimators as partial remedies left for future work. This is an honest and substantive set of limitations. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The synthetic evaluation uses only low-dimensional problems (scalar Z, scalar or bivariate Y, one W covariate); no test with high-dimensional instruments or covariates, which is common in applied econometrics (e.g., many-instruments designs). - The copula approximation for multivariate outcomes assumes an x-invariant copula C = C_x, a strong restriction with no sensitivity analysis on how much violation of this assumption degrades the joint estimate. - The four real datasets are all small (n ranging from ~60 for AJR to a few hundred for Fulton Fish), so real-data evaluation is restricted to the method's comfortable operating regime; the absence of any medium-to-large real dataset (n~5,000–50,000) leaves a gap between the synthetic scale tests and deployment in applied work. - The main comparison excludes BBCI (Bynum et al. 2025), which is cited as a related amortized causal method but whose implementation is not publicly available — a gap the authors acknowledge but do not compensate for with ablations. - The method is tested only on continuous treatments; binary or discrete treatments are entirely out of scope with no discussion. Bottom Line A practitioner working with small-to-medium tabular datasets who needs distributional IV estimates — interventional quantiles, CDFs, inequality measures — should use TabCF as a first-pass tool: it is faster, easier to tune, and comparably or more accurate than DIV/IVQR in the tested settings, and the released code makes adoption straightforward.
Citations 2605.05993 PDF
#93 — Innovative (64)

Neurosymbolic Imitation Learning with Human Guidance: A Privileged Information Approach

arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GRAIL (Gaze-guided Relational Imitation Learning), a neurosymbolic framework that uses human eye-tracking data as privileged training-time information to learn interpretable, generalizable imitation learning policies from raw Atari game pixels. - Why it's important: Purely neural imitation learning produces opaque policies that fail to generalize to scenes with varying numbers of objects and overfit to spurious visual correlations; pure symbolic approaches require pre-structured symbolic inputs and cannot handle raw pixels — GRAIL addresses both simultaneously. - How it does it: A frozen gaze predictor computes per-atom saliency scores from predicted human attention heatmaps, which multiplicatively reweight probabilistic relational atom valuations before they enter a differentiable forward-chaining reasoner (NSFR) trained via behavioral cloning. - The good: GRAIL trained on 10% of demonstrations already outperforms the gaze-free neurosymbolic baseline (NSFR-IL) trained on 100% of data, achieving mean scores of 6308 ± 3766 on Asterix vs. 2519 ± 1488 for NSFR-IL and 306 ± 223 for neural behavioral cloning. - The missing: The rule base is hand-crafted rather than learned, and the paper discloses in the appendix that a more complex rule set achieves >14,000 on Asterix versus the reported 6308 — a 2× gap that is attributed to rule body complexity but is not systematically characterized, leaving the method's sensitivity to rule engineering unresolved. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working at the intersection of neurosymbolic AI, privileged information learning, and human-guided imitation learning; can be skipped if your focus is large-scale RL, automatic rule induction, or domains where eye-tracking data is unavailable.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) — integrating LUPI/gaze as atom-level modulation in differentiable neurosymbolic IL is a genuine new method, though component pieces (NSFR, gaze, BC) are prior art. Rigor (60) — clean ablation isolating gaze contribution and 50 evaluation seeds are strengths, but only 2 games, single-player data, no code release, no gaze-predictor quality metric, and the appendix-disclosed >14,000 Asterix result with a different rule set reveals unreported sensitivity to rule engineering. Non-Obviousness (72) — the gaze-as-regularizer interpretation, supported by the NSFR-IL-degrades-with-more-data finding, is genuinely surprising. Completeness (60) and Resilience (58) — authors honestly confront the hand-crafted rule base limitation (positive), but OCAtari oracle labeling undercuts the 'raw pixel' framing and the rule-complexity gap is unresolved. Practical Impact (55) — no released artifact, Atari-scale only, rule base does not scale automatically. Composite ≈ 0.20*70 + 0.25*60 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*55 = 63.4 → 64.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 60 Resilience 58 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Imitation learning from high-dimensional visual observations requires a policy that maps raw images to actions. Neural behavioral cloning handles perception but produces opaque policies prone to covariate shift, unable to generalize to scenes containing a varying number of objects, and susceptible to spurious background correlations. Pure symbolic approaches (e.g., ILP) require pre-specified clean symbolic inputs and cannot operate on pixel observations. The specific unaddressed gap is that no prior neurosymbolic imitation learning framework has exploited privileged information — data available only at training time, such as expert eye-tracking — to guide the symbolic grounding stage, improve sample efficiency, suppress spurious relational correlations, and yield policies that generalize compositionally to new object counts at test time. Key Idea The core trick is to treat human gaze as a soft multiplicative filter over grounded relational atoms before they enter the differentiable rule learner: atoms involving objects the human expert does not look at get their truth probabilities suppressed toward zero, steering gradient updates away from rules that exploit task-irrelevant relational co-occurrences. This converts gaze — a training-time privileged signal — into a structured regularizer over the symbolic state representation, rather than a pixel-level attention mask over a neural feature map. Methodology GRAIL trains three modules in sequence. First, a neural perception network fθ (84×84×3 RGB → [0,1]^|P|) is trained to produce probabilistic valuations for all grounded first-order atoms (e.g., `closeby(player,enemy)`), supervised with NLL against binary labels extracted by OCAtari, an object-extraction oracle; this module is then frozen. Second, a gaze prediction network gφ (grayscale frame → 84×84 heatmap) is trained by KL divergence against Gaussian-smoothed ground-truth gaze coordinates from Atari-HEAD; also frozen thereafter. Third, during policy learning, the frozen gaze predictor generates a normalized heatmap; per-atom saliency scores s_i are computed as total heatmap mass within object bounding boxes (product t-conorm for multi-entity atoms); the initial atom valuations v^(0) are element-wise multiplied by s_i to yield gaze-modulated atoms v^(g). These pass through T=2 steps of differentiable forward-chaining over a hand-crafted rule base B using Łukasiewicz fuzzy logic (via NSFR), and the learnable rule weight matrix W is optimized end-to-end by minimizing negative log-likelihood of expert actions (behavioral cloning). Three design decisions stand out: (1) decoupling perception training from policy learning — the perception module cannot be corrected by policy feedback; (2) the rule base B is entirely hand-crafted (8–12 rules with body size ≤ 3 atoms), not induced; (3) multiplicative gaze scaling can push atom probabilities toward zero rather than reranking them, which is a coarse approximation of relevance weighting. Key Results Evaluated on Seaquest and Asterix from Atari-HEAD (single player; 210,374 and 154,170 samples respectively), reported over 50 evaluation seeds. GRAIL achieves mean game scores of 6308 ± 3766 on Asterix and 501.60 ± 216.84 on Seaquest. Baselines: BC 306 ± 223 / 139 ± 39; AGIL (gaze-masked neural) 228 ± 175 / 192 ± 60; BC+Mask 251 ± 153 / 182 ± 60; NSFR-IL (GRAIL without gaze) 2519 ± 1488 / 454 ± 272. Sample efficiency: GRAIL at 10% data exceeds NSFR-IL at 100% data; neural baselines plateau at low scores regardless of data volume, suggesting a representational rather than statistical bottleneck. A counter-intuitive finding: NSFR-IL's score degrades on Asterix as training data increases, interpreted as overfitting to spurious relational co-occurrences; GRAIL does not exhibit this degradation. Generalization experiment (Seaquest, 1-object vs. 2-objects training): neural baselines remain below 200 in both conditions; GRAIL reaches approximately 480 in the 2-objects condition vs. ~350 for NSFR-IL. The appendix notes that a best-performing, more complex rule set (8 rules, longer conjunctive bodies) achieves >14,000 on Asterix, but the paper reports results with a simplified 12-rule set to avoid conflating rule engineering with framework contribution. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the integration of the LUPI (Learning Using Privileged Information) paradigm into neurosymbolic imitation learning by using gaze to modulate symbolic atom valuations before differentiable rule learning — the paper claims this is the first end-to-end neurosymbolic IL framework capable of exploiting propositional privileged information. The component pieces (NSFR, behavioral cloning, gaze as training-time privileged signal) each exist in prior literature; the integration and the atom-level gaze reweighting mechanism are the novel contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Results are reported over 50 evaluation seeds with standard deviations. The GRAIL vs. NSFR-IL ablation is clean — identical architecture and rule base, differing only in the presence of gaze modulation — directly isolating the privileged information contribution. However, demonstrations come from a single human player (multi-player results absent), limiting diversity and introducing potential overfitting to one playing style. Evaluation covers only 2 games (Asterix, Seaquest); Freeway appears in dataset statistics but not in comparison tables. No code, model weights, or trained models are released or mentioned (preprint, arXiv:2605.07166v1). The gaze predictor's actual predictive quality is not quantitatively reported — the KL training loss is used but no held-out heatmap accuracy metric appears. The appendix discloses >14,000 on Asterix with the best rule set, which is a significant unreported result that undermines confidence in the reported 6,308 as the method's true ceiling. No statistical significance tests beyond std are reported. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Operates on standard 84×84 Atari RGB frames; OCAtari object extraction and NSFR inference impose no special hardware requirements beyond a commodity GPU. - Efficient: Training involves three sequential phases (perception, gaze predictor, policy) with separate optimization loops, adding overhead over pure BC; inference is lightweight — two steps of forward chaining over ~304 atoms after frozen feature extraction. No wall-clock or FLOPs comparison is reported. - Effective: Evidence shows clear improvement over all baselines on the tested games across effectiveness, sample efficiency, and generalization to unseen object counts; the gaze modulation explanation (regularization against spurious correlations) is supported by the NSFR-IL degradation-vs.-data finding. - Scalable: Tested at Atari scale (up to 49 objects, ~304 atoms, 2-chaining-step rule bases); no evaluation beyond this. The hand-crafted rule base does not scale automatically to new domains. No software artifact is released; the paper stops at preprint form. Strengths - The gaze-as-regularizer interpretation is non-obvious and well-supported: the NSFR-IL score degradation with more training data (spurious correlation overfitting) is a genuine empirical finding that the gaze modulation credibly prevents. - The ablation design is tight — GRAIL and NSFR-IL share all architecture components except gaze modulation, making the attribution of gains unambiguous. - The compositional generalization experiment (1-object vs. 2-objects at test time) cleanly demonstrates a structural advantage of first-order relational representations that pixel-based methods fundamentally lack. - Treating gaze as a PI-inference approach (augmenting the symbolic state directly) rather than distillation is well-motivated for heteroscedastic demonstration noise and produces explicit probabilistic semantics. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The rule base is hand-crafted and its design strongly influences performance (explicitly disclosed in the appendix). The authors list rule learning as immediate future work. Scalability to larger domains (e.g., clinical decision support) is flagged as an open challenge. The need for eye-tracking apparatus during demonstration collection is noted as a practical constraint, though they argue other privileged information types could substitute. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-player data: restricting to one Atari-HEAD subject for consistency is methodologically defensible but means the framework has not been tested on demonstrations with diverse behavioral styles, which is the more realistic deployment scenario. - Only 2 games in main results despite 3 appearing in dataset statistics (Freeway is omitted from the comparison table without explanation). - The gaze predictor's test-time quality is uncharacterized: if predicted heatmaps are poor on novel states, the modulation introduces noise rather than signal, but no ablation tests degraded or random gaze maps. - OCAtari provides ground-truth object labels during perception training — this is a non-trivial domain-specific engineering requirement that partially undercuts the "raw pixel" framing and would require re-engineering for new games or non-Atari domains. - The interaction between rule base complexity and gaze modulation benefit is not studied — it is unknown whether gaze modulation helps equally for simpler and more complex rule sets, or whether the benefit is specific to the hand-tuned compact rules. Bottom Line Practitioners building interpretable imitation learning systems in domains where eye-tracking or analogous attention signals can be collected during demonstration will find the gaze-modulated NSFR framework and its sample-efficiency and compositional generalization results substantively useful; the hand-crafted rule base and two-game evaluation leave open how much work the framework does versus how much is carried by rule engineering.
Search Results 2605.07166 PDF
#94 — Innovative (64)

Skill-CMIB: Multimodal Agent Skill for Consistent Action via Conditional Multimodal Information Bottleneck

arxiv.org · Julian McAuley - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Skill-CMIB, a multimodal agent skill construction framework grounded in a Conditional Multimodal Information Bottleneck (CMIB) that distills trial-and-error trajectories into a two-component reusable skill — an interpretable text skill card plus a conditional visual latent vector — to improve action consistency in multimodal LLM agents. - Why it's important: Multimodal GUI and web-navigation agents exhibit high trial-to-trial action variability, and the dominant consistency fix (self-consistency via K=3–5 repeated rollouts) incurs 3–5× inference latency, making it impractical for real deployment. - How it does it: The joint information bottleneck over a multimodal skill decomposes exactly into a sequential text-stage bottleneck (yielding a symbolic skill card c) and a conditional multimodal bottleneck (yielding a visual latent z conditioned on c), enforcing complementarity by construction and realized via variational surrogates with a QFormer encoder and lightweight MLP projector. - The good: CMIB achieves a Step Consistency (StepCons) score of 0.4144 on Multimodal-Mind2Web versus 0.1789 for Self-Consistency(k=5), while running at 3510ms/step versus 17680ms — matching the accuracy of expensive multi-sample decoding at a fraction of the cost. - The missing: Absolute Step SR improvement over the same-backbone Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct baseline on Mind2Web is only ~0.5 points (38.91% vs 38.39%), and no scaling experiments beyond 7B parameters or sensitivity analysis of the β_c/β_z compression trade-off coefficients are provided. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on multimodal agent skill libraries or inference-time efficiency; those seeking large accuracy improvements over strong baselines will find the gains modest.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (75) — the exact sequential decomposition of the multimodal joint information bottleneck into a text stage plus a conditional visual stage, proven via the chain rule of mutual information, is a clean and genuinely new formulation for multimodal skill construction. Rigor (55) — meaningful I(c;z) ablation directly testing the theory, but no error bars or seeds, β_c/β_z never reported, K and training data unspecified, code/weights unreleased. Completeness (50) and Resilience (50) hurt by the buried fact that absolute Step SR over the same-backbone baseline on Mind2Web is only ~0.5 points and fine-tuned baselines (Explorer-7B 54.3%) crush it. Impact (65) — the 5× latency win over self-consistency and the reusable StepCons metric are real, but no artifact released. Composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*65 ≈ 60.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 50 Resilience 50 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multimodal LLM agents deployed on web navigation (Mind2Web), GUI control (ScreenSpot), and similar tasks exhibit measurable trial-to-trial action variability — the same task, same policy, different action sequences across runs. The standard remedy, self-consistency via majority voting across K independent rollouts, multiplies decoding cost by K and does not produce a reusable procedure. Existing skill internalization approaches either store text-only skill cards (losing dense visual evidence — icon positions, layout cues, pixel patterns — that cannot be faithfully verbalized) or store text and visual representations independently in two parallel streams (introducing cross-modal redundancy and noise with no principled mechanism to separate what belongs in text versus what belongs in the visual channel). The result is skills that are redundant, noisy, or poorly transferable across task instances. No prior framework formally characterized what a multimodal agent skill should encode and in which channel. Key Idea The key insight is that the joint information bottleneck over a multimodal skill pair (c, z) decomposes exactly into two sequential stages via the chain rule of mutual information: (1) an unconditional text bottleneck that extracts an interpretable symbolic card c, and (2) a conditional multimodal bottleneck that extracts a residual visual latent z conditioned on c — so z can only retain visual information that remains predictive of the task outcome beyond what c already captures. This conditioning is the structural mechanism that enforces cross-modal complementarity: information already encoded in the text card is actively penalized from appearing in z. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. First, K trial-and-error trajectories τ^(k) — each containing textual prompts x_t, visual pixels m_t, actions a_t, environment observations o_t, and reward feedback f_t — form a rollout bundle B. Second, the text bottleneck produces a discrete skill card c from the aggregated textual content X via a progressive summarization prompt applied to a frozen LLM: trajectory-level evidence is extracted per rollout, aggregated across K rollouts, then formatted into a structured card minimizing a length-penalized utility objective J_c(c; B) = |c| - β_c Û(c; B) subject to a token-budget constraint. This is the tractable approximation to the text-stage IB. Third, given c, the conditional multimodal bottleneck trains a variational posterior encoder q_θ(z | M, c) and a text-conditioned prior r_φ(z | c), both parameterized as diagonal Gaussians, where the posterior is produced by a QFormer applied to visual trajectory features and conditioned on c. The training objective is the ELBO-style surrogate L_z = E[log(q_θ/r_φ)] - β_z E[log π_tsk(Y | [g_ω(z); c; B])], where g_ω is a lightweight MLP projector that maps z into soft tokens prepended to the frozen task model. At inference, c is retrieved and z is sampled from q_θ(z | M_obs, c) given the current observation, with both fused into the task model without updating its weights. Critical design decisions: (1) conditioning z on c (well-justified by the factorization lemma — it is the mechanism for complementarity); (2) using a QFormer for visual encoding (motivated by cross-modal alignment literature but not ablated against alternatives); (3) progressive summarization for c* (an engineering component not ablated for alternative strategies). Key Results On Multimodal-Mind2Web (three splits: Cross-Task, Cross-Website, Cross-Domain), using Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct as backbone: CMIB achieves an average Step SR of 38.91% versus 33.77% for vanilla inference (SC k=1) and 35.98% for SC(k=5), and 37.95% for text-card-only skill. Against the same-backbone baseline directly, the gain is 38.91% vs. 38.39% — a marginal ~0.5 point absolute improvement. StepCons: CMIB 0.4144 vs. SC(k=5) 0.1789 — a large relative advantage. On ScreenSpot (mobile/desktop/web GUI grounding), CMIB achieves 57.9% average action success rate versus 53.0% for the baseline model, with particularly strong gains on Mobile Icon/Widget (65.1% vs. 53.3%) and Web Text (60.0% vs. 51.7%). Latency: CMIB 3510ms/step vs. SC(k=5) 17680ms/step — ~5× faster. Ablation (Table 4): removing the redundancy constraint between c and z raises I(c;z) from 0.18 to 1.69 and drops Step SR from 41.66 to 39.18; removing z entirely drops Step SR to 37.95; removing all skill drops to 30.62. Models fine-tuned with synthetic data (AgentTrek-7B: 49.5%, Explorer-7B: 54.3% Step SR) substantially outperform CMIB, though they exploit additional training data. No error bars or multi-seed training results are reported. All numbers are from a preprint under review. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION + NEW METHOD. The exact sequential decomposition of the multimodal joint information bottleneck into complementary text and conditional visual stages is a genuinely new formulation for agent skill construction, with a clean proof via the chain rule of mutual information. The implementation assembles known components (variational IB surrogates, QFormer, soft prompt control, LLM-based skill cards) under this new framework; each component individually is prior work, but their principled composition under CMIB is the novel contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and meaningful: Table 4 systematically removes the redundancy constraint, removes z, and removes the entire skill, showing monotonic performance drops with quantitative I(c;z) redundancy measures. StepCons is measured across N=3 independent repeated trials, but standard training-run reproducibility (multiple seeds, training variance) is not reported. No code repository or model weights are released (no URL mentioned in the paper). Benchmark choices (Multimodal-Mind2Web, ScreenSpot) are standard and appropriate. The compression trade-off hyperparameters β_c and β_z are not reported numerically in the main paper, nor is the number of training trajectories K or the training data scale. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.08526, under review); results are self-reported with no third-party replication. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (a built framework evaluated by deployment on agent benchmarks). - Practical: CMIB runs on commercially available GPU hardware; the base model is 7B parameters and the additional components (QFormer + MLP projector) are lightweight, adding only ~27ms latency overhead per step. - Efficient: The marginal inference cost over the base model is negligible (~0.8% latency increase), compared to 3–5× overhead for self-consistency; the skill library construction requires K rollouts offline but reuses skills across task instances. - Effective: Evidence supports improved action consistency (StepCons: 0.4144 vs. 0.1789 for SC k=5) and meaningful ScreenSpot accuracy gains; however, the absolute Step SR improvement on Mind2Web over the same-backbone baseline is marginal (~0.5 points), which limits the strength of the effectiveness claim. - Scalable: Tested only on a 7B-parameter backbone and two benchmarks; no experiments at larger model scale; the soft-prompt injection mechanism is architecture-dependent and would require re-engineering for different frozen models. Scalability is claimed but not experimentally demonstrated. The work delivers no software artifact; it stops at paper form with unreleased code and weights. Strengths - The CMIB factorization lemma is theoretically clean: the exact sequential decomposition follows from the chain rule of mutual information and is fully proven in the appendix, giving the two-component skill design a rigorous foundation rather than an ad hoc architecture choice. - The inference efficiency advantage is substantial and practically significant: CMIB matches or exceeds SC(k=5) StepCons at ~5× lower latency, directly addressing the deployment bottleneck of multi-sample decoding. - The ablation design is tight: the I(c;z) mutual information measurement provides a quantitative proxy for cross-modal redundancy and confirms the conditional bottleneck is doing what the theory predicts. - The StepCons metric is a concrete, reproducible tool for measuring agent reliability that the community can reuse independently of this method.
