Assessment was applied to 60 active SACs selected non-randomly (restricted to SACs the authors know well as maintainers or long-term users); Listing- and Search-service-type SACs were assessed by their structural type rather than individual inspection. Key design decisions: coarse-grained intentionally to allow broad comparative insights rather than fine-grained benchmarking; FAIR-enabling framework synthesized rather than built from scratch to leverage existing community consensus; living dataset model adopted to allow post-publication updates. Key Results: 190 SACs total: 120 Active (63%), 61 Retired (32%), 9 Prototype (5%). Among 120 active SACs: Repository type is most prevalent (78 SACs, 65%), followed by Library (19, 13%) and Listing (16, 13%). Among active SACs, 12 generic technologies active; Skosmos most prevalent (20 SACs, 16.7%), followed by OntoPortal (9 SACs, 7.5%) and OLS (6, 5%); over 57% of active SACs use ad-hoc or undefined technology. Discipline distribution: "General" is largest category (40%), followed by Ethnic and cultural sciences, Social sciences and humanities, and Medicine and health (7–8% each); biomedicine (Medicine + Biology) accounts for 14 SACs. FAIR-enabling findings on 60 assessed active SACs: Repository-type SACs generally address Cataloguing, Resolvability, and Access Protocols well (often Level 3); Versioning, Reuse/Mappings, and Provenance are the most poorly supported dimensions even in Repository-type SACs (predominantly Level 1). Listing-type SACs score Level 1 on all 10 dimensions. OntoPortal is the only technology consistently at Level 2 or 3 for Versioning. Identifiers, Metadata, Licensing, and Provenance vary independently of technology, indicating that catalogue-level editorial policies matter more than technology choice for these dimensions. No numerical composite scores are reported; results are presented as stacked bar charts with counts. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the living, versioned open dataset of 190 SACs with multi-dimensional metadata and FAIR-enabling scores — the most comprehensive such inventory in existence — and the empirical characterization of the SAC landscape across type, discipline, technology, and FAIR-enabling dimensions. The 10-dimension FAIR-enabling framework is a synthesis of existing tools (not a new theoretical contribution). The "FAIR-enabling" reframing of SAC evaluation relative to artefact producers is a useful conceptual contribution but not a formally proved result. Rigor & Reproducibility: The living dataset is publicly released (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.12799861), which is a concrete reproducibility asset. However, the FAIR-enabling assessment is explicitly self-assessed: authors rated catalogues they maintain or use extensively, introducing systematic optimism bias. No inter-rater reliability metrics (Cohen's kappa or equivalent) are reported for either the FAIR-enabling scores or the discipline classification. The assessment covers only 46% of active SACs (60/120), selected non-randomly, which limits representativeness. Discipline classification used ChatGPT-4 as pre-classifier followed by manual correction, but inter-annotator agreement for the correction step is not reported. The FAIR-enabling levels are coarse-grained ordinal (3 levels), precluding statistical comparison; no significance tests are presented. The authors acknowledge the assessment is "interpretative and partially subjective" but do not quantify this. This is appropriate for a landscape survey — rigorous statistical analysis is not the genre's norm — but the conclusions drawn (e.g., that certain dimensions are "well addressed" by Repository-type SACs) should be read as expert opinion, not measurement. Deployability: Profile: EMPIRICAL (systematic survey methodology applied to real deployed systems). - Practical: The Zenodo dataset is immediately usable by SAC selection and infrastructure planning without computational resources. - Efficient: No computational overhead; deliverable is a community-maintained spreadsheet. - Effective: Addresses the SAC landscape knowledge gap for practitioners and EOSC infrastructure planners; however, the self-assessment design and 46% coverage limit effectiveness as a reliable comparative benchmarking tool for individual SAC selection. - Scalable: The living document model with community contributions is the scaling mechanism; 20 new SACs were added between V1 (July 2024) and this submission, suggesting the model functions, but quality control procedures at scale are not detailed. The work delivers a dataset artifact (Zenodo spreadsheet) and a journal paper. No software tool, API, or scoring system is released as a standalone tool — the paper references a MOD-API for SAC interoperability (FAIR-IMPACT project output) but that is separate prior work. Strengths: - Scale and temporal scope: 190 SACs across 17 disciplines and 12 technologies with historical coverage back to 2001 is the most comprehensive SAC inventory to date, providing a baseline that did not exist in machine-readable, open form. - Living open dataset: Versioned Zenodo release with community-update mechanism converts a static review into ongoing infrastructure — the 20 SACs added post-V1 demonstrate this works in practice. - FAIR-enabling reframing: Shifting from artefact FAIRness to SAC FAIR-enabling capability is a conceptually useful reorientation that focuses attention on infrastructure providers and their responsibilities, directly actionable for EOSC policy. - Cross-disciplinary cross-analysis: The discipline-by-technology and discipline-by-type matrices reveal genuine structural patterns (e.g., Earth Science using four different technologies, biomedical domain's disproportionate SAC density) that were not previously documented at this scope. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state directly that the FAIR-enabling assessment is "coarse-grained" and "intentionally designed to provide a broad comparative overview rather than detailed benchmarking." They acknowledge it "relies partly on expert knowledge and interpretation" and does not "systematically capture all implementation nuances." Coverage is partial (60/129 active SACs). They call for future work to define "more precise and measurable criteria" and involve SAC providers in a "more systematic validation process," including semi-automated indicators. They note the landscape evolves rapidly, making the snapshot potentially stale. These are genuine and direct confrontations of the limitations, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Self-assessment conflict of interest is understated: All co-authors are SAC providers, and many FAIR-enabling assessments were conducted by the maintainers of the very catalogues being assessed. No independent audit or inter-rater check is reported; this creates a systematic optimism bias that could affect all dimension-level conclusions and is more serious than the authors acknowledge. - The "Other" technology category (57% of active SACs) is a structural blindspot: the majority of the SAC population uses undefined/ad-hoc technology and receives only structural-type-based FAIR assessment without examination of actual capabilities, making the technology-to-FAIR mapping incomplete for most of the landscape. - No downstream validation: The study measures what SACs offer at the infrastructure level but never validates whether these features are actually used by artefact depositors or whether they translate to measurably improved FAIRness of published artefacts — a key assumption left untested. - ChatGPT-4 pre-classification without documented agreement: Using a commercial LLM as a pre-classifier for discipline assignment with manual correction but without reported inter-annotator agreement makes the discipline analysis non-reproducible at that step. - Non-random sample treated as representative: The 60-SAC FAIR-enabling sample was selected entirely on author familiarity, yet the paper draws broad landscape conclusions (e.g., which dimensions are "mostly poorly addressed") as if the sample were representative of all 120 active SACs. Bottom Line: A practitioner or infrastructure developer in the semantic web or EOSC ecosystem gains a valuable, current landscape map and the most comprehensive open SAC inventory available, but should treat the FAIR-enabling scores as directional expert opinion rather than reliable benchmarking, given the self-assessment design, partial coverage, and absence of reliability measures.
Search Results
#118 — Moderately Innovative (57)

EditSleuth: A Dataset of Grounded Reasoning Chains for Image-Edit Forensics

Van-Loc Nguyen, AprilPyone MaungMaung, Minh-Triet Tran, Isao Echizen · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EditSleuth, a dataset of 257,725 image-edit forensic triplets with deterministically assembled six-step reasoning chains grounded in computable upstream artifacts rather than LLM-synthesized rationales. - Why it's important: No existing resource combines AI-edited image triplets with auditable, evidence-grounded reasoning chains for training forensic VLMs—existing forensic datasets provide only binary labels or masks, while reasoning-supervised VLM datasets rely on LLM rationales that cannot be verified against pixel-level evidence. - How it does it: A five-stage deterministic pipeline converts image-editing triplets (source, edited, instruction) into forensic training data by computing binary edit masks from multi-signal pixel differences, difficulty scores via a three-component formula, 12-category edit labels via lookup or regex, and fixed-slot template-assembled reasoning chains populated entirely from computable upstream values. - The good: The V2 difficulty formula—motivated by a documented rank-2 correlation collapse among magnitude features—increases score standard deviation by +55% on Pico-Banana and +94% on MagicBrush relative to V1, yielding meaningfully more discriminative tertile bins. - The missing: The fine-tuning pilot uses one 2B-parameter model on ~20K examples evaluated on only 528 MagicBrush dev triplets whose labels carry a 44% rule-coverage gap, making it too underpowered to validate whether chain supervision actually improves forensic VLM performance at production scale. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building explainable forensic VLM training pipelines or studying grounded reasoning supervision; the dataset and construction pipeline are the real contribution, and practitioners should treat the fine-tuning results as preliminary hypotheses rather than evidence of downstream utility.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (60) and Completeness (65) carry the score: the deterministic pipeline and 257K-triplet dataset are real artifacts. Novelty (55) and Non-Obviousness (50) are modest — auditable chain assembly from upstream artifacts is a sensible engineering choice but not a conceptual leap. Rigor (55) and Resilience (50) are pulled down by an underpowered pilot (one 2B model, 528 noisy dev triplets with 44% rule-coverage gap, no CIs, no comparison to any prior forensic VLM or detector), leaving the central downstream-utility claim unsupported.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 65 Resilience 50 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation AI-driven image editing has shifted from pixel-level splice and copy-move artifacts to subtle, instruction-conditioned semantic manipulations—object removals, style transfers, background swaps—that may be globally diffuse. Detecting such edits usefully requires localizing the edit region, classifying its semantic type, and grounding the classification in visual evidence. Prior forensic datasets (CASIA, Coverage, DIRE) address classical forgeries with binary masks but provide neither instruction context nor reasoning explanations. Reasoning-supervised VLM datasets (Visual CoT, M3CoT) supply chain-of-thought rationales, but generate them via frontier LLMs that never directly observe the pixel evidence—producing fluent but unverifiable rationales. Image-editing datasets (MagicBrush, Pico-Banana, InstructPix2Pix) provide (source, edited, instruction) triplets for training editors but are not structured for the forensic reverse task. No existing resource bridges all three needs. Key Idea Reasoning chains for image-edit forensics can be made faithfully grounded—and thus auditable end-to-end—by assembling them deterministically from computable upstream artifacts (mask area fractions, SSIM-based structural-change scores, mask compactness, difficulty bins, category labels) rather than by prompting an LLM to narrate visual evidence it never directly measures. Category-level forensic priors (e.g., boundary discontinuities, lighting inconsistencies) are kept separately as explicitly hedged conditional knowledge so trained models can distinguish per-triplet measurements from category-typical signatures. Methodology The pipeline has five sequential stages. Stage A ingests triplets from Pico-Banana and MagicBrush via adapters normalizing to a unified schema. Stage B derives a binary edit mask by computing three pixel-level difference signals—Lab* color distance, 1−SSIM, and LPIPS perceptual distance—each normalized to [0,1] by its per-pair 99th percentile and combined by elementwise maximum; scope (local/global/ambiguous) is assigned by a two-path rule: if mean(d) > τ the edit is routed global, otherwise Otsu binarization and mask area fraction determine scope. τ is calibrated per dataset to produce ~30% global routing. Stage C scores difficulty as D = 0.55·s_struct + 0.25·s_compact + 0.20·s_instr (V2), where s_struct = 1−SSIM, s_compact captures mask spatial dispersion via a compactness ratio, and s_instr is a heuristic over instruction length, verb count, conjunction count, and spatial-reference count; empirical 33rd/66th percentile tertile binning produces easy/medium/hard labels. The critical design decision motivating V2 over V1 (which included locality s_loc = 1−mask_area_frac) is that s_loc is strongly negatively correlated with the magnitude terms (pairwise |r|∈[0.68,0.91]), collapsing the combined score to rank-2 and compressing variance to roughly 1/4–1/5 of a single component's variance. Stage D assigns one of 12 canonical edit categories (object_addition, object_removal, object_replacement, attribute_change, style_transfer, photometric, scene_transformation, background_change, text_edit, geometric, human_centric, other) via source-label lookup (Pico-Banana) or regex classifier (MagicBrush, 56% coverage). Stage E assembles a six-step, ~80–150-word reasoning chain by populating fixed-slot templates: (1) quoted instruction, (2) spatial localization descriptor, (3) structural-change and compactness scores, (4) category and classification source, (5) category-level forensic prior from 12 hand-curated templates, (6) difficulty bin and score. Category priors are explicitly hedged ("typically exhibits...") to mark them as conditional knowledge. Key Results Dataset scale: 257,725 Pico-Banana triplets (primary training) plus 528 MagicBrush dev triplets (held-out). Difficulty ablation: V2 standard deviation = 0.109 vs. V1 = 0.070 on Pico-Banana (+55%); 0.066 vs. 0.034 on MagicBrush (+94%). Threshold calibration across signal stacks (LAB+SSIM, LAB+LPIPS, LAB+LPIPS+SSIM): calibrated τ values (0.52, 0.56, 0.62) track shifts in mean combined diff signal (+0.04, +0.036, +0.06), confirming that threshold adjustments compensate for max-pool inflation rather than signal-specific artifacts. Qualitative inspection of ~50 stratified Pico-Banana triplets found zero faithfulness violations; two MagicBrush misclassifications came from regex misfires. Pilot fine-tuning (Qwen2-VL-2B + LoRA r=16, n_train≈20,000, evaluated on MagicBrush dev n=528): conditional accuracy (on parseable outputs only)—chain-target: category 33.4%, spatial 37.2%, difficulty 40.8%; label-only: 39.7%, 42.2%, 33.1%. Field extraction recall—chain-target: category 67.4%, spatial 79.9%, difficulty 68.6%; label-only: ~94–100%. Joint accuracy (all 3 fields): chain-target 1.9%, label-only 3.4%. A second training epoch does not close the recall gap. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the deterministic, artifact-grounded reasoning chain construction pipeline—no prior forensics dataset combines AI-edited image triplets with auditable, step-indexed reasoning chains. The 12-category taxonomy is an incremental extension of Pico-Banana's eight categories with forensically motivated splits (photometric separated from style_transfer; human_centric added; background_change retained). The VLM fine-tuning pilot applies standard LoRA to a commodity VLM with no methodological novelty. Rigor & Reproducibility The difficulty formula ablation is quantified with explicit variance statistics on two corpora (n=257,725 and n=528) and a mechanistic explanation (rank-2 collapse) supported by pairwise correlation evidence. Threshold calibration includes a retrospective sweep tool (Appendix C). The fine-tuning comparison is single-seed, single-model, single-scale (n≈20K train, n=528 eval) with no error bars or significance tests—the authors appropriately label it a "pilot." The MagicBrush eval labels are acknowledged as noisy (44% rule-based fallback), undermining absolute accuracy comparisons. A residual structural–compactness anti-correlation (r=−0.44) on Pico-Banana local edits is documented as an open artifact. Code, dataset, and pilot training scripts are stated to be released under a research-use license; no repository URL is provided in this preprint. No ablation isolates the contribution of individual chain steps. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS (a built dataset-construction pipeline evaluated by deployment on two corpora). - Practical: Pipeline stages run standard CV operations (SSIM, LPIPS, Otsu) on commodity hardware; no heavy LLM inference is required for dataset construction. - Efficient: LPIPS computation is the bottleneck per triplet but is GPU-parallelizable; 257K examples are processed in one pipeline run. Fine-tuning uses 2B-parameter Qwen2-VL-2B with LoRA—modest overhead. - Effective: The pipeline produces difficulty-discriminative labels and masks with no observed faithfulness violations on the inspected sample; however, a VLM trained on EditSleuth does not yet outperform a label-only baseline on held-out evaluation, so effectiveness as a forensic training resource is unconfirmed. - Scalable: The deterministic pipeline is straightforwardly parallelizable and was applied to 257K examples; extending to other image-editing corpora requires writing a new Stage A adapter. Scaling VLM training beyond 2B parameters and 20K examples is the stated next step but not demonstrated. The work delivers a dataset artifact (Parquet shards), a construction pipeline (code), and pilot training scripts. No trained model weights are released. Strengths - The deterministic chain construction is principled and auditable: every numbered chain step traces to a specific computable upstream value, providing a faithfulness guarantee that LLM-synthesized rationale datasets cannot match without dense human annotation. - The rank-2 collapse diagnosis (§5.1) is a concrete, quantified finding—not hand-waving—explaining why V1 difficulty failed and motivating the V2 design with empirical variance gains across two corpora. - The taxonomy extensions reflect forensic-domain reasoning: separating photometric from style_transfer because global tone shifts have different forensic signatures than structural texture rewrites is a substantive distinction, not arbitrary splitting. - The authors honestly confront all six limitations in §6 and propose concrete follow-up directions rather than overstating the pilot's implications. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly flag six limitations (§6): (1) residual mask-quality coupling on local edits (r=−0.44 structural–compactness anti-correlation from Stage B mask noise on subtle edits); (2) 44% MagicBrush rule-coverage gap, limiting its use to held-out evaluation rather than training; (3) single-source training corpus (all Pico-Banana triplets come from one text-conditioned editor, potentially biasing Stage B mask statistics); (4) artifact-relative faithfulness—chains are auditable against upstream masks/scores, but those artifacts are themselves computational outputs that may carry biases propagated into chains; (5) pilot scope too small to close the chain-target recall gap; (6) unverified inference-time chain faithfulness—the trained model generates six-step chains with numerical fields but does not recompute those values from upstream artifacts, so generated numbers are model predictions rather than verified measurements. The authors engage these directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The pilot evaluation accepts MagicBrush dev labels produced by the same regex classifier that fails to classify 44% of examples; those examples presumably count as incorrect for any non-other prediction, yet the paper does not exclude or reweight them, making absolute accuracy numbers unreliable. - No comparison to any existing forensic VLM, AI-generated image detector, or forgery localization method is included. EditSleuth is positioned as enabling better forensic VLMs but provides no evidence that its training signal outperforms prior datasets or approaches on any standardized forensic benchmark. - The 528-example MagicBrush dev set is very small; no confidence intervals are reported anywhere, so it is impossible to assess whether the chain-target vs. label-only differences (e.g., category conditional accuracy 33.4% vs. 39.7%) are statistically meaningful or noise. - Generalization beyond Pico-Banana-style text-conditioned edits to diffusion-model-generated manipulations, GAN-based forgeries, or manual editing is untested and unstated. - The forensic prior templates (12 hand-curated category descriptions) are not ablated; it is unknown whether including them improves downstream model behavior relative to omitting step 5 from the chain. Bottom Line EditSleuth fills a genuine gap—deterministically auditable reasoning chains for AI-edit forensics—and the construction pipeline is the real contribution; practitioners building explainable forensic VLM training pipelines should engage with it directly, though the fine-tuning pilot is too small and its evaluation too noisy to treat as evidence of downstream advantage.
Search Results 2605.08695 PDF
#119 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Ada-MK: Adaptive MegaKernel Optimization via Automated DAG-based Search for LLM Inference

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Ada-MK, an MLIR-based offline DAG search and compile-time execution-path solidification framework that adapts MegaKernel persistent-kernel fusion to resource-constrained NVIDIA Ada (L20) GPUs for LLM decode inference, achieving the first industrial MegaKernel deployment in a commercial advertising system. - Why it's important: Kernel launch overhead alone consumes 14.6% of decode-phase inference time in online advertising systems requiring 1–5 ms end-to-end latency, and prior MegaKernel solutions either hard-code Hopper/Blackwell assembly or introduce runtime branch penalties that are unacceptable at this latency target. - How it does it: By hoisting all runtime dynamic scheduling decisions to compile time — decomposing the transformer decoder to PTX-level dependency DAGs via MLIR alias analysis, offline-profiling the optimal execution trace, and serializing it branch-free into kernel code — while recovering Ada's pipeline stages through K-dimension splitting that halves peak shared memory demand. - The good: Ada-MK delivers positive throughput gains over vanilla TensorRT-LLM across every tested scenario (6.7%–23.6% on fixed short sequences; 4%–19.5% on real datasets) and up to 50.2% over vLLM at BS=1, backed by production deployment evidence. - The missing: No ablation table isolates the contribution of each claimed innovation (K-splitting vs. DAG search vs. hybrid engine), and the central 30% gain over Stanford MegaKernel is asserted in prose with no supporting table or figure. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers deploying small LLMs on Ada-class GPUs under strict decode-latency SLAs; skippable for those targeting Hopper/Blackwell, larger models (7B+), or high-throughput multi-GPU inference.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (65) is the strongest contributor — production deployment at Baidu is meaningful real-world validation — but no public artifact release caps it. Rigor (45) pulls hard: no component ablation, the headline ~30% gain over Stanford MegaKernel appears only as a prose assertion with no table, latency is never measured despite the 1–5 ms SLA motivation, and offline-batch evaluation structurally disadvantages vLLM/SGLang. Novelty (55) is moderate — MLIR-based DAG construction and compile-time path solidification on Ada are a defensible new combination, but components are established. Completeness (50) and Resilience (50) are mid — the missing ablations and unverified central claim leave the technical story exposed. Weighted: 0.15*55+0.15*45+0.10*55+0.15*50+0.15*50+0.30*65 ≈ 55.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 50 Resilience 50 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Commercial online advertising systems deploy LLMs for real-time query understanding and ranking, requiring deterministic end-to-end inference latency strictly within 1–5 ms. During token generation (decode phase), each token triggers thousands of kernel launches; Nsight profiling of Qwen2.5-1.5B under TensorRT-LLM reveals 1,655,550 launches consuming approximately 3.3 s, with launch overhead alone accounting for 14.6% of inference time. MegaKernel eliminates this by fusing the entire transformer decoder into a single persistent kernel that exchanges intermediate results through shared memory rather than HBM. However, deploying MegaKernel on NVIDIA Ada (L20) faces two hard constraints absent on Hopper/Blackwell: Ada lacks TMA hardware support (requiring PTX-level software emulation of asynchronous data movement), and its per-SM shared memory is only 128 KB versus 227 KB on H100, reducing achievable pipeline stages from 4 to 2 and causing duty-cycle losses exceeding 30%. Stanford's MegaKernel (HazyResearch, 2025) is deeply coupled to Hopper/Blackwell assembly and supports only Llama-1B, not Qwen-family models; Mirage MPK's Managed Pointer mechanism introduces runtime if-else branching on shared memory page states that degrades instruction issue efficiency below hand-tuned operator performance. No prior system closes the gap between portability and efficiency on resource-constrained Ada hardware. Key Idea Under a fixed deployment configuration (fixed model, fixed GPU, fixed batch shape), the optimal MegaKernel execution path — which shared memory pages to allocate, which instructions to overlap, which warp roles to assign — is uniquely determined. Runtime dynamic decision-making is therefore unnecessary and can be replaced entirely by offline profiling that locks in the single optimal execution trace, serializing it branch-free directly into kernel code. This "offline search, online reuse" paradigm trades one-time compile-time search cost for zero runtime scheduling overhead. Methodology Ada-MK operates in two tightly coupled phases. Phase I (Offline MegaKernel Synthesis) parses the transformer decoder and LM Head into a fine-grained DAG via MLIR Lowering (Torch-MLIR dialect stack through PTX). DAG nodes represent data migration operations (Global→Shared→Register) or computation primitives (MMA 16×8×16, dequantization INT4→FP16, epilogue); edges encode RAW data dependencies identified via MLIR's AliasAnalysis API and resource dependencies (shared memory page competition). A hardware constraint filter prunes configurations that exceed shared memory or register budgets. Offline profiling then searches the feasible space to identify the trace with maximum pipeline duty cycle, which is serialized for runtime. Three critical design decisions: (1) K-dimension splitting halves the K-dimension tile per loop iteration so each pass loads only the weight sub-tile for the current sub-block, reducing peak shared memory from 64 KB to 32 KB — recovering pipeline stages from 2 to 4 on Ada. This is well-justified by the shared memory budget model (Equations 1–2). (2) Execution-path solidification replaces MPK-style runtime if-else branching with a single serialized instruction sequence chosen at compile time; justified by the fixed-deployment-config invariant. The tradeoff is that configuration changes require recompilation. (3) Heterogeneous hybrid Prefill/Decode dispatch embeds MegaKernel as a TensorRT-LLM plugin only for Decode; Prefill retains TensorRT-LLM's native fused operators. This is well-argued: Decode is IO-bound (weight + KV Cache loading dominates), so IO-compute overlap pays off; Prefill is compute-bound and saturates GPU compute units, making MegaKernel's advantage negligible while its launch overhead is just one forward pass. Additional pipeline optimizations include: activation–weight and activation–output page reuse (releasing shared memory pages to registers before reallocating), early asynchronous prefetching (advancing stride from N+1 to N+2), gap filling (inserting non-critical instructions into synchronization-idle slots), address permutation (interleaved page remapping to reduce bank conflicts), role rebalancing (forwarding lightweight dequantization to idle Loader warps), warp allocation refinement (Consumer warps reduced 16→8, aligning Storer's cross-block Reduce latency), and TensorCore-aware offline weight reordering for GPTQ-W4A16 to enable direct LDMATRIX access without runtime layout transformation. Key Results All experiments run on a single NVIDIA L20 GPU (48 GB GDDR6, 864 GB/s bandwidth, Compute Capability 8.9), CUDA 12.2, with frameworks in isolated Docker containers. Models tested: Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen2.5-1.5B, both GPTQ-W4A16. Baselines: vLLM v0.19.0, SGLang v0.5.10, vanilla TensorRT-LLM v1.1.0rc5 — all current and actively maintained. Metric: generation throughput in tokens/s; evaluation mode is offline batch (fixed batch submitted simultaneously). Fixed short sequences (input=64, output=12): Ada-MK improves over vanilla TRT-LLM by 23.6% at BS=1 and 6.7%–15.6% across all batch sizes for Qwen2.5-1.5B; positive gains at every batch size for both models. At BS=1, gains over vLLM reach 50.2% and over SGLang 71.9% for Qwen3-1.7B. CSL dataset (medium context, ~200–1000 tokens): Ada-MK leads from BS=1–8; at BS=16, vLLM surpasses by 3.5%, indicating that system-level KV cache management advantages emerge under high concurrency. Human-eval dataset (code generation): Ada-MK maintains highest throughput at all batch sizes including BS=16, with 19.5% improvement over TRT-LLM. The DAG search component is claimed to deliver ~30% improvement over Stanford MegaKernel in Decode, but this appears only as a prose assertion in §4.2.3 with no accompanying table or figure. No latency measurements (ms) are reported despite the stated 1–5 ms SLA motivation — all results are throughput only. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the application of MLIR-based PTX-level dependency DAG construction with AliasAnalysis to drive compile-time execution-path solidification for MegaKernel on Ada-class hardware — specifically the observation that fixed deployment configs make runtime dynamic scheduling provably unnecessary. K-dimension splitting for shared memory recovery is a concrete but incremental engineering contribution. MLIR, DAG scheduling, warp specialization, MegaKernel, and operator fusion are all established; the integration for the constrained Ada deployment context and the offline-search-to-solidification pipeline is what is genuinely new. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablation table or figure appears anywhere in the paper — the three claimed contributions (K-splitting, DAG search, hybrid engine) are never isolated to measure their individual impact. The central claim of ~30% improvement over Stanford MegaKernel (§4.2.3) is supported by prose only, with no table, figure, or configuration details. Error bars, multiple seeds, and significance tests are absent (single-GPU throughput has low variability, but this is still a gap). No code, weights, or search artifacts are released; no repository URL is mentioned. Evaluation is self-reported from Baidu's production environment with no third-party reproduction. The use of offline batch mode for framework comparison may disadvantage vLLM and SGLang, which are optimized for online scheduling — a methodological choice that structurally favors Ada-MK but is at least explicitly disclosed. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.11581v1, May 2026); not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built TensorRT-LLM plugin evaluated by production deployment. - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA L20 GPU with standard CUDA 12.2 and TensorRT-LLM; no exotic hardware, custom silicon, or proprietary software stack required beyond what Baidu's production environment already uses. - Efficient: K-dimension splitting halves peak shared memory from 64 KB to 32 KB, recovering pipeline stages 2→4 on Ada's 128 KB budget; compile-time solidification eliminates all runtime branch-decision overhead; quantization (W4A16) further reduces memory bandwidth pressure, and the hybrid engine avoids MegaKernel overhead where it doesn't help (Prefill). - Effective: Positive throughput gains over TRT-LLM baseline confirmed at every tested configuration; production deployment at Baidu provides real-world validation beyond benchmark numbers, though the absence of latency measurements leaves the 1–5 ms SLA claim unverified in the paper's data. - Scalable: Tested only on 1.5B–1.7B models, batch sizes 1–16, single GPU. No multi-GPU experiments, no results for 7B+ models, and no analysis of how K-splitting tile sizes scale with larger weight dimensions. The paper explicitly defers larger-model and Blackwell adaptation to future work. Delivers a software artifact (TensorRT-LLM plugin + MegaKernel kernel) deployed in production; no public release. Strengths - The compile-time execution-path solidification insight is clean and well-motivated: once the deployment configuration is fixed, runtime dynamic scheduling is provably redundant, and eliminating it removes a whole class of instruction-issue overhead that MPK-style systems incur. - The 3D shared memory constraint model (hardware specs + model architecture + dynamic workload) enabling adaptive pipeline depth is a concrete, portable engineering formalism reusable for other resource-constrained GPU targets beyond Ada. - Production deployment at Baidu's commercial advertising system is rare validation in academic LLM-systems papers; it credibly demonstrates that the method survives real engineering constraints (quantized Qwen models, TensorRT-LLM plugin compatibility, business-logic reuse, latency SLA). - The heterogeneous Prefill/Decode dispatch design is practically well-reasoned and correctly identifies the asymmetry between IO-bound decode and compute-bound prefill, preserving TRT-LLM's compute-saturation advantages where MegaKernel cannot compete. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) Ada-MK's advantage over vLLM/SGLang narrows and reverses at BS=16 on the CSL dataset, attributed to system-level KV cache management and scheduling advantages; (2) cross-model comparisons (Qwen3-1.7B vs. Qwen2.5-1.5B) are confounded by differing parameter counts and do not exclude this influence on absolute numbers; (3) current scope is single-GPU, small-scale models, with future work explicitly targeting larger model scales and Blackwell architectures. The authors confront these boundaries directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No component ablation exists. Three independent contributions are claimed but never isolated: how much throughput does K-splitting alone provide? How much comes from the DAG search? How much from the hybrid engine? Without this, the paper's core technical claims cannot be prioritized or reproduced independently. - The ~30% Decode improvement over Stanford MegaKernel (§4.2.3) is the primary evidence for the DAG search's value and appears only as an unverified prose claim — no table, no figure, no configuration or experimental setup described for this comparison. - Latency (ms) is never measured despite the stated 1–5 ms SLA motivation; all results are throughput (tokens/s). Throughput and latency trade off differently under batching, and the paper's core motivation is latency-critical serving. - Scope is confined to 1.5B–1.7B Qwen models on a single L20. Whether K-dimension splitting remains feasible at 7B+ (where weight tiles are larger and the shared memory arithmetic changes) is unaddressed and non-obvious. - Offline batch evaluation mode structurally advantages Ada-MK: vLLM and SGLang are designed for online continuous batching with dynamic request arrivals. Reporting throughput in offline mode eliminates a core capability of competing frameworks, making the comparison flattering but potentially misleading for real serving deployments. - No public code or artifact release, making the MLIR decomposition pipeline and DAG search impossible to reproduce or extend independently. Bottom Line An ML systems engineer targeting small-batch LLM decode latency on Ada-class NVIDIA GPUs will extract actionable, immediately deployable ideas (K-splitting for shared memory recovery, compile-time trace solidification, hybrid Prefill/Decode dispatch); anyone evaluating this work as a general MegaKernel advance should demand the missing ablations and latency measurements before drawing strong conclusions.
Search Results 2605.11581 PDF
#120 — Moderately Innovative (55)

RouteProfile: Elucidating the Design Space of LLM Profiles for Routing (26 ▲ )

Jingjun Xu, Hongji Pu, Tao Feng, Haozhen Zhang, Jiaxuan You, Ge Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RouteProfile, a framework that systematically characterizes the design space of LLM capability profiles for routing along four dimensions — organizational form, representation type, aggregation depth, and learning configuration — and empirically evaluates how each dimension affects downstream routing performance across multiple routers. - Why it's important: LLM routing research has focused almost entirely on router mechanism design while treating model profiles as an afterthought; this conflation obscures where routing gains actually originate and prevents principled profile design, especially when new models must be routed without prior interaction history. - How it does it: It models LLM interaction histories as a heterogeneous graph with five node types (model, model family, domain, task, query) and derives structured model profiles via GNN-style aggregation — either LLM-based message passing in text space or embedding-based propagation, optionally with trainable self-supervised reconstruction — then evaluates these profiles on three representative routers. - The good: Structured+trainable profiles achieve cold-start scores of 0.452–0.547 for routing a newly introduced LLM (Mistral-Small-24B) while flat index profiles score near zero, demonstrating that profile structure is essential for generalization to unseen candidates. - The missing: Routing evaluation uses only 50 instances per dataset, and no confidence intervals or significance tests are reported, making it unclear whether the modest absolute gains in average performance (typically 1–4 points) are statistically reliable. - Read it or not: Routing system designers and researchers building multi-LLM orchestration should read this — it isolates a previously confounded variable (profile design) and shows it matters independently of router choice, with practical cold-start implications.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (65) — the heterogeneous-graph framing of profile design and the systematic disentangling of profile from router is a real methodological contribution. Rigor (45) is the major drag: only 50 instances per dataset, no confidence intervals or significance tests for differences in the 1–4 point range, GPT-4o as the never-ablated text-feature generator, and benchmark-built graphs that partially overlap with eval datasets. Non-Obviousness (60) — the result that query-level signals beat domain-level is a useful finding but the GNN machinery is standard. Completeness (55) and Resilience (50) — small samples make most reported deltas indistinguishable from noise, and no proprietary models or cost metrics are reported despite cost-quality being the canonical routing motivation. Impact (60) — code released, but practical relevance is hampered by the eval-scale issue. Composite: 0.20·65 + 0.25·45 + 0.15·60 + 0.15·55 + 0.15·50 + 0.10·60 = 55.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: In a multi-LLM ecosystem, individual models exhibit complementary strengths across queries, tasks, and domains, motivating routing systems that select the best model per query. Prior work has overwhelmingly focused on router architecture (preference-trained, reward-guided, graph-based) while LLM profiles — the representations of candidate model capabilities that routers consume — have been designed ad hoc and entangled with routing strategy. This makes it impossible to attribute performance differences to profiles versus routers, and prevents fair cross-router comparison. A concrete practical gap is cold-start routing: when a new model enters the candidate pool with no interaction history, existing flat index-based or coarse text-based profiles fail to generalize, yielding near-zero routing success. Key Idea: The key insight is to treat LLM profiling not as simple feature extraction but as a structured information integration problem over a heterogeneous interaction graph. By representing models, model families, capability domains, benchmark tasks, and individual query instances as typed nodes connected by relational edges, and then propagating information across this graph via K-hop aggregation (either in text space via prompted LLMs or in embedding space via GCN-style message passing), the framework captures structured relational evidence — such as the fact that a model's performance on reasoning queries relates to its task-level benchmark scores and architectural lineage — that flat feature concatenation discards. Methodology: RouteProfile constructs a heterogeneous graph G with five node types (model v_m, model family v_f, domain v_d, task v_t, query v_q) and four edge types (model–family, model–task, task–domain, task–query). Node features are initialized from: LLM-generated text descriptions (GPT-4o is used for model, domain, and task nodes); benchmark leaderboard scores on model–task edges; and encoded raw query text (via Longformer) for query nodes. The profile aggregation function f is parameterized along four axes: (1) organizational form — flat (naive concatenation) versus structured (graph-based); (2) representation type — text (LLM-summarized at each hop) versus embedding (dense vector propagation); (3) aggregation depth K ∈ {0,1,2,3,4} hops; (4) learning configuration — training-free versus trainable via a self-supervised masked reconstruction objective over HANConv backbone (MSE losses on masked node and edge features). The profile graph is built from 15 benchmark datasets spanning knowledge, reasoning, math, and coding domains, incorporating 25 LLMs (8 candidate, 17 auxiliary). Three downstream routers are evaluated: SimRouter (cosine similarity, training-free), MLPRouter (learned shared latent space projection), and GraphRouter (graph-structured router with self-supervised GNN). Routing is evaluated on 12 held-out datasets (50 instances each) with an 8-model candidate pool ranging 3B–176B parameters. A second evaluation setting simulates cold-start by excluding Mistral-Small-24B from the profile graph and measuring the fraction of queries both routed to and correctly answered by the new model. Key Results: Across all three routers, structured profiles consistently outperform flat baselines: SimRouter improves from 0.499 (flat index) to 0.613 (structured embedding, 4-hop, trainable); MLPRouter improves from 0.593 to 0.625 (structured text, 3-hop, training-free); GraphRouter improves from 0.532 to 0.614 (structured embedding, training-free). Query-level signals produce more consistent gains than domain-level signals — adding domain nodes does not reliably improve and can degrade otherwise strong profiles, while task+query signals together yield the best results. In the cold-start setting, flat profiles achieve near-zero cold-start performance (0.000–0.038) while structured+trainable profiles achieve 0.213–0.547 depending on router, with GraphRouter reaching 0.547 at Emb-Tr-4Hop. However, cold-start gains are not uniform across routers: the profile-router pairing matters, with GraphRouter benefiting most from structured profiles and SimRouter showing more modest cold-start improvement. Increasing aggregation depth is beneficial in training-free settings but can degrade trainable MLPRouter and GraphRouter (attributed to over-smoothing in discriminative embedding spaces). Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The heterogeneous graph formulation of LLM interaction history for profile construction is a genuine conceptual contribution — prior work either used scalar index vectors, coarse LLM-generated text, or task-level summary statistics, none of which exploited relational structure across query/task/domain/model-family levels. The systematic design space evaluation isolating profile design from router design is a methodological contribution to the routing literature. The specific GNN variants (text-based, embedding-based, trainable) are applications of existing graph learning techniques, not novel algorithms. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations across 15 profile configurations and 3 routers provide reasonable coverage of the design space. The cold-start evaluation (RQ3) is a well-motivated held-out generalization test. Critical weaknesses: (1) routing evaluation uses only 50 instances per dataset — extremely small sample sizes with no confidence intervals, error bars, or significance tests, making it unclear whether 1–3 point performance differences are reliable; (2) GPT-4o is used to generate text descriptions for nodes, introducing a proprietary dependency that is never ablated — using a weaker or open model could change results substantially; (3) the interaction graph is built from benchmark leaderboard scores, which may not reflect real user query distributions, creating a potential train/eval distribution mismatch. Code and data are publicly released at ulab-uiuc/RouteProfile (GitHub) and a HuggingFace collection. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: The embedding-based and trainable GNN variants run on standard GPU hardware; text-based GNN requires repeated LLM calls during profile construction (expensive at scale) but construction is offline. - Efficient: Profile construction cost scales with graph size and hop count; the trainable GNN adds training overhead but uses a lightweight HANConv backbone; routing inference is fast (similarity or MLP forward pass). - Effective: Structured profiles demonstrably improve routing accuracy and cold-start generalization over flat baselines, though absolute gains in average routing performance are modest (1–4 points) and gains in cold-start are substantial but concentrated in compatible router configurations. - Scalable: Tested with 8 candidate models and 25 total LLMs in the graph; behavior at larger candidate pools (e.g., 50+ models) or additional task domains is not evaluated and over-smoothing at deep hops suggests non-trivial scaling challenges. The work delivers a software artifact (code + HuggingFace collection). Strengths: - Cleanly disentangles profile design from router design, enabling fair attribution of routing gains — a methodological contribution the field has lacked. - The cold-start evaluation is both practically important and rigorously set up, directly measuring whether profiles transfer to unseen candidates. - The heterogeneous graph formulation unifies previously ad hoc profile approaches under a principled, tunable framework with interpretable axes. - The finding that domain-level signals introduce noise while query-level signals are reliable is a concrete, actionable design guideline for practitioners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that cold-start generalization gains depend on profile-router co-design and are not realized uniformly across routers, and attribute performance degradation at higher aggregation depths in trainable settings to over-smoothing. They note the framework's goal is to show how design choices affect performance rather than to enumerate all variants. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 50-instance evaluation sets are critically small — no confidence intervals are provided, and many reported differences (e.g., 0.610 vs. 0.617) are likely within noise. GPT-4o is used as the text-feature generator and is never ablated, making it impossible to know whether the observed gains come from the graph structure or from GPT-4o's quality descriptions. Routing evaluation excludes proprietary models entirely (no GPT-4o, Claude, or Gemini as candidates), limiting practical relevance given that cost-quality tradeoffs between proprietary and open models are a primary routing motivation — and no cost metric is reported at all. The interaction graph is built from leaderboard benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, etc.) that are heavily saturated for large models, which may limit profile discriminability for strong models. The profile construction itself is somewhat circular: the same benchmark datasets used to build profiles partially overlap with routing evaluation datasets. Bottom Line: Practitioners building multi-LLM routing systems gain a concrete, actionable insight — structured graph-based profiles with query-level signals substantially outperform flat profiles and are necessary for cold-start generalization — but should treat the reported absolute performance numbers with caution given the small evaluation sets and missing significance tests.
Recommended 2605.00180 PDF
#121 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Geometry-Guided 3D Visual Token Pruning for Video-Language Models

Han Li, Zehao Huang, Jiahui Fu, Naiyan Wang, Si Liu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Geo3DPruner, a training-free geometry-guided token pruning framework for video-language models processing 3D scenes as multi-view spatial videos, achieving 90% token reduction while retaining 92% of baseline task performance across five benchmarks. - Why it's important: 3D scene understanding with VideoLMs generates thousands of visual tokens per scene (up to 6,272 for 32-frame inputs), creating a hard inference bottleneck, yet existing token pruning methods are blind to cross-frame 3D redundancy and spatial coverage requirements. - How it does it: A frozen VGGT geometry encoder produces a global cross-frame attention map; tokens are assigned to 3D voxels via predicted depth and camera parameters; a two-stage pruner first selects the most representative token per voxel (intra-voxel view consistency), then iteratively selects a spatially diverse voxel subset (inter-voxel diversity). - The good: At 90% token reduction on 32-frame videos, Geo3DPruner retains 92.0% of baseline performance versus 82.4% for FastV and 84.8% for VisPruner across nine metrics, and under a fixed token budget it outperforms the unpruned 8-frame baseline by 9% by enabling more frames to be sampled. - The missing: The paper never reports wall-clock latency or FLOPs, so the claimed inference efficiency gain is asserted but unmeasured, despite VGGT-1B being a non-trivial additional encoder. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building efficient 3D scene understanding pipelines with VideoLMs; can be skipped by those whose domain is general-purpose video pruning or who need demonstrated latency numbers before adopting an efficiency method.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (60) clean decomposition of view-consistency vs spatial-diversity. Rigor (45) — no FLOPs/latency despite efficiency being the core claim, single-domain ScanNet, no error bars, code 'forthcoming'. Non-Obviousness (65) decomposition insight is non-obvious. Completeness (40) no limitations, missing latency that's core to claim. Resilience (45) efficiency claim unverified. Impact (55) accuracy retention strong but no released artifact and unmeasured speedup. Composite ≈ 51.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 40 Resilience 45 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation VideoLMs applied to 3D scene understanding represent each scene as an ordered sequence of RGB frames augmented with depth and camera pose, yielding thousands of visual tokens per scene (e.g., 6,272 for 32 frames at 384×384). LLM context limits and GPU memory force aggressive frame subsampling or resolution reduction, degrading 3D coverage. Existing training-free token pruning methods—text-guided approaches (FastV, PyramidDrop) and vision-guided approaches (VisPruner, VisionZip)—operate within individual frames or temporally adjacent frames, failing to exploit the key property of 3D spatial videos: features for the same physical location recur across arbitrary frames. Additionally, these methods select salient or text-relevant tokens, causing retained tokens to cluster on high-attention objects and neglecting the spatial diversity needed for object-centric 3D tasks such as grounding and dense captioning. Key Idea The core insight is that cross-frame redundancy in 3D spatial videos is geometrically structured: tokens from different frames that project to the same 3D voxel are redundant, while tokens from distinct voxels are complementary. By exploiting the global cross-frame attention map produced by a pre-trained 3D geometry encoder (VGGT), and using camera parameters and depth maps to assign 2D tokens to 3D voxels, the method decouples two orthogonal pruning objectives—view-consistency selection within each voxel, and diversity-preserving selection across voxels—and solves them sequentially. Methodology The pipeline runs two parallel encoder branches during inference. A 2D visual encoder (SigLIP-400M) extracts per-frame patch features E_s ∈ ℝ^{Hp×Wp×d}. A frozen 3D geometry encoder (VGGT-1B) processes all S frames simultaneously, producing geometry features G_s, a global attention map A ∈ ℝ^{N×N} (N = S×Hp×Wp), camera extrinsics/intrinsics, and depth maps. Geometry-augmented features F_s = E_s + G_s are assigned to 3D voxels (size δ=0.1m) via inverse camera projection. Stage 1—Intra-Voxel View Consistency Pruning (VCP): For each voxel k, extract the sub-attention matrix A_k from A restricted to the token indices in that voxel. Score each token by averaged incoming attention from all co-voxel tokens. Retain the top-α=50% tokens per voxel. This selects the best multi-view representative for each 3D location and discards redundant same-object observations from other viewpoints. Stage 2—Inter-Voxel Spatial Diversity Pruning (SDP): Formulates voxel selection as a subset selection problem under a fixed token budget. Uses an iterative greedy heuristic: (1) compute global attention received by each candidate voxel from all others; (2) select top-K=8 voxels and add them to the selected set W; (3) recompute voxel-wise attention among remaining candidates only, suppressing intra-object bias; (4) repeat until budget is met. Critical design decisions: (a) using attention-based relevance from VGGT rather than cosine feature similarity (justified by 4-point ablation gain); (b) iterative rather than one-shot selection (justified by large margin over random/uniform baselines); (c) keeping VGGT frozen (pragmatic choice—no ablation comparing against fine-tuned geometry encoder). Key Results All experiments use Video-3D LLM (LLaVA-Video 7B backbone, Qwen2-7B LLM, SigLIP encoder) augmented with VGGT-1B geometry features (denoted Video-3D LLM†) as the unpruned baseline. Baselines for pruning comparison are FastV and VisPruner, both training-free methods. All evaluations are on ScanNet-derived benchmarks. At 90% token reduction (16 frames, 3136→320 tokens): Geo3DPruner achieves 92.1% of unpruned performance; FastV 81.0%; VisPruner 84.9%. At 80% reduction: 96.1% vs. 88.7% and 91.6%. At 60% reduction: 98.9% vs. 94.5% and 95.7%. At 90% reduction (32 frames, 6272→640 tokens): 92.0% vs. FastV 82.4%, VisPruner 84.8%. Results are consistent across 8-frame settings. Fixed-budget experiment (Table 8): Using 32 frames with 75% pruning (retaining 1568 tokens) outperforms the unpruned 8-frame baseline (also 1568 tokens) by 9% on average—demonstrating that denser temporal sampling plus geometry-guided pruning strictly dominates sparser sampling without pruning. At 20% token reduction, Geo3DPruner slightly exceeds the unpruned baseline, suggesting VGGT-guided pruning removes genuinely noisy tokens. Ablation at 90% pruning (16 frames): VCP alone 89.4%, SDP alone 91.3%, both 94.0%. SDP vs. random voxel selection: 94.0% vs. 85.2%. Attention-based relevance vs. cosine similarity: 94.0% vs. 89.9%. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The novel contribution is using a pre-trained feed-forward 3D reconstruction model's internal global attention map to drive voxel-based two-stage token pruning in VideoLMs. The individual components—VGGT geometry encoding, voxelization of 2D features, attention-based token scoring, iterative greedy subset selection—are all prior work. The novelty is their integration into a coherent 3D-aware pruning pipeline that explicitly addresses cross-frame redundancy and spatial diversity as distinct objectives. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2604.18260v1); not peer-reviewed. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover the two core design dimensions (which stages to include, how to compute inter-voxel relevance, how to select voxels), and all results align with claimed mechanisms. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed results are reported. Code is promised at https://github.com/homothetic/Geo3DPruner but marked as forthcoming. Evaluation is on ScanNet-derived benchmarks only, and VGGT was trained on data that may partially overlap with ScanNet scenes—though since VGGT is used for geometry extraction rather than scene classification, Goodhart's Law risk is low. The voxel size δ=0.1m is fixed with no sensitivity analysis. The pruning ratio α=50% for intra-voxel stage is fixed with no ablation. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on commercial GPU hardware; VGGT-1B, SigLIP, and Qwen2-7B are all publicly available models; 384×384 input resolution and 8–32 frame settings are feasible on a single high-end GPU. - Efficient: The paper never reports wall-clock inference latency or FLOPs before and after pruning; adding VGGT-1B as a second encoder branch is a non-trivial computational overhead that may partially or fully offset the LLM token reduction gains—this is the most significant gap in the paper's efficiency narrative. - Effective: Retains 92% of baseline 3D scene understanding performance at 90% token reduction across grounding, dense captioning, and QA tasks on ScanNet; the evidence is internally consistent across all frame-count settings. - Scalable: Tested at 8, 16, and 32 frames on ScanNet indoor scenes; beyond this scale, and outside the RGB-D indoor domain, scalability is undemonstrated. The paper does not address scenes without calibrated depth maps or camera poses. The work delivers a method applied at inference time; no standalone library, trained model weights, or dataset are released yet. Strengths - The decomposition of 3D token pruning into view-consistency (intra-voxel) and spatial-diversity (inter-voxel) objectives is conceptually clean and directly addresses the failure modes of generic pruning methods in this setting; both stages are independently validated to contribute. - The iterative greedy inter-voxel selection with attention recomputation concretely solves the intra-object bias problem: random and uniform baselines drop to 85% while SDP achieves 94%, a gap that validates the design. - The fixed-budget experiment (Table 8) reveals a qualitatively new operating point: geometry-guided pruning enables denser temporal sampling at the same token cost, achieving 109% of the unpruned 8-frame baseline—a result prior pruning methods cannot achieve. - The attention-based cross-frame relevance from VGGT outperforms cosine feature similarity by 4 percentage points, empirically justifying the choice to rely on the geometry encoder's internal representations rather than a simpler proxy. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state no explicit limitations. The conclusion section summarizes contributions without identifying failure modes, domain restrictions, or computational overhead. This absence is a credibility concern: a paper claiming inference efficiency improvements should acknowledge that VGGT-1B adds overhead, and a paper evaluated exclusively on ScanNet should note the domain restriction. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No latency or FLOPs measurements: The efficiency claim is the paper's raison d'être, yet Tables 1–8 contain no wall-clock times. VGGT-1B processing S frames with global attention (O(N²) complexity for N=S×Hp×Wp) is potentially slower than the token savings in the LLM, making this omission critical. - Single-domain evaluation: All five benchmarks derive from ScanNet (indoor RGB-D scenes with ground-truth poses and depth). Performance on outdoor scenes, monocular video without depth, or scenes with different object scale distributions is entirely unknown. - Voxel size not ablated: δ=0.1m is stated but not justified; scenes with fine-grained objects or large-scale architecture would require different granularities. - The comparison is asymmetric: FastV and VisPruner are applied to Video-3D LLM† (VGGT-augmented), meaning they compete against a stronger backbone that they were not designed for. While this is the correct comparison for deployment (all methods use the same base), it conflates the contribution of the VGGT encoder upgrade with the pruning strategy improvement. - No statistical significance tests; all results appear to be single runs. Bottom Line A practitioner building inference-efficient 3D scene understanding systems will find the voxel-based two-stage pruning design principled and the accuracy-retention gains over FastV/VisPruner genuinely substantial, but cannot judge actual inference speedup without latency numbers—the paper's core efficiency claim remains unverified until those measurements are provided.
Recommended 2604.18260 PDF
#122 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Weakly Supervised Concept Learning for Object-centric Visual Reasoning

Sparsh Tiwari, Bettina Finzel, Gesina Schwalbe · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SlotVAE, a two-stage neuro-symbolic pipeline that trains a concept-partitioned slot-attention VAE with as little as 1% concept labels to generate symbolic predicates for downstream logical reasoning via ILP, Decision Trees, Bayesian Networks, or NS-CL. - Why it's important: Dense concept annotations for perception stages in neuro-symbolic systems are expensive and limit scalability; reducing that cost by 99× while retaining interpretable, logically usable predicates would make such systems practical in real-world domains like medical imaging. - How it does it: Each object slot's latent vector is explicitly partitioned into named concept sub-vectors, each supervised by a sparse Huber loss applied only to labeled samples, with Hungarian matching for slot-to-ground-truth alignment; argmax decoding at inference converts logits to first-order logic atoms. - The good: At 1% supervision, SlotVAE achieves 0.746 zero-shot accuracy on Melanoma, outperforming DINOv2 (0.679) and ResNet+SA (0.579), demonstrating data-efficiency advantages over heavily pretrained foundation models on a realistic medical domain shift. - The missing: Localization concept accuracy never exceeds ~44.6% even at 75% supervision in the medical domain, making multi-concept conjunctive rules universally unsolvable — the paper identifies but does not diagnose or remediate this fundamental bottleneck. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in neuro-symbolic AI or weakly supervised disentanglement who care about the perception-reasoning interface; the empirical finding on ILP noise-robustness is counter-intuitive and actionable, but the lack of code release and failure on complex real-world rules limits direct reuse.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) is the highlight — the counter-intuitive finding that ILP achieves F1=1.0 despite ~21% color accuracy is genuinely surprising and actionable. Novelty (60) is moderate: integration of established components (Slot Attention, VAE, Hungarian matching, ILP) with sparse Huber supervision. Rigor (55) suffers from no code release, no CBM baselines, an undiagnosed ~44% localization ceiling, and an unfair DINOv2 comparison (frozen probe vs. trained model); preprint status. Completeness (60) — limitations are acknowledged honestly but the central medical conjunctive-rule failure is unresolved. Resilience (50) low because the headline 1% supervision advantage may partly reflect the baseline framing. Impact (40) — no released artifacts, fails on the motivating medical use case. Weighted: 0.20*60 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*40 ≈ 55.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 60 Resilience 50 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: The symbol grounding problem in neuro-symbolic AI requires mapping raw visual inputs to discrete, human-interpretable predicates that logical reasoning engines can manipulate. Two-stage systems that decouple perception from reasoning avoid the optimization difficulties of end-to-end differentiable approaches (e.g., DeepProbLog) but traditionally require dense concept annotations for the perception module. Fully unsupervised disentanglement (β-VAE, FactorVAE) fails to align latent factors with semantically meaningful concepts without supervision, a fundamental impossibility result noted by Locatello et al. The gap being targeted is a perception module that needs minimal labels yet produces predicates grounded to a desired human concept vocabulary suitable for downstream logical induction. Key Idea: Partition each object slot's latent representation into named sub-vectors (one per concept: shape, color, size, material, coordinates, presence) and apply a sparse supervisory signal — a Huber loss computed only on labeled samples, with Hungarian matching for slot-object alignment — to steer the VAE's disentanglement toward human-interpretable concept axes rather than arbitrary statistical factors. Methodology: The pipeline has two stages. Stage 1 (perception): a CNN encoder feeds into slot attention, which produces k object-centric slot vectors; each slot's latent is partitioned per Eq. 1 (z_slot = [z_coord ∥ z_presence ∥ z_shape ∥ z_color ∥ z_size ∥ z_material]). Separate 2-layer MLP concept heads project each sub-vector to Gaussian parameters; categorical predictions come from predictor networks over sampled sub-vectors. Training minimizes L_total = L_recon (pixel MSE) + β·L_KL (with β warmup) + δ·L_sup (Huber, on labeled fraction only) + λ·L_coord (MSE) + γ·L_presence (BCE); supervisory terms use Hungarian matching to align slots to ground-truth objects. Stage 2 (symbolic): at inference, slots with presence probability below 0.5 are discarded; remaining slots produce predicates via argmax on logits; these form first-order logic background knowledge for downstream reasoners. Critical design decisions: (1) Structured latent partitioning — well-justified by the impossibility of unsupervised disentanglement; (2) Hungarian matching — standard and appropriate for permutation-equivariant slots; (3) Modular reasoner selection — the paper argues this is a feature, and the complementary specialization results support it. Key Results: On CLEVR with 1% supervision (700 of 70K labeled): shape accuracy 71.4±2.2%, size 88.1±2.9%, material 83.6±4.0%, color 21.8±2.4% (Table 1, 5 runs). ILP (Popper) achieves F1=1.000±0.000 on Simple Existential, Conjunctive, and Disjunctive rules using 15%-supervised predicates (color 78.7%), while BN, DT, and NS-CL score near zero on the same rules — establishing that ILP's search mechanism is noise-resilient for first-order combinatorial structures. ILP fails completely (F1=0) on Cardinality and Universal Quantifier rules, where BN reaches 0.760 and NS-CL 0.754 F1. In the medical domain, at 1% supervision SlotVAE achieves 0.746 zero-shot accuracy on Melanoma vs. DINOv2 0.679 and ResNet+SA 0.579, though at 15% DINOv2 becomes superior in-domain (0.883 vs. 0.800). The conjunctive medical rule (mel on back, requiring simultaneous dx and localization) fails universally across all models below 75% supervision; GlobalVAE at 75% is the sole exception (F1≈0.90-0.93), attributed to its holistic global vector being better suited to diffuse scene-level properties. Baselines (DINOv2, ResNet+SA, GlobalVAE) are current and span architectural paradigms. Datasets are standard academic scale (≤70K images), not stress-scale. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual components (Slot Attention, VAE, ILP, Hungarian matching, weak supervision) are all established; the genuine contribution is their integration via a structured, concept-named latent partition with sparse per-concept supervision, plus the empirical demonstration that ILP's symbolic search is completely resilient to perceptual noise for first-order combinatorial rules while probabilistic reasoners specialize on non-combinatorial tasks — a finding not previously characterized in the neuro-symbolic literature. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover slot count k (Table 2) and supervision level (Table 1). All results reported as mean±std over 5 runs, which is adequate for the variance levels observed. No code, trained models, or custom datasets (e.g., 2D-CLEVR) are released; 2D-CLEVR construction details are deferred to supplementary material not included in the main PDF. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.08201v1, May 2026); no peer review. Methodological concern: the medical localization concept reaches at most 44.6% accuracy (Table 2, k=2 at 75%), making conjunctive rules involving it unsolvable — the paper reports this honestly but does not ablate the concept head architecture or training signal for this concept specifically. ILP timeout failures are mentioned but not quantified with timing data. Deployability: EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: The SlotVAE uses a 5-layer CNN encoder, 4-layer decoder, 10 slots, 3 attention iterations — runs on standard GPU hardware; no unusual infrastructure required. - Efficient: Trained 200 epochs at batch 64 on up to 70K images; no FLOP counts or wall-clock times reported. ILP (Popper) can time out on complex rules at low supervision, and this runtime behavior is not quantified, which is a gap for practitioners. - Effective: The method demonstrably solves simple and structural concept learning and enables perfect ILP rule induction under noise. However, it fails on multi-concept conjunctive rules when any required concept has low fidelity — the conjunctive medical rule is unsolvable at all supervision levels for the SlotVAE, which limits practical utility in the declared medical setting. - Scalable: Tested on standard academic-scale synthetic datasets and one 10K-image medical dataset. No experiments at 10× scale or with a larger concept vocabulary. The slot-count ablation shows performance degrades for k>2 on single-object medical images, indicating that structural bias selection is fragile and must be done per dataset. - Software artifact: paper form only; no code released. Strengths: - The empirical finding that ILP (Popper) achieves F1=1.0 on combinatorial first-order rules despite ~21% color accuracy is counter-intuitive and practically important — it tells practitioners that ILP can absorb substantial perceptual noise for the right rule types, changing how one should evaluate predicate quality requirements. - The structured latent partitioning design cleanly separates the disentanglement problem into concept-specific subspaces, and the sparse Hungarian-matched Huber loss is a principled, lightweight intervention — the 1% label requirement with 88% size accuracy and 71% shape accuracy is a genuine achievement. - The asymmetric domain transfer finding — that 2D→3D transfer succeeds (shape accuracy ~99% post-finetuning) but 3D→2D causes model collapse — is a concrete, actionable result about information-loss direction-dependence in representation transfer. - The comparison covers four downstream reasoner families across five synthetic rule types and a real medical domain, providing a broader characterization of the perception-reasoning bottleneck than prior work typically offers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge that complex multi-concept conjunctive rules remain unsolvable when any required predicate has low fidelity, calling this the primary bottleneck and proposing it as future work. They note that zero-shot performance is limited and that robust surface-feature adaptation requires high source-domain visual complexity. They identify sequential perception-reasoning training as potentially suboptimal and propose joint or alternating training as future work. These are confronted honestly and represent genuine credibility positives. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The localization concept accuracy on HAM never exceeds 44.6% regardless of supervision or slot count, yet the paper provides no diagnosis — no ablation of the concept head capacity, no analysis of whether HAM's body-part localization is simply too coarsely annotated for a pixel-level slot model to learn. The comparison to DINOv2 is partially misleading: DINOv2 is a frozen backbone pretrained on internet-scale data and then probed, not fine-tuned end-to-end for concept prediction — the advantage of SlotVAE at 1% supervision may partly reflect DINOv2's domain mismatch rather than inherent data efficiency of the proposed method. No concept-bottleneck model (CBM) baselines are included, which is the most directly comparable interpretable-prediction family and an important omission. All synthetic evaluations use CLEVR-family datasets with fixed, known concept vocabularies; real-world applicability when the concept vocabulary is unknown or needs to be discovered is not addressed. No code is released, and the custom 2D-CLEVR dataset is described only in supplementary material, making exact replication difficult. Bottom Line: A competent and honest study with a genuinely useful empirical insight about ILP noise-robustness, but the unresolved failure on multi-concept conjunctive rules in the medical domain, absence of code, and missing CBM/concept-bottleneck baselines mean practitioners cannot yet deploy this system for the high-stakes interpretable AI use cases the paper motivates.
Search Results 2605.08201 PDF
#123 — Moderately Innovative (55)

From questions to insights: how domain-specific semantics boost LLM access to touristic real-time occupancy data

link.springer.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a text-to-SPARQL semantic agent, grounded in a tourism occupancy ontology (TOO) and the OBDA paradigm, that achieves 42.1% accuracy on 19 domain-specific tourism competency questions while costing $0.074/question — outperforming state-of-the-art text-to-SQL systems on both dimensions. - Why it's important: LLM-driven visitor management chatbots must query complex, real-time occupancy databases, but leading text-to-SQL systems collapse on domain-specific real-world schemas, making conversational access to overcrowding data practically infeasible today. - How it does it: Instead of generating SQL over raw database tables, the system translates natural language to SPARQL over a domain ontology, then uses the Ontop OBDA framework to rewrite SPARQL to SQL at execution time — decoupling the LLM from schema-level technicalities and letting it reason in domain vocabulary. - The good: The semantic agent (42.1% accuracy, $0.074/question, avg 0.8 DB queries/question) outperforms ReFoRCE (31.6% accuracy, $2.797/question, avg 84.8 DB queries/question) — a relative accuracy gain of ~33% at 1/38th the cost. - The missing: The evaluation rests on only 19 expert-crafted competency questions on a single proprietary database, making the claimed superiority (a margin of 2 additional correct answers) statistically unreliable. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building LLM-based querying over domain-specific structured data; Section 5's findings on ontology simplicity, prompt sensitivity, and agentic instability are concrete and actionable, but replicate on your own domain before adopting the architecture.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (55) moderate — combines existing OBDA/SPARQL ideas with LLM generation; the empirical finding that simpler ontologies help is interesting but incremental. Rigor (40) weak — n=19 questions, single proprietary database, synthetic occupancy data, no ablations, no code release, vendor-author conflict, potential co-design circularity. Non-Obviousness (55) modest — semantic mediation for LLM querying is a known pattern. Completeness (60) — honest about scope but missing key baselines. Resilience (45) — claimed superiority collapses with one-question flip. Impact (55) — working prototype but no released artifact, fails on spatial queries. Composite: 0.20*55 + 0.25*40 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*55 = 50. Rounded up slightly for the genuinely actionable Section 5 lessons.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 60 Resilience 45 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tourism destinations managing overtourism need real-time access to occupancy data — parking fill rates, hiking trail crowdedness, POI visitor counts — to reroute visitors and prevent overcrowding. This data is stored in relational databases with mixed data types, non-standardized units, and highly domain-specific labels that LLMs cannot query directly via SQL. Existing text-to-SQL systems, even those at the top of the Spider 2.0 leaderboard, achieve only 37.11/100 on that benchmark and perform far worse on real-world databases with domain-specific schemas. The gap this paper addresses: no prior system provides accurate, cost-efficient natural language access to dynamic, multi-level occupancy data by leveraging domain ontologies as semantic mediators.
