Key Idea: The central insight is that translating natural language to SPARQL over a domain ontology — rather than SQL over raw schema — removes the burden on the LLM to understand database implementation details. Via the OBDA paradigm, a conceptual mapping (M) connects ontology classes to database tables, and the Ontop framework rewrites SPARQL to SQL transparently at execution time. The LLM reasons over domain concepts (routes, parking spaces, occupancy measurements with provenance and confidence) rather than over column names like `occupancy_object` or `occupancy_determination`, producing more semantically grounded queries. Methodology: Two text-to-SPARQL approaches are implemented and compared against three text-to-SQL baselines, all using GPT-4o to keep model-level effects constant. (1) Zero-shot text-to-SPARQL: a single LLM call receives the full OWL ontology in TTL format plus a minimal prompt, generates one SPARQL query, and executes it via Ontop with no retry. (2) Semantic Agent: loads the ontology → generates SPARQL → validates syntax locally (checking for missing prefixes, unsupported functions) → appends errors to the prompt and regenerates (up to 10 iterations) → executes the first syntactically valid query via Ontop → returns results. No critic component is used (an earlier critic mechanism caused overthinking loops). Baselines are Zero-Shot SQL (single-shot), C3-SQL (up to 5 SQL candidates, Spider 1 leaderboard), and ReFoRCE (iterative refinement with exploration and consensus, leading Spider 2.0 system at ~37/100). The ontology (TOO) covers multi-level occupancy with Geography, Occupancy Core, Measurement Extension, and Determination Extension modules, enriched with natural language annotations to improve LLM interpretability. It is combined with ODTA (touristic schema.org extension) via semantic mappings. Evaluation uses 19 expert-crafted competency questions (CQs) across four categories: Occupancy, Peak Time Analysis, Proximity, and Combined. Two human experts manually score each query result as correct or incorrect (C ∈ {0,1}), with consensus on disagreements. The database is an excerpt from the Outdooractive platform (~1.2M rows across 8 tables), with synthetically generated occupancy values. Key Results: Across 19 CQs: Zero-Shot SQL achieves 10.5% accuracy ($0.008/question, 1 DB query/question); C3-SQL achieves 0.0% accuracy ($0.178/question, 3.2 DB queries/question) — it generates up to 5 SQL candidates but fails to answer any question correctly, with 8 of 11 executable queries producing execution errors due to type mismatches or incorrect filtering; ReFoRCE achieves 31.6% accuracy at $2.797/question, executing an average of 84.8 SQL queries and consuming ~952K input tokens per question; Zero-Shot SPARQL achieves 36.8% accuracy at $0.018/question; the Semantic Agent achieves 42.1% accuracy at $0.074/question with an average of only 0.8 DB queries per question (skips execution when syntax cannot be fixed). On occupancy-specific questions (Group 1), the semantic agent achieves 63.6% accuracy vs. 54.5% for zero-shot SPARQL. On proximity questions (Group 3) and combined questions (Group 4) involving spatial reasoning, all five approaches score 0 — a complete failure traced to ODTA not being adapted for LLM use and the absence of relevant OPTIONAL clauses in generated queries. C3-SQL's collapse from Spider 1 benchmark performance to 0% on this domain-specific database confirms the well-known benchmark-to-real-world transfer failure. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The SPARQL/OBDA approach to NL-database querying is not new (Xiao et al. 2019; Calvanese et al. 2015); the novelty lies in: (1) integrating a domain-specific multi-level occupancy ontology with LLM-based SPARQL generation and iterative syntax correction for real-time data access, and (2) the empirical finding that this semantic mediation outperforms leading text-to-SQL systems at a fraction of the cost on a domain-specific real-world database. The result that simpler, less expressive ontologies outperform more complex, richly annotated ones in terms of LLM interpretability is a non-obvious empirical finding with practical design implications. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are provided to isolate the contribution of individual ontology modules (TOO vs. ODTA), the natural language annotations, or the syntax-checking loop. No statistical significance tests are reported; with n=19, a margin of 2 additional correct answers (the gap between Semantic Agent and ReFoRCE) is within noise. The evaluation is run multiple times and the majority-vote score reported (due to GPT-4o's non-determinism), which is methodologically sound but not standard in the field. The database is proprietary (Outdooractive AG), available only upon request with licensing approval. Occupancy data is synthetic. No code release is mentioned. Three of the seven authors (Neubig, Göhl, Radhakrishnan) are employed by Outdooractive AG, whose platform is the sole evaluation substrate — the authors do not flag this as a limitation. The ontology and CQs share design lineage (both from Neubig et al. 2025 and an expert workshop), creating a potential circularity that would inflate text-to-SPARQL over text-to-SQL performance. The authors explicitly note the comparison is "indicative rather than definitive." Deployability (EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS): - Practical: Requires OWL ontology engineering, conceptual mapping authorship, and a running Ontop instance in addition to LLM API access — achievable for organizations with IT capacity, but non-trivial to operationalize for a new domain. - Efficient: The semantic agent uses 23,989 input tokens and 1,425 output tokens per question at $0.074, versus ReFoRCE's ~952K input tokens at $2.797 — a 38× cost reduction with higher accuracy. - Effective: 42.1% accuracy on domain-specific occupancy queries beats all baselines, but fails completely on spatial/proximity questions and is far from the reliability threshold needed for production visitor management decisions. - Scalable: Not tested beyond the single 1.2M-row Outdooractive excerpt. Spatial query failures indicate the current architecture cannot handle geospatial reasoning at any scale. No claim of scalability is tested empirically. The paper delivers a working prototype (semantic agent + Ontop integration) but no released software artifact. Strengths: - The cost-accuracy Pareto comparison is concrete and compelling: the semantic agent achieves the highest accuracy at $0.074/question while ReFoRCE costs $2.797 for lower accuracy — a practically meaningful result for resource-constrained deployments. - The finding that simpler ontologies outperform more expressive ones (ODTA's complexity hurt proximity performance) challenges the intuition that richer semantics always help and provides actionable ontology engineering guidance. - The honest, category-level failure analysis — all approaches score 0 on proximity and combined questions — traces failures to specific, correctable design decisions (missing OPTIONAL clauses, LLM-unfriendly ODTA annotations) rather than hand-waving. - The empirical observation that LLMs frequently ignore explicit instructions regardless of prompt position (contradicting Liu et al. 2024) and exhibit overthinking in critic loops is an actionable finding for practitioners designing agentic systems. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: evaluation on a single ontology and 19 expert-crafted CQs that cannot capture real-world query diversity; synthetic occupancy data reducing ecological validity; minor ontology label or annotation changes causing substantial accuracy shifts; and the comparison being "indicative rather than definitive" due to incompatible helper annotation requirements across text-to-SQL and text-to-SPARQL families. Future directions include finetuning for prompt robustness, constrained agent architectures, and RAG integration for entity recognition. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Statistical power: n=19 makes it impossible to claim reliable superiority; the 42.1% vs. 31.6% gap corresponds to 8 vs. 6 correct answers. A single-question flip reverses the narrative. - Potential circularity: the TOO ontology was designed specifically to answer tourism occupancy questions, and the CQs were drawn from an expert workshop likely involving ontology designers — the benchmark may be co-designed with the method, inflating the advantage of text-to-SPARQL over text-to-SQL. - No comparison to RAG-augmented SQL or hybrid approaches for the qualitative/spatial queries that completely defeat the current system. - The abandoned critic component is described qualitatively but never ablated — it is unclear whether a constrained critic (e.g., limited to one pass) would improve or degrade results. - More recent text-to-SQL systems (e.g., CHESS, SQLFixAgent) are not included in the comparison; the paper was accepted in February 2026 but some baselines predate late 2024 advances. - No end-to-end user evaluation: accuracy on 19 expert-crafted CQs does not measure whether real tourists formulate questions that match the CQ phrasings or whether the system handles natural query variation. Bottom Line: Practitioners building LLM-based conversational interfaces over domain-specific structured databases will find concrete value in the cost-efficiency argument and the Section 5 lessons on ontology design and agentic instability, but the 19-question proprietary evaluation is too small and potentially co-designed to trust the claimed superiority without independent replication.
Search Results
#124 — Moderately Innovative (55)

GRAPHLCP: Structure-Aware Localized Conformal Prediction on Graphs

Peyman Baghershahi, Fangxin Wang, Debmalya Mandal, Sourav Medya · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GraphLCP, a localized conformal prediction framework for GNNs that replaces embedding-space proximity with a Personalized PageRank (PPR)-based structural kernel for both anchor sampling and calibration weighting, combined with a feature-aware graph densification step to correct sparse-graph locality bias. - Why it's important: GNN embeddings are degraded by over-smoothing and over-squashing, so embedding-space kernels used by existing localized CP methods collapse to degenerate weight distributions — either concentrating all mass on a single point or giving uniform weights — destroying the locality that conditional coverage requires. - How it does it: It augments the graph topology by adding edges where PCA-projected embedding similarity exceeds a homophily-adaptive threshold, then runs PPR diffusion on the densified graph to derive anchor sampling probabilities and calibration weights that respect multi-hop structural proximity. - The good: The ablation isolating PPR versus a Gaussian kernel on the same PCA-transformed embeddings (Table 2) shows that structural localization alone, independent of the densification trick, consistently raises worst-slab coverage and tightens prediction intervals across 10 datasets. - The missing: CalLCP — the strongest baseline by worst-slab coverage on small-to-medium graphs — is excluded from large graphs due to exceeding a 3-day time limit, so the claim that GraphLCP outperforms on large classification datasets rests on an incomplete comparison field. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on uncertainty quantification for GNNs or localized conformal prediction in structured data; practitioners needing a deployable solution should note the 4–17× runtime overhead over RLCP and the absence of a public code release.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Pulled down by Rigor (55) — preprint, no code, mild circularity (homophily-tuned threshold + homophily-partitioned evaluation), strongest baseline CalLCP excluded from large graphs. Impact (45) low — 4–17× runtime overhead, no software released, small-medium graphs only. Novelty (62) moderate — PPR-as-kernel within RLCP is a clean synthesis but components established.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Practitioners deploying GNNs for high-stakes predictions (drug discovery, fraud detection, weather forecasting) need calibrated uncertainty sets, not point estimates. Classical split CP provides marginal coverage but fails to provide coverage guarantees conditional on local context. Localized CP (LCP) addresses this by weighting calibration samples by proximity to the test point, but proximity must be measured in a reliable feature space. GNN embeddings are an unreliable proxy: over-smoothing in deep architectures collapses inter-node distances in embedding space, and over-squashing restricts global information flow in sparse or heterophilic regions. Applying a Gaussian kernel in this degraded space either concentrates all weight on the test point (small bandwidth) or assigns near-uniform weights (large bandwidth), reducing LCP to either an uninformative singleton or standard split CP. Existing graph-aware CP methods that incorporate structure either require training auxiliary models or rely on rigid community partitions, both of which sacrifice the lightweight post-hoc nature of CP. Key Idea: The key insight is that graph topology — accessed via PPR diffusion on the original or lightly augmented graph — provides a more stable and discriminative notion of proximity for conformal weighting than GNN embedding distances, because PPR is robust to the lossy compression introduced by message passing. Embeddings are more reliable for binary edge-creation decisions (discrete thresholding is noise-tolerant) than for continuous kernel weighting; PPR then provides continuous, directional, multi-hop proximity over the augmented graph. Methodology: GraphLCP operates as a three-stage post-hoc wrapper around any frozen GNN. Stage 1 (Feature-Aware Densification): compute global graph homophily η_G; use it to automatically set a densification threshold τ_d via iterative edge-density feedback control; project frozen GNN embeddings to c principal components via PCA on calibration embeddings, yielding PCA matrix V_c and eigenvalue matrix Λ_c; construct an anisotropic Gaussian kernel H(x_i, x_j; h²Λ_c) = exp(−½(z_i − z_j)^T (h²Λ_c)^{−1} (z_i − z_j)) where z_i = V_c^T x_i; add edge (i,j) to Ā wherever the existing graph has no edge and kernel similarity ≥ τ_d. Stage 2 (Anchor Sampling): sample walk length k ~ Geom(α_ppr) (with K=30 truncation, β ∈ {0.2, 0.4}), then sample anchor node ũ_{n+1} by a k-step random walk on the densified transition matrix M̄ = D̄^{-1}Ā from the test node u_{n+1}; this instantiates RLCP's randomized localization with PPR as the kernel. Stage 3 (Structural Weighting): compute the PPR vector π̃_{n+1} seeded at anchor ũ_{n+1} via power iteration (K=30 steps); assign weight w̃_i = d_i^{-1}[π̃_{n+1}]_i / Σ_j d_j^{-1}[π̃_{n+1}]_j with a degree-normalization symmetry correction; compute the weighted (1−α)-quantile of calibration nonconformity scores; return the prediction set {y : s(x_{n+1}, y) ≤ q̂}. Critical design decisions: (1) PCA projection before densification is well-justified — it removes correlated dimensions that inflate embedding distances and reduce the discriminability needed for thresholding; (2) the adaptive densification threshold τ_d tied to homophily is empirically motivated (Appendix A.3) but creates a dependency between the tuning signal and the main evaluation axis; (3) the degree-normalized PPR symmetry correction for undirected graphs is necessary for computational tractability when n < m but is a pragmatic approximation. Key Results: All methods achieve target marginal coverage (α=0.1, target 0.90) across all 13 datasets (Table 1), confirming the CP guarantee holds — marginal coverage is acknowledged as merely a sanity check. The primary metric is worst-slab coverage (WSC), which adversarially searches for the linear slice of test space with minimum coverage. On WSC (Table 4), GraphLCP achieves second-best results across datasets (0.61–0.80 range), trailing CalLCP (0.70–0.80) on smaller datasets but exceeding other baselines. CalLCP is excluded from CMP (13,752 nodes), PMD (19,717 nodes), and DBLP (17,716 nodes) due to runtime exceeding 3 days. On the coverage-versus-efficiency trade-off (Figure 3), GraphLCP achieves shorter normalized prediction lengths than CalLCP at comparable WSC. Group-based conditional coverage (Figure 4) shows consistent GraphLCP superiority under homophily-based and feature-clustering-based partitioning, which are the partitions most aligned with the method's design. Ablation: replacing the PPR kernel with a Gaussian kernel on PCA embeddings (GSS) while keeping densification reduces WSC by ~0.03–0.06 across datasets (Table 2), confirming PPR contributes independently. Densification is the single most impactful ablation component on regression datasets (Table 3, %NI metric). Runtime (Table 7): GraphLCP requires 111s on Computers, 508s on DBLP, and 145s on PubMed, versus 4.4/7.5/9.2s for SCP and 29.7/28.3/30.7s for RLCP — a 4–17× overhead. Datasets range up to 19,717 nodes and 491,722 edges (Computers). Baselines include both generic localized CP (RLCP, CalLCP) and graph-specific methods (NAPS, CF-GNN, SNAPS), and are current as of 2023–2025. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is integrating PPR as a graph-native localization kernel within the RLCP randomized conformal prediction framework, combined with homophily-adaptive graph densification using embedding-space thresholding as a preprocessing step. The component techniques (PPR, PCA, anisotropic Gaussian kernels, RLCP) are individually well-known; the contribution is their synthesis into a pipeline that provably preserves marginal coverage while empirically improving conditional coverage for GNNs. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: PPR vs. GSS kernel (Table 2), per-component ablations (Table 3), sensitivity to β (Figure 7) and τ_d^(0) (Figure 8). Error bars (±std) are reported throughout; the number of seeds/runs is not stated explicitly. No code or model weights repository is mentioned anywhere in the paper. The densification threshold τ_d is set via homophily η_G, and the main group-based evaluation showing the largest GraphLCP advantages uses homophily as the partitioning variable (Figure 4) — a mild circularity concern. CalLCP's exclusion from large graphs on runtime grounds means the strongest baseline is absent from the most favorable demonstration setting. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.08074v1, May 2026); no peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090; no specialized hardware required beyond what trains the base GNN. - Efficient: 4–17× slower than RLCP (111–508s vs. 28–30s on medium graphs), driven by PPR computation over the densified graph; the authors argue sparsity thresholding keeps it practical, but the DBLP runtime of 508s is a significant bottleneck for interactive use. - Effective: Demonstrates genuine improvements in worst-slab and group-based conditional coverage over RLCP and graph-specific baselines, with shorter prediction intervals than CalLCP; effectiveness is strongest on heterophilic graphs, which are the target use case. - Scalable: Tested up to 19,717 nodes and 491,722 edges; behavior at 10× larger scale (∼200K nodes, 5M edges) is not evaluated and not claimed — PPR power iteration and graph densification both have O(|E|) cost per iteration, so quadratic densification in dense subgraphs could be problematic at scale. No software artifact is released. Strengths: - The empirical diagnosis of why embedding-space kernels fail for GNN-based CP (Figure 5, Appendix A.2) — showing that Gaussian kernels on GNN embeddings collapse to degenerate weight distributions regardless of bandwidth — is a concrete, verifiable motivation that distinguishes this work from prior methods that simply add structural features as an afterthought. - The PPR kernel ablation (Table 2) cleanly isolates the structural localization benefit from the densification benefit, providing credible evidence that the structural kernel is the core driver of improvement. - Testing across 13 datasets spanning regression and classification, including OOD benchmarks (GOOD-WebKB, GOOD-CBAS) and geospatial/social domains, is broader than typical graph CP evaluations. - The automatic densification threshold setting via homophily-guided edge-density feedback (Appendix A.5) removes a critical hyperparameter that would otherwise require per-graph tuning. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge one limitation: the method does not yet extend to dynamic graphs with evolving topologies, stated as future work. They do not discuss the runtime overhead relative to RLCP as a practical limitation (it appears only in Appendix A.9 without acknowledgment in the main paper). They do not acknowledge the circularity between homophily-based threshold tuning and homophily-based evaluation partitioning. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The exclusion of CalLCP from large-graph experiments on a time-limit basis is the most significant gap: the paper's strongest claims about outperforming on large classification datasets rely on a field missing the best baseline. The authors do not discuss whether a scalable CalLCP approximation could close the gap. - The homophily-based densification threshold τ_d is set in a way that correlates with the homophily partition used in group-based evaluation (Figure 4); while not outright circular (the threshold uses global graph homophily, not per-node homophily labels), this correlation inflates the apparent advantage on the most prominent evaluation axis. - No code is released, and PPR computation details (approximation quality, K=30 truncation error bounds) are only loosely justified; reproducibility requires non-trivial reimplementation. - All graphs tested have ≤ 19,717 nodes; it is unknown whether densification (O(n²) candidate edge pairs) or PPR power iteration remains practical on million-node graphs common in real GNN deployments. - The nonconformity score used throughout is the absolute residual (regression) or softmax-based score (classification) from a fixed GCN; the method's advantage may depend on this specific base model and score function, which is not ablated. Bottom Line: GraphLCP makes a credible and practically motivated contribution for researchers building uncertainty-aware GNN systems on small-to-medium graphs, but the 4–17× runtime cost, absent code release, and incompleteness of large-graph comparisons mean practitioners should treat it as a principled research prototype rather than a drop-in improvement over RLCP.
Search Results 2605.08074 PDF
#125 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Data Language Models: A New Foundation Model Class for Tabular Data

Eda Erol, Giuliano Pezzoli, Ozer Cem Kelahmet · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Data Language Model (DLM), a formally defined foundation model class for tabular data, and Schema-1, a 140M-parameter instantiation trained on 2.3M datasets that natively ingests raw tables without preprocessing and simultaneously outputs both domain identification and row-level predictions. - Why it's important: Tabular data is the primary substrate for enterprise AI decisions, yet every existing approach requires a human-assembled preprocessing pipeline and domain specification before inference can begin — a structural barrier that makes fully autonomous agentic AI over raw enterprise data impossible. - How it does it: Schema-1 jointly encodes four input signals (column semantics, per-column distributional summaries, raw cell values, missing value structure) under a dual training objective — predict target values AND identify the domain that generated the data — so the model learns both within-dataset conditional structure and cross-dataset distributional identity simultaneously. - The good: Schema-1 achieves mean ROC-AUC 0.9849 on OpenML-CC18 (first on all 18/18 datasets, margin 0.0510 over next-best TabPFN+AG), imputation NRMSE 0.163 (31% below the best frontier LLM, 64% below TabPFN), and 91.4% top-1 accuracy on a 10,000-class blind sector classification task that no prior tabular model can even attempt. - The missing: Schema-1's internal architecture (attention mechanism, retention and adaptive memory components) is described only by label, not mechanism; the sector classification benchmark is defined, taxonomized, and evaluated entirely by SchemaLabs on their own taxonomy, creating an unacknowledged circularity; and TabPFN-2.5 and TabICLv2 — the most directly competitive models — are excluded from the main CC18 table. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building vertical AI applications or tabular foundation models — the DLM paradigm and the empirical case that structural co-distributional learning beats world knowledge for imputation are both genuinely new contributions worth engaging with, though the commercial provenance warrants calibrated skepticism.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (75) is real — DLM formalization and the imputation finding (structural > world knowledge under MNAR) are genuine. But Rigor (40) is materially weak: no error bars on any headline, sector taxonomy is SchemaLabs-defined and Schema-1 trained on synthetic data from that same taxonomy (severe Goodhart/circularity, unacknowledged), no code/weights released, architecture components named but never described, TabPFN-2.5 and TabICLv2 excluded. Resilience (35) low — claims collapse under scrutiny of the circular benchmark. Completeness (45) — limitations partially acknowledged but key ones (architecture, circularity, compute) omitted. Impact (60) — production deployment described but no public artifact. Commercial preprint provenance further reduces Rigor. Weighted (systems): 0.15*75 + 0.15*40 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*35 + 0.30*60 = 55.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 45 Resilience 35 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Tabular data drives the most consequential deployed ML systems — healthcare dosing, fraud detection, industrial fault prediction — yet no existing model class understands a table natively. Tree-based methods and AutoML require feature engineering, encoding, and target column specification. Tabular foundation models (TabPFN family) reduce cold-start cost but still require a specified target column, normalization, and provide no account of what the input dataset represents. LLM-based approaches serialize tables to text, discarding joint distributional structure between columns and tying the model to human-readable column names that are routinely absent in production (internal codes, legacy field names, privacy-stripped headers). Critically, none of these paradigms treats domain identification as a training objective: in every case, understanding what a dataset is has been handled upstream by a human assembling the pipeline. For vertical AI applications that must operate on raw enterprise data across multiple domains at runtime — and for AI agents that encounter data sources not known at design time — this dependency on human-in-the-loop domain specification is not a manageable cost but a categorical barrier. Key Idea The key insight is that understanding what a dataset is (domain identification from raw distributional structure) and what to predict from it (row-level target values) are co-learnable objectives that reinforce one another: a model that correctly identifies 10,000 industry sectors from raw cell value distributions must have learned cross-dataset distributional signatures that also improve within-dataset conditional prediction and missing value reconstruction. Making domain identification a co-equal primary training objective — not a post-hoc annotation or a downstream head — is the architectural commitment that distinguishes the DLM class from all prior tabular model paradigms. The corollary is that column names become one input pathway among four rather than a necessary condition, enabling metadata-independent operation with a quantified, bounded degradation. Methodology Schema-1 is a 140M-parameter model trained on 2,307,000 datasets (2,000,000 synthetic generated from a controlled 10,000-sector taxonomy + 307,000 real-world datasets from public and domain-specific sources). Four input signals are fused into a joint representation where each signal conditions interpretation of the others: (1) column semantics — grounded encodings of column identifiers, zeroed when absent; (2) per-column distributional summaries — central tendency, dispersion, range, numeric/total ratio, unique value density, observed value count, computed directly from cell values without user metadata; (3) raw cell values — numeric and categorical unified in a single cell encoding; (4) missing value structure — the pattern of present and absent values encoded as a structural signal rather than an error to impute. The training objective jointly satisfies three formally stated conditions: multi-signal native ingestion (all four pathways processed jointly), dataset-level contextual inference (domain identification as a primary co-output, not annotation), and metadata-independent operation (removing all column names causes AUC degradation ≤ ε = 0.0117, empirically verified at 0.0117). The synthetic training corpus provides sector-specific distributional coverage across the full 10,000-sector taxonomy; the real-world corpus anchors representational realism for irregular distributions, measurement noise, and encoding conventions. Fine-tuning creates isolated customer-specific checkpoints; the base model weights are frozen throughout. Three critical design decisions: (1) treating missingness as a structural signal rather than an error — directly justified by Schema-1 at 70% missingness outperforming all tested systems at 50% missingness; (2) making column semantics optional rather than required — justified by the 98.8% AUC retention under full column-name removal; (3) the joint prediction + domain-identification training objective — justified by the mutual reinforcement argument but the specific architectural components (described only as a "retention component" and "adaptive memory component" for sequential fine-tuning) are named but never mechanistically described. Key Results On OpenML-CC18 (18 classification datasets, 10-fold stratified CV, 60-minute training budget, ROC-AUC one-vs-one following Hollmann et al. 2023 protocol): Schema-1 achieves mean ROC-AUC 0.9849, ranking first on all 18/18 datasets. The margin over next-best (TabPFN+AG, 0.9339) is 0.0510 — larger than the full spread across all other seven competitors (0.9196–0.9339). On the five hardest datasets (diabetes, blood-transfusion, pc1, pc3, kc2), Schema-1 moves from the 0.71–0.88 performance band of all prior models to 0.94–0.98. On missing data robustness (MCAR across 15 CC18 datasets at 0%, 10%, 20%, 30%, 50%, 70%): Schema-1 mean ROC-AUC 0.9196, declining 0.0603 from 0% to 70%; MIRRAMS (a framework specifically designed for missingness robustness) declines 0.1188; XGBoost+Mean declines 0.2820. At 70% missingness, Schema-1 (0.8815) outperforms MIRRAMS at 50% missingness (0.8721). On tabular imputation quality (20 real-world datasets, 9 missingness conditions under MCAR/MAR/MNAR at 5%, 10%, 20%, metric NRMSE lower-is-better, following Mangussi et al. 2026 methodology): Schema-1 NRMSE 0.163 versus Gemini 3.0 Flash 0.235 (−31%), missForest 0.302 (−46%), TabPFN 0.448 (−64%). Advantage widens sharply under MNAR where LLM domain priors provide no traction. On column-agnostic prediction (20 OpenML datasets, column names zeroed): Schema-1 ROC-AUC 0.9318 with no names, versus TabuLa-8B 0.8658 and ConTextTab 0.8541 — both evaluated with full column name access. Schema-1's degradation from full names to no names is 0.0117 (1.24%) versus 0.0709/0.0748 for TabuLa-8B/ConTextTab. On blind dataset sector classification (500 held-out OpenML datasets, 10,000-class task, no labels, no column names, no metadata): Schema-1 achieves 91.4% top-1 and 97.0% top-5 accuracy versus random baseline 0.01%. On sequential fine-tuning retention (50 sequential cycles on CC18+TabReD datasets, no replay of prior data): Schema-1 retains 97.8% task-1 accuracy and 98.1% mean retention; catastrophic forgetting rate 1.9%. Ablation: removing both the retention component and adaptive memory component reduces retention to 34.2%. Competitors sourced from Hollmann et al. 2023 at identical splits; TabPFN-2.5 and TabICLv2 excluded because they have not published under the Hollmann protocol. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM (blind dataset sector classification as a formal benchmark task with no prior analogue), NEW FORMULATION (DLM as a model class defined by three necessary-and-sufficient conditions with Condition 3 quantified as a formal inequality), NEW SYSTEM/TOOL (Schema-1 as the first DLM instantiation). The individual technical components — transformer architectures on tabular data, synthetic pretraining, multi-task learning, missing value encoding — are each known; the novelty is in making domain identification a co-equal primary training objective, the four-pathway multi-signal ingestion architecture that degrades gracefully without column semantics, and the empirical demonstration that structural co-distributional learning dominates world knowledge for imputation across all missingness mechanisms tested. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative for the sequential fine-tuning claim: the retention component and adaptive memory component are each ablated individually and jointly (Table 6), establishing that 97.8% retention degrades to 34.2% with both ablated — the single most compelling ablation in the paper. The column-agnostic evaluation (Figure 8, three conditions) provides a clean isolation of the column semantics pathway contribution. CC18 competitor numbers are sourced from Hollmann et al. 2023 at identical splits and dataset versions — not re-run by SchemaLabs — which is methodologically sound. The imputation evaluation follows Mangussi et al. 2026's benchmark and sources competitor numbers from that study. However: (1) no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported for any headline result; (2) the sector classification benchmark, its 10,000-sector taxonomy, the 500 test datasets, and the train/test contamination boundary are all SchemaLabs-controlled — the model was trained on synthetic data generated from this exact taxonomy and evaluated on this exact taxonomy, a circularity not acknowledged in the paper; (3) architecture details beyond input signal labels are withheld — the "retention component" and "adaptive memory component" are never mechanistically described; (4) no code, model weights, or training data are released; (5) this is an arXiv preprint from a commercial entity with no indication of peer review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS) Profile: SYSTEMS — Schema-1 is a built, deployed foundation model evaluated across six benchmarks. - Practical: Schema-1 at 140M parameters is commercially deployable for inference; training compute on 2.3M datasets is not disclosed, making total resource cost opaque. - Efficient: Inference latency relative to XGBoost, LightGBM, or TabPFN is never reported; the 60-minute per-fold budget is the user's fine-tuning allocation, not Schema-1's inference cost. - Effective: Evidence across six benchmarks consistently supports the claimed capabilities; the structural imputation argument is particularly well-supported by the MNAR results where LLM domain priors provably cannot help; the sector classification result is compelling but methodologically circular. - Scalable: Schema-1 handles 2.3M training datasets and 10,000 sectors but performance on very wide tables (100+ columns), high-cardinality categoricals, or large row counts is not evaluated; the enterprise scaling claims are stated without stress-scale experiments. The paper describes a production deployment architecture (Model Runtime, customer-specific isolated checkpoints) but delivers no public software artifact. Strengths - The DLM formalization (Definition 1 with three quantified conditions, including Condition 3 stated as a formal inequality with ε = 0.0117) converts an intuition about "native understanding" into a falsifiable model class specification with testable properties — a genuine conceptual contribution independent of Schema-1's performance. - The imputation finding challenges a prevailing assumption: structural co-distributional learning (NRMSE 0.163) beats frontier LLMs (0.235) and all classical statistical methods (0.302–0.424) on 20 real-world datasets, and the MNAR analysis provides a clean mechanistic explanation (domain priors cannot help when missingness depends on unobserved variables; distributional structure can). - The column-agnostic benchmark design is genuinely adversarial: Schema-1 without any column names outperforms ConTextTab and TabuLa-8B when those models have full column name access, isolating distributional structure as the operative mechanism and not merely demonstrating robustness to a degraded input. - Sequential fine-tuning retention (97.8% after 50 cycles, ablation-verified at 34.2% baseline) addresses a real and under-studied operational problem in enterprise ML — the perpetual retrain cycle — that no prior tabular model class was architected to solve. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors disclose that TabPFN-2.5 and TabICLv2 are excluded from the CC18 comparison due to unavailability of results under the Hollmann et al. protocol. They frame the 1.24% AUC degradation from removing column names as evidence supporting Condition 3 rather than as a limitation, which is technically accurate but understates the residual dependency. The paper does not acknowledge the sector classification taxonomy circularity, does not discuss failure modes or table types where Schema-1 underperforms, and does not disclose training compute. Architecture details of the retention and memory components are absent without acknowledgment. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Sector classification circularity: Schema-1 was trained on synthetic data generated from SchemaLabs' 10,000-sector taxonomy and evaluated on 500 held-out datasets classified against that same taxonomy. This does not test whether the model can identify domains defined by an external taxonomy (e.g., NAICS codes with independently labeled datasets). The 91.4% top-1 result may reflect taxonomy memorization as much as structural comprehension. - Missing TabPFN-2.5 and TabICLv2: These are the most directly competitive models; their absence from Table 3 is disclosed but still leaves the strongest contemporary baselines unevaluated under a common protocol. - Architecture opacity: The retention component and adaptive memory component that account for the difference between 34.2% and 97.8% sequential retention are never described at the mechanism level. A reader cannot understand what was built or replicate it. - No error bars on headline numbers: All CC18 results are single-run cross-validation means; no confidence intervals or paired significance tests are reported for the 0.0510 margin claim. - Inference efficiency not reported: Latency relative to XGBoost and TabPFN — the practical deployment competitors — is absent, making it impossible to assess deployability against the baseline that currently dominates production. - Table scale limits not tested: Performance on tables with >100 columns, high-cardinality categoricals, or millions of rows is not evaluated; the enterprise scalability claims are unsupported by stress-scale experiments. Bottom Line A practitioner building vertical AI applications, tabular foundation models, or agentic systems over enterprise data should read this paper — the DLM paradigm is a genuine conceptual contribution and the structural-vs.-world-knowledge imputation finding is the kind of empirical result that changes design intuitions — but should weight the headline numbers with appropriate skepticism given the commercial provenance, architecture opacity, absent error bars, and unacknowledged circularity in the benchmark the paper is most novel on.
Recommended 2605.06290 PDF
#126 — Moderately Innovative (55)

NoiseRater: Meta-Learned Noise Valuation for Diffusion Model Training

arxiv.org · Jure Leskovec - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NoiseRater, a meta-learned parametric noise rater that assigns per-instance importance scores to noise realizations conditioned on image content, timestep, and class label, enabling adaptive reweighting of the diffusion training objective. - Why it's important: Standard diffusion training samples noise uniformly and treats all realizations equally at a given timestep, but some noise instances produce more useful training gradients than others — exploiting this heterogeneity improves generation quality without changing the model architecture or inference procedure. - How it does it: A bilevel optimization framework trains the noise rater in an outer loop to minimize held-out validation loss after inner-loop diffusion updates on noise-weighted losses; a second stage then freezes the rater and uses it for hard top-1 noise selection during standard training, amortizing the meta-training cost across all subsequent runs. - The good: A rater trained on DiT-S/2 transfers zero-shot (frozen) to DiT-B/2 and DiT-L/2, consistently improving FID across backbone sizes — the strongest evidence that the rater captures backbone-agnostic noise quality rather than model-specific memorization. - The missing: No comparison to Immiscible Diffusion [27,28], the closest prior method for instance-level noise assignment, on any shared benchmark — its absence leaves the main empirical claim incompletely substantiated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on diffusion training efficiency or meta-learning for training optimization; practitioners using DiT-family models can extract a concrete recipe for ~2.6% FID reduction at 1.6× per-step cost.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Pulled down by Rigor (60) — no Immiscible Diffusion comparison, single-seed FID at 0.5–1.0 deltas within metric noise, theory assumptions (strong convexity) violated in practice. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (65) are moderate; cross-backbone zero-shot transfer is the strongest signal. Practical Impact (55) — 256×256 only, no rectified-flow models, anonymous-repo preprint. Composite: 0.20*65+0.25*60+0.15*65+0.15*60+0.15*55+0.10*55 = 60.0 — but limited resolution coverage and missing key baseline justify 55.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Diffusion model training optimizes a denoising MSE objective by sampling noise uniformly from an isotropic Gaussian, treating all noise realizations as equally informative at a fixed timestep. Prior work has established that different timestep levels contribute unequally and developed timestep-level reweighting strategies (Min-SNR, P2, EDM noise schedules). However, even at the same timestep, different noise instances for the same image can vary substantially in the quality of learning signal they provide — some produce gradients well-aligned with generalization, others are redundant or conflicting. This instance-level variation is unexplored as a training-time control variable. The closest prior work, Immiscible Diffusion, assigns specific noise vectors to data points via static optimal transport based on pre-diffusion Euclidean distance, ignoring the model's evolving learning dynamics. The question this paper addresses is: given a fixed timestep and image, which specific noise realization is most useful for the diffusion model at its current training state? Key Idea The core technical insight is to cast noise selection as a meta-learning problem: a parametric noise rater φ_η is trained via bilevel optimization to assign importance scores to noise instances such that weighting the diffusion loss by these scores improves the model's held-out validation performance after several gradient steps. The gradient alignment principle (Theorem 4.2) provides the theoretical basis — noise instances whose induced training gradients are better aligned with the validation gradient should receive higher weights, so the rater effectively learns to identify noise that produces directionally useful gradient updates rather than averaging over all equally. Methodology The method operates in two stages. Stage 1 (meta-training, 2,000 outer steps): for each minibatch of B images, K=4 candidate noises are drawn per (image, timestep) tuple; the rater scores all K candidates and converts scores to weights via group-wise softmax normalization within each image group. The diffusion model is updated on the noise-weighted MSE loss for S=5 inner steps, then the rater is updated via meta-gradients to minimize the validation loss on a held-out 10% split. Meta-gradients are computed via the chain rule through the inner optimization: ∇_η J(η) = -(∇_η∇_θ L_inner)^T (∇²_θ L_inner)^{-1} ∇_θ L_val, approximated in practice using Mixflow-MG scalable bilevel reparameterization. Stage 2 (rater-guided training): the rater is frozen; for each training step K'=4 candidates are sampled and the top-1 scorer is selected for standard one-sample diffusion training. Three critical design decisions: (1) group-wise normalization isolates noise quality signal from image content variation across the batch; (2) Xavier initialization (not adaLN-Zero) breaks the symmetry that would zero-initialize all rater scores and stall bilevel training; (3) decoupling meta-training from standard training avoids the per-step overhead of maintaining K candidates throughout the full training run. The rater architecture uses dual-stream DiT blocks (~60M parameters) processing image latent and noise tokens jointly, with AdaLN conditioning on timestep and class label. Key Results Evaluated via FID-50K on FFHQ 256×256 (unconditional) and ImageNet 256×256 (unconditional and class-conditional with CFG scale 1.25), using DiT-S/2 as backbone. Against vanilla i.i.d. training and naïve norm-based selection (min/max norm among k candidates): on ImageNet unconditional, NoiseRater achieves FID 59.95 vs. vanilla 61.56 at +80k steps (2.6% relative reduction) with k=4 at 1.6× per-step cost; naïve max-norm selection scores 60.82 and min-norm 63.31, confirming the learned signal is non-trivial. On ImageNet class-conditional: 48.65 vs. vanilla 49.59. On FFHQ: 10.93 vs. vanilla 11.36. Cross-backbone transfer (rater trained once on DiT-S/2 at 400k, then frozen): DiT-B/2 gains 0.85 FID at +80k (48.65→47.80); DiT-L/2 matches or beats baseline at every checkpoint. Rater ablation: removing timestep embedding causes the largest FID drop; removing image input or class label each causes moderate degradation; the full conditioning is necessary. k=4 achieves the best quality-compute tradeoff; k=8 requires 2.1× cost with smaller gains, consistent with the hypothesis that over-restricting the noise distribution away from the Gaussian prior is harmful. Rater behavior analysis at 31 DiT checkpoints: score-norm Spearman correlation is near-zero (|ρ| < 0.1) for all trained DiT stages, ruling out collapse to a norm filter; score standard deviation peaks at t ∈ [0.6, 0.7], coinciding with empirically hardest timesteps for diffusion training. Baselines are modest — only vanilla and naïve norm selection; no comparison to Immiscible Diffusion or any other instance-level noise assignment method. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. Applying bilevel meta-learning to instance-level noise valuation in diffusion training is genuinely novel — prior meta-learning work rates training data quality, not injected noise realizations. The individual building blocks (bilevel optimization, DiT architecture, MAML-style meta-gradients, group-wise normalization) are existing tools. The contribution is identifying noise instance utility as an actionable training-time axis and designing a meta-learning framework that learns it end-to-end from generalization signal. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover rater input conditioning, candidate pool size k, backbone size, and training stage — sufficient to establish which components matter. No error bars or multiple-seed FID estimates are reported; FID differences of 0.5–1.0 at this scale can fall within metric noise. Theorem 4.1 assumes strong convexity of the inner objective — unrealistic for neural networks — and Theorem 4.2 assumes L-Lipschitz validation gradients; both are standard meta-learning assumptions that fail in practice and the gap between theory and practice is unanalyzed. The 10% validation split raises a mild Goodhart's Law concern (rater could overfit to the held-out split), which is unaddressed. Code is posted at an anonymous link (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/NoiseRater-DEB116); completeness is unverified. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.08144v1, May 2026); not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: Runs on 4 NVIDIA H100 GPUs for both rater training (one-time, ~4 hours) and standard diffusion training; commercially available at cloud providers, though not accessible to single-GPU practitioners. - Efficient: Meta-training adds 4 GPU-hours on 4 H100s as a one-time cost amortized across all subsequent diffusion runs; Stage 2 adds 1.3×–1.6× per-step cost depending on k; the rater (~60M parameters) is not ablated against smaller alternatives, leaving compute optimality unclear. - Effective: Consistent FID improvement across three task configurations and three backbone sizes provides credible evidence that the method solves the stated problem, not just a proxy — the cross-backbone zero-shot transfer is particularly convincing. - Scalable: Tested only at 256×256 resolution; no evaluation at 512×512 or higher, no text-to-image or flow-matching models; claimed broad applicability to diffusion training is unsubstantiated beyond the DiT/ImageNet/FFHQ setting. The paper delivers an anonymous code repository; no pretrained rater weights are released. Strengths - The cross-backbone transfer result (rater trained once on DiT-S/2, applied frozen to DiT-B/2 and DiT-L/2 with consistent FID gains) is the paper's strongest contribution: it demonstrates that the rater learns generalizable noise quality criteria, not a model-specific policy, making the one-time training cost genuinely amortizable. - The rater behavior analysis is intellectually disciplined: score-norm Spearman correlation measurements empirically rule out the trivial explanation (norm filtering) and localize where the rater is most discriminative (intermediate timesteps, mid-training), lending credibility to the claim that the rater learns nontrivial structure. - The gradient alignment theorem (Thm 4.2) connects noise weighting to gradient alignment with the validation objective, giving a principled interpretation that distinguishes this from prior heuristic data-weighting approaches and is testable in principle. - The decoupled two-stage pipeline is a practical engineering contribution: separating meta-training from standard training avoids per-step overhead of multi-candidate evaluation + meta-gradient computation during the full run, making deployment feasible at scale. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The meta-training hyperparameters were tuned for the 400k checkpoint and not re-tuned for 100k or 500k stages; the paper acknowledges this leaves gains at early/late training stages on the table. The authors observe that large k (k=8) harms training by deviating the noise distribution from the Gaussian prior but do not provide a solution. The authors do not explicitly acknowledge the absence of Immiscible Diffusion as a baseline, the restriction to 256×256 resolution, or the compute cost of the 60M-parameter rater as limitations. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No comparison to Immiscible Diffusion [27,28] — the most directly comparable prior method for instance-level noise control — on any shared benchmark; this omission makes the empirical case for NoiseRater's advantage incomplete. - Evaluation is limited to 256×256 resolution with DiT-S/2/B/L backbones; modern production pipelines operate at 512×512–1024×1024 and use rectified flow models (Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX), none of which are evaluated. - Single FID run per configuration without variance estimates; the 0.5–1.0 FID improvements reported may not be statistically distinguishable from run-to-run noise at this scale. - The rater architecture (~60M parameters, matching DiT-S/2 in size) is never ablated against smaller architectures; a much simpler rater may achieve comparable discrimination with less overhead. - The strong convexity assumption underlying Theorem 4.1 is violated by neural network diffusion models; the practical approximation (Mixflow-MG, finite inner steps) degrades the meta-gradient in unknown ways, and no analysis of this approximation error is provided. - The 10% validation split introduces a Goodhart risk: the rater could learn to select noise that minimizes validation loss for idiosyncratic reasons unrelated to generalization; no analysis of rater overfitting to the held-out split is provided. Bottom Line A practitioner training DiT-family image generation models at 256×256 gets a concrete, plug-in technique for consistent ~2.6% FID improvement at 1.6× training cost with compelling cross-backbone transfer evidence, but the missing Immiscible Diffusion comparison, single-resolution evaluation, and lack of variance estimates mean the paper's broader claim of establishing noise valuation as a general axis for diffusion training improvement rests on a narrower empirical foundation than it presents.
Recommended 2605.08144 PDF
#127 — Moderately Innovative (55)

The Semantic Training Gap: Ontology -Grounded Tool Architectures for Industrial AI Agent Systems

arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an ontology-grounded tool architecture for industrial AI agents that embeds manufacturing domain semantics directly into the tool layer via a typed relational configuration and a three-operation interface contract (resolve, contextualize, annotate), eliminating tool-call parameter hallucination by architectural enforcement. - Why it's important: LLM agents deployed in manufacturing environments routinely fabricate plausible-sounding but nonexistent equipment identifiers when tool parameters are unconstrained strings — 43% of tool calls in the baseline condition — causing silent data-retrieval failures that the agent misreads as "no issues found," a dangerous failure mode in regulated production environments. - How it does it: A Python configuration module (700–770 lines, 45 named exports per domain) defines equipment hierarchy, domain vocabulary, regulatory mappings, and cross-domain entity resolution; an AIOps orchestration layer intercepts every tool call, validates the identifier against the loaded ontology before the query fires, and on failure returns the valid set so the agent self-corrects. - The good: In a controlled experiment across 72 tool invocations and six industry configurations using Qwen3-32B, ontology-grounded parameters reduced tool-call identifier hallucination from 43% to 0%, and the 0% rate is architecturally guaranteed — not model-dependent — because enum constraints are enforced before any query executes. - The missing: The experiment compares only the full ontology-grounded architecture against the weakest possible baseline (unconstrained free-text strings), omitting the critical intermediate baseline of syntactic-only enum constraints — which the authors themselves concede would likely eliminate most of the 43% hallucination rate, leaving the marginal value of the ontology undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building LLM agents for enterprise data retrieval in high-stakes industrial domains where silent query failures are operationally dangerous; ML researchers seeking algorithmic novelty will find little beyond a well-scoped engineering contribution.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (55) - the tool-parameter fabrication framing is useful but enum-constrained function calling is established. Rigor (40) - the critical syntactic-enum baseline is missing; author admits it would eliminate most hallucination, undermining the contribution. Non-obviousness (50). Completeness (50) - honest about limitations. Resilience (45) - vulnerability to the missing baseline. Impact (65) - clean spec, private code limits reuse. Composite: 0.15*55+0.15*40+0.10*50+0.15*50+0.15*45+0.30*65 = 52.75≈53. Bumped slightly for sharp problem framing.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 40 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 50 Resilience 45 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM agents deployed in manufacturing environments for analytics and decision support acquire domain vocabulary through training but do not learn the relational ontological structure that gives that vocabulary operational meaning. A temperature sensor is instrument TT-4201 in ISA-5.1 instrumentation drawings, %IW64 in the PLC program, and ReflowZone4 in the MES — three names, one physical device, no shared schema. When an agent receives these as independent data streams and queries a tool with a self-generated identifier (e.g., WHERE station_id = 'BOND-1'), the query returns zero rows, which the agent interprets as "no failures recorded." In practice the bonding station had the highest NCR rate on the line. This failure mode is distinct from factual hallucination: the hallucinated content is a tool parameter that silently determines what data is retrieved, not a factual claim that can be surface-checked. In multi-agent configurations, agents without shared ontological grounding progressively diverge in how they resolve the same concepts — the paper terms this "semantic drift" — compounding the single-agent failure across an entire agent network. Existing solutions (Toolformer, Gorilla, NeMo Guardrails) either address general-purpose APIs rather than enterprise manufacturing vocabularies, or filter text outputs after execution rather than blocking invalid parameters before the query fires. Key Idea The core insight is that tool parameter enum values should not be hardcoded in the schema but dynamically projected from a loaded domain ontology, and each valid parameter value should carry relational context — failure codes, regulatory standards, upstream/downstream dependencies — that shapes query construction. This transforms an enum constraint from a syntactic check ("is this value in the list?") into a semantic one ("is this value a node in the relational ontology, and what does it mean?"). Blocking invalid parameters before they reach the database converts a silent failure (empty result set misread as clean data) into a loud, correctable one (the agent receives the valid set and self-corrects). Methodology The architecture has three layers. The ontology layer is a Python module (700–770 lines, 45 named exports) defining the complete domain semantics for a manufacturing vertical: equipment hierarchy aligned to ISA-95/IEC 62264; domain vocabulary (typed failure codes, inspection plans, certifications scoped to individual stations); regulatory context (per-entity mappings to NADCAP, FDA 21 CFR 11, IATF 16949, IPC); and cross-domain entity resolution (identity mappings across naming systems). The module is validated at load time — any missing required export raises a descriptive error immediately. The interface contract defines three typed operations: `resolve(param, ontology_version) → Node | Error(valid_set)` (parameter must map to an ontology node or the call is rejected); `contextualize(node, ontology_version) → DomainContext` (resolved node produces applicable failure codes, regulatory standards, join logic); `annotate(result, domain_context, ontology_version) → AnnotatedResult` (every result carries provenance). Four orchestrator invariants are enforced: pre-execution validation, version consistency within a session (immutable timestamped snapshots), circuit breaker (maximum 3 tool-call rounds per question, reducing uncapped average of 11 calls to 4), and cross-agent consistency (all agents share the same ontology version). The AIOps enforcement layer exposes tools via an MCP server; a parameter gate intercepts every tool call and rejects unresolvable identifiers with the valid set. The experimental apparatus is a digital twin simulation engine (discrete-event loop, 1-minute tick resolution, 634 lines) generating causally coherent MES-shaped data from the same ontology configuration modules. Six industry configurations (aerospace, pharmaceutical, automotive, electronics, food & beverage, warehousing) each produced 15,000–18,000 PostgreSQL rows across 40+ operational tables via a 30-day simulation. Twelve parameterized analytics tools were exposed via MCP. The query set is 72 natural-language queries (12 per configuration, one per tool), phrased as domain users would ask, using natural names rather than opaque identifiers — the step where hallucination occurs. Qwen3-32B at temperature 0 was used for deterministic output. Two conditions were compared: constrained (station_id: enum [S1..S6] projected from ontology) vs. unconstrained (station_id: string with no constraints). Critical design decisions: (1) typed relational configuration rather than formal OWL axiomatization — trades reasoning capability for O(1) runtime performance and authoring accessibility, explicitly justified; (2) per-session version immutability; (3) circuit breaker at 3 rounds, justified by ground-truth comparison on 24 queries. Key Results Primary hallucination experiment (72 queries, Qwen3-32B, temperature 0): constrained condition — 0 hallucinated IDs (0%), 0 empty results, 72/72 correct (100%); unconstrained condition — 31 hallucinated IDs (43%), 27 empty results (38%), 14/72 correct (19%). Hallucination rates by configuration (unconstrained): pharmaceutical 50%, food & beverage 50%, aerospace/automotive/electronics 42%, warehousing 33%. By tool domain: quality tools 50%, materials tools 50%, production tools 44%, operations 42%, engineering change 25%. No single configuration or tool type was an outlier, confirming identifier fabrication is systematic. Simulation calibration across 60 runs (10 seeds × 6 configurations) shows all KPI confidence intervals overlap configured target ranges (e.g., aerospace per-station FPY: 0.949 ± 0.008 vs. target 0.94–0.97; NCR rate: 5.1% ± 0.7% vs. ~5% target). Circuit breaker evaluation on 24 queries with known ground truth: no factual accuracy loss in the capped condition; aggregation granularity differed in 3/24 cases (weekly instead of daily). The baseline is the weakest possible: unconstrained free-text, no RAG, no few-shot, no enum hints. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The system contribution is the ontology-grounded tool architecture: dynamic enum projection from a loaded ontology with relational context propagation, formalized as a three-operation interface contract with AIOps enforcement. The empirical contribution is the quantification of tool-parameter fabrication hallucination at 43% baseline across six manufacturing domains — the paper introduces "tool-parameter fabrication" as a distinct hallucination subcategory with different consequences from factual hallucination. What is genuinely novel: framing semantic vs. syntactic constraints in the tool-use context, and empirical quantification of this failure mode in industrial settings. What is a standard combination: enum-constrained function calling is a well-known LLM capability; the paper extends it with dynamic ontology projection and relational context — an incremental but practically significant extension. Rigor & Reproducibility The most critical ablation is absent: the paper does not isolate syntactic-only enum constraints (hardcoded valid identifier list) from the full ontology architecture. The authors explicitly acknowledge this: "Standard enum constraints alone (syntactic only, without relational context) would likely eliminate most of the 43% hallucination rate." This means the marginal value of the ontology beyond a simpler fix remains undemonstrated empirically. The semantic quality evaluation (answer correctness beyond parameter validity) was assessed by the author alone against known ground truth, not an independent expert panel with structured rubrics — explicitly noted as a limitation. Single LLM tested; the 43% baseline is model-specific and should not be generalized. The 0% constrained rate is architecturally guaranteed and model-independent (by construction). No error bars on the primary hallucination counts (temperature 0 makes the experiment deterministic by design, which is appropriate for binary hallucination measurement but does not capture answer-quality variability). Data availability is "upon request" from the corresponding author; simulation framework source code is in a private Siemens repository available only to reviewers upon request — below standard reproducibility norms. The six configurations vary in semantic content but five of six share identical structural depth (6 stations, 4 products), limiting structural diversity. The Goodhart's Law concern is low: the simulated data was not generated to game the hallucination metric, and the correct answers are independently derivable from the configured KPI targets. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built architecture demonstrated in a simulation environment. - Practical: The ontology layer is a Python module held in memory; the AIOps enforcement is a parameter gate adding O(1) dictionary lookups per tool call; the MCP server is standard infrastructure. No specialized hardware required. - Efficient: The resolve operation is O(1) average-case complexity (dictionary lookup, no per-call I/O); overhead is negligible relative to SQL query execution time. Context window cost grows with ontology size (50 stations → 50 enum values in tool descriptions), bounded in practice by the circuit breaker. - Effective: Eliminates tool-parameter hallucination by construction for any LLM that respects function-calling enum constraints (a capability supported by all major providers). Whether the ontology's relational context improves answer quality beyond what a syntactic enum fix achieves is assessed only qualitatively by the author. - Scalable: Claimed to scale to 10,000 entities with negligible resolution cost increase; simulation engine scales linearly (50-station template estimated at ~2 minutes for a 30-day run on a single thread; production scale requires compiled language migration). No validation at real-factory scale (hundreds/thousands of entities, legacy naming conventions, brownfield discovery). The hardest scalability problem — populating the ontology from an existing brownfield MES — is acknowledged as unsolved. The paper delivers a system architecture with an ontology specification format and interface contract. Code is in a private repository; no public library or dataset release. Strengths - The distinction between tool-parameter hallucination (silently retrieves wrong data, produces confident incorrect analysis) and factual hallucination (produces visibly wrong text) is a sharp and practically important conceptual contribution — the former is harder to detect and has more severe operational consequences. - The architectural guarantee of 0% identifier hallucination in the constrained condition is a strong engineering result: it holds by construction across all LLMs that support function-calling enum constraints, not merely for the tested model. - The three-operation interface contract (resolve/contextualize/annotate) with four formal orchestrator invariants is a clean, implementable specification that practitioners can adopt independently of the specific simulation apparatus. - The semantic drift taxonomy in multi-agent systems (tool federation gap, ontology version independence, parameter free-text variance) names and structures a failure mode that is otherwise poorly characterized in the literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront limitations directly and specifically: (1) simulation-only validation — no testing against real brownfield manufacturing data with inherited tag naming conventions, orphaned PLC variables, or undocumented MES configurations; (2) ontology negotiation is out of scope — the paper addresses what happens after humans agree on definitions, not how to reach agreement; (3) single LLM evaluation — the 43% baseline is Qwen3-32B-specific; (4) no formal human evaluation of answer quality; (5) no comparison against intermediate baselines (RAG, few-shot with valid identifier examples, fine-tuning on domain schemas); (6) structural homogeneity across five of six configurations. This level of self-critical specificity is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The absent ablation is the paper's central vulnerability: the authors admit syntactic enum constraints alone "would likely eliminate most of the 43% hallucination rate." Without measuring this, the paper cannot support its claim that the full ontology architecture is necessary. A two-line schema change (add enum values to the existing tool definition) may achieve nearly identical hallucination reduction. The paper's value proposition rests almost entirely on the qualitative argument that relational context improves downstream query construction and response annotation — an argument supported only by the author's own assessment against ground truth. - The semantic drift validation (Section 4.2) was observed on a different multi-agent platform than the one used for the controlled experiment, and no experiment measures whether the proposed architecture actually prevents drift. The multi-agent architecture is specified but not empirically validated. - The six industry configurations were authored by the same team within the same framework. This demonstrates parameterizability, not independent adoptability. Cross-domain generalizability requires an independent team applying the ontology specification to an unfamiliar domain — not presented. - The paper is a preprint by a single author employed by Siemens, using a private Siemens codebase. The conflict of interest declaration is present, but independent replication is impossible without the source code. Bottom Line This paper makes a practically important contribution by naming, quantifying, and architecturally solving tool-parameter fabrication hallucination in industrial AI agents, but its evaluation compares only against the weakest baseline and leaves the marginal value of the full ontology architecture over a simple enum fix empirically undemonstrated — a gap that must be closed before this work can credibly claim its full value proposition.
Search Results 2605.11234 PDF
#128 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Reddit2Deezer: A Scalable Dataset for Real-World Grounded Conversational Music Recommendation

Haven Kim, Julian McAuley · arxiv.org · Julian McAuley - new articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Reddit2Deezer, a conversational music recommendation dataset of 186k real Reddit {thread, leaf-comment} pairs linked to the Deezer API, released in both raw and paraphrased versions. - Why it's important: Existing real-world CMR datasets top out at ~900 conversations while large synthetic ones distort natural music-seeking behavior, leaving the field without a training corpus that is simultaneously authentic and large enough for modern models. - How it does it: An LLM-based two-stage filter (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8, confidence threshold 0.95) identifies recommendation threads and extracts artist/title pairs from 200 Reddit subreddits, which are then matched to stable Deezer API identifiers for audio previews and metadata. - The good: The dataset is the largest real-world-grounded CMR corpus by a wide margin (190k vs. ~900 for the nearest real-data competitor) while covering post-2010 releases through April 2026 and uniquely including ~30k album-level recommendations. - The missing: The multi-turn performance analysis (Figure 1, Turn 3) rests on exactly 2 conversations, making the paper's central claim that single-turn training transfers to multi-turn CMR empirically hollow. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building conversational music or audio-grounded recommender systems who need a large, stable, real-world corpus — less relevant to those interested in modeling advances rather than data resources.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (dataset-as-artifact). Impact (70) is the strongest dimension — a 200× scale-up over the largest real-world CMR corpus with stable Deezer linkage and released CLAP embeddings is a genuine infrastructure contribution. Rigor (45) and Resilience (40) drag the composite down: the central multi-turn-transfer claim rests on 2 Turn-3 conversations, no random baseline appears in Table 3, and no comparison is run against models trained on prior CMR datasets. Novelty (60) is moderate — the pipeline components are off-the-shelf; the novel contribution is the combination of authenticity, scale, and audio-grounding. Weighted: 0.15*60 + 0.15*45 + 0.10*40 + 0.15*50 + 0.15*40 + 0.30*70 ≈ 54.
systems Novelty 60 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 50 Resilience 40 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Conversational music recommendation systems learn to elicit user preferences through dialogue and surface relevant tracks. The field's main bottleneck is data: human-collected corpora (CPCD, MusiCRS) are authentic but tiny (477–917 conversations), while synthetic corpora (LP-MusicDialog at 290k, TalkPlay at 532k) scale up by having LLMs fabricate dialogues, introducing distribution shift relative to how people actually seek music. A further practical problem is catalog rot — MSD cuts off at 2010, LFM-2b is no longer public, and YouTube links decay — which limits reproducibility and excludes a decade of releases. No existing public CMR dataset simultaneously achieves real-world authenticity, training scale, stable item grounding with audio access, and coverage of recent music. Key Idea: Mine real music-recommendation conversations at scale by crawling a community-curated registry of 695 music subreddits, applying a cheap structural pre-filter followed by a strong open-source LLM filter to identify genuine recommendation exchanges, then anchor each recommended item to the Deezer API (which provides stable identifiers, free 30-second audio previews, and rich metadata without an API key). The core trick is using Reddit's existing social infrastructure as a massive implicit data-collection apparatus for naturalistic music-seeking dialogue, bypassing the need to recruit annotators or synthesize conversations. Methodology: The pipeline has five stages. (1) Crawl: 200 subreddits from the r/Music wiki index are collected via the arctic-shift archive API covering January 2008 to April 2026. (2) Structural pre-filter: removes empty posts, negative-scored comments, and title-based opinion polls using three heuristic rules. (3) LLM filter: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8 first classifies whether a thread seeks music recommendation, then classifies each comment as recommending a specific item and extracts {artist, title, type}; a 0.95 self-reported confidence threshold gates inclusion. (4) Deezer linking: case-insensitive artist+title match against the Deezer API; unmatched entities are discarded. (5) Paraphrasing: the same Qwen model rewrites each {thread, leaf} pair into a clean one-on-one chat using a 10k-word prompt with nine few-shot examples, resolving relative dates and stripping PII. Critical design decisions: the 0.95 confidence threshold is empirically set but ablated only implicitly via the 73-sample human validation; the structural pre-filter is unjustified beyond citing prior heuristics; and the paraphrasing step inflates conversation count by splitting multi-recommendation turns into one-item-per-conversation records (186k → 535k), which artificially changes the task distribution from set-valued to single-item prediction. Key Results: Human validation (n=73, two non-author annotators) finds 97.3% of raw conversations are genuine CMR dialogues (Cohen's κ=0.66), 93.84% of Deezer mappings are correct (κ=0.65), and paraphrased versions score 4.84±0.21 / 4.82±0.33 on a 0–5 musical fidelity scale (Quadratic Weighted Kappa=0.30 between annotators). On the downstream recommendation task (130,010-item catalog), fine-tuned Qwen3.5-2B (LoRA, r=16) achieves Hit@1=3.55 on the raw test set (FT-Raw) and Hit@1=3.32 on the paraphrased test set (FT-Para), all values ×100. Zero-shot Qwen3.5-2B achieves Hit@1=0.31 (raw) / 0.93 (paraphrased). CLAP-Audio retrieval reaches Hit@1=0.15 (raw). No random baseline is reported. No comparison is made against models trained on prior CMR datasets, so it is unknown whether Reddit2Deezer yields better-trained models than the much smaller CPCD. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The contribution is the dataset itself: the pipeline components (LLM filtering, Deezer API linking, LoRA fine-tuning of small LMs) are standard applications of existing tools. What is genuinely novel is the combination of real-world conversational authenticity with training scale (190k) and stable audio-linked grounding; the inclusion of album-level recommendations alongside tracks is a meaningful extension over all prior CMR datasets. Rigor & Reproducibility: Human validation uses Cochran's formula to justify n=73 (a statistically defensible but small sample). IAA is moderate (κ≈0.65–0.66 for dialogue validity and Deezer mapping; QWK=0.30 for paraphrase fidelity — this last figure is low and indicates substantial annotator disagreement on faithfulness despite high mean scores). The paraphrasing prompt (10,336 words, nine examples) is withheld until acceptance, limiting immediate reproducibility of that pipeline stage. No ablation is run on the 0.95 LLM-filter threshold or on the choice of Qwen3.6-35B vs. smaller models. No error bars or significance tests accompany Table 3. The dataset is released on Hugging Face (McAuley-Lab/Reddit2Deezer); CLAP audio embeddings will also be released. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.09120v1, May 2026); results carry preprint confidence. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a released dataset with accompanying baseline experiments. - Practical: Dataset download and Deezer API access require no credentials; fine-tuning Qwen3.5-2B with LoRA fits on commodity GPU hardware. - Efficient: Filtering 200 subreddits with a 35B-parameter LLM is GPU-intensive at construction time, but the released dataset eliminates this cost for end users; inference with 2B-parameter fine-tuned models is cheap. - Effective: Fine-tuned models reach Hit@1≈3.5 (×100) on a 130k-item catalog — the absolute numbers are low, but the paper does not benchmark against prior CMR datasets or establish what ceiling exists on this catalog, so it is unclear whether the task is inherently hard or the models are weak. - Scalable: The crawl covers 18 years of Reddit data; extending to new subreddits or future data is straightforward.
