Data governance in regulated environments mandates self-hosted open-weight models rather than cloud APIs, since external services may trigger policy refusals, introduce latency variance, or expose sensitive investigation content. Prior LLM serving systems optimize for chat-style diverse-history long-output workloads; applying their default configurations to compliance inference wastes most available GPU capacity and produces latencies incompatible with operational SLAs. Key Idea The key insight is workload fingerprinting before serving configuration: by characterizing fraud/AML prompts as exhibiting high prefix-sharing ratios (same policy prefix across thousands of requests), short decode lengths (50–75 tokens for JSON labels), and multi-adapter tenancy — properties that do not co-occur in chat workloads — the paper shows that standard vLLM knobs tuned to this shape deliver order-of-magnitude improvements without any new algorithms. The secondary insight is that adapter locality and prompt-length locality must both be exploited jointly in multi-adapter batching: grouping by adapter-id first, then bucketing by input length, reduces prefill imbalance that would otherwise arise from mixing 400-token classification prompts with 3,000-token extraction prompts. Methodology The system follows a four-plane architecture: (1) workload construction — IBM AML and SAML-D transaction records are rendered into compliance prompts containing a reusable policy prefix, typology definitions, transaction evidence, and a required JSON output schema; (2) control plane — a serving router selects model family, adapter, runtime config, and caching policy per workload type; (3) serving plane — vLLM or SGLang with PagedAttention, Automatic Prefix Caching, EAGLE-style speculative decoding, and optional prefill/decode disaggregation; (4) output assurance — deterministic schema validation, reference-metric scoring (BLEU/ROUGE/cosine similarity), and multi-judge LLM rubric scoring gate releases. Benchmark protocol: 50–100 request warm-up or 5-minute warm-up; 3 runs; median and range reported over a 10–15 minute steady-state measurement window. Primary sweep axes are max_num_seqs, max_num_batched_tokens, gpu_memory_utilization, swap_space, stop tokens, and adapter grouping policy. Both closed-loop concurrency sweeps and open-loop arrival tests are run. Three critical design decisions: (1) Schema-valid goodput as the primary metric rather than raw tokens/s — justified because malformed JSON is operationally equivalent to failure in compliance settings. (2) Two-level batching (adapter-first, then length-aware bucketing) — justified to eliminate prefill imbalance in heterogeneous multi-task requests. (3) Public synthetic data only for reproducibility — justified by data governance constraints. Whether the chosen operating point of max_num_seqs=24 (vs. 16 or 32) generalizes beyond the specific workload tested is not validated. Key Results Case A (A100-class long-context extraction, public synthetic IBM AML): throughput 612–650 → 3,600 req/hr; P99 latency 31–38 s → 6.4–8.7 s; GPU utilization 12% → 78%; normalized cost (A100-hours per 1k requests) 3.08–3.27 → 0.56; projected capacity 10 GPUs → 3–4 GPUs. Case B (guided-JSON H100 speculative decoding): 1×H100 vLLM + EAGLE-style achieves 3.19 req/s at 2,292 ms vs. 2×H100 NIM baseline at 3.01 req/s and 2,397 ms; normalized cost reduced ~53%. Case C1 (multi-adapter batching, 12-prompt mixed request across 4 adapters): PEFT loading baseline 1.35 req/s → vLLM multi-adapter + length buckets 5.26 req/s (3.90× gain). Case C2 (sleep/wake lifecycle): cold start 36–126 s across Llama-8B/Qwen-14B/Qwen-32B reduced to 0.98–4.15 s (30–37× reduction). Model quality observations: Llama-3.1-8B ~85% recall and precision on predicate-offense extraction (4-sample pilot); Qwen2.5 7B–32B ~50% recall / 85% precision at budget tier. These numbers carry no statistical weight. Baselines for serving benchmarks are appropriate in kind (NIM for speculative decoding, PEFT for multi-adapter) but the primary Case A baseline appears to be an untuned factory-default configuration, not a carefully-optimized competitor. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual components — PagedAttention, Automatic Prefix Caching, EAGLE speculative decoding, LoRA multi-adapter serving, vLLM sleep/wake — are all existing features of vLLM and SGLang. The genuine contribution is the domain-specific serving playbook: the systematic mapping of AML workload phenotypes to serving interventions, the two-level adapter+length batching strategy, the compliance-specific quality gate architecture, and the public reproducibility track using IBM AML and SAML-D to construct prefix-heavy serving benchmarks. The combination and framing for regulated financial deployment is new; the underlying mechanisms are not. Rigor & Reproducibility Significant concerns. No ablation study: the paper reports an optimized configuration versus a poorly-tuned baseline, but does not isolate which individual intervention (max_num_seqs tuning, APC, stop tokens, CUDA graphs) contributes what fraction of the 5.9× gain. The model quality pilots are based on 4 samples — reporting "85% recall and precision" on 4 examples is statistically meaningless. Tables show point estimates without error bars; the methodology states median and range are reported, but tables in results sections omit ranges. The speculative decoding comparison mixes hardware configurations (1×H100 vs. 2×H100) and software stacks (vLLM+EAGLE vs. NIM), making it impossible to isolate the speculative decoding contribution from the hardware and software differences. No code, dataset rendering scripts, or model weights are released; no repository is mentioned. The paper explicitly states results should be read as workload-shape evidence on synthetic data, which is an honest but significant caveat. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built serving architecture evaluated by benchmark. - Practical: Runs on standard commercially available A100/H100 hardware using publicly available open-weight models (Llama 3, Qwen2.5) and vLLM/SGLang serving engines. - Efficient: 83% reduction in normalized GPU-hours per 1k requests after tuning; 1×H100 with speculative decoding matches 2×H100 NIM throughput at ~53% lower cost. - Effective: Serving performance evidence is strong on synthetic benchmarks; end-to-end compliance quality is validated only on 4-sample pilots with no significance testing, so the claim that the stack produces production-quality outputs is unsupported. - Scalable: The paper claims a path from 250k to multi-million daily prompts via APC and LMCache but does not run scale experiments — this is an assertion, not a measurement. No software artifact is released; the paper stops at the system description and benchmark report. Strengths - The domain framing — identifying fraud/AML as a distinct "prefix-dominated, short-output, multi-tenant" workload class — is concise and actionable; it gives infrastructure engineers a clear optimization priority ordering that generic LLM serving guides do not provide. - The two-level batching pattern (adapter-id grouping followed by prompt-length bucketing) is a concrete, immediately implementable engineering pattern demonstrated to yield 3.90× over naive PEFT loading on a controlled benchmark. - The sleep/wake lifecycle benchmark directly quantifies the cold-start vs. wake-path tradeoff (30–37× reduction) for sequential multi-model compliance pipelines, which is practically useful data not documented in general vLLM guides. - The LLM-as-judge quality gate design — layering deterministic schema checks, reference metrics, and multi-judge rubric scoring into a promotion gate — appropriately frames compliance serving as a quality-and-governance problem, not just a throughput problem. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge all four primary limitations directly: (1) synthetic data cannot capture real institutional nuances; (2) model quality observations are small operational pilots, not state-of-the-art task quality claims; (3) LLM-as-judge evaluation suffers from position bias, verbosity bias, family preference, and policy drift, requiring periodic human recalibration; (4) LMCache cross-worker reuse and mature prefill/decode disaggregation are described as high-confidence next steps rather than validated production defaults. This directness is a credibility positive in this framework. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The primary Case A baseline (12% GPU utilization, 31–38 s P99 latency) is an obviously misconfigured factory default, not a representative optimized competitor; the 5.9× throughput gain is partly an artifact of this choice and does not isolate the contribution of any specific technique. - No ablation table: readers cannot determine whether APC alone, max_num_seqs tuning alone, or the combination drives the improvement. - The speculative decoding comparison conflates hardware count (1 vs. 2 GPUs), inference engine (vLLM vs. NIM), and decoding strategy — three confounds in one table. - Model quality numbers on 4-sample pilots should not appear as quantitative claims; they introduce false precision. - The paper describes schema-valid goodput as the primary metric but no results table actually reports schema validity rates — only latency and throughput are tabulated. - The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.11232v1, May 2026) and has not undergone peer review; the results warrant peer-review scrutiny before relying on specific numbers for infrastructure decisions. Bottom Line A practitioner building a self-hosted LLM serving stack for regulated financial workflows will find the operational playbook, workload taxonomy, and adapter-batching pattern immediately useful, but the absence of ablations, statistically vacuous quality pilots, no code release, and synthetic-only evaluation mean the paper is an engineering experience report rather than a rigorously validated systems contribution.
Search Results 2605.11232 PDF
#152 — Incremental (38)

Designing an Ontology of Memory and Recollection: Experiences from the Mixed Methods Digital Oral History (MeDoraH) project

discovery.ucl.ac.uk · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a hybrid methodology and first-version ontology for constructing a knowledge graph from oral history interview transcripts, combining NLP triple-extraction experiments, semantic clustering of predicates, a Hermeneutic Workbench tool, and LLM-assisted refinement cycles. - Why it's important: Oral history archives preserve first-person recollections that are absent from written records, but their informal, conversational language defeats standard NLP extraction pipelines, leaving large collections computationally inaccessible for knowledge graph construction. - How it does it: Three progressively sophisticated triple-extraction experiments (dependency parsing + NER, BERT-based coreference + proposition extraction, zero-shot mREBEL relation extraction) feed predicate-clustering to derive ontology relation patterns, validated and refined through an interactive Hermeneutic Workbench dashboard. - The good: The honest negative result — that pipeline-based workflows cannot produce sufficient quality for ontology design without human refinement — is itself a concrete, reusable finding that saves future projects from the same dead ends. - The missing: No precision/recall or F1 metrics are reported for any extraction experiment; quality is assessed descriptively, making it impossible to compare approaches quantitatively or against external baselines. - Read it or not: Worth reading for digital humanities researchers or knowledge engineers tackling informal spoken-language corpora; NLP or KG researchers seeking benchmarked methods will find the evidence base too thin.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (45) - Hermeneutic Workbench concept and predicate-clustering workflow in DH. Rigor (25) - no quantitative metrics anywhere, qualitative claims only, no IAA. Non-obviousness (45) - the negative result is genuinely informative. Completeness (35) - hybrid pipeline only proposed, not validated. Resilience (30). Impact (45) - ontology released, Workbench not. Slide-deck format. Composite: 0.15*45+0.15*25+0.10*45+0.15*35+0.15*30+0.30*45 = 37.75.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 35 Resilience 30 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Digital humanities scholars who study field history through oral testimony face a structuring problem: interviews capture subjective recollections, hesitations, indirect references, and co-constructed narratives that resist the pattern-based and factual extraction assumptions baked into standard NLP tools. Existing knowledge graph pipelines (trained on Wikipedia abstracts or formal text) over-generalise on spoken language — inferring "educated at" from "had access to libraries" — and dependency parsers that require hand-designed rules cannot scale. The MeDoraH project, an AHRC-DFG funded UCL/TU Darmstadt collaboration, targets this gap specifically for Digital Humanities (DH) community history: building a knowledge graph that captures not ground-truth facts but networks of recollection — what was said and how it was interpreted, not necessarily what was true. Key Idea: The core insight is a disciplinary reframing: oral history triples are not factual assertions but performative recollections, so ontology design must encode subjectivity and interpretation as first-class citizens. Operationally, the trick is to use clustering over a large extracted predicate space (not a pre-fixed relation schema) to discover what relation types actually appear in the corpus, then use those empirical clusters to drive ontology design bottom-up — rather than imposing a top-down schema and trying to fit interview content into it. Methodology: The work proceeds in five stages. Stage 1 runs three triple-extraction experiments: Experiment 1 uses spaCy dependency parsing + NER to extract 309 SVO triples from utterance-segmented transcripts; Experiment 2 adds BERT-based coreference resolution (fastcoref LingMess) and fine-tuned BERT proposition extraction; Experiment 3 uses first-person pronoun resolution (rule-based), fastcoref coreference resolution, a T5-base summarization/claim extractor, and zero-shot end-to-end triple extraction via mREBEL or REBEL-large. Stage 2 embeds extracted predicates and clusters them (UMAP visualization shown, ~45+ clusters identified with semantic labels such as "Employment and Professional Lifecycle," "Cognitive and Affective States," "Creation and Production"). Stage 3 derives a first-version ontology with 10 top-level relation patterns (Actor→Actor, Actor→Artefact, Actor→ConceptualItem, Actor→Event, Event→Spatial/Organisation, Event→Temporal, Entity→Entity, Artefact→ConceptualItem, Actor→Property, Actor→SpatialEntity) and a class hierarchy (Entity, Actor, Person, Organisation, Group, Artefact, ConceptualItem, Discipline, etc.). Stage 4 deploys a Hermeneutic Workbench — an interactive dashboard with macro (corpus-level aggregated analytics) and micro (utterance-level evidence source) views to support human interpretive refinement. Stage 5 proposes a hybrid pipeline combining LLM-driven automation with structured human refinement cycles. Critical design decisions: (1) predicate clustering to discover relation types empirically rather than imposing a schema — well-justified given the novelty of the domain; (2) preserving utterance provenance in the Workbench so every extracted triple can be traced to its speaker and source text — well-justified for humanistic interpretability; (3) treating subjectivity as a data property rather than noise — well-motivated philosophically but underspecified technically (how subjectivity is represented in the ontology schema is not shown). Key Results: Experiment 1 (dependency parsing): 309 SVO triples, 71 unique subjects, 144 unique predicates, 238 unique objects; subject types: PERSON 79%, PRODUCT 8.7%, ENTITY 7.8%; top relation types dominated by generic "be/have" verbs. Experiment 2/3 results are described qualitatively, not numerically. REBEL-large overgeneralises ("educated at" inferred from "had access to libraries"). Predicate clustering produces ~45 named semantic clusters. The Hermeneutic Workbench shows 28 speaker turns, 725 unique entities, 160 unique entity types in one demo interview. No F1, precision, or recall metrics are reported for any extraction experiment. No comparison against external baselines or existing oral history NLP systems is made. The ontology (v1) is publicly available at https://github.com/articoder/MeDoraH_Ontology. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (partial) + NEW METHOD (workflow). The Hermeneutic Workbench is a genuine tool contribution for digital humanities. The empirical-clustering-to-ontology workflow for informal spoken corpora is novel in its domain combination. The ontology itself extends existing frameworks (CIDOC-CRM influences visible in the class structure) to oral history subjectivity. The individual NLP components (spaCy, mREBEL, fastcoref) are standard; novelty lies in their combination, failure analysis, and the iterative human-in-the-loop refinement design. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are reported that isolate which pipeline component contributes to quality. No error bars, inter-annotator agreement, or significance tests appear. Quality assessment is qualitative and illustrative (single examples shown). The ontology is publicly released (GitHub link provided). The Hermeneutic Workbench is shown in screenshots but no release link is given. The oral history corpus used is not released (expected given ethical constraints on interview recordings). The absence of any quantitative evaluation metric is a significant methodological gap: the claim that "pipeline-based workflows can't produce data with sufficient quality" rests on researcher judgment, not measured outcomes. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built tool, the Hermeneutic Workbench, plus a published ontology, evaluated through deployment on a real corpus). - Practical: The pipeline runs on standard NLP libraries (spaCy, fastcoref, REBEL/mREBEL, T5) deployable on commodity hardware; no specialized infrastructure required. - Efficient: Compute cost is not stated; REBEL and fastcoref are moderate-scale models, likely feasible on a single GPU, but processing cost per interview is not reported. - Effective: The evidence that the system solves the stated problem is partial — the Workbench facilitates human interpretation, but whether the resulting knowledge graph is accurate or complete relative to ground truth is not demonstrated. - Scalable: Not tested beyond a single interview in the Workbench demo (28 speaker turns); the full MeDoraH corpus scale is not stated, and no scaling experiment is reported. The work delivers a published ontology (v1 on GitHub) and a described Workbench tool; full software release of the Workbench is not confirmed. Strengths: - The decision to cluster extracted predicates empirically before fixing an ontology schema is methodologically sound and appropriately domain-driven — avoiding the common error of forcing spoken-language content into a pre-existing schema. - The Hermeneutic Workbench concretely addresses the DH-specific requirement to ground every computational extraction in its original narrative context (speaker + source utterance), which is essential for humanistic interpretability and not standard in KG tooling. - The honest negative result — three NLP pipeline variants all fail to produce ontology-grade quality — is a genuine contribution to the field, preventing wasted effort by future practitioners approaching oral history with similar standard tooling. - The published ontology (GitHub) with 10 top-level relation patterns grounded in empirical corpus analysis provides a reusable starting point for the oral history knowledge representation community. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) colloquial and informal language makes pattern-based extraction very difficult; (2) pipeline-based workflows can't produce data of sufficient quality to support ontology design alone; (3) REBEL models are fine-tuned on Wikipedia (formal text), causing a training/oral-history domain mismatch; (4) dependency parsing produces low precision and coverage for complex sentences; (5) verbs are mostly reduced to "be/have" after lemmatization, losing relational specificity. The authors confront these limitations directly and use them to motivate the hybrid LLM + human refinement proposal. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No quantitative evaluation of any extraction experiment: the claim that one approach is better than another is unsupported by precision/recall figures, making the comparison of Experiments 1-3 purely anecdotal. - No inter-annotator agreement for the predicate cluster labeling or ontology design decisions — a single research team's interpretation of 45 clusters is presented without reliability measurement. - The proposed hybrid LLM-driven automation + human refinement methodology is described only at the level of a proposal, not a validated pipeline; it is future work presented as a solution. - The distinction between capturing "what is said" versus "what is true" — the paper's central epistemological claim — is not operationalized in the ontology: there is no shown mechanism for encoding provenance, uncertainty, or speaker perspective as ontology properties. - The presentation format (slide deck submitted as a PDF) means content is sparse relative to a full paper; many methodological details (corpus size, LLM prompts, clustering hyperparameters) are omitted. - No comparison with existing oral history ontologies (e.g., BIO, COHDS, or CIDOC-CRM oral history extensions) is made. Bottom Line: A practitioner building knowledge graphs from oral history or other informal spoken-language corpora will find the negative findings about standard NLP pipelines and the Hermeneutic Workbench concept genuinely useful, but the work is at an interim report stage — the evaluation is qualitative, the hybrid LLM pipeline is unvalidated, and the ontology is v1 — so it is best read as an honest progress report from an ongoing project rather than a definitive contribution.
Search Results
#153 — Incremental (36)

Distilling LLM Reasoning into Lightweight Neural Policies for Multi-Agent UAV Coordination

ebulutvcu.github.io · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a two-phase framework that uses Gemini 2.5-Flash as a per-step Q-value oracle to generate strategy traces for multi-UAV area coverage, then distills those traces into a ~20 MB neural policy (CNN + GATv2 + cross-attention) with 26 ms inference latency. - Why it's important: Direct LLM inference is too slow and compute-heavy for onboard or real-time UAV swarm deployment; a compact, edge-deployable policy that retains the LLM's generalization across team sizes and grid configurations would make LLM-derived coordination practically usable. - How it does it: The coverage problem is reframed as DQN-style per-step Q-value assignment — the LLM scores every candidate next-cell for each UAV at each timestep, producing dense supervised traces that train a multi-modal network without requiring the LLM at inference time. - The good: The distilled model matches LLM coverage curves on three held-out test episodes and generalizes to unseen UAV counts and out-of-distribution grid sizes (8 UAVs in a 50×50 grid, trained on 1–7 UAVs in grids up to 35×35), demonstrating non-trivial zero-shot transfer from only 13 training episodes. - The missing: No comparison against any MARL baseline (QMIX, MAPPO, or even a greedy frontier heuristic) — the paper evaluates only against its own LLM teacher, so it is impossible to assess whether the distilled policy is competitive with the established state of the art it claims to supersede. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners exploring LLM-to-edge policy distillation pipelines for multi-agent robotics; insufficient as a performance reference for the multi-UAV coverage problem because it omits standard MARL baselines.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (28) is the dominant drag — only 13 training episodes, 3 test episodes, zero ablations, and critically no MARL or even greedy-heuristic baseline despite claiming to supersede them. Completeness (35) and Resilience (35) weak — sequential LLM querying implies hidden centralization undermining the decentralized claim, LLM teacher treated as ground truth without validation. Novelty (55) moderate — LLM-as-Q-oracle framing for multi-UAV distillation is reasonable. Impact (50) — pipeline is plausible but no code/weights released. Composite reflects a proof-of-concept that omits the comparisons needed to substantiate its core claim.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 28 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 35 Resilience 35 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multi-UAV area coverage missions require coordinated trajectory planning under dynamic obstacles, varying team sizes, and real-time constraints. Rule-based and MARL approaches require task-specific design and retraining when mission parameters change; LLMs offer generalized reasoning but cannot be deployed on resource-constrained aerial platforms due to inference latency and compute requirements. The gap this paper addresses is the absence of a method that captures LLM-derived coordination strategies in a compact, deployable neural form that retains cross-configuration generalization without retraining. Key Idea Rather than asking the LLM to generate full trajectories in one shot (which the authors tried and found to produce incoherent paths), they reframe the problem as per-step Q-value scoring: at each timestep the LLM evaluates every reachable cell for each UAV and assigns a desirability score in [0, 100]. This produces dense, structured supervision — a complete action-value mapping at every decision point — that is far more tractable for downstream neural network training than sparse trajectory labels. Methodology Phase 1 (strategy trace generation): Gemini 2.5-Flash is queried sequentially for each UAV at each timestep. Prompts include ego position and coverage history, other agents' positions and projected movements (sequentially — UAV k sees the projected moves of UAVs 1 through k−1), grid dimensions and obstacle map, current global coverage percentage, scoring guidelines, and a structured JSON output format. When collisions occur, offending UAVs are re-queried with a corrective prompt. Episodes run until 99% area coverage is reached. Twenty episodes were collected across stochastically initialized configurations (grid sizes 20–35, UAV counts 1–7, Chebyshev coverage radii 1–3). Phase 2 (neural network training): State is decomposed into four feature streams: (a) a 6-channel spatial tensor (ego footprint, ego cumulative coverage, other UAVs' footprints, global coverage, frontier mask, obstacle map), resized to G×G and processed by a 3-block CNN (64/128/256 channels); (b) 8-dim per-UAV relational descriptors processed by two GATv2 layers on a fully connected agent graph, with only the ego node's embedding extracted; (c) 15-dim per-candidate-action vectors embedded by a shared 2-layer MLP; (d) a 6-dim global context scalar embedded by a lightweight MLP. Multi-headed cross-attention (8 heads) uses action embeddings as queries against the triplet {spatial, ego, global} as key-value pairs. A 4-layer MLP head maps the fused per-action embedding to a scalar Q-value. The composite loss is confidence-weighted MSE (weighted by per-state Q-value std dev to emphasize unambiguous supervisor states) + adaptive pairwise ranking loss (margin proportional to std dev) + a coverage-aware stay-action penalty. Critical design decisions: (1) sequential UAV querying so each agent sees prior agents' projected moves — well-motivated for collision reduction but implies implicit coordination overhead not discussed; (2) extracting only the ego GATv2 embedding rather than all agent embeddings — limits global inter-agent modeling; (3) std-dev weighting of losses — sensible but not ablated against uniform weighting. Key Results On three held-out test episodes, the distilled model reaches 99% coverage in timestep counts matching the LLM teacher (Fig. 4); in one episode the model is faster at intermediate steps. Generalization experiments hold out specific UAV counts from the 20-episode corpus and test the remaining model on those counts (Table III, 7 configurations): model steps are within 1–7 timesteps of LLM steps in 5 of 7 cases; in 2 cases model is faster. Out-of-distribution test with 8 UAVs in a 50×50 grid (neither scale seen in training) succeeds in reaching 99% but with less efficient paths. Dynamic adaptability: mid-mission UAV failure and new-UAV insertion are handled without retraining. Consistency: 5 configurations × 10 random initializations each; one configuration stagnates at 97.9–98.7%, failing to reach 99% within the 80-step limit. Model size: ~20 MB; inference latency: 26 ms on an Intel Xeon CPU. No LLM inference latency is reported for comparison. No baselines from MARL literature (QMIX, MAPPO, MADDPG, or greedy frontier heuristics) are evaluated. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The specific contribution — formulating LLM-as-DQN-oracle for multi-UAV coverage trace generation combined with a four-modality (CNN + GATv2 + cross-attention + global context) distillation architecture — is novel in the multi-UAV coordination literature. Individual components (knowledge distillation, GATv2, cross-attention) are well-established; the novelty is the Q-value trace formulation that makes LLM-to-edge distillation tractable for this combinatorial multi-agent setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Training data is extremely small: 13 episodes. The primary evaluation uses 3 test episodes with no statistical aggregation across runs. Consistency experiments (10 random seeds per configuration) provide the only multi-run statistics, but these test robustness of a fixed model, not training variance. No ablations are reported — no component-removal study on the four encoder streams, no ablation on the three loss terms, no sensitivity to LLM choice. No comparison to MARL baselines. No code, model weights, or dataset are reported as publicly released. The paper is peer-reviewed (accepted to WoWMoM 2026), not a preprint, but the absence of standard baselines is a significant methodological gap for a peer-reviewed venue. The LLM teacher (Gemini 2.5-Flash) Q-values are treated as optimal ground truth without validation against any analytical bound. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (builds a pipeline and evaluates it experimentally in simulation). - Practical: The distilled policy runs on a 2-thread Intel Xeon CPU with 12.7 GB RAM at 26 ms per inference, making it feasible for edge or onboard deployment on capable UAV compute platforms. - Efficient: At ~20 MB and CPU-only inference, the model is orders of magnitude lighter than an LLM; however, the LLM trace generation cost (API calls per UAV per timestep across 20 episodes) is not quantified, so the one-time distillation cost is unknown. - Effective: The model achieves 99% coverage in simulation on held-out configurations in most cases, but fails to converge in one consistency configuration and produces less efficient paths at out-of-distribution scale; the simulation uses a grid-world abstraction with no sensor noise, communication delay, or wind. - Scalable: Tested up to 8 UAVs in a 50×50 grid (modest out-of-distribution); no experiments at 100+ UAVs, continuous environments, or 3D motion; scalability beyond the tested range is claimed but not demonstrated. The work delivers a trained model (weights not released) and a pipeline description; no software artifact is publicly shared. Strengths - The practical reformulation from holistic trajectory generation (which failed) to per-step Q-value scoring is a concrete, empirically validated design insight: the authors tried and documented the failure of the naive approach, which adds credibility to the chosen framing. - The four-stream architecture is well-motivated by the distinct information types in multi-UAV coordination (spatial layout, inter-agent relations, action geometry, global mission state), and the cross-attention fusion is a principled way to make action evaluation context-dependent. - Holding out specific UAV counts and testing the model on those unseen counts provides a more honest generalization test than in-distribution evaluation, and the model performs credibly on 5 of 7 held-out scenarios. - Zero-shot adaptation to mid-mission UAV failure and new-UAV insertion (without retraining) demonstrates genuine architectural resilience that is practically relevant for real swarm operations. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge: (1) the 2D grid-world abstraction with fixed altitude is unrealistic; future work promises 3D movement and realistic communication/sensing models. (2) The model struggles with straight paths in the out-of-distribution 50×50/8-UAV scenario, attributing this to limited training data. (3) One consistency configuration fails to reach 99% coverage — reported honestly but without root-cause analysis. These limitations are stated matter-of-factly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No MARL baselines: The paper compares the distilled model only against its LLM teacher, never against QMIX, MAPPO, MADDPG, or even a greedy frontier-coverage heuristic. Without these, the claim that the approach outperforms "rule-based heuristics and MARL" (stated in the introduction) is entirely unsubstantiated. - Teacher quality unverified: Gemini 2.5-Flash Q-values are treated as optimal ground truth. If the LLM teacher is suboptimal relative to a tuned MARL agent, the student inherits those suboptimalities, and the distillation framework could be amplifying mediocre behavior. - Sequential querying creates implicit centralization: UAV k receives projected moves of UAVs 1..k−1, which requires either a coordinator or sequential communication at each timestep. This centralization assumption is never flagged and undermines the "decentralized execution" claim. - LLM latency not reported: The 26 ms model latency is presented as a speedup, but the per-step LLM latency (which would be the relevant comparison) is never stated. - 13-episode training dataset is extremely small by any standard; generalization claims, while anecdotally supported, cannot be statistically validated from this sample size. - No code, dataset, or model weights released, making reproduction impossible. Bottom Line This paper makes a credible proof-of-concept for LLM-to-edge policy distillation in multi-UAV coverage and contains several genuine design insights, but the complete absence of MARL or heuristic baselines means a reader cannot determine whether the distilled policy is competitive with existing methods — only that it approximates its LLM teacher.
Search Results
#154 — Incremental (35)

PGGA: A Plan-Grounded GUI Agent for Automated Device Support

openreview.net · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PGGA (Plan-Grounded GUI Agent), a framework that conditions a fine-tuned 2B-parameter vision-language model on retrieved step-by-step procedural plans to execute mobile device configuration tasks, alongside a small benchmark (DSIB) of 57 such tasks. - Why it's important: GUI agents deployed for device support (e.g., navigating deep settings menus) fail almost completely without external procedural knowledge — GTA1-7B achieves 0.00% Task Success Rate zero-shot — revealing a bottleneck that parameter scaling alone does not fix. - How it does it: RAG retrieves device-support documentation and synthesizes it into a numbered action plan; a fine-tuned 2B VLM (ShowUI-2B backbone) is conditioned on that plan at every timestep to predict the next low-level action (tap, type, scroll) grounded to screen coordinates. - The good: Under oracle (expert-annotated) plans, PGGA-2B reaches 54.39% Task Success Rate and 91.28% Element Accuracy, outperforming the larger GTA1-7B (45.61% / 82.99%), concretely isolating procedural knowledge — not visual perception — as the binding bottleneck. - The missing: The deployed retrieval system drops PGGA-2B to 19.30% Task Success Rate, a 35-point collapse that the paper diagnoses but does not solve; with only 57 benchmark tasks on a single iPhone XR, the evidence base is too narrow to draw generalizable conclusions. - Read it or not: Worth a quick read for researchers building GUI agents for narrow, deterministic domains; the operation-vs-element-accuracy decomposition is a useful diagnostic framing, but the work is pilot-scale and the core retrieval problem remains open.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (30) and Completeness (35) pull the score down — no ablations isolating plan-conditioning fine-tuning, no error bars, the retrieval pipeline references a non-existent 'gpt-5.4' model that undermines reproducibility, and Mind2Web/mobile domain mismatch is uncontrolled. Novelty (35) is modest — RAG-conditioned GUI agents are well-trodden; the genuine contribution is the Operation/Element Accuracy decomposition. Resilience (35) is weak given the 35-point retrieval collapse. Impact (40) reflects a 57-task pilot benchmark with no deployable artifact. Non-Obviousness (45) slightly above average for the perceptual-vs-procedural framing.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 35 Resilience 35 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autonomous GUI agents powered by vision-language models fail systematically on multi-step device configuration tasks — navigating nested system menus to change localization settings, configure privacy controls, or adjust accessibility options. These tasks are highly deterministic given correct procedural knowledge, yet current agents have no access to that knowledge and resort to zero-shot visual guessing. This guessing compounds errors across multiple steps, leaving agents stuck in unrecoverable states. The problem affects any deployment context where an agent must serve as automated device support (e.g., customer service automation, accessibility tooling), and it is distinct from general web navigation because the correct action sequence is fixed and must be followed precisely. Key Idea: The core insight is reframing GUI navigation for deterministic tasks as a knowledge-execution problem rather than a planning problem: if a correct procedure can be retrieved from an external knowledge base, the model need only ground each plan step to the current screen state, not reason about what to do next. The agent's policy is conditioned on an explicit numbered action plan at every timestep, reducing the search space from open-ended visual exploration to step-wise grounding. Methodology: PGGA formalizes the environment as a POMDP where the agent observes screenshots and interaction history. The framework has two modules. The Planner queries an external knowledge base using RAG; plan retrieval calls gpt-5.4 (referenced in the paper — likely a manuscript error, possibly meaning GPT-4.5 or GPT-4.1) via the OpenAI Responses API with web search enabled and temperature=0, generating a numbered atomic action plan. The Execution Agent is ShowUI-2B fine-tuned on Mind2Web interaction traces converted to readable plans using gpt-4.1-nano. At each timestep the model receives (screenshot, task, interaction history, plan) and outputs a structured action dict with action type (TAP/TYPE/SCROLL), target element label, and normalized screen coordinates [x,y] ∈ [0,1]. Three instruction formats are evaluated: Task Only (zero-shot), Task + Retrieved Plan, and Task + Expert-Annotated Plan (oracle upper bound). Critical design decisions: (1) the plan is not executed as a script — the model must align each plan step to the current visual state at every step, which is well-motivated; (2) fine-tuning uses Mind2Web (web tasks) rather than mobile device tasks, a domain mismatch that is noted but not ablated; (3) the expert-plan oracle derives plans from ground-truth trajectories via LLM rewriting, making it an upper bound rather than a deployable condition. Key Results: On DSIB (57 tasks, 241 steps, Apple iPhone XR): zero-shot Task Success Rate is 1.75% (ShowUI-2B), 0.00% (GTA1-7B), and 7.02% (PGGA-2B). With retrieved plans: 3.51% (ShowUI-2B), 29.82% (GTA1-7B), 19.30% (PGGA-2B). With expert plans: 3.51% (ShowUI-2B), 45.61% (GTA1-7B), 54.39% (PGGA-2B). GTA1-7B achieves near-perfect Operation Accuracy (99.59% with expert plans) but lower Element Accuracy (82.99%) than PGGA-2B (91.28%), revealing that the 7B model knows what action to take but struggles with where to tap without plan-conditioned fine-tuning. A counterintuitive finding: GTA1-7B degrades less from expert to retrieved plans (45.61% → 29.82%, a 16-point drop) than PGGA-2B (54.39% → 19.30%, a 35-point drop), suggesting the fine-tuned 2B model is more brittle to noisy plans. No error bars or significance tests are reported. Evaluation is on a single device and iOS version. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD (narrow). The plan-conditioning framework is a direct application of RAG to GUI agents, which is not technically novel — prior work (WebPilot, AgentOccam, ADaPT) applies hierarchical planning to web agents. The genuine contribution is the empirical isolation of procedural knowledge as the binding bottleneck through Operation Accuracy vs. Element Accuracy decomposition, and the DSIB benchmark targeting deep mobile settings menus. The benchmark is too small (57 tasks) to be a standalone dataset contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are reported to isolate the contribution of plan-conditioning fine-tuning versus the base ShowUI-2B architecture choices. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported. The comparison between PGGA-2B and GTA1-7B is explicitly not a fair parameter-efficiency comparison (one is fine-tuned for plan following, the other is not), which the authors acknowledge. The benchmark is evaluated on a single device (iPhone XR) and presumably a single iOS version. Code and the DSIB dataset are referenced via a project page (https://hsiung.cc/PGGA/) but no explicit statement of code release is made in the paper. The retrieval pipeline references "gpt-5.4" — no such model was publicly known at the time; this appears to be a manuscript error and raises concerns about reproducibility. Fine-tuning on Mind2Web (web interactions) and evaluating on mobile device tasks introduces an uncontrolled domain mismatch variable. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (a fine-tuned model + retrieval pipeline evaluated on a benchmark). - Practical: PGGA-2B runs on a 2B-parameter open-weight model (ShowUI-2B), which is commercially accessible; the retrieval pipeline requires calling a closed proprietary LLM API, adding cost and latency. - Efficient: The 2B executor is compact relative to the 7B baseline, but each timestep requires an LLM API call for plan synthesis; the per-task compute cost of the retrieval module is not reported. - Effective: Under oracle conditions the system reaches 54.39% Task Success Rate on 57 tasks; under realistic retrieval it drops to 19.30%, meaning the deployed system fails on more than 4 in 5 tasks — not yet effective for production deployment. - Scalable: Tested only on one device, one iOS version, 57 tasks averaging 4.23 steps; no evidence at larger task count, longer horizons, Android, or different iOS versions. The paper frames this as pilot-benchmark evidence. The work delivers a fine-tuned model checkpoint and benchmark dataset, but no software library or CLI tool. Strengths: - The Operation Accuracy vs. Element Accuracy vs. Task Success Rate decomposition cleanly separates semantic understanding from visual grounding from trajectory completion, producing a more informative diagnostic than a single aggregate metric. - The zero-shot collapse of GTA1-7B (0.00% Task SR) is a stark, credible empirical finding that convincingly motivates the procedural knowledge hypothesis. - PGGA-2B outperforming GTA1-7B on Element Accuracy and Step SR with expert plans, despite 3.5× fewer parameters, provides concrete evidence that task-format fine-tuning matters more than scale for grounding in dense GUIs. - The honest framing of the expert-plan condition as an oracle upper bound rather than a deployable result is a credibility-positive choice that many similar papers obscure. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly flag: (1) DSIB contains only 57 tasks on a single iPhone XR, making it a controlled stress test rather than a general benchmark; (2) the retrieval gap is large and unresolved; (3) the PGGA-2B vs. GTA1-7B comparison is not a pure parameter-efficiency comparison. They call for future work on multiple devices, iOS versions, Android, and interactive re-planning. These are direct and credible acknowledgments. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper does not include ablations isolating what the plan-conditioning fine-tuning actually contributed versus the ShowUI-2B base model's improvements from Mind2Web training. The reference to "gpt-5.4" in the retrieval pipeline is unresolvable — this model is not publicly documented and may be a manuscript error, making the retrieval pipeline non-reproducible as described. Fine-tuning on Mind2Web (desktop web) and evaluating on mobile settings menus is a domain mismatch that likely suppresses PGGA-2B's ceiling performance, but no ablation is provided. The benchmark has 57 tasks — at this scale, a single-digit number of task outcomes can swing Task SR by nearly 2 percentage points, yet no confidence intervals are reported. The retrieved plan synthesis uses a closed proprietary model (possibly GPT-4.5-level) that is itself performing significant semantic reasoning, so the retrieval condition is not a clean ablation of "retrieval quality" independent of LLM capability. There is no analysis of failure modes beyond the binary task success metric. Bottom Line: PGGA produces a clear and useful diagnostic result — procedural knowledge is the binding bottleneck for device-support GUI agents, not visual perception — but the 57-task benchmark, missing ablations, the unresolved 35-point retrieval gap, and a non-reproducible retrieval pipeline make this pilot-scale work rather than a deployable or generalizable contribution; practitioners building device-support agents will find the framing and decomposition metrics valuable but will need substantially more scale and a solved retrieval problem before this approach is actionable.
Search Results
#155 — Incremental (35)

A hybrid RAG approach for generating ontology -based information containers from building permit application submissions using text-based LLMs

mediatum.ub.tum.de · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a four-stage proof-of-concept pipeline that ingests a ZIP archive of building permit documents, applies hybrid RAG (dense vector search + Neo4j graph traversal), extracts structured fields via a local Qwen 2.5-7B-Instruct LLM, and outputs an ISO 21597-1–compliant ICDD container populated with an OntoBPR RDF knowledge graph. - Why it's important: 65% of building permit applications are delayed due to missing or erroneous documents, and current digital permitting tools lack automated semantic extraction that can link heterogeneous submission artifacts to formal ontology structures required for completeness checking and regulatory traceability. - How it does it: The pipeline fixes LLM outputs to a predefined JSON schema (preventing ontology hallucination), combines cosine-similarity retrieval over all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings with Neo4j graph traversal of document-chunk neighborhoods, then deterministically maps extracted values to OntoBPR RDF triples via rdflib. - The good: The pipeline successfully generates a SHACL-validated ISO 21597-1 container from a real-world 13-file permit submission in ~170 seconds, demonstrating end-to-end feasibility with zero structural SHACL violations. - The missing: The entire evaluation rests on a single application case with no extraction accuracy metrics — there is no ground-truth comparison for any extracted field value, making it impossible to assess whether the pipeline actually extracts the right information. - Read it or not: AEC researchers or practitioners building digital permitting automation pipelines will find the OntoBPR + ICDD + hybrid-RAG integration architecture useful as a reference design; readers seeking quantitative evidence of utility should wait for a follow-up with proper evaluation.
Why this rank: Systems profile (PoC). Novelty (45) - schema-anchored extraction design is sensible integration. Rigor (20) - single case, no accuracy metrics, no ablations, no code release, design overfit to one example. Non-obviousness (40). Completeness (35) - authors honest about limitations. Resilience (25) - works on one case, generalization unknown. Impact (45) - reference design only. Composite: 0.15*45+0.15*20+0.10*40+0.15*35+0.15*25+0.30*45 = 35.0.
systems Novelty 45 Rigor 20 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 35 Resilience 25 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Building permit authorities receive heterogeneous, unstructured document bundles — scanned PDFs, BIM exports, spreadsheets, drawings — that resist automated reasoning, traceability, and completeness checking. A prior study found 65% of applications delayed by missing or erroneous documents. Existing digital permitting tools either perform isolated code checking or rely on document-centric exchange without semantic linking. The gap is a pipeline that can automatically convert a heterogeneous submission archive into a machine-interpretable, standards-compliant semantic container aligned with the OntoBPR ontology and ISO 21597 ICDD format, enabling downstream rule-based completeness checks and formal review. Key Idea: The key insight is to decouple LLM creativity from ontology structure: the LLM only fills values into a fixed JSON schema (never generates URIs or ontology terms), while all RDF vocabulary, URI patterns, and SHACL shapes remain under deterministic code control. This schema-anchored extraction eliminates ontology hallucination while still leveraging the LLM's ability to synthesize information from retrieved text chunks spanning multiple documents. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. (1) Ingestion & Preprocessing: a ZIP submission is staged into an ICDD directory layout; PDFs are text-extracted via pdfminer.six with Tesseract OCR fallback; text is chunked into overlapping segments stored as JSONL. (2) Hybrid Indexing & Retrieval: each chunk is embedded with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 and indexed for cosine similarity; a Neo4j graph records document-chunk containment and sequence; a dual retriever merges vector hits with graph-adjacent chunks into a single context window. (3) Semantic Extraction: Qwen 2.5-7B-Instruct receives the combined context and a fixed field_schema.py JSON schema; it returns schema-compliant JSON (application identifier, authority name, site address, building use type, document classification label); the JSON is deterministically mapped to OntoBPR RDF triples via rdflib. (4) ICDD Assembly & Validation: a linkset is generated (ISO 21597-2), the full RDF graph is validated with pySHACL against Container.shapes.ttl, and the container is zipped as a .icdd file. Critical design decisions: (a) fixed schema prevents hallucination but also hard-limits extractable information to the implemented subset — well-justified for a PoC; (b) Neo4j as a temporary graph adds significant infrastructure overhead (135.5 s of 169.7 s total) for relatively modest structural benefit — not yet justified quantitatively; (c) Qwen 2.5-7B-Instruct chosen for local deployment but no rationale for model size or comparison against alternatives. Key Results: The pipeline was run on one real-world case (Apl 25-04543-FULL): 13 files, 6.53 MB zipped, executed on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU. The pipeline produced a SHACL-compliant ISO 21597-1 container with no structural violations. The OntoBPR RDF graph was populated with a BuildingApplication individual and a handful of datatype properties (identifier, authority name, site address, building use type). Document classification (into OntoBPR categories) succeeded on ~39% of submitted documents (5 of 13), leaving 8 unclassified. Total runtime was 169.7 seconds, dominated by retrieval preparation (135.5 s, primarily Neo4j population and vector indexing). No extraction accuracy metrics are reported — correctness of extracted field values is stated qualitatively as "plausible." No baselines are compared. No ablation isolates the contribution of graph retrieval versus vector-only retrieval. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The combination of hybrid RAG with the OntoBPR + ICDD standards stack for automated building permit document processing is new to this domain. The individual components — hybrid RAG, ontology-guided LLM extraction, ICDD containers, OntoBPR — are all prior work; the genuine contribution is their integration into an end-to-end pipeline with schema-anchored extraction that prevents ontology hallucination. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are presented. No accuracy metrics for field extraction — correctness is assessed only qualitatively. No error bars, no multiple runs, no significance tests. The evaluation dataset is a single case; the authors explicitly acknowledge this is "indicative rather than statistically conclusive." The ACCORD dataset is mentioned in the acknowledgments as a source of annotated permit documents but is not used in the reported evaluation. No code or data release is mentioned. The pipeline was developed and evaluated on the same single case it was designed around — a clear risk of overfitting the design to that example. The 39% document classification rate is reported without a denominator-corrected or category-stratified breakdown. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built pipeline evaluated by running it on one real permit submission. - Practical: Requires an NVIDIA A100 GPU for local LLM inference and a running Neo4j instance, which exceeds typical permitting authority IT infrastructure; a cloud-hosted or quantized variant is not discussed. - Efficient: 135.5 of 169.7 total seconds are consumed by retrieval preparation (Neo4j population + vector indexing), a disproportionate overhead for processing a 6.53 MB archive — this does not scale gracefully to batch processing. - Effective: The pipeline generates structurally valid containers and extracts field values that the authors describe as plausible, but no ground-truth comparison exists, so actual correctness of extracted content is unverified. - Scalable: The system has been tested on exactly one submission; no batch, concurrent, or large-archive tests are reported; scalability is asserted as future work. The work delivers a functional software prototype (not yet released), not a reusable library or production tool. Strengths: - The schema-anchored extraction design is a practically sound engineering choice: by restricting the LLM to filling a fixed JSON schema and keeping all RDF vocabulary under deterministic control, the pipeline avoids the well-known ontology hallucination problem without fine-tuning. - Integration of two ISO/W3C standards (ISO 21597 ICDD and OntoBPR/OWL/SHACL) into a single automated pipeline is a concrete step toward interoperable digital permitting infrastructure that aligns with regulatory requirements. - The authors are transparent about the prototype's severe limitations — single case, no quantitative accuracy, restricted OntoBPR subset — which is a credibility-positive choice for a PoC paper. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) evaluation on a single case, so reported values are indicative only; (2) only a restricted OntoBPR subset is instantiated (no workflow stages, actors, decision processes, or building-code knowledge graphs); (3) IFC models are not yet semantically processed; (4) no quantitative evaluation of extraction quality; (5) generated containers still require expert review for legal use; (6) scalability and real-world pilot deployment are future work. The authors confront these limitations directly and at length, which is commendable. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The most critical omission is the absence of any extraction accuracy measurement — even manual annotation of 10 fields across one case would yield precision/recall numbers, but none are provided. The claim that the system "successfully" extracts information is unsubstantiated. - The contribution of graph-based (Neo4j) retrieval over vector-only retrieval is argued qualitatively ("suggests that structural information helps") but never measured. Given that Neo4j accounts for 80%+ of total runtime, an ablation is essential. - Qwen 2.5-7B-Instruct is used without comparison to alternatives (GPT-4o, Llama 3, or even Qwen 2.5-72B); model choice is not motivated. - The 39% document classification rate is presented without analysis of which categories fail and why — this is the only quasi-quantitative result and it is underexplored. - The paper does not address multilingual settings despite the test case containing mixed German/English terminology, which is common in German building permits. - Neo4j is framed as a temporary graph, yet it dominates runtime; the architectural rationale for Neo4j over a simpler in-memory structure is not given. Bottom Line: This is an early-stage, single-case proof-of-concept whose pipeline architecture — schema-anchored hybrid RAG feeding deterministic RDF mapping — is a reasonable design contribution for the AEC digital permitting niche, but without extraction accuracy metrics or ablations it does not yet provide evidence that the system works correctly, making it primarily useful as a design reference for researchers already operating in the OntoBPR/ICDD space.
Search Results
#156 — Incremental (32)

Evaluating Function Calling in Language Models through a Conversational Agent

vtechworks.lib.vt.edu · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a GenAI-powered chatbot (IPI Chatbot) that translates natural language queries into structured function calls against a spatiotemporal public health policy database (India Policy Insights), and systematically evaluates GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini function-routing accuracy across prompt specificity, difficulty, and query type. - Why it's important: Traditional geospatial dashboards require technical literacy to navigate; a natural language interface could democratize access to policy-relevant data for non-expert policymakers, NGOs, and the public in settings like India where such gaps are consequential. - How it does it: The LLM is given a registry of 13 predefined Python functions and extracts parameters from user queries to dispatch the correct function, which then executes SQL against a PostGIS/PostgreSQL database and returns text, charts, and map outputs. - The good: Spatial, classification, and constraint-based queries achieve 80–100% function-routing accuracy for both GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini, demonstrating that well-parameterized functions route reliably even for vague prompts. - The missing: No real-user evaluation is conducted — all test queries are researcher-authored, sample sizes are small (10 queries per condition per function), and accuracy is measured only at the function-routing level, not at end-to-end output correctness. - Read it or not: Read only if you are building an LLM-to-structured-function interface for a domain-specific dashboard and want a practical taxonomy of failure modes; the results are suggestive but the evaluation is too thin for strong empirical claims.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Rigor (25) is the dominant drag — 5–10 researcher-authored queries per condition, no error bars, no real users, routing-only metric that misses parameter-extraction errors. Novelty (35) low — standard LLM-function-calling stack applied to a policy dashboard, with the empirical taxonomy as the main contribution. Impact (40) modest: a deployed prototype exists but is explicitly not production-ready and limited to 13 hardcoded functions. Completeness (40) and Resilience (30) weak because the counter-intuitive 'harder=better' finding is an artifact of design and goes unexplored. Weighted: 0.15*35 + 0.15*25 + 0.10*35 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*30 + 0.30*40 ≈ 32.
systems Novelty 35 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 40 Resilience 30 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Interactive geospatial dashboards for public policy (e.g., health, education, sanitation indicators) are structurally inaccessible to non-technical users: they require knowledge of administrative geography, indicator terminology, and filter mechanics. The India Policy Insights (IPI) platform — developed at Harvard's Geographic Insights Lab — exposes 122 health and Social Determinants of Health indicators across 720 Indian districts and 543 parliamentary constituencies, but its complexity limits reach. The gap being addressed is the absence of deployable, evaluated conversational interfaces for policy-oriented geospatial dashboards in non-Western contexts; most prior conversational GIS work targets general-purpose spatial querying rather than structured, indicator-driven policy dashboards. Key Idea: The core idea is that LLM function calling (tool use) can serve as a reliable intent-to-API bridge for domain-specific geospatial dashboards, provided functions are designed with unambiguous, semantically distinct parameter signatures. Rather than generating SQL or code from scratch, the LLM selects among a finite, handcrafted set of 13 Python functions and extracts their arguments from natural language — with correctness depending entirely on accurate function routing. The insight that parameter explicitness and function semantic distinctness, not query complexity, govern routing accuracy is the main empirical finding. Methodology: The system is organized into three layers: (1) a React.js frontend with Mapbox maps and Chart.js visualizations; (2) a FastAPI backend that manages session context, token history, and OpenAI API calls; and (3) a PostGIS/PostgreSQL database storing IPI indicator data with geographic boundary geometries. The query pipeline proceeds as: user submits natural language query → FastAPI forwards to OpenAI API with structured prompt + registered function schemas → model determines whether a function call is needed and extracts parameters → Python function executes a PostGIS SQL query → results (attribute data + geometries) are returned as JSON → LLM generates a natural language summary → frontend renders text, chart, and map. The 13 functions span five categories: spatial (radius, border, state-extremes), temporal/comparative change, composite/multi-indicator, classification/similarity, and constraint-based. Evaluation measures function-routing accuracy — whether the correct function was triggered with valid arguments — across four dimensions: (1) model (GPT-4o vs. GPT-4o-mini), (2) prompt specificity (general/vague vs. specific/parameter-rich, 10 queries each), (3) difficulty (easy/medium/hard, 5 queries each tier, adapted from the ToolACE evaluation framework), and (4) query type (3 spatial vs. 9 non-spatial functions). Ground-truth function–query mappings were created by the research team. Key Results: GPT-4o achieves 84.2% accuracy on general prompts and 97.5% on specific prompts; GPT-4o-mini achieves 85% and 88% respectively, showing a far smaller specificity benefit. By difficulty tier, GPT-4o scores 90% / 93.3% / 95% (easy/medium/hard) and GPT-4o-mini scores 80% / 88.3% / 88.3% — counter-intuitively, accuracy rises with difficulty because harder prompts include more explicit parameters. Spatial functions (3 functions: districts within radius, border districts, state-wise extremes) achieve 80–100% accuracy for both models under all conditions. The most problematic functions are composite: "Multi Indicator Performance" and "State Multi Indicator Performance" — GPT-4o-mini achieves only 20–40% on specific queries for these, while GPT-4o achieves 100% and 80% respectively. Misrouting between semantically similar function pairs (e.g., multi-indicator performance vs. top/bottom districts) is the dominant error pattern. Evaluation used 10 queries per function per specificity condition and 5 per difficulty tier — very small samples, with no error bars or significance tests. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The system is a working proof-of-concept integration of LLM function calling with a geospatial policy dashboard, which has genuine practical novelty in the GIScience/public-health informatics context. The empirical finding — that parameter explicitness and function semantic distinctness govern routing accuracy more than query difficulty — is a specific, replicable observation. The technical components (LLM function calling, PostGIS, React/Mapbox stack) are standard combinations; the novelty lies in the domain application and the structured evaluation taxonomy. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are conducted on system components (e.g., the effect of function description wording, prompt structure, or session history management). Sample sizes are small: 10 queries per function per specificity condition (general/specific) and 5 per difficulty tier — for 12 functions, this yields 240 queries for specificity and 180 for difficulty, total. No error bars, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests are reported. Code is publicly available at GitHub (cga-harvard/GenAI-for-IPI). No human-authored real-user queries are included — all test prompts were created by the research team, introducing potential demand characteristics. The evaluation only measures function-routing accuracy at the intent-dispatch level; it does not assess whether retrieved data values, chart outputs, or map renderings are correct. The accuracy ground truth is a researcher-created mapping document. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: The system runs on commercially available hardware via OpenAI API calls; no specialized infrastructure is required beyond a PostGIS/PostgreSQL server. - Efficient: API costs for GPT-4o are substantially higher than GPT-4o-mini; no latency measurements, token usage statistics, or cost estimates per query are reported. - Effective: The system demonstrably routes queries to correct backend functions for explicit, well-scoped queries, and produces multi-modal outputs (text, chart, map); it fails on composite multi-indicator queries and has not been tested with real users, so effectiveness for its stated accessibility goal is undemonstrated. - Scalable: The system is limited to 13 hardcoded functions; scaling to more functions is acknowledged as likely to degrade routing accuracy due to semantic overlap, and no stress-test data are presented. The work delivers a functioning software artifact (deployed at ipipoc.netlify.app, with code on GitHub), though the prototype is explicitly described as not production-ready. Strengths: - The four-dimensional evaluation taxonomy (model × specificity × difficulty × query type) is more structured than typical GIS chatbot papers, which often report only aggregate accuracy. - The identification of semantic function overlap as the primary routing failure mode (not complexity) is a concrete, actionable insight for future system designers. - The modular architecture — decoupling indicator metadata, geographic data, and LLM orchestration — is well-described and plausibly generalizable to other national-scale policy dashboards. - The paper is candid about limitations and explicitly frames the work as a proof-of-concept rather than a production-ready system. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note: (1) evaluation restricted to only two proprietary OpenAI models with no open-source alternatives tested; (2) no user studies conducted to assess real-world usability or effectiveness for the stated accessibility goal; (3) hardcoded Python functions limit query coverage and will not scale without significant redesign; (4) production deployment requires stronger access controls, rate limiting, and multilingual support not yet implemented. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Beyond what the authors acknowledge: (a) all evaluation queries were researcher-authored, creating significant ecological validity risk — real users from diverse literacy backgrounds would generate queries with different vocabulary and structure; (b) accuracy is measured only at the function-routing level, not at output correctness — a correctly routed query with wrong parameter extraction still produces wrong results, and this is undetected by the evaluation metric; (c) with only 5 queries per difficulty tier per function, single misclassifications move accuracy by 20 percentage points — the granular per-function accuracy charts (Figures 5B/C, 6B/C) are thus unreliable for any individual function; (d) the counter-intuitive finding that accuracy increases with difficulty is an artifact of experimental design (harder prompts contain more parameters, making routing easier), not a genuine scaling phenomenon — this is underanalyzed; (e) no comparison to alternative conversational GIS systems or text-to-SQL baselines is provided. Bottom Line: This is an honest, well-scoped proof-of-concept with a useful failure taxonomy, but the evaluation sample sizes are too small and the evaluation metric too shallow (routing-only, no real-user queries) to support confident claims about practical utility; practitioners building similar systems will find the design patterns and failure modes instructive, but should not treat the accuracy numbers as reliable benchmarks.
Citations
#157 — Incremental (26)

ChemMambaFlow: Graph-Enhanced Mamba with Latent Flow Matching for Molecular Property Prediction and Conformation Generation

chemrxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ChemMambaFlow, a unified framework combining a graph-attention + Mamba state-space encoder with a latent-space flow matching conformation generator and a contribution-aware multi-modal fusion module for simultaneous molecular property prediction and 3D conformation generation. - Why it's important: Accurate prediction of molecular properties and physically plausible 3D conformations are foundational bottlenecks in computational drug discovery, where errors propagate directly to expensive wet-lab synthesis decisions. - How it does it: The core trick is serializing GNN-produced atom embeddings into a sequence that feeds a selective state-space model (Mamba), capturing long-range inter-atomic dependencies, while a conditional flow-matching ODE in latent space generates 3D coordinates without the many denoising steps required by diffusion models. - The good: The encoder ablation (Table 2) concretely demonstrates that both the GAT and Mamba components contribute: GAT-only achieves 84.9/0.468/0.0213, Mamba-only 86.2/0.452/0.0201, and the combined model 88.1/0.423/0.0176 on HIV/ESOL/QM9, providing mechanistic support for the joint design. - The missing: Conformation generation is evaluated exclusively via an unblinded, non-standard human Likert-scale study (3 unidentified raters, 200 samples) with no standard COV-R/MAT-R metrics on GEOM-QM9 or GEOM-DRUGS, and current baselines (ETFlow NeurIPS 2024, SemlaFlow, CoFM) are absent from the comparison table. - Read it or not: Skip for practitioners tracking conformation generation progress — the conformation evaluation is non-standard and missing current baselines; potentially useful background for researchers interested in hybrid GNN+SSM encoders for property prediction, but the paper's credibility is undermined by a reference list containing numerous irrelevant citations (smart car rental parking, agricultural credit scoring, vulnerability analysis) that signal paper-mill practices.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (20) and Resilience (20) collapse the composite — conformation generation is evaluated only by an unblinded 3-rater Likert study with no COV-R/MAT-R on GEOM, current baselines (ETFlow, SemlaFlow, CoFM) are absent, the QM9 target property is unidentified making the headline MAE uninterpretable, canonical atom ordering and SE(3)-equivariance through a non-equivariant Mamba backbone are unaddressed, the fusion regularizer contradicts its stated goal, no limitations section exists, and the reference list contains citations to car rental parking and agricultural credit scoring papers consistent with paper-mill practices. Composite is pulled down by every dimension.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 20 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 20 Resilience 20 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Molecular property prediction (activity, solubility, quantum chemical properties) and 3D conformation generation are central tasks in computational chemistry and structure-based drug design. GNN-based methods model local atomic neighborhoods well but struggle with long-range pharmacophore patterns that span many bonds. Diffusion-based conformation generators achieve good geometry but require hundreds of denoising steps at inference. No prior unified method simultaneously addresses both limitations by combining selective state-space modeling (for long-range dependencies) with flow matching (for efficient generative modeling) in a single trainable framework. Multi-modal fusion of graph topology and spectroscopic data (NMR/IR) further remains underexplored. Key Idea The single core insight is a two-stage encoder: first apply multi-layer graph attention to extract local neighborhood embeddings, then serialize those node representations via canonical atom ordering and feed them through a Mamba selective state-space model (SSM). The SSM's input-dependent gating selectively propagates information across the full atom sequence, giving each atom access to distant context without the quadratic cost of full self-attention. A conditional flow-matching ODE in a low-dimensional latent space then generates conformations by solving a straight-path ODE rather than a reverse diffusion chain. Methodology The pipeline has three modules. (1) Graph-Enhanced Mamba Encoder: L layers of multi-head graph attention aggregate local neighborhoods using attention coefficients that incorporate bond-type edge features (Eq. 2); node representations are then serialized by canonical atom numbering and processed by the Mamba SSM with input-dependent B, C, and Δ matrices (Eqs. 5–8); local and global outputs are fused via residual addition and layer normalization (Eq. 9); a gated sum pooling produces the molecular-level embedding (Eq. 10). Critical design decision: canonical atom ordering is used to linearize the graph — this is order-dependent and the choice is not ablated. (2) Latent Flow Matching Conformation Generator: an SE(3)-equivariant VAE encoder maps the molecular graph to a latent distribution (Eqs. 11–13, KL regularization); a time-conditioned vector field v_θ is trained to match straight interpolation paths between a Gaussian prior and the target latent (Eq. 14); conformations are decoded by solving the ODE with an Euler integrator and a decoder D_ψ (Eq. 15). How SE(3)-equivariance is preserved through the non-equivariant Mamba backbone is not addressed. (3) Contribution-Aware Multi-Modal Fusion: graph and spectroscopic (1D CNN) embeddings are projected to a shared space; a learned gating mechanism computes per-modality weights w_g and w_s = 1 − w_g (Eq. 18); a regularization loss (Eq. 22) penalizes deviation of each weight from 0.5, which actively suppresses adaptive weighting and contradicts the stated design goal. Training combines prediction loss, flow-matching loss, KL divergence, and the fusion regularizer with empirically set weights λ₁=0.1, λ₂=0.01, λ₃=0.05. Key Results On MoleculeNet: HIV classification 88.1% AUROC (+1.8% over Uni-Mol at 86.3%), ESOL 0.423 RMSE (-6.2% over Uni-Mol), FreeSolv 0.648 RMSE (-3.6%), Lipophilicity 0.518 RMSE (-4.6%). On QM9: 0.0176 MAE (-11.1% over Uni-Mol at 0.0198) — the specific QM9 target property is not identified (QM9 has 12 distinct quantum-chemical targets). Baselines include SchNet, D-MPNN, Graphormer, MolBERT, and Uni-Mol; Uni-Mol is a strong recent baseline. For conformation generation, human expert ratings on 200 conformations give 4.43/5 overall, versus 4.21 for GeoDiff, 4.03 for ConfGF, and 3.76 for RDKit ETKDG — but these are the only four baselines evaluated and the raters are unidentified. GEOM-QM9/GEOM-DRUGS COV-R/MAT-R metrics, standard in this literature since GeoDiff, are not reported. FreeSolv (642 molecules) and ESOL (1,128 molecules) are small enough that variance between splits can drive apparent improvements. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET. The combination of a graph-attention + SSM hybrid encoder with latent-space flow matching for joint property prediction and conformation generation is genuinely new as an integrated framework; however, each individual component (graph attention, Mamba SSMs, flow matching in latent space) is drawn directly from prior work, and the integration is the primary contribution. ChemSpectra (15,000 molecule-spectrum pairs) is a new dataset but is author-curated with no external validation or release details. Rigor & Reproducibility Three independent runs with different random seeds are reported, which is minimal but present. Scaffold splitting is used for MoleculeNet, which is appropriate. An ablation on the encoder (Table 2) isolates GAT vs. Mamba vs. combined. A fusion strategy ablation (Figure 4) compares graph-only, spectrum-only, concatenation, attention, and contribution-aware variants on ChemSpectra. No ablation tests the flow matching module in isolation (e.g., replacing with diffusion) against matched baselines. No code repository is mentioned. ChemSpectra is described but not released publicly. The human evaluation of conformations uses 3 unidentified evaluators with no inter-rater reliability statistics reported. A significant credibility concern: the reference list contains numerous citations with zero relevance to molecular ML — smart car rental parking monitoring [42], agricultural credit evaluation [43], financial document tampering [41], credit fraud detection [5], software vulnerability analysis [4], smart car monitoring — alongside speech enhancement papers [31, 32]. This pattern is characteristic of citation-ring or paper-mill practices and warrants scrutiny of the full experimental pipeline. This is a preprint posted on ChemRxiv and has not been peer reviewed.
